# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper 1)]**
*Define General Purpose RAM in a microcontroller. How is it different from Special Function Registers (SFRs)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** General Purpose RAM (GP-RAM) is the volatile, byte-addressable read/write memory region in a microcontroller used by application software to store temporary data, stack frames, heap variables, and runtime computational results. It is uniformly addressable, meaning any byte can be read or written in a single memory cycle.

**Difference from SFRs (1 mark):** Unlike GP-RAM, SFRs are hardware-mapped registers with dedicated addresses tied to specific peripherals (UART, Timer, Port, etc.). Writing to an SFR directly controls hardware, whereas writing to GP-RAM only stores data without any hardware side effect.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Supplementary)]**
*Why is SRAM preferred over DRAM for on-chip General Purpose RAM in microcontrollers?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Point 1 (1 mark):** SRAM cells are built from 6 transistors forming a bi-stable latch that retains data as long as V<sub>CC</sub> is applied, without any periodic refresh operation. DRAM cells use a single capacitor that leaks charge and must be refreshed every ~64 ms.

**Point 2 (1 mark):** Because no refresh cycles are needed, the CPU is not interrupted, giving deterministic access times — critical for real-time embedded systems.

**Point 3 (1 mark):** SRAM is fully compatible with standard CMOS logic processes used to fabricate the microcontroller, allowing the memory array, CPU, and peripherals to be integrated on a single die with a unified manufacturing flow.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — Track 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper 2)]**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a 6-transistor (6T) SRAM cell used in microcontroller General Purpose RAM. Describe the read and write operations in detail.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A microcontroller has 12 address lines dedicated to its internal General Purpose RAM. Calculate the total addressable GP-RAM size in (i) bytes, (ii) kilobytes, and (iii) bits. If each memory cell stores 1 bit, how many 6T cells are required to build this memory?* **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Cell architecture (3 marks):**

A 6T SRAM cell consists of:
- **Two cross-coupled CMOS inverters (M1–M4):** M1 (PMOS) and M2 (NMOS) form inverter 1; M3 (PMOS) and M4 (NMOS) form inverter 2. The output of inverter 1 drives the input of inverter 2 and vice-versa, creating a **bi-stable latch** with two stable states (Q = 0 or Q = 1).
- **Two access NMOS transistors (M5, M6):** M5 connects the storage node Q to the bit line BL; M6 connects node Q-bar to bit line BL-bar. Both gates are tied to the **Word Line (WL)**.
- **Two storage nodes (Q and Q-bar):** These are the complementary outputs of the latch, holding the bit value and its complement.

**Step 2 — Read operation (2 marks):**

1. Pre-charge both BL and BL-bar to V<sub>DD</sub>/2 using pre-charge transistors.
2. Activate the Word Line; access transistors M5, M6 turn ON.
3. The cell (depending on stored state) pulls one bit line slightly higher and the other slightly lower, creating a small differential voltage ΔV.
4. A **sense amplifier** detects ΔV and amplifies it to a full-swing logic level, driving the data bus.
5. Deactivate the Word Line to isolate the cell; the latch state is preserved.

**Step 3 — Write operation (2 marks):**

1. The write driver forces BL and BL-bar to the desired complementary levels (e.g., BL = 1, BL-bar = 0 for writing `1`).
2. The Word Line is activated; access transistors connect the strong write drivers to the storage nodes.
3. The write drivers overpower the weak latch, forcing the cross-coupled inverters to flip (or hold) into the new state.
4. The Word Line is deactivated; the cell now holds the new bit indefinitely without refresh.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameter (1 mark):**

The internal GP-RAM uses $n = 12$ address lines.

**Step 2 — Compute byte-addressable locations (2 marks):**

$$
C_{bytes} = 2^{n} = 2^{12}
$$

$$
C_{bytes} = 4096 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kilobytes (2 marks):**

$$
C_{KB} = \frac{2^{12}}{2^{10}} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total bits (1 mark):**

$$
C_{bits} = 8 \times 4096 = 32{,}768 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute number of 6T cells (1 mark):**

Since each cell stores 1 bit, the number of 6T cells equals the number of bits:

$$
N_{cells} = 32{,}768 \text{ cells}
$$

The total number of transistors in the memory array is:

$$
N_{transistors} = 6 \times 32{,}768 = 196{,}608 \text{ transistors}
$$

---

### QUESTION B — Track 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3]**

**(a)** *Explain the memory organization of the 8051 microcontroller. Differentiate between the Lower 128 bytes of GP-RAM, the Upper 128 bytes of SFRs, and the 16-byte bit-addressable region.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive the relationship between the number of address lines and the addressable memory size of GP-RAM. A system designer wants 8 KB of GP-RAM. Calculate the minimum number of address lines required and the size of the row decoder needed.* **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — 8051 memory map overview (2 marks):**

The 8051 microcontroller has a **Harvard architecture** with separate address spaces for Program Memory (code) and Data Memory (variables). The internal data memory is organized as **256 bytes (00H–FFH)** further divided into:

- **Lower 128 bytes (00H–7FH):** General Purpose RAM.
- **Upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH):** Special Function Registers (SFRs).

**Step 2 — Lower 128 bytes GP-RAM structure (2 marks):**

| Sub-region | Address Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Register Banks 0–3 | 00H – 1FH | 4 banks × 8 registers (R0–R7) |
| Bit-addressable | 20H – 2FH | 16 bytes = 128 individually addressable bits |
| General purpose scratch pad | 30H – 7FH | 80 bytes of free user RAM |

**Step 3 — Upper 128 bytes SFR region (2 marks):**

The upper 128 bytes from 80H–FFH are **not general purpose**. They are reserved for SFRs that control peripherals: P0 (80H), SP (81H), DPL/DPH (82H–83H), PCON (87H), TCON (88H), TMOD (89H), TL0/TH0 (8AH–8BH), TL1/TH1 (8CH–8DH), SCON (98H), SBUF (99H), IE (A8H), IP (B8H), Port registers (P0–P3), and so on. SFRs with addresses ending in `0` or `8` (e.g., 80H, 88H, 90H, 98H…) are also **bit-addressable**.

**Step 4 — Bit-addressable region significance (1 mark):**

The 16 bytes from 20H to 2FH (128 bits total) can be accessed either as full bytes or as individual bits using bit-manipulation instructions (e.g., `SETB 20H.0`, `CLR 2FH.7`). This enables single-bit boolean operations without read-modify-write overhead.

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — General derivation (3 marks):**

Let $n$ be the number of address lines. Each line carries 1 bit, giving $2^{n}$ unique binary patterns. Each pattern selects one unique byte. Therefore:

$$
C_{bytes} = 2^{n}
$$

To express capacity in bits:

$$
C_{bits} = 8 \times 2^{n} = 2^{n+3}
$$

To express in kilobytes (using $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 = 2^{10}$ bytes):

$$
C_{KB} = \frac{2^{n}}{2^{10}} = 2^{n-10}
$$

**Step 2 — Given requirement (1 mark):**

Required GP-RAM capacity $= 8 \text{ KB} = 8 \times 1024 = 8192 \text{ bytes}$.

**Step 3 — Solve for n (2 marks):**

$$
2^{n} = 8192
$$

$$
2^{n} = 2^{13}
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 13 \text{ address lines}}
$$

**Step 4 — Decoder size (1 mark):**

A row decoder must accept $n = 13$ input lines and assert exactly one of $2^{13} = 8192$ word lines. Therefore the decoder size is:

$$
\text{Decoder} = 13\text{-to-}8192 \text{ line decoder}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing GP-RAM with Program Memory:** Students frequently mix up Flash/ROM (non-volatile code storage) with SRAM (volatile data). Always state explicitly which is which.
> 2. **Forgetting the 8-bit byte width:** When asked for memory size, convert $2^{n}$ to **bytes** (not bits) unless the question specifies bits.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps:** Even for a "simple" formula like $2^{n}$, KTU examiners award partial marks only if the *counting principle* is shown. A bare answer `4096` with no working = 0 marks.
> 4. **Mixing 1 KB = 1000 and 1 KB = 1024:** In digital electronics and KTU papers, **1 KB = 1024 bytes (2¹⁰)**, not 1000. Wrong unit = mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring bit-addressable nature:** The 8051's 16-byte bit-addressable region (20H–2FH) gives 128 individual bit addresses — a high-yield KTU question. Omit this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Claiming DRAM is used in MCUs:** It is not, in standard 8051/ARM Cortex-M devices. The correct technology for on-chip GP-RAM is **SRAM**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **GP-RAM = volatile, byte-addressable, freely R/W** scratchpad memory inside the MCU.
- **Built from 6T SRAM cells** — bi-stable latch + 2 access transistors, **no refresh needed**.
- **Capacity formula:** $C_{bytes} = 2^{n}$ where $n$ = number of internal data address lines.
- **8051 layout:** 128 B GP-RAM (00H–7FH) = 4 register banks + 16 B bit-addressable + 80 B scratch pad; 128 B SFR (80H–FFH) = hardware-mapped.
- **Bit-addressable regions:** 20H–2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits) in lower GP-RAM; SFRs with addresses ending in 0 or 8 in upper region.
- **SRAM vs DRAM:** SRAM has 6T cell, no refresh, faster; DRAM has 1T+1C cell, needs ~64 ms refresh, denser. **SRAM is used on-chip**, DRAM off-chip.
- **Access time:** $t_{ACC} = t_{decode} + t_{cell} + t_{sense}$; faster sensing with smaller ΔV but worse noise margin.
- **Volatility threshold:** Data lost when V<sub>CC</sub> falls below V<sub>retention</sub> (~0.6 V for CMOS).
- **Energy per access:** $E_{bit} \propto C_{eff} \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ — lower supply voltage = lower dynamic power.
- **Applications:** stack, heap, peripheral buffers, interrupt context saves, runtime variables.
- **KTU hot keywords to memorize:** *6T cell, bi-stable latch, bit-addressable, register banks, SFR, scratch pad, Harvard architecture, volatile, random access, pre-charge, sense amplifier.*
- **Common 3-mark question:** "Why SRAM for on-chip GP-RAM?" — answer in 3 points: no refresh, deterministic timing, CMOS-compatible.
- **Common 7-mark question:** Draw and explain 6T SRAM cell read/write OR derive memory size from address lines with numerical substitution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bit Addressable RAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bit Addressable RAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller architecture**, **Bit Addressable RAM** refers to specific regions of the internal data memory where every individual bit can be independently accessed, manipulated, set, cleared, or tested using dedicated single-bit instructions. This feature is a hallmark of the 8051 family and is one of the most powerful constructs in embedded systems programming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> *"Bit Addressable RAM is that portion of the internal RAM of the 8051 microcontroller (bytes 20H to 2FH) and certain Special Function Registers (SFRs) whose byte addresses end in 0H or 8H, where each of the 128 individual bits can be directly addressed using bit addresses 00H to 7FH (for RAM) and 80H to FFH (for SFRs)."*

### Memory Map Snapshot (8051 Internal Data Memory)

| Address Range (Hex) | Size (Bytes) | Region Name | Addressability |
|---|---|---|---|
| `00H - 1FH` | **32 bytes** | Register Banks (R0–R7, 4 banks) | Byte only |
| `20H - 2FH` | **16 bytes** | **Bit-Addressable RAM** | **Bit AND Byte** |
| `30H - 7FH` | **80 bytes** | General Purpose RAM | Byte only |
| `80H - FFH` | **128 bytes** | Special Function Registers (SFRs) | Mostly byte; **16 SFRs are bit-addressable** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine the 8051's internal RAM as a large **apartment building**:

- Most apartments (`30H – 7FH`) are **standard studios**. You can only rent/inspect the whole apartment (byte-level access).
- The lower 32 apartments (`00H – 1FH`) are **group housing** with shared facilities (Register Banks). You access a *whole room* (R0–R7) at a time.
- The middle 16 apartments (`20H – 2FH`) are **special duplex units**. Each duplex has **8 individual rooms** that can be locked, unlocked, or rented **independently** — this is the **Bit-Addressable RAM**.
- The top floor (`80H – FFH`) houses **luxury penthouses** (SFRs). Some of these penthouses have a special design with **8 independent switches each** (bit-addressable SFRs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the 8051 bother with bit-addressability?**
> Because embedded systems frequently deal with **boolean signals** — a switch pressed or not, a motor ON/OFF, a flag raised or cleared, a relay energized or not. Instead of wasting an entire 8-bit byte to store a single 0 or 1, the 8051 lets you store **128 such single-bit flags** in just 16 bytes, saving precious on-chip RAM.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Total Bit-Addressable bits in internal RAM (20H–2FH):** $16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = \mathbf{128 \text{ bits}}$
- **Bit Address range for RAM bits:** `00H – 7FH`
- **Bit-Addressable SFR count:** **16 SFRs**
- **Bit Address range for SFR bits:** `80H – FFH`
- **Total single-bit addressable locations in 8051:** $\mathbf{256 \text{ bits}}$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** *"128 bits in RAM, 128 bits in SFRs — Total 256 bits of single-bit power!"*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Bit-Addressable Regions

### Region A: Internal RAM Bit-Addressable Zone (20H – 2FH)

The 8051 dedicates **16 contiguous bytes** from address `20H` to `2FH` exclusively for bit-level storage. Each of these 16 bytes has a **unique byte address** (e.g., `2AH`) and each of the 128 bits within this zone has a **unique bit address** (e.g., bit `53H`).

### Region B: Bit-Addressable SFRs

Only those SFRs whose **byte addresses end in 0H or 8H** (i.e., divisible by 8) are bit-addressable. There are exactly **16 such SFRs**.

| # | SFR Name | Byte Address | Bit Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `P0` | 80H | 80H – 87H |
| 2 | `TCON` | 88H | 88H – 8FH |
| 3 | `P1` | 90H | 90H – 97H |
| 4 | `SCON` | 98H | 98H – 9FH |
| 5 | `P2` | A0H | A0H – A7H |
| 6 | `IE` | A8H | A8H – AFH |
| 7 | `P3` | B0H | B0H – B7H |
| 8 | `IP` | B8H | B8H – BFH |
| 9 | `PSW` | D0H | D0H – D7H |
| 10 | `ACC (A)` | E0H | E0H – E7H |
| 11 | `B` | F0H | F0H – F7H |

*(Note: 4 SFRs in the 80H–FFH range like SP=81H, DPL=82H, etc., are NOT bit-addressable because their addresses don't end in 0H or 8H.)*

## 2.2 Bit Address Calculation Methodology (KTU Favourite!)

Given any bit position in the bit-addressable RAM (20H–2FH), the bit address is computed as:

$$
\text{Bit Address} = (\text{Byte Address} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + \text{Bit Position}
$$

Where:
- $\text{Byte Address} \in [20\text{H}, 2\text{FH}]$
- $\text{Bit Position} \in [0, 7]$ (D0 = LSB, D7 = MSB)

### Worked Calculation Examples

**Example 1:** Find the bit address of bit position D3 of byte `2AH`.

$$
\text{Bit Address} = (2\text{AH} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 3
$$

$$
= (0\text{AH}) \times 8 + 3 = 10 \times 8 + 3 = 80 + 3 = 83\text{H}
$$

**Example 2:** Find the bit address of D7 of byte `25H`.

$$
\text{Bit Address} = (25\text{H} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 7
$$

$$
= (05\text{H}) \times 8 + 7 = 5 \times 8 + 7 = 40 + 7 = 47\text{H}
$$

## 2.3 Instructions Used With Bit-Addressable RAM

The 8051 provides a powerful set of single-bit instructions that operate ONLY on the bit-addressable region:

| Mnemonic | Operation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `SETB bit` | Set bit to 1 | `SETB 2AH.3` |
| `CLR bit` | Clear bit to 0 | `CLR 0E7H` (ACC.7) |
| `MOV bit, C` | Copy Carry flag into bit | `MOV 2AH.0, C` |
| `MOV C, bit` | Copy bit into Carry | `MOV C, 2AH.0` |
| `ANL C, bit` | AND Carry with bit | `ANL C, /2AH.0` |
| `ORL C, bit` | OR Carry with bit | `ORL C, /2AH.0` |
| `CPL bit` | Complement bit | `CPL 2AH.3` |
| `JB bit, rel` | Jump if bit set | `JB P1.7, LABEL` |
| `JNB bit, rel` | Jump if bit clear | `JNB P1.7, LABEL` |
| `JBC bit, rel` | Jump if bit set & clear it | `JBC 2AH.3, LABEL` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bit-addressable RAM is the *backbone of single-bit I/O control*. A program can simply execute `SETB P1.0` to turn ON an LED connected to Port 1 pin 0, without disturbing the other 7 pins of Port 1.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Size of bit-addressable RAM | $16 \text{ bytes}$ | $20\text{H} - 2\text{FH}$ |
| Total bits in bit-addressable RAM | $16 \times 8 = 128 \text{ bits}$ | Bit addr $00\text{H} - 7\text{FH}$ |
| Bit Address Calculation | $(B - 20\text{H}) \cdot 8 + P$ | $B \in [20\text{H}, 2\text{FH}], P \in [0,7]$ |
| SFR bit-addressability rule | Byte address ends in 0H or 8H | 16 SFRs qualify |
| Total 8051 bit-addressable bits | $128 + 128$ | $= 256 \text{ bits}$ |
| Bit-Address range (SFR) | $80\text{H} - \text{FFH}$ | Maps to SFR bits |
| Reverse Lookup (bit → byte) | Byte $= 20\text{H} + \lfloor \text{bit}/8 \rfloor$ | For RAM bits only |
| Reverse Lookup (bit → pos) | Position $= \text{bit} \bmod 8$ | Within that byte |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Students confuse the **bit address** with the **byte address**. The bit address `00H` refers to the LSB (D0) of byte `20H`, NOT the byte address `00H` (which is R0 of Bank 0).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reverse Bit Address → Byte Address + Position

Given an arbitrary bit address in the range `00H` to `7FH`, derive the (byte address, bit position) pair.

### Setup

Let $\beta$ = the bit address, with $\beta \in [00\text{H}, 7\text{FH}]$.

We need to find:
- $B$ = Byte address in the range $[20\text{H}, 2\text{FH}]$
- $P$ = Bit position in the range $[0, 7]$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Convert the bit address $\beta$ into its position relative to the bit-addressable RAM start.

The starting bit address is `00H` which corresponds to byte `20H`, bit position `0`.
Each new byte adds 8 to the bit address.

$$
\text{Byte Offset} = \left\lfloor \frac{\beta}{8} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the bit position within that byte.

$$
P = \beta \bmod 8
$$

**Step 3:** Convert the byte offset back to the byte address by adding to the base address `20H`.

$$
B = 20\text{H} + \text{Byte Offset}
$$

**Step 4:** Combine.

$$
\boxed{B = 20\text{H} + \left\lfloor \frac{\beta}{8} \right\rfloor, \quad P = \beta \bmod 8}
$$

### Worked Reverse Example

**Problem:** A bit instruction uses the bit address `4DH`. Identify the byte address and bit position.

**Step 1:** Compute byte offset.

$$
\text{Byte Offset} = \left\lfloor \frac{4\text{DH}}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{77}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 9.625 \right\rfloor = 9
$$

**Step 2:** Compute bit position.

$$
P = 4\text{DH} \bmod 8 = 77 \bmod 8 = 77 - 9 \times 8 = 77 - 72 = 5
$$

**Step 3:** Compute byte address.

$$
B = 20\text{H} + 9 = 29\text{H}
$$

**Verification using forward formula:**

$$
\text{Bit Address} = (29\text{H} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 5 = 9 \times 8 + 5 = 72 + 5 = 77 = 4\text{DH} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Bit address `4DH` corresponds to **byte `29H`, bit position D5**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Addressable Bit Space

### Given

- Internal RAM bit-addressable zone: 16 bytes from `20H` to `2FH`
- Bit-addressable SFRs: 16 SFRs (each byte-addressable bit-addressable SFR contributes 8 bits)

### Derivation

$$
N_{\text{bits, RAM}} = \text{Size of zone} \times \text{Bits per byte}
$$

$$
N_{\text{bits, RAM}} = 16 \times 8 = 128 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
N_{\text{bits, SFR}} = N_{\text{SFR, bit-addr}} \times 8 = 16 \times 8 = 128 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{total bits}} = 128 + 128 = 256 \text{ bits}}
$$

### Equivalent in Bytes

$$
\text{Equivalent byte space} = \frac{256}{8} = 32 \text{ bytes}
$$

This means the 8051 can store up to 256 boolean flags in a memory space equivalent to just **32 bytes** — a **4× memory efficiency** over byte-level flag storage.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bit Address Lookup Table (KTU Quick Reference)

This is a **high-yield KTU exam matrix**. Memorize the boundary bit addresses:

| Byte Address (Hex) | D7 | D6 | D5 | D4 | D3 | D2 | D1 | D0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `20H` | 07H | 06H | 05H | 04H | 03H | 02H | 01H | **00H** |
| `21H` | 0FH | 0EH | 0DH | 0CH | 0BH | 0AH | 09H | **08H** |
| `22H` | 17H | 16H | 15H | 14H | 13H | 12H | 11H | **10H** |
| `23H` | 1FH | 1EH | 1DH | 1CH | 1BH | 1AH | 19H | **18H** |
| `24H` | 27H | 26H | 25H | 24H | 23H | 22H | 21H | **20H** |
| `25H` | 2FH | 2EH | 2DH | 2CH | 2BH | 2AH | 29H | **28H** |
| `26H` | 37H | 36H | 35H | 34H | 33H | 32H | 31H | **30H** |
| `27H` | 3FH | 3EH | 3DH | 3CH | 3BH | 3AH | 39H | **38H** |
| `28H` | 47H | 46H | 45H | 44H | 43H | 42H | 41H | **40H** |
| `29H` | 4FH | 4EH | 4DH | 4CH | 4BH | 4AH | 49H | **48H** |
| `2AH` | 57H | 56H | 55H | 54H | 53H | 52H | 51H | **50H** |
| `2BH` | 5FH | 5EH | 5DH | 5CH | 5BH | 5AH | 59H | **58H** |
| `2CH` | 67H | 66H | 65H | 64H | 63H | 62H | 61H | **60H** |
| `2DH` | 6FH | 6EH | 6DH | 6CH | 6BH | 6AH | 69H | **68H** |
| `2EH` | 77H | 76H | 75H | 74H | 73H | 72H | 71H | **70H** |
| `2FH` | **7FH** | 7EH | 7DH | 7CH | 7BH | 7AH | 79H | **78H** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit address 7FH is the highest bit-addressable bit in internal RAM**, located at byte `2FH`, position D7.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: SFR Bit Address Generation

For a bit-addressable SFR with byte address `S` (where `S` ends in 0H or 8H), the bit address of bit position `P` is:

$$
\boxed{\text{SFR Bit Address} = S + P}
$$

### Example

For SFR `ACC (A)` with byte address `E0H`:

- Bit `ACC.0` (D0) → bit address = `E0H + 0 = E0H`
- Bit `ACC.7` (D7) → bit address = `E0H + 7 = E7H`

For SFR `PSW` with byte address `D0H`:

- Bit `PSW.0` (D0) → bit address = `D0H`
- Bit `PSW.7` (D7) → bit address = `D7H`

> [!NOTE]
> This is why `CLR 0E7H` clears the MSB of the Accumulator, and `SETB 0D3H` sets bit D3 of PSW (which is the `RS0` register select bit).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Internal Data Memory Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeStart["8051 Internal Data Memory 00H to FFH 256 Bytes Total"] --> nodeA["00H to 1FH 32 Bytes Register Banks R0 to R7 4 Banks of 8 Bytes"]
    nodeStart --> nodeB["20H to 2FH 16 Bytes BIT ADDRESSABLE RAM 128 Individual Bits"]
    nodeStart --> nodeC["30H to 7FH 80 Bytes General Purpose Scratchpad RAM"]
    nodeStart --> nodeD["80H to FFH 128 Bytes Special Function Registers SFRs"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Byte 20H Bits 00H to 07H"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Byte 21H Bits 08H to 0FH"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Byte 2AH Bits 50H to 57H Example Favourite"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Byte 2FH Bits 78H to 7FH Final Byte"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["16 SFRs Bit Addressable Addresses ending in 0H or 8H"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["112 SFR Bytes Byte Addressable Only"]

    nodeD1 --> nodeD1a["P0 80H Bits 80H to 87H"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1b["TCON 88H Bits 88H to 8FH"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1c["P1 90H Bits 90H to 97H"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1d["SCON 98H Bits 98H to 9FH"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1e["ACC E0H Bits E0H to E7H"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeD1f["B F0H Bits F0H to F7H"]

    nodeD2 --> nodeD2a["SP 81H Not Bit Addressable"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2b["DPL 82H Not Bit Addressable"]
    nodeD2 --> nodeD2c["TMOD 89H Not Bit Addressable"]
```

## 4.2 Bit Addressing Process — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInst["Assembly Instruction Example SETB 2AH 3"] --> nodeDec{"Decoder Logic Determines Operand Type"}
    nodeDec --> nodeRAM["Operands 00H to 7FH Map to RAM Bits 20H to 2FH"]
    nodeDec --> nodeSFR["Operands 80H to FFH Map to SFR Bits"]

    nodeRAM --> nodeCalc["Byte Address equals 20H plus floor of bit divided by 8"]
    nodeCalc --> nodeFinal1["For 2AH bit 3 Byte 2AH Bit Position D3 RAM Cell"]

    nodeSFR --> nodeDirect["Bit Address minus Byte Address equals Position"]
    nodeDirect --> nodeFinal2["For E0H bit E0H Position D0 of SFR ACC"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Why Bit Addressability Matters

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeReal["Real World Boolean Signal Examples"] --> nodeEx1["Switch Pressed or Released"]
    nodeReal --> nodeEx2["LED ON or OFF"]
    nodeReal --> nodeEx3["Motor Running or Stopped"]
    nodeReal --> nodeEx4["Alarm Triggered or Reset"]

    nodeEx1 --> nodeEff["Bit Addressable RAM Stores 128 Flags in 16 Bytes"]
    nodeEx2 --> nodeEff
    nodeEx3 --> nodeEff
    nodeEx4 --> nodeEff

    nodeEff --> nodeCompare["Comparison Standard RAM would need 128 Bytes to store 128 Flags"]

    nodeEff --> nodeWin["8x Memory Efficiency for Boolean Storage"]
    nodeCompare --> nodeWin

    nodeWin --> nodeUse["Engineering Result Compact Fast Single Bit Control Code"]
```

## 4.4 Sample SFR Bit Map — Port P1 (Byte Address 90H)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1["P1 Byte Address 90H Bit Addressable SFR"] --> nodeP17["Bit P1 7 Bit Address 97H"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP16["Bit P1 6 Bit Address 96H"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP15["Bit P1 5 Bit Address 95H"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP14["Bit P1 4 Bit Address 94H"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP13["Bit P1 3 Bit Address 93H"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP12["Bit P1 2 Bit Address 92H"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP11["Bit P1 1 Bit Address 91H"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP10["Bit P1 0 Bit Address 90H"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the elegant symmetry: `P1.0` has bit address = byte address itself (`90H`). This is a direct consequence of the "addresses ending in 0H or 8H" rule — every bit-addressable SFR has its D0 bit coincident with its byte address.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 3 Marks]**
> **"What is bit-addressable RAM in the 8051 microcontroller? Mention its address range and total capacity in bits."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** Bit-addressable RAM is the portion of the 8051's internal data memory in which each individual bit can be independently accessed, set, cleared, or tested using dedicated single-bit instructions.

**Address Range (1 Mark):** The bit-addressable RAM occupies internal data memory addresses `20H to 2FH`, which corresponds to **16 bytes**.

**Total Bit Capacity (1 Mark):** Total bits = $16 \times 8 = \mathbf{128 \text{ bits}}$, with bit addresses ranging from `00H` to `7FH`.

> [!TIP]
> KTU expects the answer in three crisp parts: *Definition → Address Range → Bit Count*. Missing any one results in losing 1 mark.

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / 3 Marks]**
> **"List any six Special Function Registers that are bit-addressable in the 8051."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The SFRs whose byte addresses end in `0H` or `8H` are bit-addressable. Any six of the following (0.5 Mark each):

1. `P0` — 80H
2. `TCON` — 88H
3. `P1` — 90H
4. `SCON` — 98H
5. `P2` — A0H
6. `IE` — A8H
7. `P3` — B0H
8. `IP` — B8H
9. `PSW` — D0H
10. `ACC (A)` — E0H
11. `B` — F0H

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the bit-addressable RAM organization of the 8051 microcontroller. With a neat diagram, show how the 16 bytes from `20H` to `2FH` map to 128 individual bits. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the bit address `5EH`, determine the corresponding byte address and bit position. Show the derivation. Also, write a sample 8051 assembly code snippet that toggles `P1.5` continuously using bit-addressable instructions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**(a) Bit-Addressable RAM Organization (7 Marks):**

**Memory Map Description (2 Marks):**

The 8051 internal RAM contains 128 bytes of user-accessible memory from `00H` to `7FH`. Of these:

- `00H–1FH` (32 bytes): Four register banks (Bank 0–3), each containing 8 registers R0–R7.
- `20H–2FH` (16 bytes): **Bit-addressable zone**.
- `30H–7FH` (80 bytes): General-purpose scratchpad RAM.

**Bit Mapping Logic (3 Marks):**

Each byte in the range `20H–2FH` is subdivided into 8 individually addressable bits. The relationship between byte address $B$ and bit address $\beta$ is:

$$
\beta = (B - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + P
$$

where $P$ is the bit position (0 = D0, 7 = D7). The bit addresses run sequentially from `00H` (bit D0 of byte `20H`) to `7FH` (bit D7 of byte `2FH`).

**Mapping Table (2 Marks):**

| Byte | D7 | D6 | D5 | D4 | D3 | D2 | D1 | D0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `20H` | 07 | 06 | 05 | 04 | 03 | 02 | 01 | 00 |
| `21H` | 0F | 0E | 0D | 0C | 0B | 0A | 09 | 08 |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| `2FH` | 7F | 7E | 7D | 7C | 7B | 7A | 79 | 78 |

---

**(b) Bit Address Decoding + Assembly Code (7 Marks):**

**Given:** Bit address $\beta = 5\text{EH} = 94$ in decimal.

**Step 1 — Compute byte offset (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Byte Offset} = \left\lfloor \frac{5\text{EH}}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{94}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 11.75 \right\rfloor = 11
$$

**Step 2 — Compute bit position (2 Marks):**

$$
P = 5\text{EH} \bmod 8 = 94 - 11 \times 8 = 94 - 88 = 6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute byte address (1 Mark):**

$$
B = 20\text{H} + 11 = 2\text{BH}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
(2\text{BH} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 6 = 11 \times 8 + 6 = 88 + 6 = 94 = 5\text{EH} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Result:** Bit address `5EH` corresponds to **byte `2BH`, bit position D6**.

**Assembly Code (2 Marks):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV SP, #70H          ; Initialize stack pointer

LOOP:   CPL P1.5              ; Toggle bit P1.5 (bit address 95H)
        ACALL DELAY           ; Call delay subroutine
        SJMP LOOP             ; Repeat forever

DELAY:  MOV R0, #0FFH         ; Simple delay loop
D1:     MOV R1, #0FFH
D2:     DJNZ R1, D2
        DJNZ R0, D1
        RET

        END
```

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the internal RAM structure of the 8051 microcontroller. Highlight the bit-addressable region and the bit-addressable SFRs. Explain why certain SFRs are bit-addressable while others are not. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the bit addresses of the following: **(i)** D4 of byte `2CH`, **(ii)** D0 of byte `28H`, **(iii)** D7 of SFR ACC. Hence determine the byte address and bit position of the bit address `6AH`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution:

**(a) Internal RAM Structure & Bit-Addressable SFRs (7 Marks):**

**Internal RAM Layout (3 Marks):**

The 8051 internal data memory has 256 bytes (`00H–FFH`):
- `00H–7FH`: User RAM (128 bytes)
- `80H–FFH`: SFR space (128 bytes)

Within user RAM, the bit-addressable zone `20H–2FH` (16 bytes) provides 128 individual bit locations (`00H–7FH`).

**Bit-Addressable SFRs (3 Marks):**

Out of 21+ SFRs, only those whose byte addresses end in `0H` or `8H` are bit-addressable. This is a **hardware design choice** — only SFRs at addresses divisible by 8 allow clean bit-level decoding. The 16 bit-addressable SFRs include: P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, PSW, ACC, B (and 5 others at C8H, D8H, etc.).

**Why some SFRs are not bit-addressable (1 Mark):**

SFRs like `SP (81H)`, `DPL (82H)`, `DPH (83H)`, `TMOD (89H)`, `TH0 (8CH)`, etc., have addresses not ending in `0H` or `8H`. Since the hardware bit-decoder needs 8 consecutive aligned addresses (one per bit) to map without overlap, only aligned addresses qualify.

---

**(b) Bit Address Computations (7 Marks):**

**(i)** Bit address of D4 of byte `2CH` (2 Marks):

$$
\beta = (2\text{CH} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 4
$$

$$
= (0\text{CH}) \times 8 + 4 = 12 \times 8 + 4 = 96 + 4 = 100 = 64\text{H}
$$

**Answer:** Bit address = `64H`.

**(ii)** Bit address of D0 of byte `28H` (2 Marks):

$$
\beta = (28\text{H} - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + 0 = 8 \times 8 + 0 = 64 = 40\text{H}
$$

**Answer:** Bit address = `40H`.

**(iii)** Bit address of D7 of SFR ACC (1 Mark):

ACC byte address = `E0H`, so D7 bit address = `E0H + 7 = E7H`.

**Answer:** Bit address = `E7H`.

**(iv)** Byte + Position of bit address `6AH` (2 Marks):

$$
\text{Byte Offset} = \left\lfloor \frac{6\text{AH}}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{106}{8} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 13.25 \right\rfloor = 13
$$

$$
P = 6\text{AH} \bmod 8 = 106 - 13 \times 8 = 106 - 104 = 2
$$

$$
B = 20\text{H} + 13 = 2\text{DH}
$$

**Answer:** Bit address `6AH` is **byte `2DH`, bit position D2**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing `2AH` (byte address) with `2AH` (bit address)** — In the range `20H–2FH`, both interpretations are mathematically valid for different contexts. Always state which.
> 2. **Forgetting the offset** — Writing `(2CH - 20H) = CH = 12` correctly but then mistakenly adding the bit position *before* multiplying by 8. Order of operations: multiply first, then add.
> 3. **Ignoring that `D0` is the LSB** — In the formula, position 0 corresponds to `D0` (LSB), not `D7`. Reversing this flips your answer by 7.
> 4. **SFR bit addresses in `80H–FFH`** — Some students mistakenly write `SETB 80H` thinking it is RAM bit address; it actually sets bit P0.0 of Port 0.
> 5. **Decimal vs Hexadecimal confusion** — Always convert to decimal before arithmetic, then express the result in hex.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ⭐ Bit-addressable RAM occupies **internal RAM bytes `20H` to `2FH`** (16 bytes total).
- ⭐ These 16 bytes yield **128 individually addressable bits**, with bit addresses `00H` to `7FH`.
- ⭐ **Forward formula:** $\text{Bit Address} = (B - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + P$
- ⭐ **Reverse formula:** $B = 20\text{H} + \lfloor \beta / 8 \rfloor, \quad P = \beta \bmod 8$
- ⭐ **16 SFRs are bit-addressable** because their byte addresses end in `0H` or `8H`.
- ⭐ SFR bit addresses occupy the range `80H` to `FFH`.
- ⭐ **Total single-bit addressable memory in 8051 = 256 bits** (128 RAM + 128 SFR).
- ⭐ Bit-addressable SFRs include: `P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, PSW, ACC, B,` and 5 others (`T2CON=C8H`, `RCAP2L=CAH`, `RCAP2H=CBH`, `TL2=CCH`, `TH2=CDH`).
- ⭐ SFRs like `SP (81H)`, `DPL (82H)`, `DPH (83H)`, `PCON (87H)`, `TMOD (89H)` are **NOT** bit-addressable.
- ⭐ Single-bit instructions include: `SETB`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `MOV bit,C`, `MOV C,bit`, `ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`.
- ⭐ Bit addressing enables **8× memory efficiency** for boolean flag storage.
- ⭐ **Engineering applications:** switch debouncing flags, relay control, LED control, motor ON/OFF, alarm latches, status flags, software timers.
- ⭐ The MSB of bit-addressable RAM is at **byte `2FH`, position D7, bit address `7FH`**.
- ⭐ The MSB of ACC (D7) has bit address `E7H` — often used as a custom flag by programmers.
- ⭐ The LSB of `PSW (D0)` has bit address `D0H` — this is the **Parity flag** `P`.
- ⭐ KTU frequently asks reverse lookup (bit addr → byte+position) — practice it thoroughly.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic: Bit Addressable RAM**
> This concludes the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 premium notes for *Bit Addressable RAM* from Module 1 of *PCEOT501 - Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems*. Practice the worked examples and the reverse-lookup derivation multiple times to secure full marks in the KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Function Registers (SFRs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Function Registers (SFRs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are dedicated, on-chip memory-mapped registers inside the **8051 microcontroller** that occupy the upper internal RAM address space from **0x80 to 0xFF** (128 byte locations). Unlike general-purpose RAM, SFRs are *not* used to store user data; instead, they act as the **control, configuration, status, and data interface registers** between the CPU and the microcontroller's integrated peripherals (I/O ports, timers, serial port, interrupt system, etc.).

In the standard 8051 architecture, only **21 of the 128 SFR addresses are physically implemented**. Each SFR has a fixed 8-bit address, a symbolic name defined by the manufacturer, and a precise functional role. Certain SFRs (those whose byte addresses end in **0H or 8H**) are additionally **bit-addressable**, meaning any of their 8 bits can be individually set, cleared, or tested using single-bit instructions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Cockpit of a Fighter Jet":**
> Imagine the 8051 CPU as a **pilot**, and the microcontroller chip as the **fighter jet**. The pilot cannot directly touch the engines, radar, or weapons. Instead, every system on the jet is wired to a labelled **switch, dial, or gauge on the cockpit dashboard**. The pilot flips switches to *configure* the engine, reads gauges to *monitor* fuel, and pushes buttons to *trigger* weapons.
>
> **SFRs ARE that dashboard.**
> - `P0`, `P1`, `P2`, `P3` → the **throttle levers and control surfaces** (I/O ports)
> - `TMOD`, `TCON`, `TH0`, `TL0` → the **engine RPM and timer dials**
> - `SCON`, `SBUF` → the **radio transmitter/receiver dials**
> - `IE`, `IP` → the **warning light and priority panel**
> - `ACC`, `B`, `PSW`, `SP`, `DPTR` → the **pilot's own hand-controls and instruments**
>
> When your assembly instruction says `MOV P1, #0xFF`, it is the same as the pilot pushing the throttle lever to its maximum position. The CPU never directly touches the pin; it writes a value into the **SFR memory location**, and the microcontroller's hardware translates that into voltage on the physical pin.

## 1.3 Key Quantitative Facts (Bolded Highlights)

- **SFR Address Range:** **0x80 – 0xFF** (decimal **128 – 255**)
- **Total SFR Bytes Available:** **128 bytes**
- **Implemented (used) SFRs in standard 8051:** **21 registers**
- **Bit-addressable SFRs:** **11 registers** (addresses ending in 0H or 8H)
- **Total bit-addressable locations in SFR area:** **11 × 8 = 88 bits** (addresses 0x80–0xFF mapped as individual bits)
- **Stack Pointer default value after reset:** **0x07**
- **Program Status Word reset value:** **0x00**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Theory: The 8051 Memory Map

The 8051 has a **Harvard-style separated** program and data memory, but the data memory has two distinct zones:

| Zone | Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|------|---------------|------|---------|
| Lower RAM | 0x00 – 0x7F | 128 bytes | General-purpose user RAM + register banks |
| Upper RAM / SFR | 0x80 – 0xFF | 128 bytes | **Special Function Registers** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** Although the lower 128 bytes and the upper 128 bytes are at distinct addresses, the standard 8051 has only **128 bytes of physical RAM**. The upper 128-byte range (0x80–0xFF) is a **separate address space** accessed only by *direct addressing mode*. Trying to access 0x80–0xFF using *indirect* (R0/R1) addressing actually returns a re-mapped access to the *lower* 128 bytes (this is a common KTU trick question).

## 2.2 Classification of SFRs

### (A) CPU / Core Registers

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addr? | Reset Value | Function |
|-----|---------|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **ACC** (A) | **0xE0** | Yes | 0x00 | Accumulator — primary ALU operand |
| **B** | **0xF0** | Yes | 0x00 | Used in MUL / DIV secondary operand |
| **PSW** | **0xD0** | Yes | 0x00 | Program Status Word (flags) |
| **SP** | **0x81** | No | **0x07** | Stack Pointer (points to top of stack) |
| **DPH** | **0x83** | No | 0x00 | Data Pointer High byte |
| **DPL** | **0x82** | No | 0x00 | Data Pointer Low byte |
| **DPTR** (DPH+DPL) | 0x82/83 | No | 0x0000 | 16-bit pointer for MOVX, code-table lookups |

### (B) I/O Port Latch Registers

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addr? | Function |
|-----|---------|-----------|----------|
| **P0** | **0x80** | Yes | Port 0 latch (also multiplexed low-byte address bus) |
| **P1** | **0x90** | Yes | Port 1 latch (general-purpose bidirectional I/O) |
| **P2** | **0xA0** | Yes | Port 2 latch (high-byte address bus) |
| **P3** | **0xB0** | Yes | Port 3 latch (alternate functions: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |

### (C) Timer/Counter Registers

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addr? | Function |
|-----|---------|-----------|----------|
| **TMOD** | **0x89** | No | Timer Mode register (selects mode 0/1/2/3 for T0, T1) |
| **TCON** | **0x88** | Yes | Timer Control register (run flags + overflow flags + edge flags) |
| **TL0** | **0x8A** | No | Timer 0 low byte |
| **TH0** | **0x8C** | No | Timer 0 high byte |
| **TL1** | **0x8B** | No | Timer 1 low byte |
| **TH1** | **0x8D** | No | Timer 1 high byte |

### (D) Serial Port Registers

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addr? | Function |
|-----|---------|-----------|----------|
| **SCON** | **0x98** | Yes | Serial Control (mode + flags) |
| **SBUF** | **0x99** | No | Serial Buffer (write = transmit, read = receive) |
| **PCON** | **0x87** | No | Power Control (includes SMOD baud-rate doubler bit) |

### (E) Interrupt System Registers

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addr? | Function |
|-----|---------|-----------|----------|
| **IE** | **0xA8** | Yes | Interrupt Enable (master + 5 sources) |
| **IP** | **0xB8** | Yes | Interrupt Priority (high/low per source) |

## 2.3 The Program Status Word (PSW) — Bit-By-Bit Decoded

The PSW at **0xD0** is the most-examined SFR in KTU papers. Its bit layout is:

| Bit | Name | Address (bit) | Meaning |
|-----|------|---------------|---------|
| 7 | **CY** | 0xD7 | **Carry flag** |
| 6 | **AC** | 0xD6 | **Auxiliary carry** (carry between bits 3→4, used for BCD) |
| 5 | **F0** | 0xD5 | User-defined flag 0 (general purpose) |
| 4 | **RS1** | 0xD4 | Register bank select bit 1 |
| 3 | **RS0** | 0xD3 | Register bank select bit 0 |
| 2 | **OV** | 0xD2 | **Overflow flag** |
| 1 | – | 0xD1 | Reserved (undefined) |
| 0 | **P** | 0xD0 | **Parity flag** (set if ACC has odd number of 1s — even parity) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Register Bank Selection Formula (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):**
>
> Bank = (RS1 × 2) + (RS0 × 1)
>
> | RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | RAM Address |
> |-----|-----|---------------|-------------|
> | 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 0x00 – 0x07 |
> | 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 0x08 – 0x0F |
> | 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 0x10 – 0x17 |
> | 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 0x18 – 0x1F |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for SFRs:**

| # | Formula / Rule | Purpose |
|---|----------------|---------|
| 1 | $\text{Bit Address (SFR)} = \text{Byte Address} + \text{Bit Position}$ | Maps a bit in an SFR to its unique 0x80–0xFF bit address |
| 2 | $\text{Register Bank} = (RS1 \cdot 2) + (RS0 \cdot 1)$ | Determines active R0–R7 bank |
| 3 | $\text{Stack grows UPWARD: } SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1$ | 8051 stack pushes *increment* SP then write |
| 4 | $P = (ACC \bmod 2 == 1) \rightarrow 1$ | Parity flag set when ACC has odd number of 1s |
| 5 | $\text{SFR byte count available} = 128$ | Range 0x80–0xFF |
| 6 | $\text{Bit-addressable SFRs} = 11 \rightarrow 88 \text{ bits}$ | Total individual bit addresses in SFR area |
| 7 | $\text{Reset vector: } PC \leftarrow 0x0000$ | Execution always begins at ROM address 0x0000 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

SFRs are the foundation of **bare-metal embedded programming**. Every industrial 8051 application — from washing-machine controllers and automotive dashboard ECUs to medical infusion pumps and IoT sensor nodes — manipulates SFRs either directly (assembly) or indirectly (via C header files like `<reg51.h>` or `<mcs51/8051.h>`). Understanding the SFR memory map is essential for:
- Configuring timers for **PWM motor control**
- Setting up **UART** for sensor communication
- Enabling **interrupts** for real-time response
- Driving **LCD/Keypad matrices** through ports
- Optimizing power via **PCON (idle/power-down) modes**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bit Address Calculation for an SFR

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Find the unique bit-address of `PSW.5` (the F0 flag).
>
> **Conceptual Logic:** In the 8051, bits inside the 16 SFRs whose byte addresses are multiples of 8 (i.e., end in 0H or 8H) are assigned a unique 8-bit address in the range 0x80–0xFF. The mapping rule is:
>
> $$\text{BitAddress} = \text{ByteAddress} + \text{BitPosition}$$
>
> where BitPosition ranges 0 (LSB) to 7 (MSB).

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\text{PSW byte address} = 0\text{xD0} = 208_{10}$$

$$\text{Bit position of F0} = 5$$

$$\text{BitAddress} = 0\text{xD0} + 0\text{x05}$$

$$\text{BitAddress} = 0\text{xD5}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each of the 16 bit-addressable SFRs contributes 8 consecutive bit addresses. For PSW starting at 0xD0, the 8 bit addresses are 0xD0, 0xD1, …, 0xD7. So PSW.5 = 0xD5. The assembler therefore accepts the instruction `SETB 0xD5` as equivalent to `SETB F0`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Verification of the Register Bank Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** If `RS1 = 1` and `RS0 = 0` in the PSW, which register bank is active, and what is the RAM address of R3 in that bank?

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\text{Bank Number} = (RS1 \cdot 2) + (RS0 \cdot 1)$$

$$\text{Bank Number} = (1 \cdot 2) + (0 \cdot 1) = 2$$

$$\text{Base Address of Bank 2} = 2 \times 8 = 16_{10} = 0\text{x}10$$

$$\text{Address of R3} = \text{Base} + 3 = 0\text{x}10 + 0\text{x}03 = 0\text{x}13$$

**Conversion logic:** The 32 bytes of lower RAM (0x00–0x1F) are divided into four 8-byte banks. Bank *n* begins at address $n \times 8$. Within that bank, R0–R7 occupy offsets 0–7 from the base.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stack Pointer After Pushing Two Bytes

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** After reset, SP = 0x07. The CPU executes two `PUSH` instructions (e.g., `PUSH ACC` and `PUSH PSW`). What is the final SP value, and at what RAM address does the second byte reside?

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$\text{SP}_{initial} = 0\text{x}07$$

$$\text{After 1st PUSH: } SP_1 = 0\text{x}07 + 1 = 0\text{x}08$$
$$\text{ACC value is stored at RAM } 0\text{x}08$$

$$\text{After 2nd PUSH: } SP_2 = 0\text{x}08 + 1 = 0\text{x}09$$
$$\text{PSW value is stored at RAM } 0\text{x}09$$

$$\text{Final SP} = 0\text{x}09$$

**Conversion logic:** Unlike the 8085 or x86, the 8051 stack **grows upward** (toward higher addresses). Each push first increments SP, then stores the byte at the new SP location. This is a classic KTU short-answer question.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Parity Flag Computation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** After executing `MOV A, #0x3A`, what is the state of the P flag in PSW?

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$ACC = 0\text{x}3A = 0011\,1010_2$$

$$\text{Number of 1s} = 4 \text{ (even)}$$

$$\text{Parity rule: } P = 1 \text{ if number of 1s is ODD (even parity generation)}$$

$$\text{Number of 1s} = 4 \rightarrow \text{EVEN} \rightarrow P = 0$$

**Conversion logic:** The 8051 uses **even parity** convention. The P flag is set (=1) only when the ACC contains an *odd* count of 1-bits. The hardware auto-updates P after every instruction that writes to ACC (e.g., `ADD`, `MOV A,...`, `INC A`).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Map of Bit-Addressable SFRs

| SFR | Byte Addr | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|-----|-----------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| **P0** | 0x80 | P0.7 | P0.6 | P0.5 | P0.4 | P0.3 | P0.2 | P0.1 | P0.0 |
| **TCON** | 0x88 | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
| **P1** | 0x90 | P1.7 | P1.6 | P1.5 | P1.4 | P1.3 | P1.2 | P1.1 | P1.0 |
| **SCON** | 0x98 | SM0 | SM1 | SM2 | REN | TB8 | RB8 | TI | RI |
| **P2** | 0xA0 | P2.7 | P2.6 | P2.5 | P2.4 | P2.3 | P2.2 | P2.1 | P2.0 |
| **IE** | 0xA8 | EA | – | – | ES | ET1 | EX1 | ET0 | EX0 |
| **P3** | 0xB0 | P3.7 | P3.6 | P3.5 | P3.4 | P3.3 | P3.2 | P3.1 | P3.0 |
| **IP** | 0xB8 | – | – | – | PS | PT1 | PX1 | PT0 | PX0 |
| **PSW** | 0xD0 | CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | – | P |
| **ACC** | 0xE0 | ACC.7 | ACC.6 | ACC.5 | ACC.4 | ACC.3 | ACC.2 | ACC.1 | ACC.0 |
| **B** | 0xF0 | B.7 | B.6 | B.5 | B.4 | B.3 | B.2 | B.1 | B.0 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of 8051 SFR Subsystems

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions between the CPU, the SFR bus, and the on-chip peripherals** that each SFR controls.

```mermaid
graph TB
    cpu["CPU Core 8051"]
    sfrBus["Internal SFR Bus 0x80 to 0xFF"]
    cpu --> sfrBus
    
    sfrBus --> coreGroup["CPU Core Registers"]
    sfrBus --> portGroup["I O Port Group"]
    sfrBus --> timerGroup["Timer Counter Group"]
    sfrBus --> serialGroup["Serial Port Group"]
    sfrBus --> intGroup["Interrupt Group"]
    sfrBus --> pwrGroup["Power Control"]
    
    coreGroup --> acc["ACC 0xE0"]
    coreGroup --> bReg["B 0xF0"]
    coreGroup --> psw["PSW 0xD0"]
    coreGroup --> sp["SP 0x81"]
    coreGroup --> dptr["DPTR 0x82 0x83"]
    
    portGroup --> p0["P0 0x80"]
    portGroup --> p1["P1 0x90"]
    portGroup --> p2["P2 0xA0"]
    portGroup --> p3["P3 0xB0"]
    p0 --> p0pins["Pins 0 to 7 Physical Port 0"]
    p1 --> p1pins["Pins 0 to 7 Physical Port 1"]
    p2 --> p2pins["Pins 0 to 7 Physical Port 2"]
    p3 --> p3pins["Pins 0 to 7 Physical Port 3 with Alt Functions"]
    
    timerGroup --> tmod["TMOD 0x89"]
    timerGroup --> tcon["TCON 0x88"]
    timerGroup --> th0["TH0 0x8C"]
    timerGroup --> tl0["TL0 0x8A"]
    timerGroup --> th1["TH1 0x8D"]
    timerGroup --> tl1["TL1 0x8B"]
    tmod --> tcon
    tcon --> th0
    tcon --> th1
    tcon --> tl0
    tcon --> tl1
    th0 --> ovf0["Timer 0 Overflow to CPU"]
    th1 --> ovf1["Timer 1 Overflow to CPU"]
    
    serialGroup --> scon["SCON 0x98"]
    serialGroup --> sbuf["SBUF 0x99"]
    serialGroup --> pconReg["PCON 0x87"]
    scon --> sbuf
    sbuf --> txpin["TXD Pin P3.1"]
    sbuf --> rxpin["RXD Pin P3.0"]
    pconReg --> scon
    
    intGroup --> ie["IE 0xA8"]
    intGroup --> ip["IP 0xB8"]
    ie --> cpu
    ip --> ie
    
    pwrGroup --> pconMod["PCON Idle and Power Down Bits"]
    pconMod --> cpu
```

## 4.2 SFR Memory-Map Schematic (Linear Address View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    lowRam["Lower RAM 0x00 to 0x7F 128 bytes"]
    regBanks["Register Banks 0x00 to 0x1F"]
    bitAddr["Bit Addressable 0x20 to 0x2F"]
    scratch["Scratchpad 0x30 to 0x7F"]
    sfrArea["SFR Area 0x80 to 0xFF 128 bytes"]
    portGrp["Port Group 0x80 0x90 0xA0 0xB0"]
    tmrGrp["Timer Group 0x88 0x89 0x8A 0x8B 0x8C 0x8D"]
    srlGrp["Serial Group 0x98 0x99 0x87"]
    intGrp2["Interrupt Group 0xA8 0xB8"]
    cpuGrp["CPU Group 0xE0 0xF0 0xD0 0x81 0x82 0x83"]
    
    lowRam --> regBanks
    lowRam --> bitAddr
    lowRam --> scratch
    sfrArea --> portGrp
    sfrArea --> tmrGrp
    sfrArea --> srlGrp
    sfrArea --> intGrp2
    sfrArea --> cpuGrp
```

## 4.3 Data Flow — Writing a Value to a Port

```mermaid
graph LR
    instr["Assembly Instruction MOV P1 0xFF"]
    decode["Instruction Decode Unit"]
    busWr["Internal SFR Bus Write Cycle"]
    p1reg["P1 SFR Latch 0x90"]
    outputDriver["Output Driver Stage"]
    physicalPin["Physical Pin P1.0 to P1.7 on Chip"]
    
    instr --> decode
    decode --> busWr
    busWr --> p1reg
    p1reg --> outputDriver
    outputDriver --> physicalPin
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Special Function Registers (SFRs) in 8051. Mention the SFR address range and list any four important SFRs with their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

Special Function Registers (SFRs) are the dedicated on-chip registers in the 8051 microcontroller used to control the CPU, I/O ports, timers, serial port, and interrupt system. They occupy the internal RAM address range **0x80 to 0xFF** (128 byte locations), of which only 21 addresses are physically implemented.

Four important SFRs:

| SFR | Address | Function |
|-----|---------|----------|
| ACC | 0xE0 | Accumulator — primary operand of all arithmetic/logic operations |
| B | 0xF0 | Secondary operand for MUL and DIV instructions |
| PSW | 0xD0 | Holds arithmetic flags (CY, AC, OV, P) and register bank select bits |
| SP | 0x81 | Stack Pointer — tracks the top of the on-chip stack (reset to 0x07) |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supple Exam]**
**What is the significance of the PSW register? Explain the function of CY, AC, OV, and P flags.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Program Status Word (PSW) at address 0xD0** is a bit-addressable SFR that holds the status flags of the ALU and controls the active register bank.

| Flag | Bit | Function |
|------|-----|----------|
| **CY** (Carry) | PSW.7 | Set when an arithmetic operation generates a carry out of bit 7 (addition) or borrow (subtraction) |
| **AC** (Auxiliary Carry) | PSW.6 | Set when there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4; used for BCD arithmetic correction (DA A) |
| **OV** (Overflow) | PSW.2 | Set when a signed arithmetic result is out of range; for signed bytes, indicates that the result cannot fit in -128 to +127 |
| **P** (Parity) | PSW.0 | Automatically set to 1 if the ACC contains an odd number of 1-bits (even parity convention) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the 8051 internal RAM with a neat diagram. How is the SFR area different from the general-purpose RAM area? List all bit-addressable SFRs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the PSW register bit-by-bit. Given that the contents of ACC = A3H and the previous carry = 0, find the contents of the PSW after executing the instruction `ADD A, #5CH`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The 8051 internal data memory (RAM) is 128 bytes wide, located at addresses **0x00 to 0x7F**. It is logically divided into three zones:

1. **Register Banks (0x00 – 0x1F):** Four banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7). Bank selection is done by RS1, RS0 bits of PSW.
2. **Bit-Addressable Area (0x20 – 0x2F):** 16 bytes = 128 individual bit-addressable locations (bit addresses 0x00–0x7F).
3. **General-Purpose Scratchpad (0x30 – 0x7F):** 80 bytes of user RAM for stack and variables.

The **SFR area (0x80 – 0xFF)** lies *above* the 128-byte RAM but is part of the same logical address space. It is accessed only via **direct addressing mode**. The SFR area controls the peripheral hardware; general-purpose RAM stores user data.

**Bit-addressable SFRs (11 total):**
P0 (0x80), TCON (0x88), P1 (0x90), SCON (0x98), P2 (0xA0), IE (0xA8), P3 (0xB0), IP (0xB8), PSW (0xD0), ACC (0xE0), B (0xF0).

**Model Solution for (b):**

PSW bit layout (from MSB D7 to LSB D0):

| D7 | D6 | D5 | D4 | D3 | D2 | D1 | D0 |
|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | – | P |

**Performing the addition:**

$$A = 0\text{xA3} = 1010\,0011_2$$
$$\text{Addend} = 0\text{x}5C = 0101\,1100_2$$

$$\text{Binary addition:}$$
$$1010\,0011$$
$$+\,0101\,1100$$
$$= 1111\,1111_2 = 0\text{xFF}$$

**Flag evaluation:**

- **CY:** No carry out of bit 7 → CY = 0
- **AC:** Bit 3 of A = 1, bit 3 of addend = 1, sum bit 4 = 1. Carry from bit 3→4? Yes (1+1 = 0 with carry 1) → **AC = 1**
- **OV:** Carry into bit 7 = 0, carry out of bit 7 = 0 → OV = 0
- **P:** Result ACC = 0xFF = 1111 1111, eight 1s (even) → **P = 0**

**Final PSW contents:** `0 1 0 0 0 0 0 0` = **0x40**

(RS1, RS0, F0, P all remain 0; only AC = 1.)

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of the following SFRs in the 8051: (i) SP, (ii) DPTR, (iii) PCON, (iv) IE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the function of TMOD and TCON registers? If TMOD = 0x21 and TH1 = 0xF3, calculate the time delay generated by Timer 1 in Mode 2, assuming an 11.0592 MHz crystal. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Stack Pointer (SP, 0x81):**
A single-byte register that points to the top of the on-chip stack in internal RAM. After reset, SP = **0x07**. Each `PUSH` increments SP first, then stores the byte; each `POP` reads first, then decrements SP. The stack grows **upward** toward higher RAM addresses.

**(ii) Data Pointer (DPTR, 0x82/0x83):**
A 16-bit register formed by DPH (0x83) and DPL (0x82). It is used to access **external data memory** via the `MOVX` instructions and to perform **code-table lookups** using `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. It can be loaded as one 16-bit word: `MOV DPTR, #data16`.

**(iii) Power Control (PCON, 0x87):**
A non-bit-addressable SFR that controls power-saving modes. Its SMOD bit (PCON.7) **doubles the baud rate** of the serial port. Bits IDL (PCON.0) and PD (PCON.1) place the 8051 into **Idle** or **Power-Down** modes, halting the CPU but optionally keeping timers/interrupts running.

**(iv) Interrupt Enable (IE, 0xA8):**
A bit-addressable SFR that globally and individually enables the 8051's five interrupt sources. **EA (IE.7)** is the master enable; ET2, ET1, ET0 enable timer overflows; EX1, EX0 enable external interrupts; ES enables the serial port interrupt.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**TMOD (0x89):** A non-bit-addressable SFR that selects the **mode of operation** (Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3) and the **function** (timer or counter) for Timer 0 and Timer 1. The high nibble (bits 4–7) configures Timer 1, the low nibble (bits 0–3) configures Timer 0.

**TCON (0x88):** A bit-addressable SFR that contains the **timer run control bits** (TR0, TR1), the **overflow flag bits** (TF0, TF1), and the **external interrupt edge/flag bits** (IT0, IE0, IT1, IE1).

**Mode 2 of Timer 1** is an **8-bit auto-reload timer**. TH1 holds the auto-reload value; TL1 is the actual counter that increments with each timer tick. When TL1 overflows from 0xFF to 0x00, TF1 is set, and TL1 is automatically reloaded from TH1.

**Given:**
- TMOD = 0x21 → Timer 1 in Mode 2 (M1=1, M0=0), Timer 0 in Mode 1 (M1=0, M0=1)
- TH1 = 0xF3 = 243 decimal
- Crystal frequency = 11.0592 MHz

**Step 1 — Machine cycle frequency:**

$$f_{MC} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921.6 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Timer tick period:**

$$T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{1}{921.6 \times 10^3} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Number of timer counts before overflow:**

$$N = 256 - TH1 = 256 - 243 = 13 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 4 — Time delay:**

$$T_{delay} = N \times T_{tick} = 13 \times 1.085\,\mu s$$

$$T_{delay} = 14.105\,\mu s$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract TH1 from 256** — Mode 2 is *auto-reload 8-bit*, so the count to overflow is $256 - TH1$, not simply $TH1$. Marks deducted: -2.
> 2. **Using $12 \times f_{osc}$** instead of $f_{osc}/12$ for machine-cycle frequency — common sign error. Marks deducted: -1.
> 3. **Confusing Timer 0 with Timer 1** — TMOD's *high* nibble controls Timer 1. With TMOD = 0x21, students often analyze Timer 0 (low nibble) by mistake. Marks deducted: -2.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU strictly requires "$\mu s$" or "ms". Marks deducted: -1.
> 5. **Forgetting that Mode 2 auto-reloads** — many students assume Mode 1 (16-bit) behavior, which would give 65536 − 243 counts, an entirely wrong answer. Marks deducted: -3.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SFRs (Module 1):**
>
> - **SFR location:** 0x80 – 0xFF (128 bytes), only **21 implemented**
> - **SFRs are accessed ONLY by direct addressing** (e.g., `MOV A, 0x80`)
> - **Bit-addressable SFRs:** 11 registers (byte address ends in 0H or 8H)
> - **Bit address formula:** $\text{BitAddr} = \text{ByteAddr} + \text{BitPos}$ (range 0x80–0xFF)
> - **ACC (0xE0)** → primary ALU operand, 8-bit accumulator
> - **B (0xF0)** → used only in MUL AB and DIV AB
> - **PSW (0xD0)** → flags + register bank select
>   - **CY** = carry, **AC** = auxiliary carry (BCD), **OV** = signed overflow, **P** = even parity of ACC
>   - Bank = (RS1×2) + (RS0×1)
> - **SP (0x81)** → reset value 0x07; stack grows **upward** (increment on PUSH)
> - **DPTR (0x82/0x83)** → 16-bit pointer for MOVX and MOVC
> - **P0–P3 (0x80/0x90/0xA0/0xB0)** → I/O port latches
> - **TMOD (0x89)** → mode select (high nibble = T1, low nibble = T0)
> - **TCON (0x88)** → timer run flags (TR0, TR1) and overflow flags (TF0, TF1)
> - **TH0/TL0 (0x8C/0x8A)** → Timer 0 high/low bytes
> - **TH1/TL1 (0x8D/0x8B)** → Timer 1 high/low bytes
> - **SCON (0x98)** → serial mode (SM0, SM1) + flags (TI, RI)
> - **SBUF (0x99)** → write to transmit, read to receive
> - **PCON (0x87)** → SMOD (baud doubler), IDL, PD bits
> - **IE (0xA8)** → EA (master) + ES, ET1, EX1, ET0, EX0
> - **IP (0xB8)** → priority for each interrupt source
> - **Mode 2 delay formula:** $T = (256 - TH) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$
> - **Parity rule:** P = 1 if ACC has an **odd** number of 1s (even parity)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to 8051 Assembly Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to 8051 Assembly Programming ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Assembly Language** is a low-level symbolic programming language that represents the machine code instructions of a microprocessor or microcontroller in a human-readable form using **mnemonics** (short alphabetic codes) and **labels**, where each assembly statement corresponds to exactly one machine instruction executed by the target processor. For the **Intel 8051 microcontroller**, assembly programming involves writing instructions from the **MCS-51 Instruction Set**, which consists of **111 base instructions** (with many variations, totaling 255 opcodes) that directly manipulate the internal registers, program counter, data memory (RAM), and special function registers (SFRs) of the 8051 architecture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > "Introduction to 8051 Assembly Programming — Assembly language, Assembler directives, Instruction set, Data transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Branch instructions, Simple programs." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Chef's Recipe Card:** > Think of a microcontroller as a **chef in a kitchen** who only understands very specific, atomic commands. The chef cannot understand English sentences like "Please chop the onions finely and put them in the bowl." Instead, the chef only understands a fixed list of small actions written on index cards: > - Card #1: "TAKE onion" (Mnemonic: `MOV A, #data`) > - Card #2: "CHOP onion" (Mnemonic: `INC A` or arithmetic operation) > - Card #3: "PUT in bowl" (Mnemonic: `MOV R0, A`) > > **Assembly language is exactly this — a stack of instruction cards that the 8051 reads one by one.** Each card (line of code) performs one tiny, unambiguous action. The 8051 has a built-in decoder (the instruction decoder) that reads each card and performs the exact action. The **assembler** is like a translator who converts your written recipe (assembly code) into the chef's native numerical language (machine code/hex file). ### 1.3 Why Learn 8051 Assembly Programming? - **Direct Hardware Control**: Every register, port pin, and memory location is directly accessible. - **Time-Critical Applications**: Real-time systems require precise instruction-cycle knowledge. - **Code Efficiency**: Hand-optimized assembly produces the smallest, fastest firmware. - **Foundation for Embedded Systems**: Understanding assembly is the gateway to C programming for embedded targets. > [!TIP] > **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expectation):** Every 8051 instruction executes in either **1 machine cycle (12 oscillator clocks)** or **2 machine cycles (24 oscillator clocks)**. This timing precision is a common exam question. ### 1.4 Structure of an 8051 Assembly Program A typical 8051 assembly program file (`.asm`) has the following structural components: ``` [Label:] Mnemonic [Operands] ; Comment ``` | Field | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | **Label** | Symbolic name for an address (optional) | `START:`, `LOOP:` | | **Mnemonic** | The operation code (opcode) | `MOV`, `ADD`, `SJMP` | | **Operands** | Data or addresses the operation uses | `A, #25H` | | **Comment** | Human-readable explanation (ignored by assembler) | `; Load 25H into Accumulator` | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The 8051 Programming Model The 8051 programmer works with a well-defined set of registers and memory areas. Understanding this model is **essential** before writing any code. #### 2.1.1 Register Banks The 8051 contains **4 register banks**, each with **8 registers (R0–R7)**, located in the lower 32 bytes of internal RAM (addresses `00H` to `1FH`). $$\text{Register Bank Addresses} = \begin{cases} \text{Bank 0:} & 00H - 07H \\ \text{Bank 1:} & 08H - 0FH \\ \text{Bank 2:} & 10H - 17H \\ \text{Bank 3:} & 18H - 1FH \end{cases}$$ Selection of the active bank is controlled by the **RS0 and RS1 bits** in the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register. #### 2.1.2 Special Function Registers (SFRs) The SFRs occupy the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (addresses `80H` to `FFH`). Key SFRs include: | SFR | Address | Function | |---|---|---| | **A (Accumulator)** | `E0H` | Primary arithmetic/logic register | | **B** | `F0H` | Used in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` | | **PSW** | `D0H` | Program Status Word (flags) | | **SP** | `81H` | Stack Pointer (default 07H) | | **DPTR (DPH:DPL)** | `83H, 82H` | 16-bit Data Pointer | | **P0, P1, P2, P3** | `80H, 90H, A0H, B0H` | Four I/O Ports | | **PCON** | `87H` | Power Control | | **TCON, TMOD** | `88H, 89H` | Timer Control | #### 2.1.3 Program Status Word (PSW) — The Flags Register The PSW register holds the conditional flags. Each flag bit is critical for branch instructions. | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function | |---|---|---|---| | PSW.7 | **CY** | `D7H` | **Carry Flag** — set on arithmetic overflow/borrow | | PSW.6 | **AC** | `D6H` | **Auxiliary Carry** — for BCD arithmetic | | PSW.5 | **F0** | `D5H` | **User Flag 0** — general purpose | | PSW.4 | **RS1** | `D4H` | Register Bank Select bit 1 | | PSW.3 | **RS0** | `D3H` | Register Bank Select bit 0 | | PSW.2 | **OV** | `D2H` | **Overflow Flag** — signed arithmetic | | PSW.1 | — | `D1H` | Reserved (undefined) | | PSW.0 | **P** | `D0H` | **Parity Flag** — even parity of A | > [!NOTE] > **Bank Selection via RS1:RS0:** > - `00` → Bank 0 (default, 00H–07H) > - `01` → Bank 1 (08H–0FH) > - `10` → Bank 2 (10H–17H) > - `11` → Bank 3 (18H–1FH) ### 2.2 Addressing Modes of the 8051 The 8051 supports **5 primary addressing modes** that determine how the operand is interpreted. | Mode | Syntax | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Immediate** | `#data` | The data itself is in the instruction | `MOV A, #25H` | | **Register** | `Rn` | Operands are in R0–R7 | `MOV A, R0` | | **Direct** | `address` | Operand is at the given RAM/SFR address | `MOV A, 30H` | | **Register Indirect** | `@Ri` | Address is held in R0 or R1 | `MOV A, @R0` | | **Indexed (Base+Index)** | `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` | Used for look-up tables | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** The `MOVC` instruction uses code memory, while `MOVX` uses external data memory. `MOV` uses internal RAM/SFRs. ### 2.3 Assembler Directives (Pseudo-Instructions) **Assembler directives** are instructions to the **assembler program itself**, not the 8051. They do NOT produce machine code. They are heavily tested in KTU exams. | Directive | Full Form | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---|---| | **ORG** | Origin | Sets the location counter (program memory address) | `ORG 0000H` | | **END** | End | Marks the end of the source program | `END` | | **EQU** | Equate | Defines a constant symbol | `COUNT EQU 25H` | | **DB** | Define Byte | Stores byte(s) in program memory | `DATA: DB 10H, 20H, 30H` | | **DW** | Define Word | Stores 16-bit word(s) | `TABLE: DW 1234H, 5678H` | | **DS** | Define Storage | Reserves bytes in RAM | `BUFFER: DS 10` | | **BIT** | — | Assigns a bit address to a symbol | `LED BIT P1.0` | | **SET** | — | Defines a variable symbol (redefinable) | `COUNT SET 0` | | **DATA** | — | Internal data RAM address | `TEMP DATA 30H` | | **IDATA** | — | Indirect internal RAM | `X IDATA 80H` | | **CODE** | — | Code memory address | `START CODE 0000H` | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---| | Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | **seconds**; $f_{OSC}$ in Hz | | Instruction Time (1-cycle) | $T_1 = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | For instructions like `NOP`, `MOV` | | Instruction Time (2-cycle) | $T_2 = \dfrac{24}{f_{OSC}}$ | For instructions like `SJMP`, `LCALL` | | Baud Rate (Timer 1, Mode 2) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - X)}$ | X = TH1 reload value | | Timer Overflow Count | $T_{overflow} = (2^n - X) \cdot T_{MC}$ | n = mode bit size | | Stack Pointer Reset | $SP = 07H$ (default) | Grows upward in RAM | | Parity Bit | $P = 1$ if odd number of 1-bits in A | Even parity convention | | Maximum Stack Depth | $128 - 7 = 121$ bytes | Internal RAM above 07H | | PSW Register | `$D0H$` | Bit-addressable SFR | | Internal RAM | $128$ bytes | `00H`–`7FH` | | SFR Space | $128$ bytes | `80H`–`FFH` | | Program Memory (8051) | $4$ KB on-chip | `0000H`–`0FFFH` (extendable to 64 KB) | | External Data Memory | up to $64$ KB | Accessed via `MOVX` | ### 2.5 The 8051 Instruction Set — Categories The 8051 instruction set is grouped into **5 functional categories**, which form the backbone of KTU Module 2 questions. | Category | Function | Key Instructions | |---|---|---| | **Data Transfer** | Move data between registers, RAM, SFRs, code, and external memory | `MOV`, `MOVC`, `MOVX`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`, `XCHD` | | **Arithmetic** | Addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, increment, decrement | `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `DA A` | | **Logical** | AND, OR, XOR, NOT, rotate, swap | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `RL`, `RR`, `RLC`, `RRC`, `SWAP` | | **Boolean (Bit)** | Bit-level operations on bit-addressable memory | `SETB`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`, `MOV C,bit` | | **Branching** | Conditional/unconditional jumps, calls, returns | `SJMP`, `LJMP`, `AJMP`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `DJNZ`, `CJNE`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** The `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal) and `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) instructions are the **most commonly tested** in Part B questions, as they implement loops and decision logic. ### 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Industrial Automation**: Assembly programs run PLCs, motor controllers, and sensor interfaces. - **Automotive ECUs**: Engine control units use 8051-family chips for timing-critical sensor polling. - **Consumer Electronics**: Washing machines, microwave ovens, remote controls — all use 8051-class MCUs. - **IoT Edge Devices**: Low-power 8051 variants (e.g., CC2530, EFM8) power Zigbee/Thread sensor nodes. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Machine Cycle Timing The 8051 uses a **12-clock oscillator period** as its fundamental machine cycle. This is a derivation that KTU often expects students to compute. **Step 1 — Identify the oscillator frequency.** Let $f_{OSC}$ be the crystal oscillator frequency connected to pins XTAL1 and XTAL2. $$f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz (common for serial communication)}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the oscillator period.** $$T_{OSC} = \dfrac{1}{f_{OSC}}$$ **Step 3 — Relate to machine cycle.** By definition, one machine cycle equals **12 oscillator periods**: $$T_{MC} = 12 \cdot T_{OSC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the standard value.** $$T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}$$ $$T_{MC} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$ **Step 5 — Determine instruction execution time.** For a 1-cycle instruction: $$T_{inst} = 1 \cdot T_{MC} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$ For a 2-cycle instruction: $$T_{inst} = 2 \cdot T_{MC} = 2.17 \text{ µs}$$ **Step 6 — Compute instructions per second.** $$\text{IPS} = \dfrac{1}{T_{inst}} = \dfrac{1}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 921,600 \text{ instructions/second}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why 11.0592 MHz?** This exact frequency is chosen because $11.0592 \times 10^6 / (12 \times 32) = 28800$, a divisor that produces standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400) with **zero error** — critical for UART communication. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Program Analysis: Register Bank Switching **Question:** Given the following code segment, determine the register bank selection and the effective RAM addresses. ``` MOV PSW, #08H ; Set RS1=0, RS0=1 → Bank 1 MOV R0, #55H ; Load 55H into R0 of active bank MOV R7, A ; Copy A to R7 of active bank ``` **Step 1 — Decode `MOV PSW, #08H`.** `08H` in binary: `0000 1000` - CY = 0, AC = 0, F0 = 0 - RS1 = 0, **RS0 = 1** → Bank 1 selected - OV = 0, P = 0 **Step 2 — Identify effective addresses.** With Bank 1 active, registers R0–R7 map to RAM addresses: $$\text{Effective addresses} = 08H + n, \text{ where } n = 0 \text{ to } 7$$ So R0 → `08H`, R1 → `09H`, ..., R7 → `0FH`. **Step 3 — Verify `MOV R7, A` location.** $$\text{R7 effective address} = 08H + 7 = 0FH$$ The content of `0FH` becomes equal to the content of the Accumulator. ### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Stack Operation Analysis **Code segment:** ``` MOV SP, #60H ; Move Stack Pointer to 60H MOV A, #25H ; Load 25H into A PUSH ACC ; Push A onto stack MOV A, #40H ; Modify A POP ACC ; Restore A from stack ``` **Step 1 — Initial state.** - SP = `60H` - Internal RAM `60H` = XX (undefined) **Step 2 — Execute `PUSH ACC`.** The 8051 increments SP first, then writes: $$SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1 = 60H + 1 = 61H$$ $$\text{RAM}[61H] = ACC = 25H$$ **Step 3 — Execute `MOV A, #40H`.** A now contains `40H`. The stack is unchanged. **Step 4 — Execute `POP ACC`.** The 8051 reads, then decrements: $$ACC = \text{RAM}[SP] = \text{RAM}[61H] = 25H$$ $$SP_{new} = SP_{old} - 1 = 61H - 1 = 60H$$ **Final Result:** A = `25H`, SP = `60H`. The original value of A is restored — a classic stack-swap pattern. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Instruction Encoding Length | Instruction Type | Opcode Bytes | Operand Bytes | Total Size | Machine Cycles | |---|---|---|---|---| | `NOP` | 1 | 0 | 1 byte | 1 | | `MOV A, Rn` | 1 | 0 | 1 byte | 1 | | `MOV A, #data` | 1 | 1 | 2 bytes | 1 | | `MOV direct, #data` | 2 | 1 | 3 bytes | 2 | | `MOV direct, direct` | 2 | 1 | 3 bytes | 2 | | `LJMP addr16` | 2 | 2 | 3 bytes | 2 | | `SJMP rel` | 1 | 1 | 2 bytes | 2 | | `LCALL addr16` | 2 | 2 | 3 bytes | 2 | | `MUL AB` | 1 | 0 | 1 byte | 4 | | `DIV AB` | 1 | 0 | 1 byte | 4 | > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** KTU board examiners love asking: *"How many bytes does `MOV DPTR, #data16` occupy?"* — Answer: **3 bytes** (1 opcode + 2 data bytes) in **2 machine cycles**. ### 3.5 Derivation: Timer Reload Value for Specific Delay **Problem:** Generate a delay of 50 ms using Timer 0, Mode 1, with $f_{OSC} = 12$ MHz. **Step 1 — Compute machine cycle period.** $$T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \text{ µs}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the required timer count.** Mode 1 is a **16-bit timer**, so it counts from 0000H to FFFFH and overflows at $2^{16} = 65536$ counts. $$\text{Total counts needed} = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 50,000$$ **Step 3 — Check feasibility.** Maximum counts in Mode 1 = 65,536. Since 50,000 < 65,536, **a single timer overflow is sufficient** (no loop needed). **Step 4 — Compute the timer reload value.** $$X = 2^{16} - \text{count} = 65,536 - 50,000 = 15,536$$ Convert to hex: $15,536 = 3CB0H$. **Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0.** $$TH0 = 3CH, \quad TL0 = B0H$$ **Step 6 — The resulting assembly code.** ``` MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 MOV TH0, #3CH MOV TL0, #B0H SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Loop until overflow CLR TR0 CLR TF0 ``` ### 3.6 Worked Example: Sum of an Array **Problem:** Compute the sum of 5 numbers stored in internal RAM starting at `30H`. Store the result at `40H`. **Step-by-step logic derivation:** **Step 1 — Initialize pointers and accumulator.** - `R0` will point to current array element (start = `30H`). - `R7` is the counter (start = `05H`). - `A` holds the running sum (start = `00H`). **Step 2 — Iterate: add element, increment pointer, decrement count.** $$A \leftarrow A + \text{RAM}[R0]$$ $$R0 \leftarrow R0 + 1$$ $$R7 \leftarrow R7 - 1$$ **Step 3 — Repeat until `R7 = 0` (use `DJNZ`).** $$R7 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \text{goto Step 2}$$ $$R7 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{exit loop}$$ **Step 4 — Final assembly code.** ``` MOV R0, #30H ; Point to array start MOV R7, #05H ; Counter = 5 MOV A, #00H ; Sum = 0 LOOP: ADD A, @R0 ; A = A + RAM[R0] INC R0 ; Move to next element DJNZ R7, LOOP ; Decrement R7, loop if not zero MOV 40H, A ; Store result ``` --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 8051 Programming Model — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SFRs["Special Function Registers (80H-FFH)"] ACC["A - Accumulator (E0H)"] BREG["B Register (F0H)"] PSW["PSW (D0H) - Flags"] SP["SP (81H) - Stack Pointer"] DPTR["DPTR (82H,83H) - 16-bit Data Pointer"] P0["P0 (80H) - Port 0"] P1["P1 (90H) - Port 1"] P2["P2 (A0H) - Port 2"] P3["P3 (B0H) - Port 3"] end subgraph RAM["Internal RAM (00H-7FH)"] RB0["Register Bank 0 (00H-07H)"] RB1["Register Bank 1 (08H-0FH)"] RB2["Register Bank 2 (10H-17H)"] RB3["Register Bank 3 (18H-1FH)"] BITAR["Bit Addressable (20H-2FH)"] GEN["General Purpose RAM (30H-7FH)"] end PC["PC - Program Counter"] --> CODE["Program Memory (0000H-0FFFH on-chip)"] ROMEXT["External Code (1000H-FFFFH)"] --> CODE CPU["8051 CPU"] --> ACC CPU --> BREG CPU --> PSW CPU --> SP CPU --> DPTR CPU --> P0 CPU --> P1 CPU --> P2 CPU --> P3 CPU --> RAM CPU --> PC ``` ### 4.2 Assembly Program Development Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Source Code
