The 8051 has **15 opcodes** in the data transfer group, but the **KTP high-yield** ones are organized below by **function**.

### 2.1.1 Internal RAM Data Transfer — The `MOV` Family

The general template is: `MOV <destination>, <source>`

| Mnemonic | Operation | Addressing Modes Allowed | Bytes | Cycles |
|----------|-----------|--------------------------|-------|--------|
| `MOV A, Rn` | A ← Rn | Register | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV Rn, A` | Rn ← A | Register | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV A, direct` | A ← (direct) | Direct | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV direct, A` | (direct) ← A | Direct | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | Rn ← immediate | Immediate | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV direct, #data` | (direct) ← immediate | Immediate (3-byte form) | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV A, @Ri` | A ← ((Ri)) | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV @Ri, A` | ((Ri)) ← A | Indirect | 1 | 1 |
| `MOV direct, direct` | (dest) ← (src) | Direct-to-Direct | 3 | 2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Forbidden Move:** There is **NO** `MOV Rn, Rn` instruction in the 8051. To move R1 to R2, you must go through the accumulator: `MOV A, R1` followed by `MOV R2, A` (2 machine cycles). This is a classic KTU short-answer pitfall.

### 2.1.2 External Data Memory Transfer — The `MOVX` Family

`MOVX` is the elevator to the basement storeroom. It uses **only indirect addressing** via R0, R1 (for 256-byte pages) or DPTR (for 64 KB external RAM).

| Mnemonic | Operation | Notes |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | Read external RAM pointed to by DPTR into A | Most common — DPTR holds 16-bit address. |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | Write A to external RAM pointed to by DPTR | |
| `MOVX A, @Ri` | Read external RAM (low 8-bit address from Ri, high 8-bit from P2) | P2 must be pre-loaded. |
| `MOVX @Ri, A` | Write to external RAM using Ri + P2. | |

### 2.1.3 Program Memory (Look-up Table) Transfer — The `MOVC` Family

`MOVC` is the recipe book reader. It is **read-only** and uses **only A + DPTR** or **A + PC**.

| Mnemonic | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A ← Code[DPTR + A] (Look-up table read) |
| `MOVC A, @A+PC` | A ← Code[PC + A] (Relative look-up) |

### 2.1.4 Stack and Exchange Operations

| Mnemonic | Operation |
|----------|-----------|
| `PUSH direct` | SP ← SP + 1; (SP) ← (direct) |
| `POP direct` | (direct) ← (SP); SP ← SP − 1 |
| `XCH A, Rn` | Swap A and Rn contents |
| `XCHD A, @Ri` | Exchange only the **lower nibble** of A and the lower nibble of (Ri) |
| `SWAP A` | Swap the upper and lower nibbles of A internally |

## 2.2 The 8051 Arithmetic Group — Complete Taxonomy

### 2.2.1 Addition Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Flags Affected |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| `ADD A, <src>` | A ← A + src | CY, AC, OV, P |
| `ADDC A, <src>` | A ← A + src + CY | CY, AC, OV, P |
| `INC <byte>` | byte ← byte + 1 | **NONE** (does not affect CY!) |
| `INC DPTR` | DPTR ← DPTR + 1 | NONE |
| `DA A` | Decimal-Adjust A after BCD addition | CY only |

> [!NOTE]
> **The CRITICAL Flag Rule (Most-Tested in KTU):** `INC` affects **NO flags**, but `ADD` affects **CY, AC, OV, P**. This is why you can never use `INC` to do multi-byte addition reliably — you must use `ADDC` for the higher bytes so that the carry from the lower byte propagates correctly.

### 2.2.2 Subtraction Instructions

The 8051 uses **2's complement arithmetic**, and the carry flag is repurposed as a **borrow flag** in subtraction.

| Mnemonic | Operation | Flags Affected |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| `SUBB A, <src>` | A ← A − src − CY | CY (borrow), AC, OV, P |
| `DEC <byte>` | byte ← byte − 1 | **NONE** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** There is **no `SUB` instruction** in the 8051. Every subtraction uses `SUBB`. To do a simple `A ← A − R1`, you must **first clear the carry** using `CLR C` (i.e., `CLR CY` bit of PSW) — otherwise the previous borrow gets subtracted again!

### 2.2.3 Multiplication and Division

| Mnemonic | Operation | Result Location | Flags Affected |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| `MUL AB` | A × B (unsigned) | A ← low byte, B ← high byte | OV (overflow if result > 255), CY = 0, P |
| `DIV AB` | A ÷ B (unsigned) | A ← quotient, B ← remainder | OV (set if B = 0, i.e., divide-by-zero), CY = 0, P |

### 2.2.4 Decimal-Adjust Instruction

`DA A` is a special instruction used **only after `ADD` or `ADDC`** on BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) numbers. It checks the **AC and CY** flags and adds `00H, 06H, 60H,` or `66H` to the accumulator to correct the BCD result.

## 2.3 The Program Status Word (PSW) — The Flag Bible

The PSW (SFR at address **D0H**) is a bit-addressable register. The KTU-required bits are:

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|-----|--------|---------|----------|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | **Carry flag** (also borrow in SUBB) |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | **Auxiliary carry** (carry from bit 3 to bit 4, i.e., lower nibble overflow) |
| PSW.5 | F0 | D5H | User-defined flag |
| PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register bank select bit 1 |
| PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register bank select bit 0 |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | **Overflow flag** (set on signed overflow, i.e., carry into XOR carry out of MSB) |
| PSW.1 | − | D1H | Reserved (User-defined on 8052) |
| PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | **Parity flag** (set if A has an odd number of 1s — i.e., even parity is maintained in A) |

The Register Bank selection is determined by **RS1:RS0**:
- **00** → Bank 0 (R0–R7 at addresses 00H–07H) — Default after reset
- **01** → Bank 1 (08H–0FH)
- **10** → Bank 2 (10H–17H)
- **11** → Bank 3 (18H–1FH)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Arithmetic Flag Calculations

For unsigned 8-bit addition `A + src`:

| Flag | Formula / Condition |
|------|---------------------|
| **CY** | Carry out of bit 7 (D7). Set if unsigned sum > 255. |
| **AC** | Carry out of bit 3 (D3) into bit 4. |
| **OV** | $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$ (carry into MSB XOR carry out of MSB) |
| **P** | Set if A has even number of 1-bits (parity is always even in A). |

For unsigned 8-bit subtraction `A − src − CY`:

| Flag | Formula / Condition |
|------|---------------------|
| **CY** (Borrow) | Set if a borrow was needed, i.e., if `A < src + CY` (unsigned). |
| **AC** | Borrow from bit 4 (set if lower nibble of A < lower nibble of src + CY). |
| **OV** | $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$ applied to the subtraction internal addition. Set if signed result is wrong. |

For `MUL AB`:
- **OV = 1** if the product > 255 (i.e., B ≠ 0 after multiplication).
- **CY = 0** always.

For `DIV AB`:
- **OV = 1** if B = 0 at the time of division (divide-by-zero protection).
- **CY = 0** always.

## 2.5 Addressing Modes in Data Transfer and Arithmetic

The 8051 supports **6 addressing modes**, and KTU questions frequently test identification. The mnemonic `<src>` in arithmetic and `MOV` can be:

1. **Immediate** → `#data` (e.g., `MOV A, #25H`)
2. **Register** → `Rn`, n ∈ {0..7} (e.g., `ADD A, R3`)
3. **Direct** → `direct address` (e.g., `MOV A, 30H`)
4. **Register Indirect** → `@Ri`, i ∈ {0,1} (e.g., `MOVX A, @R0`)
5. **Indexed** → `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` (used only with MOVC)
6. **Implied / Register-Specific** → `A`, `DPTR`, `AB` (e.g., `MUL AB`, `INC DPTR`)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Short-Answer Trick:** To identify the addressing mode of `ADD A, @R0` → "Register Indirect on R0, destination is Register (A)". For `MOV DPTR, #1234H` → "Immediate (16-bit data loaded to DPTR)".

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Instruction Family | Real Embedded Application |
|--------------------|--------------------------|
| `MOV P1, A` | Writing to a GPIO port to drive LEDs, relays, or LCD data lines. |
| `MOVX A, @DPTR` | Reading a byte from an external ADC, EEPROM, or external SRAM. |
| `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Fetching a 7-segment display pattern from a look-up table. |
| `ADD` / `ADDC` | Multi-byte addition (e.g., adding two 32-bit sensor readings). |
| `SUBB` | Comparing two values (used in conditional jumps like `JNC` / `JC`). |
| `MUL AB` | Computing a weighted average or scaling (e.g., `val * 10` for display). |
| `DIV AB` | Extracting digits from a BCD number for 7-segment multiplexing. |
| `DA A` | Performing BCD addition for RTC (Real-Time Clock) hour/minute updates. |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This section presents **exhaustive, traced execution** of canonical KTU programs. Each program shows the **opcode encoding**, the **register/memory map after every instruction**, and the **flag-by-flag derivation** so that nothing is left implicit.

## 3.1 Program 1 — Data Transfer: Block Copy from Internal RAM 20H–2FH to External RAM 1000H–100FH

### Problem Statement
*"Write an 8051 ALP to copy 16 bytes of data from internal RAM locations 20H–2FH to external RAM locations starting at 1000H. Use Register Indirect addressing."*

### Conceptual Setup
- We need a **counter** to track 16 bytes. Use **R2**.
- We need a **source pointer** for internal RAM. Use **R0** (only R0/R1 allowed for `@Ri`).
- We need a **destination pointer** for external RAM. Use **DPTR** (16-bit, allowed for `@DPTR`).
- We use **`DJNZ R2, label`** (from the branching group, but commonly used here) to loop.

### Full Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG     0000H           ; Program starts at 0000H in code memory
        MOV     DPTR, #1000H    ; DPTR ← destination start address
        MOV     R0,  #20H       ; R0 ← source start address (internal RAM)
        MOV     R2,  #10H       ; R2 ← counter = 16 (decimal)
LOOP:   MOV     A,   @R0        ; A ← (R0)        ; 1 byte read
        MOVX    @DPTR, A        ; (DPTR) ← A      ; write to external RAM
        INC     R0              ; R0 ← R0 + 1     ; next source
        INC     DPTR            ; DPTR ← DPTR + 1 ; next destination
        DJNZ    R2,  LOOP       ; R2--, if not zero jump to LOOP
        END                     ; end of program
```

### Step-by-Step Trace Table

Let us assume internal RAM 20H–2FH already contains the hex bytes `11, 22, 33, ..., FF` (just as an example — for the first iteration we trace the first byte, which is the value at address 20H).

| Step | Instruction Executed | R0 | R2 | DPTR | A | Action |
|------|----------------------|----|----|------|---|--------|
| Init | — | xxH | xxH | xxxxH | xxH | Random initial state |
| 1 | `MOV DPTR, #1000H` | xxH | xxH | **1000H** | xxH | DPTR loaded |
| 2 | `MOV R0, #20H` | **20H** | xxH | 1000H | xxH | Source pointer loaded |
| 3 | `MOV R2, #10H` | 20H | **10H** | 1000H | xxH | Counter = 16 |
| 4 | `MOV A, @R0` (iter 1) | 20H | 10H | 1000H | **(20H)** | A ← contents of RAM[20H] |
| 5 | `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 20H | 10H | 1000H | (20H) | External RAM[1000H] ← A |
| 6 | `INC R0` | **21H** | 10H | 1000H | (20H) | Source pointer ++ |
| 7 | `INC DPTR` | 21H | 10H | **1001H** | (20H) | Dest pointer ++ |
| 8 | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 21H | **0FH** | 1001H | (20H) | R2 = 15, not zero → loop |
| ... | ... loops 15 more times ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Last | `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 30H | **00H** | 1010H | (2FH) | R2 = 0 → fall through, exit |

### Machine Cycle Calculation (KTU Frequently Asked)

For the 8051 with a **12 MHz crystal**, one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods = **1 µs**.

| Instruction | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|-------------|-------|----------------|
| `MOV DPTR, #data` | 3 | 2 |
| `MOV R0, #data` | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV R2, #data` | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV A, @R0` | 1 | 1 |
| `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 1 | **2** |
| `INC R0` | 1 | 1 |
| `INC DPTR` | 1 | 2 |
| `DJNZ R2, LOOP` | 2 | 2 |

**Per iteration of the loop (steps 4–8):** 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 + 2 = **8 machine cycles**.
**Total cycles** = (1-time setup: 2+1+1 = 4) + (16 × 8) = 4 + 128 = **132 machine cycles**.
**Total time at 12 MHz** = 132 × 1 µs = **132 µs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does `MOVX` take 2 cycles?** The 8051 needs an extra cycle to activate the external bus and latch the address. This is why `MOVX` is *slower* than `MOV` — a critical concept when designing time-critical embedded firmware.

---

## 3.2 Program 2 — Arithmetic: 16-Bit Addition with Carry Propagation

### Problem Statement
*"Write an 8051 ALP to add two 16-bit numbers. The first number is stored in internal RAM locations 30H (low byte) and 31H (high byte). The second number is stored in 32H (low) and 33H (high). Store the 32-bit result in 34H (low), 35H (next), 36H (next), 37H (high)."*

Wait — for a 16-bit + 16-bit, the result is **at most 17 bits**. The KTU board often asks **16-bit + 16-bit → 17-bit (with CY)** OR **two 16-bit numbers → 32-bit sum**. We will do the **17-bit case with CY** (the standard 8051 idiom).

### Full Assembly Code (16-bit + 16-bit, result in A:B with CY for bit 16)

Actually, a cleaner KTU question is **multi-byte addition using `ADDC`**:

```asm
        ; Add 4-byte (32-bit) numbers: NUM1 at 30H–33H + NUM2 at 34H–37H
        ; Result stored at 40H–43H
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0,  #30H       ; R0 points to NUM1 low byte
        MOV     R1,  #34H       ; R1 points to NUM2 low byte
        MOV     R2,  #40H       ; R2 points to result low byte
        MOV     R3,  #04H       ; counter = 4 bytes
        CLR     C               ; CY ← 0 (essential before first ADD!)
LOOP:   MOV     A,   @R0        ; A ← NUM1[i]
        ADDC    A,   @R1        ; A ← A + NUM2[i] + CY
        MOV     @R2, A          ; result[i] ← A
        INC     R0              ; next byte of NUM1
        INC     R1              ; next byte of NUM2
        INC     R2              ; next byte of result
        DJNZ    R3,  LOOP       ; loop 4 times
        ; After loop, CY holds the final carry (bit 32) — store it if needed
        END
```

### Why `CLR C` Before the First `ADD` / `ADDC`?

The `ADDC` instruction **always** adds CY. If we had a stale carry from a previous operation, the first byte would be wrong. The first `ADD` would be a `ADDC` semantically because CY must be cleared first — this is the **single most-tested KTU concept** in arithmetic ALPs.

### Step-by-Step Trace

Assume:
- NUM1 = `78 56 34 12` (at 30H, 31H, 32H, 33H) → This is the number **0x12345678**
- NUM2 = `F0 DE BC 0A` (at 34H, 35H, 36H, 37H) → This is the number **0x0ABCDEF0**
- Expected sum = `0x12345678 + 0x0ABCDEF0 = 0x1CF13568`
  - Low byte: `0x78 + 0xF0 = 0x168` → result = `0x68`, CY = 1
  - Next: `0x56 + 0xDE + 1 = 0x135` → result = `0x35`, CY = 1
  - Next: `0x34 + 0xBC + 1 = 0xF1`, CY = 0
  - High: `0x12 + 0x0A + 0 = 0x1C`, CY = 0
- Result should be `68 35 F1 1C` at 40H–43H. ✓

| Iter | Instruction | A (after ADDC) | CY (after ADDC) | Stored at |
|------|-------------|----------------|------------------|-----------|
| 1 | `MOV A,@R0` (A=78H) `ADDC A,@R1` (A=F0H) | 78H+F0H+0 = **68H** | **1** | (40H)=68H |
| 2 | `MOV A,@R0` (A=56H) `ADDC A,@R1` (A=DEH) | 56H+DEH+1 = **35H** | **1** | (41H)=35H |
| 3 | `MOV A,@R0` (A=34H) `ADDC A,@R1` (A=BCH) | 34H+BCH+1 = **F1H** | **0** | (42H)=F1H |
| 4 | `MOV A,@R0` (A=12H) `ADDC A,@R1` (A=0AH) | 12H+0AH+0 = **1CH** | **0** | (43H)=1CH |

The CY after the last `DJNZ` would be **0**, indicating no 33rd bit — i.e., the result fits in 32 bits.

---

## 3.3 Program 3 — BCD Addition with `DA A` (Decimal Adjust)

### Problem Statement
*"Add two BCD numbers stored at internal RAM locations 50H (lower nibble of A) and 51H. The result should be a valid BCD number stored at 52H."*

### Background: What is BCD?
BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) stores each decimal digit in **4 bits (a nibble)**. So decimal 95 = `1001 0101` = `95H` (NOT `5FH` which is binary 95). The `DA A` instruction corrects the result of an `ADD` to produce valid BCD.

### Code

```asm
        MOV     A,   50H        ; A ← BCD1 (e.g., 95H)
        ADD     A,   51H        ; A ← A + BCD2 (e.g., 17H = A = ACH, NOT valid BCD!)
        DA     A                ; A ← 12H (i.e., 1 1 2 → 112 in BCD if we had 3 digits)
        MOV     52H, A          ; store BCD result
        END
```

### Numerical Trace

Suppose BCD1 = `95H` (= decimal 95) and BCD2 = `17H` (= decimal 17). Expected: 95 + 17 = 112.

- **Step 1:** `MOV A, 50H` → A = `95H` = `1001 0101`
- **Step 2:** `ADD A, 51H` → A = `95H + 17H = ACH` = `1010 1100`
  - Lower nibble `CH` (12) > 9 → invalid BCD; CY not set by lower nibble internally
  - Upper nibble `AH` (10) > 9 → invalid BCD
- **Step 3:** `DA A` checks:
  - Lower nibble of A (`CH`) > 9 OR AC = 1 → add `06H` to A: `ACH + 06H = B2H`, AC = 1
  - Upper nibble of A (`BH` after carry) > 9 OR CY = 1 → add `60H` to A: `B2H + 60H = 12H`, CY = 1
- **Step 4:** A = `12H` (lower byte), and CY = 1 (upper digit = 1). Combined as a 2-digit BCD, with carry the result is `1 1 2` = **112 decimal** ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic for `DA A`:** *"If low nibble > 9 OR AC = 1, add 06. Then if high nibble > 9 OR CY = 1, add 60."* This is a 5-mark favorite in KTU ESE.

---

## 3.4 Program 4 — Multiplication and Division (Digit Extraction for Display)

### Problem Statement
*"A BCD number (00–99) is stored at internal RAM location 60H. Write an ALP to split it into two BCD digits and store the tens digit at 61H and the units digit at 62H."*

### The Algorithm
1. Load the number into A.
2. **Divide by 10** → A = quotient (tens), B = remainder (units).
3. Store A to 61H (tens) and B to 62H (units).
4. Use `SWAP A` and `ADD A, B` if you want to pack the two BCD digits into one byte (e.g., `MOV 63H, A` after `SWAP A; ADD A, B`).

### Code

```asm
        MOV     A,   60H        ; A ← BCD number (e.g., 87)
        MOV     B,   #0AH       ; B ← 10 (divisor)
        DIV     AB              ; A = 08 (tens), B = 07 (units)
        MOV     61H, A          ; store tens
        MOV     62H, B          ; store units
        SWAP    A               ; A = 80H (tens in upper nibble)
        ADD     A,   B          ; A = 80H + 07H = 87H (packed BCD)
        MOV     63H, A          ; store packed BCD
        END
```

### Trace (with input 87)
- A = `57H` (87 decimal)
- B = `0AH` (10)
- `DIV AB` → A = `08H`, B = `07H`
- After `MOV 61H,A`: (61H) = 08H
- After `MOV 62H,B`: (62H) = 07H
- After `SWAP A`: A = `80H`
- After `ADD A, B` = `80H + 07H = 87H`
- (63H) = `87H` — packed BCD representation of 87 ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Context:** This is exactly how a digital clock extracts individual digits of "seconds = 47" to display "4" and "7" on a multiplexed 7-segment display. KTU often frames this as a 14-mark application question.

---

## 3.5 Program 5 — Look-up Table using `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

### Problem Statement
*"Write an 8051 ALP to find the square of a number (0–9) stored in internal RAM 70H using a look-up table. Store the result in 71H."*

### The Concept
We store the table of squares (`0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81`) in **program memory** starting at, say, address 0200H. The DPTR is loaded with the table base, and A is loaded with the input (which also serves as the offset). `MOVC` reads the byte at `DPTR + A`.

### Code

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0200H    ; DPTR ← table base
        MOV     A,   70H        ; A ← input (0–9)
        MOVC    A,   @A+DPTR    ; A ← table[A]
        MOV     71H, A          ; store square
        END

        ; --- Look-up table data ---
        ORG     0200H
TABLE:  DB      00H, 01H, 04H, 09H, 10H, 19H, 24H, 31H, 40H, 51H
        ;        0²   1²   2²   3²   4²   5²   6²   7²   8²   9²
        END
```

### Trace (input = 7 at 70H)
- DPTR = `0200H`
- A = `07H`
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A = Code[`0200H + 07H`] = Code[`0207H`] = `31H` (49 decimal) ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Why use `MOVC` and not `MOVC A, @A+PC`?** The `A+PC` form uses the **current PC value**, which is the address of the *next* instruction's opcode. The base offset then becomes fragile. In KTU examinations, the `A+DPTR` form is preferred for portability.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — At a Glance

| Operation Type | Instruction | Byte Size | Cycles | CY | AC | OV | P | Common Use |
|----------------|-------------|-----------|--------|----|----|----|----|------------|
| **Copy Register** | `MOV A, R3` | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — | Load data into A |
| **Copy to Port** | `MOV P1, A` | 2 | 1 | — | — | — | — | GPIO write |
| **External Read** | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | 1 | 2 | — | — | — | — | Read ADC |
| **External Write** | `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 1 | 2 | — | — | — | — | Write to LCD |
| **Look-up Read** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | 1 | 2 | — | — | — | — | 7-seg pattern |
| **Single-byte Add** | `ADD A, R1` | 1 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Add values |
| **Multi-byte Add** | `ADDC A, @R0` | 1 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Propagate carry |
| **Increment** | `INC R5` | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — | Loop counter |
| **Increment DPTR** | `INC DPTR` | 1 | 2 | — | — | — | — | Pointer advance |
| **Subtract** | `SUBB A, R2` | 1 | 1 | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Compare values |
| **Multiply** | `MUL AB` | 1 | 4 | 0 | — | ✓ | ✓ | Scaling |
| **Divide** | `DIV AB` | 1 | 4 | 0 | — | ✓ | ✓ | Digit extraction |
| **Decimal Adjust** | `DA A` | 1 | 1 | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | BCD fix-up |
| **Stack Push** | `PUSH PSW` | 2 | 2 | — | — | — | — | Save context |
| **Stack Pop** | `POP PSW` | 2 | 2 | — | — | — | — | Restore context |
| **Exchange** | `XCH A, R0` | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — | Quick swap |
| **Nibble Exchange** | `XCHD A, @R1` | 1 | 1 | — | — | — | — | BCD digit swap |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

This section provides **engineered Mermaid diagrams** depicting the data flow, memory architecture, and instruction fetch-decode-execute cycle as it relates to the programs above. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are clean text only (no markdown formatting inside Mermaid labels).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 CPU Data Paths for MOV and Arithmetic

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    A["ACCUMULATOR A SFR E0H"] -->|MOV direct A| RAM["INTERNAL RAM 00H TO 7FH"]
    RAM -->|MOV A direct| A
    R0R1["R0 OR R1 POINTER"] -->|MOVX A at Ri| EXRAM["EXTERNAL DATA RAM 0000H TO FFFFH"]
    EXRAM -->|MOVX at Ri A| R0R1
    DPTR["DPTR 16 BIT SFR 82H 83H"] -->|MOVX A at DPTR| EXRAM
    EXRAM -->|MOVX at DPTR A| DPTR
    DPTR -->|MOVC A at A plus DPTR| ROM["PROGRAM ROM CODE MEMORY"]
    ROM -->|MOVC A at A plus DPTR| A
    A -->|ADD A src| ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT ALU"]
    ALU -->|result back| A
    ALU -->|flag outputs| PSW["PSW SFR D0H WITH CY AC OV P FLAGS"]
    BREG["B REGISTER SFR F0H"] -->|MUL AB or DIV AB| ALU
    ALU -->|MUL low byte| A
    ALU -->|MUL high byte| BREG
<!-- mermaid -->

## 4.2 Flowchart — Generic Structure of an 8051 Block-Move ALP

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    start1(["START"]) --> init1["INITIALIZE DPTR WITH DESTINATION ADDRESS"]
    init1 --> init2["INITIALIZE R0 WITH SOURCE BASE 20H"]
    init2 --> init3["INITIALIZE R2 WITH COUNTER 10H"]
    init3 --> loop1["READ BYTE FROM INTERNAL RAM VIA MOV A AT R0"]
    loop1 --> loop2["WRITE BYTE TO EXTERNAL RAM VIA MOVX AT DPTR A"]
    loop2 --> loop3["INCREMENT R0 AND DPTR"]
    loop3 --> dec1["DECREMENT R2 USING DJNZ"]
    dec1 -->|"R2 NOT ZERO"| loop1
    dec1 -->|"R2 ZERO"| end1(["END TRANSFER 16 BYTES COPIED"])
<!-- mermaid -->

## 4.3 Flowchart — 16-Bit / 32-Bit Addition with Carry Propagation

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    start2(["START"]) --> clr1["CLR C CLEAR CARRY FLAG FOR FIRST BYTE"]
    clr1 --> loadp1["R0 POINTS TO NUM1 BYTE 0 LOW BYTE"]
    loadp1 --> loadp2["R1 POINTS TO NUM2 BYTE 0 LOW BYTE"]
    loadp2 --> loadp3["R2 POINTS TO RESULT BUFFER LOW BYTE"]
    loadp3 --> loadc1["R3 COUNTER EQUALS NUMBER OF BYTES 4"]
    loadc1 --> step1["MOV A AT R0 LOAD NUM1 BYTE"]
    step1 --> step2["ADDC A AT R1 ADD NUM2 BYTE PLUS CARRY"]
    step2 --> step3["MOV AT R2 A STORE RESULT BYTE"]
    step3 --> step4["INC R0 INC R1 INC R2 ADVANCE POINTERS"]
    step4 --> dec2["DJNZ R3 LOOP DECREMENT COUNTER"]
    dec2 -->|"R3 NOT ZERO"| step1
    dec2 -->|"R3 ZERO"| check["FINAL CARRY FLAG INDICATES OVERFLOW BIT"]
    check --> end2(["END 32 BIT SUM COMPLETE"])
<!-- mermaid -->

## 4.4 Flowchart — BCD Addition with DA A

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TD
    bcd1(["START"]) --> bcd2["LOAD BCD OPERAND 1 INTO A"]
    bcd2 --> bcd3["ADD A OPERAND 2 BINARY ADDITION"]
    bcd3 --> bcd4["DA A DECIMAL ADJUST ACCUMULATOR"]
    bcd4 --> bcd5["IF LOWER NIBBLE GREATER 9 OR AC 1 ADD 06H"]
    bcd5 --> bcd6["IF UPPER NIBBLE GREATER 9 OR CY 1 ADD 60H"]
    bcd6 --> bcd7["STORE CORRECTED BCD RESULT"]
    bcd7 --> bcd8(["END"])
<!-- mermaid -->

## 4.5 State Diagram — PSW Flag Transitions for ADD Instruction

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    S0["INITIAL STATE CY 0 AC 0 OV 0"] -->|"ADD A R1 WHERE A 0FFH R1 02H"| S1["CALCULATE SUM 101H"]
    S1 --> S2["LOW BYTE 01H STORED IN A"]
    S1 --> S3["CY BECOMES 1 BECAUSE BIT 7 OVERFLOWED"]
    S1 --> S4["AC BECOMES 0 BECAUSE LOWER NIBBLE 0FH PLUS 02H EQUALS 11H NO CARRY FROM BIT 3 TO BIT 4 INTERNAL"]
    S1 --> S5["OV COMPUTED C7 XOR C6 EQUAL 0 XOR 0 EQUAL 0"]
    S3 --> S6["FINAL PSW CY 1 AC 0 OV 0 P EVEN"]
<!-- mermaid -->

## 4.6 Schematic — Stack Operation during PUSH and POP

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart TB
    SPREG["STACK POINTER SP SFR 81H INITIAL VALUE 07H"] -->|"PUSH ACC"| UPI["SP INCREMENTED TO 08H"]
    UPI --> WRITE1["INTERNAL RAM 08H RECEIVES ACC VALUE"]
    WRITE1 --> POP1["POP ACC TRIGGERED"]
    POP1 --> READ1["INTERNAL RAM 08H READ INTO ACC"]
    READ1 --> DOWN["SP DECREMENTED BACK TO 07H"]
<!-- mermaid -->

## 4.7 Memory Map — Internal RAM Layout for 8051 (KTU Critical Diagram)

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    M0["00H TO 07H BANK 0 R0 R7 DEFAULT"] --> M1["08H TO 0FH BANK 1 R0 R7"]
    M1 --> M2["10H TO 17H BANK 2 R0 R7"]
    M2 --> M3["18H TO 1FH BANK 3 R0 R7"]
    M3 --> M4["20H TO 2FH BIT ADDRESSABLE AREA 16 BYTES 128 BITS"]
    M4 --> M5["30H TO 7FH GENERAL PURPOSE RAM 80 BYTES"]
    M5 --> M6["80H TO FFH SFR SPECIAL FUNCTION REGISTERS MAP"]
<!-- mermaid -->

## 4.8 Block Diagram — MOVC Look-up Table Read Mechanism

<!-- mermaid -->
flowchart LR
    INP["INPUT INDEX 0 TO 9 IN ACCUMULATOR A"] --> BASE["DPTR LOADED WITH TABLE BASE 0200H"]
    BASE --> SUMADDR["ALU COMPUTES 0200H PLUS A AS 16 BIT ADDRESS"]
    SUMADDR --> FETCH["PROGRAM ROM OUTPUTS BYTE AT COMPUTED ADDRESS"]
    FETCH --> OUT["ACCUMULATOR A RECEIVES SQUARE VALUE"]
<!-- mermaid -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

The following questions mirror the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** style. Marks are distributed per the official KTU template (Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice). Model answers are exhaustive.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]
*"Explain the function of the `MOVX` instruction in the 8051. How is it different from `MOV` and `MOVC`? Give one example of each."* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The `MOVX` (Move eXternal) instruction is used to transfer data between the **Accumulator (A)** and the **external data memory** of the 8051, which lies outside the chip and is accessed via external address/data bus lines (P0, P2, ALE, RD, WR). It uses **only indirect addressing** through `@Ri` (with P2 providing the high byte) or `@DPTR` (16-bit full address).

| Instruction | Memory Type | Direction | Example |
|-------------|-------------|-----------|---------|
| `MOV` | Internal RAM (00H–7FH) and SFRs (80H–FFH) | Bidirectional, all addressing modes | `MOV A, 30H` |
| `MOVX` | External Data RAM (0000H–FFFFH, up to 64 KB) | Only via accumulator + indirect | `MOVX A, @DPTR` |
| `MOVC` | Program Memory (Code ROM, read-only) | Read-only, via A + DPTR or A + PC | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |

`MOVX` is critical for interfacing external peripherals such as ADC0804, external SRAM (6264), or external EEPROM. Each `MOVX` takes **2 machine cycles** (vs 1 for `MOV`) because the 8051 must activate the external bus.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*"Differentiate between `ADD` and `ADDC` instructions of the 8051. Why is `CLR C` necessary before multi-byte addition?"* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

`ADD A, src` performs the operation **A ← A + src** and sets the flags CY, AC, OV, and P based on the result. It does **not** consider any previous carry.

`ADDC A, src` performs **A ← A + src + CY** — i.e., it adds the contents of the source, the accumulator, **and** the current value of the carry flag. This is essential when adding numbers that span more than 8 bits.

In multi-byte (e.g., 16-bit, 32-bit) addition, the carry from the lower byte must be propagated to the higher byte. Since `ADD` ignores CY, the programmer must use `ADDC` for all bytes **after the first**. For the very first byte, CY must be **explicitly cleared** using `CLR C` (or `CLR 0D7H`), otherwise a stale carry from a previous unrelated operation would corrupt the first byte's sum.

**Example for 16-bit addition:**
```asm
    CLR C                ; CY ← 0 (no carry into byte 0)
    MOV A, 30H           ; A ← low byte of NUM1
    ADD A, 32H           ; A ← low byte of NUM1 + low byte of NUM2
    MOV 40H, A           ; store low byte of sum
    MOV A, 31H           ; A ← high byte of NUM1
    ADDC A, 33H          ; A ← high byte of NUM1 + high byte of NUM2 + CY
    MOV 41H, A           ; store high byte of sum
```

If `CLR C` is omitted and CY was already 1, the first `ADD` would erroneously compute a sum 1 higher than intended.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**(a)** *Write an 8051 assembly language program to add two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations 20H and 21H. Store the sum in 22H and the carry (if any) in bit 7 of PSW.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the function of `DA A` with a suitable example. How does it differ from a normal `ADD` instruction?* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Algorithm:**
1. Load the accumulator with the first operand.
2. Add the second operand (no `CLR C` is required here because we want any carry to be captured).
3. Store the result in 22H.
4. Move CY to a memory location (or simply leave it in PSW.7, which the question allows).

**Assembly Code:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     A,   20H        ; A ← NUM1 (1 byte, 1 cycle)
        ADD     A,   21H        ; A ← NUM1 + NUM2, sets CY if overflow (1 byte, 1 cycle)
        MOV     22H, A          ; store sum (2 bytes, 1 cycle)
        ; The carry is now in PSW.7 (CY bit, address D7H).
        ; If the question wants it stored elsewhere:
        MOV     0D7H, C         ; (already in CY, but explicit store)
        ; Alternative: MOV 23H.0, C  -- store CY into bit 0 of internal RAM 23H
        SJMP    $               ; infinite loop (stop here)
        END
```

**Trace Example:**
Let (20H) = `F0H` and (21H) = `20H`. Then A = `F0H + 20H = 110H`. The low byte is `10H`, stored in (22H). CY = 1, AC = 0 (since `0 + 0 = 0` no lower-nibble carry), OV = 0 (no signed overflow because both operands were negative, so adding two negatives cannot overflow the signed range), P is set according to parity of `10H` (`0001 0000` has one '1' → P = 1 for odd parity, which means the parity bit is set to make the total even? No — in 8051, P is set if A has an odd number of 1s. `10H` has 1 one → odd → P = 1).

**Result:** (22H) = `10H`, CY = 1. ✓

**Register Summary Table (for valuation):**

| Register / Flag | Value after execution |
|-----------------|------------------------|
| A | `10H` |
| (20H) | `F0H` (unchanged) |
| (21H) | `20H` (unchanged) |
| (22H) | `10H` (sum) |
| CY (PSW.7) | 1 |
| AC (PSW.6) | 0 |
| OV (PSW.2) | 0 |
| P (PSW.0) | 1 |

**Mark Allocation Hint:**
- 2 marks: Correct structure of the program (ORG, END).
- 2 marks: Correct use of `MOV` and `ADD` with valid addressing.
- 1 mark: Proper storage of the result in 22H.
- 1 mark: Identification that CY holds the carry naturally.
- 1 mark: Correct trace / flag explanation.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

`DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) is a special instruction that adjusts the binary result of an `ADD` or `ADDC` to produce a valid **BCD (Binary Coded Decimal)** representation. It does this by conditionally adding `00H, 06H, 60H,` or `66H` to the accumulator based on the values of the **AC (auxiliary carry)** and **CY (carry)** flags and the nibble values.

**Rules of `DA A`:**
1. If the **lower nibble of A > 9** OR **AC = 1**, then add `06H` to A.
2. If the **upper nibble of A > 9** OR **CY = 1**, then add `60H` to A.

**Example:** Add BCD 95 and BCD 17. Expected BCD result: 112 (with carry 1 into a hypothetical next BCD digit).

```asm
        MOV     A,   #95H       ; A = 95H (BCD 95)
        ADD     A,   #17H       ; A = ACH (binary 172, but NOT valid BCD!)
        DA      A               ; A = 12H, CY = 1 (BCD 1 1 2 = 112)
```

**Step-by-step DA A action:**
- After `ADD`: A = `ACH`. Lower nibble = `CH` = 12 > 9 → add `06H`. A becomes `ACH + 06H = B2H`. AC was also set by the addition of the lower nibbles `5H + 7H = 12H = 0CH` with carry into the upper nibble.
- Now upper nibble of A (`BH`) > 9 → add `60H`. A becomes `B2H + 60H = 12H`. CY becomes 1.
- Final: A = `12H`, CY = 1. The combined result in BCD is **1 1 2** = 112 decimal ✓

**Difference from `ADD`:**
- `ADD` performs pure binary addition.
- `DA A` is a **post-correction** that fixes the BCD result; it does not add the operands by itself. It must follow an `ADD` or `ADDC`.
- `DA A` affects only the **CY** and **P** flags (not AC or OV).

**Mark Allocation Hint:**
- 2 marks: Definition of `DA A` and its purpose (BCD correction).
- 2 marks: Statement of the two correction rules.
- 2 marks: Worked numerical example (the 95 + 17 = 112 case is the KTU classic).
- 1 mark: Difference from `ADD` (post-correction vs operand addition).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] — Internal Choice Alternative
**(a)** *Write an 8051 ALP to copy a block of 10 bytes of data from code memory starting at address 0200H to internal RAM locations starting at 30H. Use the `MOVC` instruction.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *With a neat diagram, explain the function of the `PSW` register. List the roles of the CY, AC, OV, and P flags with one example each.* **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Algorithm:**
- Use DPTR to point to the source in code memory.
- Use R0 to point to the destination in internal RAM.
- Use R2 as the counter.
- In a loop, read a byte using `CLR A; MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (or `INC A` then `MOVC`) and store using `MOV @R0, A`.

**Assembly Code:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     DPTR, #0200H    ; DPTR ← source base in code memory
        MOV     R0,   #30H      ; R0 ← destination base in internal RAM
        MOV     R2,   #0AH      ; R2 ← counter = 10
LOOP:   CLR     A               ; A ← 0 (offset for MOVC)
        MOVC    A,   @A+DPTR    ; A ← Code[DPTR + 0] = Code[DPTR]
        MOV     @R0, A          ; (R0) ← A
        INC     DPTR            ; advance source pointer
        INC     R0              ; advance destination pointer
        DJNZ    R2,  LOOP       ; loop 10 times
        SJMP    $               ; stop
        END
```

**Trace (first iteration):**
- DPTR = `0200H`, R0 = `30H`, R2 = `0AH`
- `CLR A` → A = `00H`
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A = Code[`0200H + 00H`] = Code[`0200H`]
- `MOV @R0, A` → (30H) = byte at code memory 0200H
- `INC DPTR` → DPTR = `0201H`
- `INC R0` → R0 = `31H`
- `DJNZ R2, LOOP` → R2 = `09H`, not zero → loop

After 10 iterations, internal RAM 30H–39H contains a copy of code memory 0200H–0209H.

**Mark Allocation Hint:**
- 2 marks: Correct initialization (DPTR, R0, R2).
- 2 marks: Correct loop structure with `MOVC`.
- 1 mark: Storage using `MOV @R0, A`.
- 1 mark: Proper use of `INC DPTR`, `INC R0`, `DJNZ R2`.
- 1 mark: Trace of at least one iteration.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 Marks)

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an **8-bit, bit-addressable Special Function Register (SFR)** located at internal RAM address `D0H` (SFR space). It contains the arithmetic flags and the register bank select bits.

**Bit Map of PSW:**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | − | P |
| **Byte Addr** | D7H | D6H | D5H | D4H | D3H | D2H | D1H | D0H |

**Roles of Key Flags:**

1. **CY (PSW.7) — Carry Flag:**
   Set when an arithmetic operation produces a carry out of bit 7 (for addition) or requires a borrow (for subtraction).
   *Example:* `MOV A, #0FFH; ADD A, #02H` → A = `01H`, CY = 1.

2. **AC (PSW.6) — Auxiliary Carry Flag:**
   Set when there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (i.e., lower nibble overflow). Used by `DA A` to perform BCD correction.
   *Example:* `MOV A, #0FH; ADD A, #01H` → A = `10H`, AC = 1.

3. **OV (PSW.2) — Overflow Flag:**
   Set when a **signed** arithmetic operation produces a result outside the range −128 to +127. Computed as $C_7 \oplus C_6$ (carry into MSB XOR carry out of MSB).
   *Example:* `MOV A, #7FH; ADD A, #01H` → A = `80H` (interpreted as −128 in signed 2's complement), CY = 0, OV = 1.

4. **P (PSW.0) — Parity Flag:**
   Automatically set if the accumulator contains an **odd** number of 1-bits. (This way, the total of 1s including P is always even.)
   *Example:* `MOV A, #05H` (binary `0000 0101`, two 1s — even) → P = 0. `MOV A, #07H` (three 1s — odd) → P = 1.

**RS1:RS0 — Register Bank Select:**
- 00 → Bank 0 (default)
- 01 → Bank 1
- 10 → Bank 2
- 11 → Bank 3

**Mark Allocation Hint:**
- 2 marks: Correct PSW bit map (table or diagram).
- 4 marks: One mark each for correctly defining CY, AC, OV, P with example.
- 1 mark: Mention of register bank selection role.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `CLR C` before `ADD` / `ADDC` in multi-byte addition (loses 2 marks).** The examiner's key explicitly checks for `CLR C` or equivalent `ANL PSW, #7FH` before the first `ADD`.
>
> 2. **Confusing `MOVX` with `MOV` for external RAM access (loses 2 marks).** `MOV` cannot access external RAM — only `MOVX` can. Writing `MOV A, @DPTR` is a syntax error.
>
> 3. **Using `SUB` instead of `SUBB` (loses 1 mark).** There is no `SUB` instruction. Always use `SUBB A, src` after `CLR C`.
>
> 4. **Stating that `INC` affects CY (loses 1 mark).** `INC` affects **NO** flags. Only `ADD` and `ADDC` set CY.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to clear the accumulator before `MOVC` look-up (loses 1 mark).** If A contains garbage (e.g., from a previous operation), the look-up will return the wrong byte. Always `CLR A` (or load the desired offset) before `MOVC`.
>
> 6. **Wrong addressing mode identification (loses 1 mark).** A common error is calling `MOV A, @R0` "Direct Addressing". The correct answer is "Register Indirect Addressing".
>
> 7. **Not stating the unit of machine cycles (1 µs at 12 MHz crystal).** If the question asks for time, you must multiply cycles by 1 µs. Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Accumulator (A)** is the *hub* of the 8051. Almost every arithmetic and data-transfer instruction involves A.
- **B register** is used *exclusively* by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`. In `MUL`, A gets the low byte and B gets the high byte. In `DIV`, A gets the quotient and B gets the remainder.
- **Three flavours of MOV:**
  - `MOV` → Internal RAM / SFR (any addressing mode).
  - `MOVX` → External data RAM (only `@Ri` or `@DPTR`, only A is involved).
  - `MOVC` → Program ROM (only `A+DPTR` or `A+PC`, read-only).
- **`CLR C` is mandatory** before the first `ADD` in any multi-byte addition (and before any `SUBB`).
- **`INC` does NOT affect any flag** — including CY. This is critical when extending addition to multi-byte (use `ADDC` for higher bytes).
- **There is no `SUB` instruction** — only `SUBB`. The CY flag acts as a *borrow* flag in subtraction.
- **Multiplication overflow:** `OV = 1` if the product > 255 (i.e., if B ≠ 0 after `MUL AB`).
- **Divide-by-zero protection:** `OV = 1` if B = 0 at the time of `DIV AB`. The result registers A and B are left in an undefined state.
- **`DA A`** corrects BCD addition by adding `06H` and/or `60H` based on the lower nibble, upper nibble, AC, and CY.
- **Stack grows upward** in the 8051: `PUSH` increments SP first, then writes; `POP` reads first, then decrements SP.
- **PSW (SFR D0H)** is bit-addressable. CY, AC, OV, P are arithmetic flags; RS1, RS0 select register banks.
- **Default register bank** after reset is **Bank 0** (addresses 00H–07H).
- **Look-up tables** are placed in code memory using `DB` directives. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` reads a byte from the table.
- **The `XCHD` instruction** exchanges only the *lower nibble* of A with the lower nibble of the memory location pointed to by `@Ri` — extremely useful for BCD digit manipulations.
- **Machine cycles at 12 MHz crystal = 1 µs.** Standard `MOV` = 1 cycle, `MOVX`/`MOVC` = 2 cycles, `MUL AB`/`DIV AB` = 4 cycles.
- **NO `MOV Rn, Rn`** in the 8051 — always go through A. This is the single most-asked 8051 "absence" question in KTU.
- **`SWAP A`** swaps the upper and lower nibbles of A (no memory involved). Very handy in BCD packing after `DIV AB`.
- **The `DA A` instruction does NOT work after `SUBB`.** It is only valid after `ADD` / `ADDC`. This is a classic trick question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 Programming in C: Data Types and Time Delay in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 8051 Programming in C: Data Types and Time Delay in 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition: 8051 C Data Types

In the context of the **MCS-51 (8051) family of microcontrollers** programmed using the C language (typically the **Keil µVision / C51 compiler**), a **data type** is a classification that specifies:
1. The amount of **internal data memory (IRAM)** or **code memory** the variable will occupy.
2. The **range of values** the variable can represent.
3. The set of **operations (operators)** that can be performed on it.

The 8051 is an **8-bit accumulator-based Harvard architecture** processor. Therefore, its native word size is **8 bits**, and the compiler maps standard ANSI C data types onto the 8051's underlying 8-bit register and memory architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike a 32-bit ARM Cortex or general-purpose PC, the 8051 has a native 8-bit ALU. C data types are therefore *scaled down* — an `int` is still 16-bit, but `char` is the most natural and efficient type for the hardware.

### 1.2 Formal Definition: Time Delay in 8051

**Time delay** in 8051 programming refers to the intentional consumption of CPU clock cycles (and therefore real time) by executing a known number of instructions in a controlled loop, without performing any useful I/O operation. It is generated when the CPU is "kept busy" doing nothing useful for a calculated duration.

The fundamental unit of delay is the **machine cycle**, which for the original 8051 equals **12 oscillator periods**.

> **Oscillator Period:** $T_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}}$
>
> **Machine Cycle Time:** $T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — Data Types as Containers:**
Think of 8051 data types as **boxes in a warehouse**:
- A `bit` is a **single small envelope** — holds one yes/no (1 or 0). Perfect for a light switch state.
- A `unsigned char` is a **small shoebox** that can hold numbers from 0 to 255. Great for storing one ASCII character or a small sensor reading.
- An `unsigned int` is a **large carton** that can hold 0 to 65535. Used for bigger counts like timer overflow values.
- A `float` is a **precision measuring cup** that can hold decimal numbers — but it costs more "shelf space" (32 bits) and the CPU has to work harder to use it.

**Analogy 2 — Time Delay as a Bathroom Timer:**
Imagine you are told to "stand and do nothing for exactly 5 minutes." You count: "1-Mississippi, 2-Mississippi..." Each "Mississippi" takes about 1 second. By counting to 300, you consume 5 minutes. Similarly, the 8051 executes empty loop iterations — each iteration takes a known number of "Mississippis" (machine cycles). By carefully choosing the loop count, we consume exactly the desired time.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics to Remember

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Native ALU width of 8051 | **8 bits** |
| Clock cycles per machine cycle | **12** |
| 8051 SFR bus width | **8 bits** |
| IRAM (internal RAM) size | **128 bytes** (standard 8051) |
| XRAM (external) addressable | **64 KB** |

> [!NOTE]
> The original 8051 divides its oscillator frequency by 12 internally. This is a fixed hardware design choice and **cannot be changed by software**. Every textbook delay formula is built on this fact.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8051 C Data Type Family

The Keil C51 compiler extends standard ANSI C with **8051-specific data types** that map directly onto the hardware's special-purpose areas. These are **high-yield for KTU** because they appear in every peripheral programming question.

#### 2.1.1 Standard C Data Types (Re-mapped for 8051)

| Data Type | Size (Bits) | Size (Bytes) | Range | 8051 Hardware Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `bit` | 1 | — (bit-addressable) | 0 or 1 | Stored in bit-addressable RAM (20H–2FH), 16 bytes = 128 bits |
| `signed char` | 8 | 1 | **−128 to +127** | Uses 2's complement; one bit for sign |
| `unsigned char` | 8 | 1 | **0 to 255** | Most natural type; full byte, no sign bit wasted |
| `enum` | 8 or 16 | 1 or 2 | Implementation-defined | |
| `signed short` | 16 | 2 | −32 768 to +32 767 | |
| `unsigned short` | 16 | 2 | 0 to 65 535 | |
| `signed int` | 16 | 2 | −32 768 to +32 767 | Note: still 16-bit on 8051 (not 32-bit) |
| `unsigned int` | 16 | 2 | 0 to 65 535 | Used for `THx/TLx` timer reload values |
| `signed long` | 32 | 4 | −2 147 483 648 to +2 147 483 647 | |
| `unsigned long` | 32 | 4 | 0 to 4 294 967 295 | |
| `float` | 32 | 4 | ±1.175×10⁻³⁸ to ±3.4×10³⁸ | IEEE 754 single precision; uses **floating-point library** |
| `double` | 32 or 64 | 4 or 8 | Same as float in Keil C51 | Compiler-dependent |
| `*` (pointer) | 16 (idata) / 24 (generic) | 2 or 3 | Memory address | 16-bit for on-chip RAM, 24-bit (3 bytes) for code/XDATA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** A common error is assuming `int` is 32-bit on the 8051. It is **always 16-bit** in C51. The 8051's math operations on larger types are slow and use library routines.

#### 2.1.2 8051-Specific Data Types (The "s-family")

These are **not in standard ANSI C** — they are Keil C51 extensions that directly address the 8051's special hardware regions. They are tested almost every semester.

| Data Type | Purpose | Memory Region | Example Declaration |
|---|---|---|---|
| `sfr` | Declares a name for an **8-bit Special Function Register** | SFR space (80H–FFH) | `sfr P1 = 0x90;` |
| `sfr16` | Declares a name for a **16-bit SFR** (timers only) | Two consecutive SFR bytes | `sfr16 DPTR = 0x82;` |
| `sbit` | Declares a name for a **single bit** in a bit-addressable SFR or RAM | Bit-addressable space | `sbit LED = P1^0;` |
| `bit` | Declares a **bit variable** in IRAM | Bit-addressable RAM (20H–2FH) | `bit flag;` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention Rule:** `sfr` and `sfr16` declarations must refer to **actual hardware addresses** and must be made *outside* any function. `sbit` has three legal forms: `sbit name = SFRname^bit_position;`, `sbit name = 0x80^bit_position;`, or `sbit name = absolute_address;`.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Data Type Choice

- **Why `unsigned char` is the workhorse:** The 8051's accumulator (A), all SFRs, and all general-purpose registers (R0–R7) are 8-bit. Using `unsigned char` avoids sign-extension overhead and matches the hardware bus width perfectly.
- **Why `sfr` exists:** SFR addresses (80H–FFH) overlap with internal RAM in the 8051's logical address map, but they are accessed through a *different physical bus* selected by the hardware. `sfr` tells the compiler to generate the correct instruction (e.g., `MOV 0x90, A`).
- **Why `sbit` exists:** Eleven SFRs in the original 8051 (P0, P1, P2, P3, PSW, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, P4 — on some variants) are bit-addressable. `sbit` lets the programmer give a human name to a single bit (e.g., `sbit LED = P1^0;`) so they can write `LED = 1;` instead of `P1 = P1 | 0x01;`.

### 2.3 Time Delay — The Core Theory

#### 2.3.1 Machine Cycle First Principles

The 8051's oscillator drives an internal divider. **Every 12 oscillator periods become one machine cycle**, which is the basic "tick" for all instruction timings.

> **Key Equations:**
>
> $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$
>
> $$\text{Number of machine cycles in time } t = \frac{t}{T_{MC}} = \frac{t \cdot f_{osc}}{12}$$

#### 2.3.2 Common Crystal Frequencies and Their Machine Cycle Times

| Crystal Frequency $f_{osc}$ | Machine Cycle Time $T_{MC}$ | Why This Crystal? |
|---|---|---|
| 11.0592 MHz | **1.085 µs** | Gives exact integer baud rates for serial communication |
| 12.0000 MHz | **1.000 µs** | Simplest math; used in academic examples |
| 16.0000 MHz | 0.750 µs | Used in some modern variants like AT89S52 enhanced |
| 24.0000 MHz | 0.500 µs | Used in fast variants like STC15 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Reality:** Almost all numerical delay problems in KTU papers assume either **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz**. The examiner expects you to write the answer with **at most 2 decimal places** in microseconds (µs) or milliseconds (ms). Showing the machine cycle time calculation explicitly earns full method marks.

#### 2.3.3 The Delay Loop — How it Works

A C-language delay is constructed using a `for` (or `while`) loop whose *only* purpose is to consume clock cycles. The compiler translates the C loop into 8051 assembly — and we must know how many machine cycles each compiled instruction costs.

**A typical simple for loop:**
```c
for(i = 0; i < X; i++);
```

The Keil C51 compiler generates approximately the following 8051 assembly (academic simplification):

| Assembly Instruction | Machine Cycles | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `MOV R7, #00H` (initialize `i` to 0) | 1 | Loop setup, executes **once** |
| Loop label: | — | |
| `INC R7` (i++) | 1 | |
| `CJNE R7, #X, loop` (compare & branch) | 2 | |
| *(continue back to INC)* | — | |
| Total per iteration after the first | **3 machine cycles** | |
| First iteration overhead | 1 machine cycle | Initialization |

> **Total Machine Cycles for `for(i=0; i<X; i++);`:**
>
> $$N_{MC} = 1 + 3X$$
>
> But note: in the **startup time of the C function call** (saving registers, returning), an additional ~2–4 machine cycles is consumed. The most commonly used *textbook* value is:
>
> $$N_{MC} \approx 8X \text{ (for inner loop, simplified 8051 textbook formula)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the two values?** Pure academic analyses (Mazidi) use **8 cycles/iteration** as a clean approximation that includes overhead. Rigorous Keil-disassembly-based analyses use the exact 1 + 3X figure. **For KTU 2024, use the formula your module notes / class lecture used** — typically the simplified 8X or 1+3X.

#### 2.3.4 Nested Loop Delay — The General Case

For long delays (milliseconds and above), a *single* loop with the largest possible X would still be too short because `unsigned char` only allows X ≤ 255. The solution is **nesting**:

```c
for(i = 0; i < M; i++){     // Outer loop
    for(j = 0; j < N; j++){  // Inner loop
        ;                     // Empty body
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The **inner loop** runs N times per outer iteration. Each inner iteration consumes a constant $C_{inner}$ machine cycles (after the first inner iteration).
2. The **outer loop** runs M times. Each outer iteration runs the inner loop fully, then does its own bookkeeping.
3. The total machine cycles:

> **Nested Delay Formula:**
>
> $$N_{MC,\text{total}} = (C_{outer,\text{overhead}}) + M \times \big[(C_{inner,\text{overhead}}) + N \times C_{inner,\text{body}}\big]$$
>
> **Simplified KTU Standard Form:**
>
> $$N_{MC,\text{total}} \approx M \times (N \times C_{inner} + C_{outer})$$
>
> where $C_{inner} = 8$ machine cycles (inner loop body + bookkeeping) and $C_{outer} = 8$ machine cycles (outer loop bookkeeping per iteration), and there is a one-time startup cost.

> **Real-world delay in seconds:**
>
> $$t_{\text{delay}} = N_{MC,\text{total}} \times T_{MC} = N_{MC,\text{total}} \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

#### 2.3.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Machine Cycle Time | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds (convert to µs by ×10⁶) |
| Number of machine cycles in time $t$ | $N_{MC} = \dfrac{t}{T_{MC}} = \dfrac{t \cdot f_{osc}}{12}$ | dimensionless count |
| Single `for` loop cycles (Mazidi) | $N_{MC} = 8X$ | dimensionless |
| Single `for` loop cycles (rigorous) | $N_{MC} = 1 + 3X$ | dimensionless |
| Nested loop total cycles | $N_{MC} = M \times (N \times C_{in} + C_{out})$ | dimensionless |
| Time delay from cycle count | $t = N_{MC} \times T_{MC}$ | seconds |
| Solving for inner loop count $N$ | $N = \dfrac{t}{M \cdot T_{MC} \cdot C_{in}} - \dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint Boundary:** For `unsigned char` loop counters, $0 \leq X \leq 255$. For `unsigned int`, $0 \leq X \leq 65\,535$. If the calculated $N > 255$, you *must* use nested loops or switch to `int`.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Time-delay loops are **historically essential** in 8051 programming and still appear in modern embedded systems:

- **Debouncing mechanical switches:** A 5–20 ms delay ignores the contact-bounce noise.
- **LCD character-write pacing:** HD44780 LCDs require ~40 µs between commands and ~1.64 ms after clear-display.
- **Seven-segment multiplexing:** Each digit is shown for ~1–5 ms before switching to the next.
- **Sensor settling time:** Many analog sensors (e.g., LM35) need a few µs after channel switch.
- **Tone generation on a buzzer:** A precise delay loop generates a square wave of known frequency.

In modern design, **hardware timers** (T0, T1, T2) replace these software loops, but the *principle of counting machine cycles* is identical and **always tested** in KTU Module 2.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Machine Cycle Time for Standard Crystals

**Statement:** For an 8051 system using a 11.0592 MHz crystal oscillator, derive the machine cycle time.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.**
The 8051 hardware divides the external oscillator by a fixed factor of **12** to produce the internal machine cycle clock.

$$f_{MC} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known crystal frequency.**

$$f_{MC} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}{12} = 0.9216 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} = 921.6 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Take the reciprocal to obtain the period.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{1}{0.9216 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert seconds to microseconds (× 10⁶).**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{10^{6}}{0.9216 \times 10^{6}} \text{ µs} = \frac{1}{0.9216} \text{ µs}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$T_{MC} \approx 1.085 \text{ µs}$$

**Conclusion:** Each machine cycle of the 8051 lasts **1.085 µs** when $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz. This is the **single most-used number** in all KTU 8051 delay problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via direct formula:** $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085069... \times 10^{-6}$ s $= 1.085$ µs. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Delay from a Simple `for` Loop (Rigorous)

**Statement:** An 8051 runs at 12 MHz. Find the delay produced by:
```c
for(i = 0; i < 200; i++);
```

**Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle time.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Use the rigorous formula for cycle count.**

$$N_{MC} = 1 + 3X$$

where $X = 200$ is the loop limit. The `1` accounts for the initial `MOV` to set `i = 0`, and `3X` accounts for each subsequent `INC` (1 cycle) + `CJNE` (2 cycles).

**Step 3 — Substitute X = 200.**

$$N_{MC} = 1 + 3(200) = 1 + 600 = 601 \text{ machine cycles}$$

**Step 4 — Convert cycles to time using the cycle time from Step 1.**

$$t = N_{MC} \times T_{MC} = 601 \times 1 \text{ µs} = 601 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check with the simplified Mazidi formula (8X) for sanity.**

$$N_{MC,\text{approx}} = 8 \times 200 = 1600 \text{ cycles} \Rightarrow t \approx 1600 \text{ µs} = 1.6 \text{ ms}$$

The two methods give *different* answers because the rigorous 1+3X excludes function-call overhead, while the 8X approximation includes it. **For KTU, the value the examiner marks depends on which formula is in your module notes.**

> **Conclusion (12 MHz, X = 200):**
> - Rigorous (1+3X): **601 µs**
> - Textbook approximate (8X): **1600 µs = 1.6 ms**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solving for the Loop Count (Inverse Problem)

**Statement:** An 8051 with a 11.0592 MHz crystal must generate a delay of **500 µs** using a single `for` loop. Find the value of $X$.

**Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle time.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.085 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of machine cycles needed.**

$$N_{MC} = \frac{t}{T_{MC}} = \frac{500 \text{ µs}}{1.085 \text{ µs/cycle}} = 460.83 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Use the loop formula and solve for X.**
Using the simplified formula $N_{MC} = 8X$:

$$X = \frac{N_{MC}}{8} = \frac{460.83}{8} = 57.60$$

Since $X$ must be an integer, we round:

$$X = 58$$

**Step 4 — Verify by computing back the actual delay.**

$$N_{MC,\text{actual}} = 8 \times 58 = 464 \text{ cycles}$$

$$t_{\text{actual}} = 464 \times 1.085 \text{ µs} = 503.44 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 5 — Report the percent error.**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{503.44 - 500}{500} \times 100\% = +0.69\%$$

**Conclusion:** Using $X = 58$ gives a delay of ≈ **503 µs**, which is within 1% of the target 500 µs — acceptable for embedded applications.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Nested Loop Delay (Long Delay Problem)

**Statement:** An 8051 with a 12 MHz crystal must generate a delay of **100 ms** using a nested loop:
```c
for(i = 0; i < M; i++){
    for(j = 0; j < N; j++){
        ;
    }
}
```
Find suitable integer values of $M$ and $N$ (both fitting in `unsigned char`).

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle time.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total machine cycles required.**

$$N_{MC,\text{total}} = \frac{t}{T_{MC}} = \frac{100 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ µs}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 100\,000 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Choose an outer loop count $M$ to make the math clean.**
We must keep $N \leq 255$. Let's choose $M = 200$.

**Step 4 — Compute the required inner loop cycles per outer iteration.**

$$N_{MC,\text{per outer}} = \frac{N_{MC,\text{total}}}{M} = \frac{100\,000}{200} = 500 \text{ cycles per outer iteration}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for N using the inner loop formula.**
Using $N_{MC,\text{inner}} = 8N$ (per inner loop body), and accounting for outer bookkeeping:

$$N_{MC,\text{per outer}} = 8N + \text{outer overhead per iteration}$$

Ignoring overhead for simplicity (standard KTU approach):

$$N = \frac{N_{MC,\text{per outer}}}{8} = \frac{500}{8} = 62.5$$

Rounding to $N = 63$:

$$N_{MC,\text{inner}} = 8 \times 63 = 504 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the total cycles actually produced.**

$$N_{MC,\text{total,actual}} = M \times 504 = 200 \times 504 = 100\,800 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 7 — Convert back to time.**

$$t_{\text{actual}} = 100\,800 \times 1 \text{ µs} = 100.8 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the percent error.**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{100.8 - 100}{100} \times 100\% = +0.8\%$$

**Conclusion:** With $M = 200$ and $N = 63$, we get a delay of **100.8 ms**, which is within 1% of the target 100 ms. This is an **excellent** solution for KTU 14-mark nested delay questions.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** When asked to "design a delay of X ms," the examiner's expected approach is:
> 1. Compute $T_{MC}$
> 2. Compute $N_{MC}$
> 3. Choose $M$ such that the required inner count $N$ falls in the range **10 to 250** (not too small, not at the boundary).
> 4. State your final answer *with units* and *with the actual achieved delay* (not the requested one).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Situation | Recommended Approach | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Delay < 256 µs (at 12 MHz) | Single loop, `unsigned char` X | One iteration is ≈ 8 µs; X ≤ 255 gives ≤ 2040 µs. |
| Delay 1–10 ms (at 12 MHz) | Single loop, **OR** outer M small | Pick X = 255, get ≈ 2 ms; or use nested with M=1, N=255. |
| Delay 10 ms – 1 s | Nested loops with `unsigned char` | Use 2-level nesting; pick M × 8N to span the range. |
| Delay > 1 s | 3-level nested OR use **hardware timer** | Software delay is wasteful; the 8051 cannot do anything else. |
| Precise baud-rate generation | **Hardware timer in auto-reload mode** | Loop-based delays cannot be interrupt-safe. |
| Multi-tasking | Use **RTOS** or cooperative flags | Pure delay loops block the CPU. |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: 8051 C Data Type Memory Map

The following diagram shows how each C data type maps onto the 8051's physical memory regions. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (per the protocol, since a true physical memory-map drawing would be a pixel grid).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["C Source Program with Data Declarations"] --> B{"Data Type Used?"}
    B --> C1["bit"]
    B --> C2["unsigned char / signed char"]
    B --> C3["unsigned int / signed int"]
    B --> C4["unsigned long / float"]
    B --> C5["sfr"]
    B --> C6["sbit"]
    B --> C7["sfr16"]

    C1 --> D1["Bit-Addressable RAM Region 20H to 2FH\n128 addressable bits stored in 16 bytes"]
    C2 --> D2["Lower 128 Bytes IRAM 00H to 7FH\nGeneral purpose user variables"]
    C3 --> D3["IRAM or XDATA region\nCompiler may use 2 bytes per int"]
    C4 --> D4["XDATA External Memory\n4 bytes for long or float"]
    C5 --> D5["SFR Space 80H to FFH\nSpecial Function Registers"]
    C6 --> D6["Bit Addressable within SFRs\nP0 P1 P2 P3 TCON SCON IE IP PSW A B"]
    C7 --> D7["Two Consecutive SFR Bytes\nTimer 0 Timer 1 or DPTR"]

    D1 --> E["Compiled Code Reads Writes Memory Bus During Execution"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Nested Loop Time Delay Logic

The following is the **exact flow** of execution during a nested-loop delay. This is the diagram the KTU examiner expects you to sketch in 14-mark delay problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Program Counter Reaches Delay Function]) --> InitOut["Initialize outer counter i = 0"]
    InitOut --> CheckOut{"Is i less than M?"}
    CheckOut -- No --> Exit([Exit Delay Function Return to Caller])
    CheckOut -- Yes --> InitIn["Initialize inner counter j = 0"]
    InitIn --> CheckIn{"Is j less than N?"}
    CheckIn -- No --> IncOut["Increment outer i = i + 1"]
    IncOut --> CheckOut
    CheckIn -- Yes --> Empty["Empty Body No Operation Consumes Cycles"]
    Empty --> IncIn["Increment inner j = j + 1"]
    IncIn --> CheckIn
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: From Crystal to Time Delay

This shows the chain of hardware and software that converts a crystal frequency into a measurable software delay.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Crystal Oscillator\n11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz"] --> B["Internal Divider\nDivide by 12"]
    B --> C["Machine Cycle Clock\n921.6 kHz or 1 MHz"]
    C --> D["Each Instruction Consumes\n1 2 or 4 Machine Cycles"]
    D --> E["Compiled for Loop\nConsumes 8 Cycles per Iteration"]
    E --> F["Total Cycles = M times 8N plus 8"]
    F --> G["Multiply by T sub MC 1.085 us or 1 us"]
    G --> H["Final Time Delay in microseconds or milliseconds"]
```

### 4.4 Concept Map: 8051 C Data Types and Their Use Cases

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root["8051 C Data Types"] --> Std["Standard C Types"]
    Root --> Ext["8051 Specific Types"]

    Std --> Char["char and unsigned char\n8 bits, 1 byte, range 0 to 255"]
    Std --> Int["int and unsigned int\n16 bits, 2 bytes, range 0 to 65535"]
    Std --> Lng["long and unsigned long\n32 bits, 4 bytes"]
    Std --> Flt["float and double\n32 bits, IEEE 754, slow math"]

    Ext --> Sfr["sfr for 8 bit registers\nExample sfr P1 = 0x90"]
    Ext --> Sfr16["sfr16 for 16 bit registers\nExample sfr16 DPTR = 0x82"]
    Ext --> Sbit["sbit for single bit access\nExample sbit LED = P1^0"]
    Ext --> Bit["bit for single bit variable\nStored in bit addressable RAM"]

    Char --> Use1["Loop counters up to 255\nASCII character storage"]
    Int --> Use2["16 bit math and timer reload values"]
    Lng --> Use3["Long counters and 32 bit arithmetic"]
    Flt --> Use4["Decimal sensor values like temperature"]
    Sfr --> Use5["Access hardware registers P0 P1 P2 P3 TMOD TCON SCON IE IP"]
    Sfr16 --> Use6["Read 16 bit timer value or DPTR in one step"]
    Sbit --> Use7["Direct bit control LED Relay Motor Pin"]
    Bit --> Use8["Software flags and boolean state variables"]
```

### 4.5 Comparison Table: Software Delay vs Hardware Timer Delay

| Aspect | Software Delay (for loop) | Hardware Timer Delay |
|---|---|---|
| CPU occupancy | **100% blocked** during delay | CPU free to do other work |
| Accuracy | ±1% (rounding) | Exact, crystal-controlled |
| Interrupt response | **Destroyed** by delay | Interrupts fire normally |
| Maximum delay | Practical limit ≈ 100 ms | Hours possible (with prescaler) |
| Code complexity | 4–6 lines of C | 5–8 lines of C (config TMOD, TCON) |
| KTU exam use | Module 2 (this topic) | Module 3 (timers) |
