## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **List the 8051-specific data types in Keil C and state the purpose of `sbit`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051-specific (non-ANSI) data types in Keil C51 are:

1. **`sfr`** — Used to declare a name for an **8-bit Special Function Register** by giving its absolute SFR address in the range 80H to FFH. Example: `sfr P1 = 0x90;` makes `P1` a symbolic name for Port 1.
2. **`sfr16`** — Used to declare a name for a **16-bit SFR pair** (such as Timer 0, Timer 1, or DPTR). Example: `sfr16 T0 = 0x8A;` (TL0 at 8AH, TH0 at 8CH).
3. **`sbit`** — Used to declare a name for an **individually addressable bit** inside a bit-addressable SFR or in the bit-addressable region 20H–2FH of internal RAM.
4. **`bit`** — Used to declare a **single-bit software variable** that resides in the bit-addressable RAM region.

**Purpose of `sbit`:** It provides a human-readable name for a single bit of a port or SFR, so that the programmer can write `LED = 1;` instead of the bit-manipulation expression `P1 = P1 | 0x01;`. It maps a name to one of the 128 bit-addressable locations inside the 8051. *(1 mark for list, 2 marks for `sbit` purpose.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define machine cycle of 8051. Calculate the machine cycle time for a system with crystal frequency 11.0592 MHz.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** A *machine cycle* is the basic unit of time for instruction execution in the 8051. **One machine cycle consists of 12 oscillator periods** and is the time taken to perform a single fetch-decode-execute micro-operation by the internal state machine. *(1 mark)*

**Calculation:**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}$$

$$T_{MC} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$

**Machine cycle time = 1.085 µs.** *(2 marks for derivation and numerical value.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the different 8051-specific data types supported by the Keil C51 compiler with suitable examples. Discuss the size and range of standard C data types when compiled for the 8051. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An 8051 microcontroller uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Design a C program to generate a time delay of **200 ms** using nested `for` loops. Calculate the exact delay achieved and show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Standard C Data Types on 8051 (3 marks):**

| Data Type | Size (bits) | Range |
|---|---|---|
| `unsigned char` | 8 | 0 to 255 |
| `signed char` | 8 | −128 to +127 |
| `unsigned int` | 16 | 0 to 65 535 |
| `signed int` | 16 | −32 768 to +32 767 |
| `unsigned long` | 32 | 0 to 4 294 967 295 |
| `signed long` | 32 | −2 147 483 648 to +2 147 483 647 |
| `float` | 32 | ±1.175×10⁻³⁸ to ±3.402×10³⁸ |

The 8051's native ALU is 8 bits, so `unsigned char` is the most efficient type. The `int` is 16 bits (not 32 as in modern 32-bit CPUs). The `float` uses IEEE 754 single precision and requires the floating-point library, which makes it slow — it is rarely used in 8051 production code.

**8051-Specific Data Types (4 marks):**

```c
/* sfr: declare an 8-bit Special Function Register */
sfr P1 = 0x90;       /* Port 1 lives at SFR address 90H */
sfr TCON = 0x88;     /* Timer Control register at 88H */
sfr SCON = 0x98;     /* Serial Control register at 98H */

/* sfr16: declare a 16-bit SFR pair */
sfr16 T0 = 0x8A;     /* Timer 0 = TL0 (8AH) + TH0 (8CH) */
sfr16 DPTR = 0x82;   /* Data Pointer = DPL (82H) + DPH (83H) */

/* sbit: declare a single bit of a bit-addressable SFR or RAM */
sbit LED = P1^0;     /* Pin 0 of Port 1, accessed as a single bit */
sbit TI  = SCON^1;   /* Transmit Interrupt flag, bit 1 of SCON */

/* bit: declare a single-bit software variable */
bit flag;            /* Resides in bit-addressable RAM (20H-2FH) */
```

**Key rules (to earn full marks):**
- `sfr` and `sfr16` declarations must be **outside any function** (global scope).
- `sbit` has three valid forms: `sfr_name^bit_position`, `0x80^bit_position`, or `absolute_bit_address`.
- The 8051 has **128 byte-addressable bits** (20H–2FH) plus **bit-addressable SFRs** (P0, P1, P2, P3, PSW, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, A, B).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle time.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total machine cycles required for 200 ms.**

$$N_{MC,\text{total}} = \frac{t}{T_{MC}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} = 184\,331.8 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Choose outer loop count M = 200.**

**Step 4 — Compute required inner cycles per outer iteration.**

$$N_{MC,\text{per outer}} = \frac{184\,331.8}{200} = 921.66 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for N using the standard 8-cycles-per-inner-iteration formula.**

$$N = \frac{921.66}{8} = 115.21$$

Rounding down to $N = 115$ keeps us closest to the target:

$$N_{MC,\text{per outer,actual}} = 8 \times 115 = 920 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the total cycles actually achieved.**

$$N_{MC,\text{actual}} = 200 \times 920 = 184\,000 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the actual time delay.**

$$t_{\text{actual}} = 184\,000 \times 1.085 \text{ µs} = 199\,640 \text{ µs} = 199.64 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 8 — Write the C program.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void delay_200ms(void) {
    unsigned char i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < 200; i++) {   /* Outer loop: 200 iterations */
        for(j = 0; j < 115; j++) { /* Inner loop: 115 iterations */
            ;                       /* Empty body — consumes cycles */
        }
    }
}

void main(void) {
    while(1) {
        P1 = 0x00;     /* Turn ON all LEDs of P1 */
        delay_200ms();
        P1 = 0xFF;     /* Turn OFF all LEDs of P1 */
        delay_200ms();
    }
}
```

**Step 9 — State the result clearly.**

> **Achieved Delay: 199.64 ms** (Target: 200 ms, Error: −0.18%, well within 1% tolerance).

---

> **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the 8051 internal data memory map. Distinguish between bit-addressable and byte-addressable regions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An 8051 runs at **12 MHz**. Generate a delay of **500 µs** using a single C `for` loop. Write the C program and justify your choice of loop count. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**8051 Internal Data Memory Map (5 marks):**

The 8051 has **128 bytes of internal RAM (IRAM)** at addresses 00H to 7FH, and **128 bytes of Special Function Register (SFR) space** at addresses 80H to FFH. The structure is:

| Address Range | Region | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 00H – 1FH | **Register Banks** | 32 bytes | Four banks (RB0–RB3), each 8 bytes (R0–R7); selected by PSW bits RS0, RS1 |
| 20H – 2FH | **Bit-Addressable RAM** | 16 bytes | 128 individually addressable bits (bit 00H–7FH) |
| 30H – 7FH | **General Purpose RAM** | 80 bytes | Byte-addressable only; used for stack, variables, arrays |

**SFR Space (addresses ≥ 80H, only certain addresses are defined):**

| Address | Register | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 80H | P0 | Port 0 |
| 81H | SP | Stack Pointer |
| 82H, 83H | DPL, DPH | Data Pointer low/high |
| 87H | PCON | Power Control |
| 88H | TCON | Timer Control |
| 89H | TMOD | Timer Mode |
| 8AH, 8CH | TL0, TH0 | Timer 0 Low/High |
| 8BH, 8DH | TL1, TH1 | Timer 1 Low/High |
| 90H | P1 | Port 1 |
| 98H | SCON | Serial Control |
| 99H | SBUF | Serial Buffer |
| A0H | P2 | Port 2 |
| A8H | IE | Interrupt Enable |
| B0H | P3 | Port 3 |
| B8H | IP | Interrupt Priority |
| D0H | PSW | Program Status Word |
| E0H | A (ACC) | Accumulator |
| F0H | B | B register |

**Bit-Addressable vs Byte-Addressable (2 marks):**

- **Bit-addressable region (20H–2FH):** Each of the 128 bits (bit-address 00H–7FH) can be individually set, cleared, ANDed, ORed using bit instructions (`SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL`, `ORL`). Example: `RAM_byte bit 0x20 = 0;` sets the LSB of byte 20H.
- **Byte-addressable region (30H–7FH):** Only whole-byte access is possible; bit manipulation requires mask-and-set operations like `RAM_byte |= 0x80;`.
- **SFR space:** Only certain SFRs (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, PSW, A, B) are bit-addressable, giving another 128 bit-addressable locations (bit 80H–FFH).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle time at 12 MHz.**

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total machine cycles required.**

$$N_{MC} = \frac{500 \text{ µs}}{1 \text{ µs}} = 500 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the simplified single-loop formula $N_{MC} = 8X$ (Mazidi textbook standard).**

$$X = \frac{500}{8} = 62.5 \approx 63$$

**Step 4 — Verify the achieved delay.**

$$N_{MC,\text{actual}} = 8 \times 63 = 504 \text{ cycles}$$

$$t_{\text{actual}} = 504 \times 1 \text{ µs} = 504 \text{ µs}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the error.**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{504 - 500}{500} \times 100\% = +0.8\%$$

**Step 6 — C Program.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void delay_500us(void) {
    unsigned char i;
    for(i = 0; i < 63; i++) {
        ;   /* Empty body — each iteration takes ~8 machine cycles */
    }
}

void main(void) {
    while(1) {
        P1 = 0x00;
        delay_500us();
        P1 = 0xFF;
        delay_500us();
    }
}
```

**Step 7 — Justification of loop count.**

- $X = 63$ was chosen because it is the **nearest integer** to 62.5 (the exact floating-point solution), giving the **smallest error** without needing nested loops.
- $X = 63$ fits comfortably in `unsigned char` (max 255), so no variable-type change is required.
- Using $X = 62$ would give 496 µs (error −0.8%) and $X = 63$ gives 504 µs (error +0.8%); both are equally acceptable, but $X = 63$ is the *standard rounding* answer.
- The achieved delay of 504 µs is within **1%** of the target, which is excellent for embedded applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Formula mismatch (most common mistake, −2 marks):** Students frequently write $N_{MC} = 3X + 1$ (rigorous) when their module notes use $N_{MC} = 8X$ (textbook). Both are valid *if* the formula is stated at the start. **Always declare your formula before plugging in numbers.**
> 2. **Unit confusion (loses 1 mark):** Writing $T_{MC} = 1.085$ without the unit (µs or s) loses method marks. Always state the unit.
> 3. **Integer rounding silently dropped (−1 mark):** After computing a non-integer $X$, you must show the rounding step explicitly and re-compute the *actual* delay produced.
> 4. **No "actual achieved delay" reported (−1 mark):** The KTU 14-mark rubric awards marks for *both* the requested value and the *value actually produced* by the chosen integer counts. Always report both.
> 5. **Using `int` for inner loop with $X > 255$:** The 8051 C compiler allows it, but a more efficient solution uses `unsigned char` for the inner loop and `unsigned char` (or `int`) for the outer.
> 6. **Confusing `^` in `sbit` with bitwise XOR:** In `sbit LED = P1^0;`, the `^` is **not** the XOR operator — it is the Keil-specific bit-position operator. This is a C-language extension; it is *only* valid inside `sbit` declarations.
> 7. **Forgetting `#include <reg51.h>`:** Without this header (or equivalent SFR definition file), the symbols `P0`, `P1`, `P3`, `TCON`, etc. are undefined, and the program will not compile.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 8051 C Data Types and Time Delay**

- [x] The 8051 has an **8-bit ALU**; its most natural C data type is `unsigned char` (0 to 255, 1 byte).
- [x] `int` on the 8051 is **16 bits** (not 32 bits as on modern PCs); range is 0 to 65 535 for `unsigned int`.
- [x] `float` is **32-bit IEEE 754** and uses a software library — it is slow; avoid in time-critical code.
- [x] The 8051-specific data types are: **`sfr`** (8-bit SFR name), **`sfr16`** (16-bit SFR pair), **`sbit`** (single-bit alias in SFR or bit-RAM), and **`bit`** (single-bit variable).
- [x] `sfr` and `sfr16` must be declared at **global scope**, outside any function.
- [x] `sbit` uses the **`^` operator** which is *not* bitwise XOR — it is a Keil extension for bit position.
- [x] One **machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** — this is fixed in the 8051 hardware.
- [x] **Machine cycle time:** $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$.
- [x] At **11.0592 MHz** crystal, $T_{MC} = 1.085$ µs (the most common KTU value).
- [x] At **12 MHz** crystal, $T_{MC} = 1.000$ µs (the cleanest KTU value).
- [x] For a single `for(i=0; i<X; i++);` loop, the **textbook cycle count** is $N_{MC} = 8X$; the **rigorous count** is $N_{MC} = 1 + 3X$.
- [x] For a **nested loop**, total cycles $= M \times (8N + \text{outer overhead}) \approx M \times 8N$.
- [x] Time delay $= N_{MC} \times T_{MC}$. Always state the **actual achieved delay**, not just the requested value.
- [x] Both `i` and `j` in nested loops are typically `unsigned char` (max 255). For larger counts, use `unsigned int`.
- [x] **Invert the design problem:** Given a target delay, compute $N_{MC}$, then choose $M$ and $N$ such that the result is *close to* the target (within ~1% is excellent).
- [x] **Pure software delay blocks all interrupts** — for serious real-time work, use **hardware timers** (covered in Module 3).
- [x] Standard KTU 14-mark rubric awards marks for: (1) data type table/diagram, (2) machine cycle calculation, (3) loop formula stated, (4) loop counts chosen, (5) C program written, (6) actual delay verified, (7) error/justification discussed.

> **Mnemonic for 8051 Specific Data Types — "S³B":**
> - **S**fr (8-bit)
> - **S**fr16 (16-bit)
> - **S**bit (single bit in SFR)
> - **B**it (single bit variable in RAM)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Programming in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** I/O Programming in 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# I/O Programming in 8051 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**I/O Programming in 8051** refers to the systematic process of configuring, controlling, and exchanging digital data between the 8051 microcontroller's four bidirectional I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) and external peripheral devices using Special Function Registers (SFRs), bit-manipulation instructions, and memory-mapped I/O addressing techniques.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Definition (PCEOT501 Module 2):**
> The 8051 family provides **four 8-bit parallel I/O ports** — P0, P1, P2, and P3 — mapped to SFR addresses **80H, 90H, A0H, and B0H** respectively. Each port can be used as either input or output, but NOT bidirectionally at the same instant.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the 8051 microcontroller as a **bank manager** sitting inside a glass cabin. The bank has **four counters (P0, P1, P2, P3)**, each with **8 service windows (the 8 pins of each port)**. A customer (external device) interacts with the bank through these windows.

- **Output operation** = The bank manager *pushes out* a document/coin (data) to the customer through a specific window.
- **Input operation** = The customer *hands in* a form (data) to the bank manager through a window.
- **Bidirectional (Read-Modify-Write) issue** = The manager cannot simultaneously push out and receive at the same window — he must first commit to one direction (set the latch), then perform the action.

Just like a bank has different counters for different services (loans, deposits, foreign exchange), the 8051's P3 has **alternate special functions** (UART, Timers, Interrupts), while P0 and P2 double as the **lower and upper address bus** during external memory access.

### Fundamental Characteristics of 8051 I/O Ports

| Port | SFR Address (Hex) | Bit-Addressable | Internal Pull-up | Alternate Function |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | 80H | Yes | **No** (open drain) | AD0–AD7 (Lower Address/Data Bus) |
| **P1** | 90H | Yes | **Yes** | None (general purpose) |
| **P2** | A0H | Yes | **Yes** | A8–A15 (Upper Address Bus) |
| **P3** | B0H | Yes | **Yes** | RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A common 2-mark question is "Which port lacks an internal pull-up resistor?" The answer is **Port 0** — this is a HIGH-YIELD fact because Port 0 *requires* external pull-up resistors (typically **10 kΩ**) when used as a general-purpose output port.

### The Three SFRs Behind Every Port

1. **Latch (SFR itself)** — Holds the data you wrote. It is a D-type flip-flop storage.
2. **Pin (Physical I/O pad)** — Where the actual electrical signal appears.
3. **Read Pin vs Read Latch** — Critical distinction. The 8051 has separate internal paths; "read-modify-write" instructions (like `ANL P1, A`) read the **latch** (last value written), while `MOV A, P1` reads the **physical pin** state.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam Recall:** *"P0 is Poor (no pull-up), P3 is Pimpped-out (alternate functions galore), P1 and P2 are Plain Professionals."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Internal Architecture of an 8051 Port Pin

Every 8051 port pin consists of **three major sub-circuits**:

### 1. The Latch (1-bit D Flip-Flop)
- Stores the value written by the CPU via the SFR bus.
- Driven by instructions like `SETB P1.0` or `MOV P1, #55H`.
- Holds the state during the entire clock cycle until a new write occurs.

### 2. The Output Driver
- A **tri-state buffer** controls whether the latch value reaches the pin.
- For P1, P2, P3: An internal FET (~$30 \, \Omega$ on-resistance) acts as an *active pull-up* — sourcing current when outputting logic '1'.
- For P0: Uses an **open-drain** configuration (two back-to-back FETs, no pull-up) — meaning it can *sink* current but cannot *source* current from the chip; external pull-up is mandatory.

### 3. The Input Buffer
- A separate tri-state buffer reads the physical pin voltage.
- It is enabled only during a **read-pin** instruction (`MOV A, PX`) or a conditional jump on a bit.
- Reading a pin set as output gives the *pin's actual electrical level* (which may differ from the latch if an external load is connected).

## Detailed Port-by-Port Analysis

### Port 0 (P0) — The "Dual-Purpose" Port
- **Dual role:** When accessing external memory (EA̅ = 0 or beyond internal 4 KB), P0 emits the **lower byte of address (A0–A7) multiplexed with data (D0–D7)**. An external latch (74HC573/74LS373) is required to de-multiplex.
- **No internal pull-up:** Every pin needs an external pull-up (typically **4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ**).
- **Maximum sink current per pin:** ~**10 mA** (entire port total not to exceed 26 mA for older 8051 variants).
- **SFR Address:** 80H | **Bit-addressable:** Yes

### Port 1 (P1) — The "Pure" General-Purpose Port
- **No alternate function** in the standard 8051. It is the safest, simplest port for general I/O.
- **Internal pull-ups** present (active pull-up FETs).
- **Quasi-bidirectional:** Writing '1' to P1 makes it input-ready; writing '0' actively drives the pin low.
- **SFR Address:** 90H | **Bit-addressable:** Yes

### Port 2 (P2) — The "Upper Address" Port
- **Alternate function:** Outputs the **upper address byte (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- Like P1, has internal pull-ups. Used as general I/O only when no external memory is used.
- **SFR Address:** A0H | **Bit-addressable:** Yes

### Port 3 (P3) — The "Multifunction Specialist"
Each pin has an alternate special function:

| Pin | Alternate Function | SFR Bit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | **RXD** | B0H | Serial Input (UART) |
| P3.1 | **TXD** | B1H | Serial Output (UART) |
| P3.2 | **INT0̅** | B2H | External Interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1̅** | B3H | External Interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | B4H | Timer 0 Input |
| P3.5 | **T1** | B5H | Timer 1 Input |
| P3.6 | **WR̅** | B6H | External Memory Write Strobe |
| P3.7 | **RD̅** | B7H | External Memory Read Strobe |

> [!NOTE]
> **For a P3 pin to function as general I/O, the corresponding SFR bit must contain a logic '1' (i.e., the alternate function must be disabled).** This is because the alternate function output overrides the latch.

## I/O Programming Instructions — The Instruction Categories

The 8051 has a rich set of instructions for I/O manipulation:

### Category 1: Byte-Level I/O Instructions
- `MOV P1, A` — Copy accumulator to P1.
- `MOV A, P1` — Copy P1 to accumulator.
- `ANL P1, A` — AND accumulator with P1 (latch read), write back to P1.
- `ORL P1, A` — OR operation on P1.
- `XRL P1, A` — XOR operation on P1.
- `CPL P1` — Complement all 8 bits of P1.
- `INC P1` — Increment P1.
- `DEC P1` — Decrement P1.

> [!WARNING]
> **`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL` on ports are READ-MODIFY-WRITE instructions** — they read the **LATCH** (not the pin) to avoid the classic "read-modify-write hazard" of early microcontrollers. This is a classic 7-mark exam question.

### Category 2: Bit-Level I/O Instructions
- `SETB P1.0` — Set bit 0 of P1 to 1.
- `CLR P1.7` — Clear bit 7 of P1 to 0.
- `CPL P3.3` — Toggle bit 3 of P3.
- `JB P1.2, LABEL` — Jump if bit P1.2 is set.
- `JNB P3.2, LABEL` — Jump if bit P3.2 is not set.
- `JBC P1.0, LABEL` — Jump if P1.0 is set, then clear it.

### Category 3: Bit Manipulation in Carry
- `MOV C, P1.0` — Copy P1.0 into the Carry flag.
- `MOV P1.0, C` — Copy Carry into P1.0.
- `ANL C, P1.1` — AND Carry with P1.1.
- `ORL C, P3.3` — OR Carry with P3.3.

## KTU High-Yield Formula & Logic Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| External pull-up for P0 | $R_{pullup} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ | $\Omega$; typical $4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ to $10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| Maximum source current (P1, P2, P3) | $I_{OH} \le 60 \, \mu A$ per pin | microamp range (weak drive) |
| Maximum sink current (any port) | $I_{OL} \le 1.6 \text{ mA}$ to $10 \text{ mA}$ per pin | depends on variant |
| Port 0 address latch timing | $t_{LHLL} \ge 50 \text{ ns}$ | nanoseconds (ALE pulse width) |
| Read-Modify-Write latency | $1$ machine cycle = $12$ oscillator periods | $T_{cy} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$ |
| Bit-addressable range | SFRs 80H–FFH ending in 0H or 8H | 16 SFRs are bit-addressable |
| Number of bit-addressable pins | $4 \text{ ports} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ bits}$ | plus 16-bit SFR area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Understanding port internal architecture is critical for **PCB design** (decoupling, current budgeting), **interfacing** (LEDs need current-limiting resistors $\approx 330 \, \Omega$ for 5V supply), and **EMI compliance** (slow slew rates from weak pull-ups require debouncing in switch input designs).

## Why "Quasi-Bidirectional"?

The 8051 ports are technically called **quasi-bidirectional** because:
- Writing '0' → strong low (active drive, can sink up to 10 mA).
- Writing '1' → weak high (internal pull-up ~30 kΩ to ~50 kΩ, sources only ~50 µA).
- This asymmetry makes them **excellent for input reading** (no need to reconfigure direction) but **limited for driving high-current loads** directly — hence the need for transistor drivers, ULN2003, or buffer ICs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Calculating External Pull-Up Resistor for Port 0

When Port 0 is used as a general-purpose output, it is an open-drain. To output a logic HIGH, we MUST connect an external pull-up resistor. Let's derive the correct value.

### Given:
- Supply voltage: $V_{CC} = 5 \text{ V}$
- Minimum logic HIGH voltage for the next stage: $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0 \text{ V}$ (TTL threshold)
- Maximum current the load can sink without dropping below $V_{IH(min)}$: $I_{load} = 200 \, \mu A$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

We require the voltage at the P0.x pin to remain $\ge V_{IH(min)}$ even when the load sinks current. Using Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$V_{CC} = V_{R_{pullup}} + V_{pin}$$

$$V_{R_{pullup}} = I_{load} \times R_{pullup}$$

For safe HIGH detection:
$$V_{pin} \ge V_{IH(min)}$$

Therefore the maximum allowable drop across the pull-up is:

$$V_{R_{pullup}(max)} = V_{CC} - V_{IH(min)}$$

Substituting $V_{CC} = 5 \text{ V}$ and $V_{IH(min)} = 2.0 \text{ V}$:

$$V_{R_{pullup}(max)} = 5 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

Then the minimum pull-up resistance is:

$$R_{pullup(min)} = \frac{V_{R_{pullup}(max)}}{I_{load}} = \frac{3.0 \text{ V}}{200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}} = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Therefore we must choose $R_{pullup} \ge 15 \text{ k}\Omega$. A standard value of **$10 \text{ k}\Omega$** is commonly used (giving more margin), while a value of **$4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$** is used for faster switching speeds (at the cost of higher current consumption when driving LOW).

## Derivation 2: LED Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation

A common I/O programming task is to interface an LED to a port pin.

### Given:
- $V_{CC} = 5 \text{ V}$
- LED forward voltage: $V_F = 2.0 \text{ V}$ (red LED)
- Desired LED current: $I_F = 10 \text{ mA}$ (bright but safe)

### Derivation:

Applying KVL around the loop (Port Pin → Resistor → LED → GND):

$$V_{CC} = V_{OL} + V_{R} + V_F$$

Where $V_{OL} \approx 0.4 \text{ V}$ is the port pin low-level output voltage.

Solving for $V_R$:

$$V_R = V_{CC} - V_{OL} - V_F = 5 \text{ V} - 0.4 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 2.6 \text{ V}$$

By Ohm's law:

$$R_{LED} = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{2.6 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ mA}} = 260 \, \Omega$$

Standard value chosen: **$R_{LED} = 330 \, \Omega$** (closest standard E12 value, gives ~8 mA — still bright).

## Derivation 3: Machine Cycle Timing for I/O Operations

The 8051 uses a **12-clock oscillator cycle** per machine cycle. For a typical **$f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$** crystal (standard for serial communication), derive the time per instruction.

### Step-by-step:

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \approx 90.42 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{machine} = 12 \times T_{osc} = 12 \times 90.42 \text{ ns} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

For a single-cycle instruction like `SETB P1.0`:

$$t_{SETB} = 1 \times T_{machine} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

For a two-cycle instruction like `MOV A, P1`:

$$t_{MOV} = 2 \times T_{machine} = 2.17 \, \mu s$$

This timing is critical for:
- **Generating delays** using software loops.
- **Bit-banging** serial protocols.
- **Debouncing** mechanical switches (typical bounce time $\approx 5 \text{ ms}$, so a delay of ~10 ms is required).

## Analytical Matrix: Read Latch vs Read Pin Behavior

| Instruction Type | Source of Data | Writes Back? | Hazard Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, P1` | **Pin** (physical level) | No | None — pure read |
| `ANL P1, A` | **Latch** (last written) | Yes (to latch & pin) | Safe — RMW protected |
| `ORL P1, #0FH` | **Latch** | Yes | Safe |
| `CPL P1.0` | **Latch** | Yes (toggled) | Safe |
| `INC P1` | **Latch** | Yes | Safe |
| `SETB P1.0` | Direct bit set | Yes | Safe (bit-addressed) |
| `JNB P1.0, ...` | **Pin** | No | None — pure read |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the distinction matters:** Consider driving a motor through P1.0. If a transient load spike pulls the pin voltage down (even though the latch says '1'), `MOV A, P1` would read the low pin and `ANL P1, A` (if it read the pin) might mistakenly clear the bit. By reading the *latch*, the 8051 prevents this accident.

## Worked Example: Generating a Square Wave on P1.0

### Problem (KTU Style): Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a continuous **$1 \text{ kHz}$ square wave** on P1.0 using a **$11.0592 \text{ MHz}$** crystal.

### Solution Outline:

1. Half-period of $1 \text{ kHz}$ signal: $T/2 = 1/(2 \times 1000) = 500 \, \mu s$.
2. Subtract the overhead of `SETB` + `CLR` + jump (~$7 \, \mu s$ for the 3 instructions × 1 machine cycle each, plus loop overhead).
3. Required delay loop count: $\approx 500 \, \mu s - 7 \, \mu s = 493 \, \mu s$.
4. With $T_{machine} = 1.085 \, \mu s$, we need $\approx 454$ machine cycles of delay.
5. Use a nested loop: outer loop count = 2, inner loop count = $\approx 227$ (each iteration = 1 machine cycle for `DJNZ`).

### Assembly Code:

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   SETB P1.0      ; Set P1.0 HIGH (1 machine cycle)
BACK:   CPL P1.0       ; Toggle P1.0 (1 machine cycle)
        ACALL DELAY    ; Call 500 us delay (2 cycles for ACALL)
        SJMP BACK      ; Short jump back (2 cycles)
        ; Total period = 4 cycles instruction + delay ≈ 500 us
DELAY:  MOV R0, #0FFH  ; 1 cycle
LOOP1:  MOV R1, #0FBH  ; 1 cycle
LOOP2:  DJNZ R1, LOOP2 ; 2 cycles
        DJNZ R0, LOOP1 ; 2 cycles
        RET            ; 2 cycles
        END
```

> [!NOTE]
> This example is a KTU favorite. Marks are awarded for: (1) correct half-period identification, (2) delay loop calculation, (3) using `CPL` for symmetric toggling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Internal Architecture of a Single 8051 Port Pin

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[CPU Write Bus] --> nodeB[D-Type Latch 1-bit]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Output Tri-state Buffer]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Physical Pin Px.y]
    nodeE[Read Pin Bus] --> nodeF[Input Tri-state Buffer]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeG[Read Latch Bus for RMW] --> nodeB
    nodeB -.-> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH[External Pull-up Resistor R pullup]
    nodeH --> nodeI[VCC 5V]
    classDef cpu fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef latch fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef pin fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    class A,E,G nodeA,nodeE,nodeG
    class B nodeB
    class C,F nodeC,nodeF
    class D,H nodeD,nodeH
    class I nodeI
```

**Caption:** Schematic of a single 8051 port pin showing the latch, output driver, input buffer, read-latch path (for read-modify-write instructions), and external pull-up resistor.

## Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8051 I/O System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_CORE[CPU Core and SFR Bus]
        nodeCore[8051 CPU]
        nodeSFR[SFR Address Decoder]
    end

    subgraph PORT_BLOCK[Four Parallel I/O Ports]
        nodeP0[Port 0 - SFR 80H - AD0 to AD7 - Open Drain]
        nodeP1[Port 1 - SFR 90H - General Purpose Only]
        nodeP2[Port 2 - SFR A0H - A8 to A15 - Internal Pull-up]
        nodeP3[Port 3 - SFR B0H - RXD TXD INT0 INT1 T0 T1 WR RD]
    end

    subgraph EXT_DEV[External Peripherals]
        nodeLED[LED Array 8 bits]
        nodeLCD[LCD 16x2 Display]
        nodeKEY[Keypad Matrix 4x4]
        nodeUART[Serial Device via MAX232]
    end

    nodeCore --> nodeSFR
    nodeSFR --> nodeP0
    nodeSFR --> nodeP1
    nodeSFR --> nodeP2
    nodeSFR --> nodeP3
    nodeP0 --> nodeLED
    nodeP1 --> nodeLCD
    nodeP2 --> nodeKEY
    nodeP3 --> nodeUART
    classDef coreStyle fill:#007bff,stroke:#003366,color:#fff
    classDef portStyle fill:#ffc107,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef extStyle fill:#28a745,stroke:#155724,color:#fff
    class nodeCore,nodeSFR coreStyle
    class nodeP0,nodeP1,nodeP2,nodeP3 portStyle
    class nodeLED,nodeLCD,nodeKEY,nodeUART extStyle
```

**Caption:** Block diagram showing how the CPU's SFR bus addresses the four ports, which in turn connect to typical external peripherals.

## Diagram 3: I/O Programming Instruction Categories

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((8051 I O Programming Instructions))
    Byte Level
      MOV PX A
      MOV A PX
      ANL PX data
      ORL PX data
      XRL PX data
      CPL PX
      INC PX
      DEC PX
    Bit Level
      SETB PX y
      CLR PX y
      CPL PX y
      JB PX y label
      JNB PX y label
      JBC PX y label
    Bit to Carry
      MOV C PX y
      MOV PX y C
      ANL C PX y
      ORL C PX y
      CPL C
    Logical
      ANL A PX
      ORL A PX
```

**Caption:** Mind-map classification of the 8051's I/O programming instructions, separating byte-level, bit-level, and carry-related operations.

## Diagram 4: Read Pin vs Read Latch Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[CPU Executes I/O Instruction] --> nodeQ1{Is it a READ operation?}
    nodeQ1 -- No WRITE --> nodeWrite[Write to Latch SFR]
    nodeWrite --> nodeOutput[Output Driver Updates Physical Pin]
    nodeOutput --> nodeEnd1[Done]
    nodeQ1 -- Yes READ --> nodeQ2{Is it a Read-Modify-Write instruction?}
    nodeQ2 -- Yes RMW --> nodeReadLatch[Read from LATCH]
    nodeQ2 -- No plain read --> nodeReadPin[Read from PHYSICAL PIN]
    nodeReadLatch --> nodeMod[Apply AND OR XOR etc]
    nodeReadPin --> nodeReturn[Return value in Accumulator or to PC]
    nodeMod --> nodeWriteBack[Write modified value back to Latch]
    nodeWriteBack --> nodeOutput2[Output Driver Updates Pin]
    nodeReturn --> nodeEnd2[Done]
    nodeOutput2 --> nodeEnd2
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
    class Q1,Q2 decision
    class Write,Output,ReadLatch,ReadPin,Mod,WriteBack,Output2,Return action
```

**Caption:** Decision flowchart illustrating the critical difference between read-pin (used by `MOV A, PX`) and read-latch (used by `ANL PX, A`) operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the I/O port structure of 8051 with a neat diagram. Why is Port 0 called a "true bidirectional" port while others are "quasi-bidirectional"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 has **four 8-bit I/O ports** — P0, P1, P2, P3 — each consisting of a latch (SFR), output driver, and input buffer.

- **Port 0** uses an **open-drain** output configuration with no internal pull-up. To output a '1', an external pull-up resistor (typically 4.7 kΩ–10 kΩ) must be connected. The data direction can be fully controlled by software — hence "true bidirectional."
- **Ports 1, 2, 3** have **internal pull-up FETs**. Writing a '1' to a pin weakly drives it high (high-impedance state), making it input-ready without reconfiguration. Writing a '0' strongly drives it low. Since the output cannot be tri-stated internally without external help, they are termed **"quasi-bidirectional."**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the differences in *alternate functions* and miss the technical reason (open-drain vs internal pull-up). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List the alternate functions of Port 3 pins.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Pin | Alternate Function |
|---|---|
| P3.0 | **RXD** (Serial Input) |
| P3.1 | **TXD** (Serial Output) |
| P3.2 | **INT0̅** (External Interrupt 0) |
| P3.3 | **INT1̅** (External Interrupt 1) |
| P3.4 | **T0** (Timer 0 external input) |
| P3.5 | **T1** (Timer 1 external input) |
| P3.6 | **WR̅** (External memory write strobe) |
| P3.7 | **RD̅** (External memory read strobe) |

For any P3 pin to function as general I/O, the corresponding latch bit must hold a logic '1' so that the alternate function hardware does not override it.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Internal Choice Option (a) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Draw the internal structure of an 8051 port pin and explain the function of each block. List any two instructions that perform "read-modify-write" operation on a port. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to read the status of switches connected to Port 1 and display the same pattern on LEDs connected to Port 2, with a 100 ms delay between successive reads. Assume crystal frequency = 11.0592 MHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

The internal structure of a single 8051 port pin consists of:

1. **D-Type Latch (1 bit):** Stores the output data written by the CPU. Addressed via the SFR (e.g., P1 maps to 90H). On a `SETB P1.0` or `MOV P1, #55H`, the latch captures and holds the value.
2. **Output Tri-State Buffer:** When activated, it passes the latch value to the physical pin. The buffer has a control signal that enables it only during write operations.
3. **Internal Pull-Up FET** (for P1, P2, P3 only): A weak PMOS/NMOS transistor (~30 kΩ effective) that weakly sources current when '1' is latched. For P0, this is absent (open drain).
4. **Input Tri-State Buffer:** A separate buffer (enabled during read operations) that captures the physical pin's voltage level and routes it to the internal data bus.
5. **Read-Latch Multiplexer:** A switch controlled by the CPU that selects either the **latch output** (for read-modify-write) or the **pin input** (for direct read). This is the key to the read-modify-write safety mechanism.

**Two Read-Modify-Write Instructions:**

```assembly
ANL P1, A        ; AND accumulator with P1 (latch), write back
XRL P3, #0FFH    ; XOR P3 with 0FFH, write back
```

Other examples: `ORL PX, data`, `CPL PX`, `INC PX`, `DEC PX`, `DJNZ PX, label`, `SETB PX.y`, `CLR PX.y`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (1 mark lost):** Students often say "read-modify-write reads the pin." This is wrong — it reads the **latch** to avoid hazards when external loads modify the pin voltage.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

### Step 1: Delay Calculation
For 100 ms delay, with $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$:

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

Required machine cycles: $N = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 92,165$ cycles.

### Step 2: Use 16-bit delay with two registers
We use a triple-nested delay to achieve 100 ms:

### Assembly Program:

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV P2, #0FFH      ; Initialize P2 (LEDs OFF assuming active LOW)
LOOP:   MOV A, P1           ; Read switch status from P1
        MOV P2, A           ; Display same pattern on LEDs (P2)
        ACALL DELAY_100MS   ; Wait 100 ms
        SJMP LOOP           ; Repeat forever

;---------- 100 ms Delay Subroutine ----------
DELAY_100MS:
        MOV R3, #02H        ; Outer loop count
OUTER:  MOV R2, #0FFH
MID:    MOV R1, #0FFH
INNER:  DJNZ R1, INNER      ; 2 cycles per iteration
        DJNZ R2, MID        ; 2 cycles per iteration
        DJNZ R3, OUTER      ; 2 cycles per iteration
        RET                 ; 2 cycles
        END
```

### Step 3: Verify the delay
Approximate delay = $2 \times 255 \times 255 \times 2 \times 1.085 \, \mu s \approx 141 \text{ ms}$. This is more than 100 ms, so it satisfies the requirement (software delays are approximate).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (2 marks lost if missed):** Students often forget to initialize the ports. P2 should be configured as output by writing `0FFH` first (since the upper nibble would otherwise be in undefined input state).

---

### Question B — Internal Choice Option (b) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**(a)** Differentiate between "read pin" and "read latch" operations in 8051 I/O. Why is this distinction critical in real applications? Give example instructions for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Interface 8 LEDs to Port 0 of an 8051 and write an ALP to generate the following patterns sequentially, each lasting 500 ms:
- Pattern 1: LEDs ON from P0.0 to P0.7 (walking bit left to right)
- Pattern 2: LEDs ON from P0.7 to P0.0 (walking bit right to left)
Assume crystal = 12 MHz. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

### Read Pin Operation:
- The CPU reads the **physical voltage level** at the port pin through the input tri-state buffer.
- It reflects the *actual* electrical state, including effects of external loads.
- **Example Instructions:** `MOV A, P1`, `MOV A, P3`, `JB P1.0, LABEL`, `JNB P3.2, LABEL`.

### Read Latch Operation:
- The CPU reads the **last value written** to the port's SFR latch, bypassing the physical pin.
- Used by all read-modify-write instructions to prevent corruption from transient pin states.
- **Example Instructions:** `ANL P1, A`, `ORL P2, #0FH`, `XRL P3, A`, `CPL P1`, `INC P3`, `SETB P1.0`, `CLR P3.3`.

### Why the Distinction is Critical:

**Scenario:** P1.0 drives a relay coil. The coil's inductance can cause voltage transients (back-EMF) that momentarily drag the pin LOW even though the latch says '1'. 

- If you used `MOV A, P1` to read the pin state for diagnostic purposes, you correctly observe the transient.
- But if `ANL P1, A` mistakenly read the *pin* (instead of latch), the low transient would clear the bit, turning off the relay — a dangerous situation in control systems.

The 8051's design (separate read-pin and read-latch paths) prevents this hazard and makes I/O programming more robust.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (1 mark lost):** Students often say "read-modify-write is for safety" but don't give a concrete example. Examiners want the relay/motor/transistor load scenario.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

### Hardware Connection:
- P0.0–P0.7 → 330 Ω current-limiting resistor → LED anode → LED cathode → GND.
- **Important:** Since P0 is open-drain, LEDs will only light when P0 outputs '0' (active-low configuration) OR we add external pull-ups to P0 (10 kΩ each to VCC) and wire LEDs in active-high configuration.

### Delay Calculation for 12 MHz Crystal:
$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s$$

For 500 ms delay, we need 500,000 machine cycles. Using a triple-nested loop:

### Assembly Program:

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV DPTR, #PATTERN_TABLE  ; Point DPTR to pattern lookup table
LOOP:   MOV R4, #08H              ; 8 patterns total
        MOV R0, #00H              ; Pattern index
NEXT:   MOV A, R0                 ; Get index
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR           ; Read pattern from code memory
        MOV P0, A                 ; Output to LEDs
        ACALL DELAY_500MS         ; Wait 500 ms
        INC R0                    ; Next pattern
        DJNZ R4, NEXT             ; Loop 8 times
        SJMP LOOP                 ; Repeat sequence

;---------- Pattern Data (Code Memory) ----------
PATTERN_TABLE:
        DB 00000001B   ; P0.0 ON
        DB 00000010B   ; P0.1 ON
        DB 00000100B   ; P0.2 ON
        DB 00001000B   ; P0.3 ON
        DB 00010000B   ; P0.4 ON
        DB 00100000B   ; P0.5 ON
        DB 01000000B   ; P0.6 ON
        DB 10000000B   ; P0.7 ON

;---------- 500 ms Delay Subroutine ----------
DELAY_500MS:
        MOV R5, #05H         ; Outer counter
LOOP1:  MOV R6, #0FFH
LOOP2:  MOV R7, #0FFH
LOOP3:  DJNZ R7, LOOP3
        DJNZ R6, LOOP2
        DJNZ R5, LOOP1
        RET
        END
```

### Verification:
- $5 \times 255 \times 255 \times 2 \times 1 \, \mu s = 650.25 \text{ ms}$ (close to 500 ms; tunable by adjusting R5).
- Total program: 8 walking-bit patterns, each held for ~500 ms, then looped.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (1 mark lost):** Students frequently forget that **P0 needs external pull-ups** for this kind of active-high LED driving. Examiners will deduct marks for omitting this hardware detail.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Triggers for I/O Programming Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SFR address of ports** — P0 = 80H, P1 = 90H, P2 = A0H, P3 = B0H. Writing these wrong = 1 mark lost per error.
> 2. **Forgetting external pull-up on P0** — Most common hardware-side mistake.
> 3. **Stating read-modify-write reads the pin** — It reads the LATCH. Always.
> 4. **Not disabling alternate functions on P3** — The SFR latch must hold '1' to use P3 as GPIO.
> 5. **Wrong delay calculation** — Confusing machine cycles with clock cycles (12:1 ratio).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four 8-bit ports:** P0, P1, P2, P3. Each is bidirectional (one direction at a time), bit-addressable, and SFR-mapped.
- **SFR addresses (memorize):** P0 = **80H**, P1 = **90H**, P2 = **A0H**, P3 = **B0H**.
- **Port 0 special:** **No internal pull-up** → open-drain → requires external 4.7 kΩ–10 kΩ pull-up. Doubles as lower address/data bus (AD0–AD7).
- **Port 1:** "Pure" general-purpose port with internal pull-ups. No alternate function.
- **Port 2:** Internal pull-up. Outputs upper address byte (A8–A15) during external memory access.
- **Port 3:** Multiplexed with UART (RXD/TXD), Interrupts (INT0/INT1), Timers (T0/T1), and control strobes (WR/RD). SFR bit must be '1' to use as GPIO.
- **Quasi-bidirectional:** Writing '1' → weak high (input-ready); writing '0' → strong low.
- **Read Pin vs Read Latch:** Read-pin (`MOV A, PX`) gives physical voltage; read-latch (RMW instructions like `ANL PX, A`) gives last-written value.
- **RMW Instructions:** `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `INC`, `DEC`, `SETB`, `CLR`, `DJNZ` on ports all use read-latch.
- **Bit Instructions:** `SETB Px.y`, `CLR Px.y`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC` are the primary bit-level I/O tools.
- **Timing:** $T_{machine} = 12/f_{osc}$. For $11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ crystal, $T_{machine} = 1.085 \, \mu s$. This is the basis for all software delays.
- **Current Limits:** ~10 mA sink per pin (any port), ~60 µA source per pin (P1/P2/P3). Use driver ICs for high-current loads.
- **Decoupling:** Always place a 0.1 µF capacitor near the VCC pin of the 8051 for stable I/O operation.
- **Initialization:** Always configure ports explicitly with a `MOV PX, #0FFH` (for input) or `MOV PX, #00H` (for output) at the start of your program.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 Timer/Counter Programming in Embedded C: Programming 8051 Timers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 8051 Timer/Counter Programming in Embedded C: Programming 8051 Timers

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

The **8051 Timer/Counter** is an on-chip peripheral hardware module that operates as either a programmable **time-interval generator** (Timer mode) or an **event counter** (Counter mode). The standard 8051 contains **two 16-bit timers**, designated **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**, each composed of two cascaded 8-bit registers: a high-byte register (TH0/TH1) and a low-byte register (TL0/TL1). The operating mode is selected via the **Timer Mode (TMOD)** register, while the start/stop control and overflow flag monitoring are handled by the **Timer Control (TCON)** register.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCEOT501 Module 2**
> Programming 8051 timers in **Embedded C** mandates mastery of: (i) TMOD configuration, (ii) delay calculation using crystal frequency, (iii) overflow-flag polling vs. interrupt-driven approaches, and (iv) auto-reload mode (Mode 2) for serial baud-rate generation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen Stopwatch" 🍳

Imagine a **digital kitchen stopwatch** that has two distinct personalities:

| Personality | Behaviour | 8051 Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Timer Mode** ⏱️ | Counts the **internal clock pulses** generated by the crystal oscillator. It measures *elapsed time*. | Used for delay generation, debouncing, periodic sampling. |
| **Counter Mode** 🔢 | Counts **external events** (e.g., pressing a button, pulses on a pin). | Used for revolution counting, object counting on a conveyor belt. |

The stopwatch starts at a value you set, and when it "overflows" past its maximum (FFFFh → 0000h), it rings a bell — this bell is the **Timer Overflow Flag (TF0/TF1)** in 8051 terminology. You, the programmer, hear this bell either by **continuously listening (polling)** or by **being automatically interrupted (interrupt-driven)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Crystal Frequency Range:** $\mathbf{4\ \text{MHz}\ \text{to}\ 24\ \text{MHz}}$ (standard KTU boards use **11.0592 MHz** for accurate serial communication)
> - **Machine Cycle:** $\mathbf{12\ \text{clock periods}}$ (i.e., 12 oscillator cycles = 1 machine cycle)
> - **Maximum Count Value (16-bit Mode):** $\mathbf{2^{16} = 65{,}536}$ counts (0 to 65535)
> - **Register Width:** 16 bits, split into **THx (8 bits) + TLx (8 bits)**
> - **Total Timers:** **2** (Timer 0 and Timer 1)
> - **Operating Modes:** **4** (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hardware Architecture of the 8051 Timer Subsystem

The timer subsystem consists of three major components interacting with the CPU:

1. **Oscillator & Machine-Cycle Generator** — Divides the crystal frequency by 12 to produce the machine cycle.
2. **TMOD Register (89h)** — Mode and function selector.
3. **TCON Register (88h)** — Run control and overflow flags.
4. **THx / TLx Register Pairs** — The 16-bit counter storage.

## 2.2 The TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register, Address 89h)

TMOD is a **bit-addressable, byte-wide** register divided into two identical 4-bit nibbles:

- **Lower nibble (T0)** → controls Timer 0
- **Upper nibble (T1)** → controls Timer 1

| Bit | Symbol | Function | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **GATE1** | Software/Hardware control | When set, Timer 1 is controlled by external pin **INT1** AND software TR1. |
| 6 | **C/T̄1** | Counter/Timer select | **1 = Counter mode**, **0 = Timer mode** |
| 5 | **M11** | Mode bit 1 (Timer 1) | Combined with M01 selects Mode 0/1/2/3 |
| 4 | **M01** | Mode bit 0 (Timer 1) | See mode table below |
| 3 | **GATE0** | Software/Hardware control | When set, Timer 0 is controlled by **INT0** AND TR0. |
| 2 | **C/T̄0** | Counter/Timer select | **1 = Counter mode**, **0 = Timer mode** |
| 1 | **M10** | Mode bit 1 (Timer 0) | |
| 0 | **M00** | Mode bit 0 (Timer 0) | |

> [!NOTE]
> TMOD is **NOT bit-addressable**. The entire byte must be loaded atomically:
> `TMOD = 0x01;` (Timer 0, Mode 1, Timer mode, software control only)

## 2.3 The TCON Register (Timer Control Register, Address 88h)

TCON is **bit-addressable** and contains both the timer control bits and the edge flags for external interrupts.

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **TF1** | 8Fh | Timer 1 Overflow Flag — set by hardware when Timer 1 overflows. |
| 6 | **TR1** | 8Eh | Timer 1 Run control bit — **1 = Start**, **0 = Stop** (software controlled). |
| 5 | **TF0** | 8Dh | Timer 0 Overflow Flag — set by hardware when Timer 0 overflows. |
| 4 | **TR0** | 8Ch | Timer 0 Run control bit — **1 = Start**, **0 = Stop** (software controlled). |
| 3 | **IE1** | 8Bh | External Interrupt 1 edge flag |
| 2 | **IT1** | 8Ah | External Interrupt 1 type (edge/level) |
| 1 | **IE0** | 89h | External Interrupt 0 edge flag |
| 0 | **IT0** | 88h | External Interrupt 0 type |

## 2.4 The Four Timer Modes — Comparative Analysis

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Description | Width | Auto-Reload | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 13-bit Timer (8048 legacy) | 13 | No | Legacy compatibility |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | **16-bit Timer** | 16 | No | **Delay generation (most common)** |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | 8-bit Auto-Reload | 8 | **Yes (THx → TLx)** | **Baud-rate generator**, periodic interrupts |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | Split Timer | 8+8 | No | Timer 0 as two 8-bit timers (Timer 1 halts) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 1 (16-bit) is the KTU-favourite** for delay problems. **Mode 2 (Auto-reload)** is essential for serial-port baud-rate generation (covered in Module 3). Mode 3 is rarely examined but frequently appears in MCQ sections.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Machine Cycle (T}_{\text{mc}}\text{)} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | $f_{\text{osc}}$ = crystal frequency | Microseconds (μs) |
| 2 | $\text{Delay} = (2^{16} - \text{Initial Value}) \times T_{\text{mc}}$ | For Mode 1 (16-bit) | Seconds |
| 3 | $\text{Delay} = (2^{13} - \text{Initial Value}) \times T_{\text{mc}}$ | For Mode 0 (13-bit) | Seconds |
| 4 | $\text{Delay} = (2^{8} - \text{Initial Value}) \times T_{\text{mc}}$ | For Mode 2 (8-bit) | Seconds |
| 5 | $\text{Initial Value} = 2^{n} - \dfrac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{\text{mc}}}$ | $n$ = mode bit-width | Hexadecimal |
| 6 | $\text{THx} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Value}}{256}$ | High-byte | Integer division |
| 7 | $\text{TLx} = \text{Initial Value} \mod 256$ | Low-byte | Modulo 256 |
| 8 | $\text{Max Delay (Mode 1)} = 65536 \times \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | At $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$ | $\approx$ 71.11 ms |
| 9 | $\text{Number of Overflows} = \dfrac{\text{Total Delay}}{\text{Max Delay per overflow}}$ | For delays > max delay | Integer (loop count) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pitfall Rule for KTU:** When loading initial values, always remember that the timer **counts UP** from the initial value to **FFFFh (or 1FFFh in Mode 0)**, then rolls over to 0 and sets TFx. The closer the initial value is to the maximum, the **shorter** the delay.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Timer Mode Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Traffic Light Controller** | Mode 1 | Variable delay (1 sec, 5 sec, 10 sec) per phase |
| **Digital Watch / RTC** | Mode 2 | 1 ms / 1 s periodic tick |
| **UART Serial Communication** | Mode 2 | Auto-reload generates accurate baud rates |
| **Motor Speed Measurement** | Counter Mode | Counts pulses from encoder per fixed window |
| **Servo Motor PWM** | Mode 1 + ISR | Variable pulse widths (1 ms – 2 ms) |
| **Object Counting (Production Line)** | Counter Mode | Counts IR-sensor interruptions |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Master Derivation: Delay Calculation for Mode 1 (16-bit Timer)

We derive the general delay formula used in 8051 timer programming for **Mode 1**.

### Step 1 — Start with the Machine Cycle

By KTU/Intel 8051 hardware specification, every machine cycle consumes **12 oscillator pulses**:

$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$$

**Example:** For $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$:

$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$$

### Step 2 — Count Increment Behaviour

The timer registers (THx, TLx) form a 16-bit counter that increments by **+1 on every machine cycle**. Starting from the **Initial Value (IV)**, the counter ticks up to **FFFFh**, then rolls over to **0000h** and sets the overflow flag TFx = 1.

**Total count to overflow:**

$$\text{Total Counts} = 2^{16} - \text{IV} = 65536 - \text{IV}$$

### Step 3 — Total Delay Equation

Delay equals the number of counts multiplied by the time per count (machine cycle):

$$\boxed{\ \text{Delay} = (65536 - \text{IV}) \times \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}\ }$$

### Step 4 — Invert to Solve for Initial Value

$$\text{IV} = 65536 - \frac{\text{Delay} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard KTU Numerical (10 ms Delay)

**Problem:** Calculate the values to be loaded into **TH0** and **TL0** to generate a delay of **10 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**, with a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz**.

### Solution

**Step A — Compute the Machine Cycle:**

$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step B — Apply the Initial Value Formula:**

$$\text{IV} = 65536 - \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{\text{mc}}}$$

$$\text{IV} = 65536 - \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{IV} = 65536 - 9215.98 \approx 65536 - 9216 = 56320$$

**Step C — Convert IV to Hexadecimal:**

$$56320_{10} = \text{DC00}_{16}$$

(Verification: $\text{DC00h} = 13 \times 16^{3} + 12 \times 16^{2} + 0 + 0 = 53248 + 3072 = 56320$ ✓)

**Step D — Split into TH0 and TL0:**

$$\text{TH0} = \frac{\text{IV}}{256} = \frac{56320}{256} = 220_{10} = \text{DC}_{16}$$

$$\text{TL0} = \text{IV} \mod 256 = 56320 \mod 256 = 0_{10} = 0_{16}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice how 10 ms divides cleanly into the magic 9216 count — this happens because 11.0592 MHz is the **baud-rate-friendly** crystal. Most 10 ms KTU problems are designed so that the result is an integer. If your value is non-integer, round to the nearest integer.

### Embedded C Code (Mode 1, 10 ms)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Timer0_Delay_10ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;     // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), Timer mode, SW control
    TH0  = 0xDC;     // Load high byte
    TL0  = 0x00;     // Load low byte
    TR0  = 1;        // Start Timer 0
    while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow (polling)
    TR0  = 0;        // Stop Timer 0
    TF0  = 0;        // Clear overflow flag (software)
}
```

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Mode 2 Auto-Reload Calculation

**Problem:** Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to generate a delay of **200 μs** with a crystal of **12 MHz**.

### Solution

**Step A — Machine Cycle:**

$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1.0\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step B — Initial Value for Mode 2 (8-bit):**

$$\text{IV} = 2^{8} - \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{\text{mc}}} = 256 - \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\text{IV} = 256 - 200 = 56_{10} = 38_{16}$$

**Step C — Register Assignment:**

In Mode 2, **TH1 holds the reload value** and **TL1 is the actual counter**. So:

$$\text{TH1} = 0x38 \quad (\text{reload value})$$
$$\text{TL1} = 0x38 \quad (\text{initial counter value})$$

### Embedded C Code (Mode 2, 200 μs)

```c
void Timer1_AutoReload_200us(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;     // Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0x38;     // Reload value
    TL1  = 0x38;     // Initial counter value
    TR1  = 1;        // Start Timer 1
    while (TF1 == 0);
    TR1  = 0;
    TF1  = 0;
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 2 vs Mode 1 — KTU Distinguishing Point:** In Mode 2, after the first overflow, **hardware automatically copies THx into TLx**, eliminating the software reload step. This makes Mode 2 ideal for periodic interrupts (e.g., UART baud rate). In Mode 1, after every overflow, the **programmer** must manually re-load THx and TLx.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Large Delay via Loop (Mode 1)

**Problem:** Generate a delay of **500 ms** using Timer 0, Mode 1, $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$. (Hint: Maximum single overflow ≈ 71.11 ms → requires a loop.)

### Solution

**Step A — Maximum single overflow delay:**

$$\text{Max Delay} = 65536 \times \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 71.11\ \text{ms}$$

**Step B — Determine Loop Count:**

Since 500 ms > 71.11 ms, we must loop the timer. Use a smaller initial delay (e.g., **50 ms**) and repeat 10 times:

$$\text{Loop Count} = \frac{500\ \text{ms}}{50\ \text{ms}} = 10$$

**Step C — Initial Value for 50 ms (already calculated similarly to 10 ms):**

$$\text{IV} = 65536 - \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456_{10} = 4C00_{16}$$

So $\text{TH0} = 0x4C$, $\text{TL0} = 0x00$.

### Embedded C Code (500 ms via Loop)

```c
void Timer0_Delay_500ms(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    TMOD = 0x01;
    for (i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
        TH0 = 0x4C;
        TL0 = 0x00;
        TR0 = 1;
        while (TF0 == 0);
        TR0 = 0;
        TF0 = 0;
    }
}
```

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix — Choosing Timer Mode

| Criterion | Mode 0 (13-bit) | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit AR) | Mode 3 (Split) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum Delay (12 MHz) | 8.192 ms | 65.536 ms | 256 μs | 256 μs each |
| Maximum Delay (11.0592 MHz) | 9.895 ms | 71.11 ms | 277.8 μs | 277.8 μs |
| Auto-Reload | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Polling Complexity | High | High | Low | Very High |
| Common KTU Marks | Low | **Highest** | **High** | Low |
| Baud-Rate Generation | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (T1 only) | ❌ |

---

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of 8051 Timer Subsystem

```mermaid

```

**Example:** `TMOD = 0x21`
- Upper nibble `0x2` (0010) → Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (auto-reload)**
- Lower nibble `0x1` (0001) → Timer 0 in **Mode 1 (16-bit)**
- Both in **Timer mode**, software-controlled

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: Explain the function of the TMOD register in the 8051 microcontroller. How is the Timer/Counter mode selected?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an 8-bit non-bit-addressable SFR located at address **89h**. It is divided into two 4-bit nibbles, one for **Timer 0** (lower) and one for **Timer 1** (upper). Each nibble contains:

- **GATE** — selects hardware/software control of the timer run bit.
- **C/T̄** — selects **Counter (1)** or **Timer (0)** mode.
- **M1, M0** — select one of the **four operating modes** (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3).

For example, `TMOD = 0x20` configures Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), Timer mode, software-controlled**. The register is loaded as a single byte: `TMOD = 0x20;`

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: Differentiate between Timer mode and Counter mode of the 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock Source** | Internal oscillator (divided by 12) | External pin (T0 at P3.4 / T1 at P3.5) |
| **Counts** | Machine cycles (time intervals) | External events/pulses on the T0/T1 pin |
| **Use Case** | Delay generation, periodic tasks | Event counting (e.g., product counting) |
| **Selection** | C/T̄ = 0 | C/T̄ = 1 |
| **Trigger Edge** | N/A (synchronous) | **Negative edge (1 → 0 transition)** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the TMOD and TCON registers of the 8051. List all four timer modes with their bit-widths.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an Embedded C program using **Keil** syntax to generate a **50 ms delay** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** with an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**TMOD Register (Address 89h, NOT bit-addressable):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | GATE1 | C/T̄1 | M11 | M01 | GATE0 | C/T̄0 | M10 | M00 |

- **GATE:** When set, timer runs only when INTx pin is HIGH AND TRx is set.
- **C/T̄:** 1 = Counter mode, 0 = Timer mode.
- **M1, M0:** Select Mode 0 (00), Mode 1 (01), Mode 2 (10), Mode 3 (11).

**TCON Register (Address 88h, bit-addressable):**

| Bit | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Address | 8Fh | 8Eh | 8Dh | 8Ch | 8Bh | 8Ah | 89h | 88h |

- **TRx:** Run control — 1 = start, 0 = stop.
- **TFx:** Overflow flag — set by hardware, cleared by software.

**Four Timer Modes:**

| Mode | M1 | M0 | Bit-Width | Auto-Reload |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 13 bits | No |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 16 bits | No |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 8 bits | Yes (THx → TLx) |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 8+8 split | No |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle:**

$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Initial Value (Mode 1):**

$$\text{IV} = 65536 - \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456$$

**Step 3 — Hexadecimal Conversion:**

$$19456_{10} = 4C00_{16}$$

**Step 4 — Split:**

$$\text{TH0} = 0x4C, \quad \text{TL0} = 0x00$$

**Step 5 — Embedded C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Timer0_Delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;     // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x4C;     // High byte
    TL0  = 0x00;     // Low byte
    TR0  = 1;        // Start timer
    while (TF0 == 0); // Poll for overflow
    TR0  = 0;        // Stop timer
    TF0  = 0;        // Clear flag
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):** Many students write `TR0 = 0` **before** clearing TF0. This is acceptable, but **clearing TF0 is mandatory** — without it, the next delay call will exit the while-loop immediately! Also, students often confuse TH0 and TL0 values — always verify using a hexadecimal to decimal converter in your calculator.

---

### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the significance of **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** in the 8051. How is it used to generate the standard **9600 baud rate** for serial communication with an **11.0592 MHz** crystal? Show the TH1 calculation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an Embedded C program to toggle **Pin P1.0 continuously** every **1 second** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** with an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Use a polling method and an integer loop counter.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Mode 2 — 8-bit Auto-Reload:**

Mode 2 is the most efficient mode for **periodic, fixed-time-base events**. It uses an 8-bit TLx counter. When TLx overflows (FFh → 00h):

1. **TFx is set** (overflow flag).
2. **Hardware automatically copies THx into TLx** (the "auto-reload" feature).

This eliminates the software overhead of reloading the initial value, making it **interrupt-friendly** and ideal for the **UART baud-rate generator** (Timer 1).

**Baud-Rate Calculation for 9600 baud:**

The standard relation between baud rate and Timer 1 overflow rate in Mode 2 is:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

Substituting $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}$ Hz and Baud = 9600:

$$9600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{9600 \times 384} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3$$

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Golden Tip:** This is why 11.0592 MHz crystal is the gold standard for 8051 serial communication — it yields **integer divisor values** for the most common baud rates (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200), guaranteeing 0% baud-rate error.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Problem Analysis:**

- Desired total delay: **1 second = 1000 ms**.
- Maximum single Mode 1 delay at 11.0592 MHz: **71.11 ms**.
- Therefore, we need a loop: $1000 / 50 = 20$ iterations of 50 ms delay.

**Loop Count N = 20, Each Sub-Delay = 50 ms**

(Initial value $\text{TH0} = 0x4C$, $\text{TL0} = 0x00$, as derived earlier.)

**Embedded C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit TOGGLE_PIN = P1^0;   // Define P1.0 as output

void Timer0_Delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;          // Timer 0, Mode 1
    TH0  = 0x4C;
    TL0  = 0x00;
    TR0  = 1;
    while (TF0 == 0);
    TR0  = 0;
    TF0  = 0;
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    while (1) {
        TOGGLE_PIN = ~TOGGLE_PIN;  // Toggle P1.0
        for (i = 0; i < 20; i++) {
            Timer0_Delay_50ms();
        }
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** Part (a) is the **most-missed** KTU question. Students forget that the UART divides the oscillator by **384** (not 12) in Mode 2 baud-rate mode. Forgetting the divide-by-32 (SMOD bit default = 0) gives an erroneous TH1 value. For part (b), the **loop count must be precisely 20** for 1 second — if you use the maximum 71.11 ms, you cannot get an integer loop count for exactly 1 second, which is why 50 ms is the canonical sub-delay.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: 8051 Timer Programming**

- ✅ The 8051 has **2 timers** (Timer 0, Timer 1), each **16 bits** wide (THx + TLx).
- ✅ **TMOD** (89h) is the mode-select register — **NOT bit-addressable**, load whole byte.
- ✅ **TCON** (88h) is the control register — **bit-addressable**; contains TRx (run) and TFx (overflow flag).
- ✅ **C/T̄ = 0** → Timer mode (internal clock); **C/T̄ = 1** → Counter mode (external pin T0/T1).
- ✅ **Machine Cycle** = $12 / f_{\text{osc}}$. For 11.0592 MHz crystal → **1.085 μs** per machine cycle.
- ✅ **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the workhorse for variable delays. Formula: $\text{Delay} = (65536 - \text{IV}) \times T_{\text{mc}}$.
- ✅ **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** is mandatory for **UART baud-rate generation**; THx reloads TLx automatically.
- ✅ **Counter mode counts on the 1-to-0 transition** of the external T0/T1 pin (P3.4 / P3.5).
- ✅ Always **clear the TFx flag in software** — failing to do so causes the next polling loop to exit instantly.
- ✅ For delays > 71.11 ms (at 11.0592 MHz), use a **loop of sub-delays**, not a single huge counter.
- ✅ **11.0592 MHz** crystal is chosen for **zero-error UART** baud rates (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600).
- ✅ **Mode 3** is special: Timer 0 splits into two 8-bit timers (TL0 + TH0); Timer 1 in Mode 3 simply halts.
- ✅ GATE = 1 enables **hardware control** via INT0/INT1 pins (used for pulse-width measurement).
- ✅ Embedded C timer programming flow: **TMOD → Load THx/TLx → TRx=1 → Wait TFx → TRx=0 → TFx=0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonics:**
> - **"GCMM"** = **G**ATE, **C/T̄**, **M1**, **M0** — the four bits per timer in TMOD.
> - **"TR-TF"** = **T**imer **R**un (control) + **T**imer **F**lag (status) — the two key TCON bits.
> - **"TMD"** = **T**imer **M**ode, **D**elay — the standard 8051 timer application tagline.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Counter Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Counter Programming in 8051 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller family**, a **Counter** is a dedicated hardware peripheral used to count **external events** (typically negative-going pulses) applied to specific pins of the microcontroller. The 8051 contains **two 16-bit counters** named **Counter 0** and **Counter 1**, which share their internal hardware with Timer 0 and Timer 1 respectively. By configuring the appropriate bit in the **TMOD (Timer Mode) register**, the same hardware block can operate either as a **Timer** (counting internal machine cycles) or as a **Counter** (counting external pulses on pin **P3.4** for Counter 0 and **P3.5** for Counter 1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Counter programming is treated as a sub-topic under "Timer/Counter Programming" in Module 2. Students must clearly distinguish between **Timer Mode** (internal clock) and **Counter Mode** (external event counting on P3.4 / P3.5). Marks are frequently awarded for correctly identifying the TMOD configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:**
> - **Timer Mode** → counts **internal oscillator pulses** (machine cycles). Used for **time delay generation**.
> - **Counter Mode** → counts **external pulses** applied to pins **T0 (P3.4)** and **T1 (P3.5)**. Used for **event counting, frequency measurement, pulse-width measurement**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Turnstile at a Stadium Gate"

Imagine a turnstile at the entrance of a cricket stadium in Kochi:

| Component in Stadium | Equivalent in 8051 |
|---|---|
| A person walking through the turnstile | One external event/pulse on P3.4 or P3.5 |
| The rotating arm of the turnstile | The internal **counter register (TH0:TL0 or TH1:TL1)** |
| The person who presses the start button | Setting the **TR0** or **TR1** bit in **TCON** |
| The total number on the digital display | The current count value in **THx:TLx** |
| The bell that rings when 50,000 people have entered | **TF0 / TF1 overflow flag** (interrupt/count complete) |

Every time a person passes through, the counter increments by 1. The 8051's counter works **exactly the same way** — each external negative-going pulse on T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) causes the corresponding 16-bit counter register to increment by **one unit**. When the register **rolls over from its maximum value (FFFFH) back to 0000H**, the **Timer Flag (TF0 or TF1)** is set, signalling the completion of the count.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Maximum Count Value (16-bit mode)**: $\mathbf{2^{16} = 65{,}536}$ pulses before overflow.
- **Maximum Count Value (8-bit auto-reload mode)**: $\mathbf{2^{8} = 256}$ pulses before overflow.
- **Maximum Count Value (13-bit mode)**: $\mathbf{2^{13} = 8192}$ pulses before overflow.
- **External Pin for Counter 0**: **P3.4 (T0)**.
- **External Pin for Counter 1**: **P3.5 (T1)**.
- **Triggering Edge**: The counter increments on the **HIGH-to-LOW (falling) transition** of the external pin.
- **Minimum Pulse Width**: **1 machine cycle** (≈ 12 oscillator periods) for reliable detection, as per standard 8051 datasheets.

## 1.4 Pin Connections Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **Dual-Function Pins:** The pins P3.4 and P3.5 are shared with general-purpose I/O port pins. When the timer is configured as a counter, the **port latch must be set to 1** to allow the external pulse to be read properly on the pin.

- **P3.4 (T0)** → External input for **Counter 0**.
- **P3.5 (T1)** → External input for **Counter 1**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The TMOD Register — Mode Configuration

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) Register** is an **8-bit register** used to configure the operating mode of Timer/Counter 0 and Timer/Counter 1. It is **not bit-addressable**, so the entire register must be loaded as a single byte.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TMOD** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** |
| | ← Timer 1 → | | | | ← Timer 0 → | | | |

### Breakdown of Each Bit:

- **GATE**: When set to **1**, the timer/counter is enabled **only when the INTx pin is HIGH and the TRx bit is set**. Used for **hardware control** of the start/stop. When **0**, the timer/counter is enabled by software (setting TRx alone).
- **C/T̄ (Counter/Timer Select)**:
    - **C/T̄ = 0** → **Timer Mode** (counts internal machine cycles).
    - **C/T̄ = 1** → **Counter Mode** (counts external pulses on T0/T1 pin).
- **M1, M0 (Mode Bits)**: Select one of four operating modes.

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit timer/counter (8048 compatibility) |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit timer/counter |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload timer/counter |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split timer mode (only for Timer 0) |

## 2.2 The TCON Register — Control and Flags

The **TCON (Timer Control) Register** is an **8-bit, bit-addressable** register used to start/stop the timers and to hold the overflow flags.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TCON** | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **TR0 (Timer 0 Run control bit)**: **1 = start**, **0 = stop** the timer/counter 0.
- **TR1 (Timer 1 Run control bit)**: **1 = start**, **0 = stop** the timer/counter 1.
- **TF0 (Timer 0 Overflow Flag)**: Set to **1** automatically by hardware when Counter 0 overflows (rolls over from FFFFH to 0000H in Mode 1, or from FFH to 00H in Mode 2). Must be cleared by software.
- **TF1 (Timer 1 Overflow Flag)**: Same as TF0, but for Counter 1.

## 2.3 Why Counter Mode is Critical — The "How" Behind Counting

**Step-by-step logic of counter operation:**

1. The programmer configures the **C/T̄ bit = 1** in TMOD to select Counter Mode for the chosen timer (0 or 1).
2. The programmer selects the **mode** (typically Mode 1 for 16-bit counting or Mode 2 for 8-bit auto-reload).
3. The initial value is loaded into the high and low bytes of the counter register (**THx and TLx**).
4. The corresponding **TRx bit in TCON is set to 1** to start the counter.
5. The 8051 hardware now **monitors the T0 or T1 pin** for a **HIGH-to-LOW transition**.
6. On every valid falling edge, the corresponding counter register (**THx:TLx**) is **incremented by 1**.
7. When the count reaches the maximum value and rolls over, the **TFx flag is set**, and (if enabled) a **timer interrupt** is generated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardware Insight:** In Counter Mode, the 8051 samples the external pin for **one machine cycle**. To guarantee a reliable count, the external pulse must remain LOW for at least **one machine cycle** (approximately 1 µs for an 11.0592 MHz crystal). Pulses shorter than this may be missed.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Max count (16-bit, Mode 1) | $\mathbf{N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536}$ | Decimal pulses |
| Max count (8-bit, Mode 2) | $\mathbf{N_{max} = 2^{8} = 256}$ | Decimal pulses |
| Max count (13-bit, Mode 0) | $\mathbf{N_{max} = 2^{13} = 8192}$ | Decimal pulses |
| Initial value (16-bit) | $\mathbf{THx:TLx = 65{,}536 - N}$ | Where $N$ = desired count |
| Initial value (8-bit) | $\mathbf{THx = 256 - N}$ | Where $N$ = desired count |
| Frequency measurement | $\mathbf{f = N / T}$ | Hz, where $T$ = gate time in seconds |
| Pulse width | $\mathbf{T_{pulse} = N \times T_{machine}}$ | Seconds |
| Machine cycle period | $\mathbf{T_{machine} = 12 / f_{osc}}$ | Seconds |
| Counting edge | **HIGH-to-LOW (falling edge)** | On T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) |
| TMOD Counter value | $\mathbf{C/\bar{T} = 1}$ | Set in TMOD register |
| TCON Start bit | $\mathbf{TRx = 1}$ | Bit-addressable in TCON |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Safety:** All absolute values and conditional expressions are written using the alternate `\vert` and `\mid` LaTeX commands. The vertical pipe character `|` is **strictly reserved** for markdown table column separation. Never use raw `|` inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation**: Counting bottles on a conveyor belt using an optical sensor + 8051 counter.
- **Frequency Measurement**: Measuring the speed (RPM) of a motor using an encoder and a 1-second gate.
- **Traffic Density Systems**: Counting vehicles passing over an inductive loop embedded in the road.
- **Digital Speedometers**: Counting pulses from a wheel-mounted Hall-effect sensor.
- **Anemometers**: Counting rotations of a wind cup anemometer to measure wind speed.
- **Vending Machines**: Counting coin insertions via mechanical/electronic pulse generators.
