# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement 1 — Counting 1000 External Pulses Using Counter 0 in Mode 1

**Given:**
- 8051 microcontroller with $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz.
- Use **Counter 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit)** to count **N = 1000** external pulses on pin **P3.4**.
- After 1000 pulses, the overflow flag TF0 should be set.

**Step 1: Identify the maximum count for 16-bit Mode 1.**

$$N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ pulses}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the initial value that must be loaded into TH0:TL0.**

The counter starts at the initial value, increments once per external pulse, and overflows (sets TF0) when it reaches 65536 (i.e., rolls over from 65535 + 1 → 0).

$$TH0:TL0 = N_{max} - N$$

$$TH0:TL0 = 65{,}536 - 1000 = 64{,}536$$

**Step 3: Convert 64,536 decimal to hexadecimal.**

$$64{,}536 \div 4096 = 15 \text{ remainder } 3192$$

$$15 \text{ in hex} = F$$

$$3192 \div 256 = 12 \text{ remainder } 120$$

$$12 \text{ in hex} = C, \quad 120 \text{ in hex} = 78$$

$$64{,}536_{10} = FC78H$$

So, **TH0 = FCH** and **TL0 = 78H**.

**Step 4: Configure the TMOD register.**

For Counter 0, Mode 1, software-controlled start, no gate:

- GATE = 0
- C/T̄ = 1 (Counter Mode)
- M1 = 0, M0 = 1 (Mode 1)

Lower nibble = **0001** in binary. Timer 1 is unused (let's say Mode 0, Timer, GATE=0): Upper nibble = **0000**.

$$TMOD = 0000\,0001_{2} = 01H$$

**Step 5: 8051 Assembly Program.**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H     ; Timer/Counter 0, Mode 1, Counter Mode
        MOV     TH0, #0FCH     ; Load high byte of initial value
        MOV     TL0, #078H     ; Load low byte of initial value
        SETB    P3.4           ; Make P3.4 input-ready (port latch = 1)
        SETB    TR0            ; Start Counter 0
HERE:   JNB     TF0, HERE      ; Wait here until TF0 is set (1000 pulses counted)
        CLR     TR0            ; Stop Counter 0
        CLR     TF0            ; Clear overflow flag
        SJMP    $              ; Stay here forever
        END
```

---

## 3.2 Problem Statement 2 — Frequency Measurement Using Counter 1 in Mode 2

**Given:**
- Use **Counter 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to count external pulses for exactly **1 second**.
- Calculate the **frequency** of an unknown signal connected to P3.5.
- $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz, so $T_{machine} = 12 / 12{,}000{,}000 = 1$ µs.

**Step 1: Use Timer 0 to generate a precise 1-second gate.**

We need Timer 0 in Mode 1 to create a 1-second delay. But 1 second requires a 16-bit delay which gives max 65,536 µs (since $T_{machine} = 1$ µs). So we will loop Timer 0 **multiple times** to reach 1 second.

**Step 2: Determine how many times Timer 0 must overflow for 1 second.**

If we use 50 ms delay per loop, then 1000/50 = 20 loops.

For 50 ms delay using Mode 1 (16-bit):

$$N = \frac{Delay}{T_{machine}} = \frac{50{,}000}{1} = 50{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

Since $N = 50{,}000 > 65{,}536$ is false (it is less), it fits in 16-bit.

$$TH0:TL0 = 65{,}536 - 50{,}000 = 15{,}536$$

$$15{,}536_{10} = 3CB0H$$

So **TH0 = 3CH** and **TL0 = B0H** initially, and we need to reload each time.

**Step 3: Counter 1 setup.**

Counter 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload). Let it count the unknown pulses for 1 second.

$$TMOD = 0110\,0001_{2} = 61H$$

- Upper nibble (Timer 1): GATE=0, C/T̄=1, M1=1, M0=0 → **0110**
- Lower nibble (Timer 0): GATE=0, C/T̄=0, M1=0, M0=1 → **0001**

**Step 4: 8051 Assembly Program.**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #61H     ; T0 = Timer Mode 1, T1 = Counter Mode 2
        MOV     TH0, #03CH     ; 50 ms delay high byte
        MOV     TL0, #0B0H     ; 50 ms delay low byte
        MOV     TH1, #00H      ; Counter 1 starts from 0
        MOV     TL1, #00H
        SETB    P3.5           ; Make P3.5 input-ready
        MOV     R2, #20        ; Loop counter (20 × 50 ms = 1 s)
        SETB    TR1            ; Start Counter 1

LOOP1:  SETB    TR0            ; Start Timer 0 (50 ms gate)
LOOP2:  JNB     TF0, LOOP2     ; Wait for 50 ms overflow
        CLR     TF0            ; Clear TF0
        CLR     TR0            ; Stop Timer 0
        MOV     TH0, #03CH     ; Reload Timer 0
        MOV     TL0, #0B0H
        DJNZ    R2, LOOP1      ; Repeat 20 times = 1 second

        CLR     TR1            ; Stop Counter 1 after 1 s
        ; Frequency = value in TH1:TL1 (in Hz)
        ; since we counted for exactly 1 second
        ; FREQ_HI = TH1, FREQ_LO = TL1
        SJMP    $              ; Stay here
        END
```

**Step 5: Compute the result.**

After 1 second, **TH1:TL1 contains the frequency in Hz** (since 1 count per second = 1 Hz). If TH1:TL1 = 0064H = 100 decimal, then the frequency is **100 Hz**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Initial Value Calculation

| Desired Count (N) | Mode | $N_{max}$ | Formula | Initial Value (Decimal) | Initial Value (Hex) | THx | TLx |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | Mode 1 (16-bit) | 65,536 | $65536 - 100$ | 65,436 | FF9CH | FFH | 9CH |
| 200 | Mode 1 (16-bit) | 65,536 | $65536 - 200$ | 65,336 | FF38H | FFH | 38H |
| 500 | Mode 1 (16-bit) | 65,536 | $65536 - 500$ | 65,036 | FE0CH | FEH | 0CH |
| 100 | Mode 2 (8-bit) | 256 | $256 - 100$ | 156 | 9CH | 9CH | (auto-reload) |
| 200 | Mode 2 (8-bit) | 256 | $256 - 200$ | 56 | 38H | 38H | (auto-reload) |

flowchart TD

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Timer Mode and Counter Mode in 8051. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **C/T̄ bit in TMOD** | 0 | 1 |
| **Clock source** | Internal oscillator (divided by 12) | External pulse on P3.4 (T0) or P3.5 (T1) |
| **Use case** | Generating time delays | Counting external events |
| **Trigger** | Automatic (every machine cycle) | Falling edge (HIGH-to-LOW transition) of external pin |
| **Common application** | Delay routines, baud rate generation | Event counting, frequency measurement, RPM measurement |

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the C/T̄ bit in the TMOD register? (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

The **C/T̄ (Counter/Timer) bit** in the **TMOD register** is used to select the operating mode of the timer/counter hardware.

- When **C/T̄ = 0**, the corresponding timer operates in **Timer Mode**, where it counts the **internal machine cycle pulses** derived from the oscillator. This mode is used to generate precise time delays.
- When **C/T̄ = 1**, the corresponding timer operates in **Counter Mode**, where it counts **external events/pulses** applied to the corresponding pin (**P3.4 for Timer 0** and **P3.5 for Timer 1**). The count increments on every **falling edge** of the external input.

The 8051 has **two such C/T̄ bits**, one for Timer 0 and one for Timer 1, allowing each to be configured independently.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: Counter Programming with 16-bit Mode

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the TMOD and TCON registers of the 8051 with reference to counter programming. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register):**
- 8-bit register, **not bit-addressable**.
- Lower nibble (bits 0–3) controls **Timer/Counter 0**.
- Upper nibble (bits 4–7) controls **Timer/Counter 1**.
- Each nibble has 4 bits: **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0**.
- **GATE**: Hardware gating via INTx pin.
- **C/T̄**: Selects Timer (0) or Counter (1) mode.
- **M1, M0**: Select one of four operating modes (13-bit, 16-bit, 8-bit auto-reload, split timer).

**TCON Register (Timer Control Register):**
- 8-bit register, **bit-addressable** (address 88H).
- **TR0 (bit 4)** and **TR1 (bit 6)**: Start/stop control for Timer/Counter 0 and 1 respectively. Set to 1 to start, 0 to stop.
- **TF0 (bit 5)** and **TF1 (bit 7)**: Overflow flags. Automatically set by hardware when the counter rolls over. Must be cleared by software.
- Other bits (IE0, IT0, IE1, IT1) are for external interrupts.

For counter programming, the typical setup is:
- **C/T̄ = 1** in TMOD.
- **TRx = 1** in TCON to start.
- **TFx** monitored to detect count completion.

---

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to count **500 external pulses** arriving on pin **P3.4** using **Counter 0 in Mode 1**. When the count is complete, toggle **P1.0**. Assume $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate the initial value.**

$$N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$$

$$Initial = 65{,}536 - 500 = 65{,}036 = FE0CH$$

So, **TH0 = FEH** and **TL0 = 0CH**.

**Step 2: Configure TMOD.**

For Counter 0, Mode 1, software start, no gate:
- GATE = 0, C/T̄ = 1, M1 = 0, M0 = 1 → lower nibble = 0001
- Timer 1 unused: upper nibble = 0000
- $TMOD = 01H$

**Step 3: Assembly Program.**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H     ; Counter 0, Mode 1, software start
        MOV     TH0, #0FEH     ; Load high byte (FEH)
        MOV     TL0, #00CH     ; Load low byte (0CH)
        SETB    P3.4           ; Make P3.4 ready for external input
        SETB    TR0            ; Start Counter 0

WAIT:   JNB     TF0, WAIT      ; Wait until 500 pulses are counted
        CLR     TR0            ; Stop counter
        CLR     TF0            ; Clear overflow flag
        CPL     P1.0           ; Toggle P1.0 (output signal)
        SJMP    $              ; Stay here

        END
```

---

### Question B: Frequency Measurement Using Counter

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain how an 8051 microcontroller can be used to measure the **frequency of an external signal** using its counter feature. Include the necessary register configuration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 can measure frequency by:
1. **Feeding the unknown signal** into the **Counter 1 input pin P3.5 (T1)**.
2. **Using Timer 0** to generate a **precise gate time** (e.g., 1 second).
3. During this gate time, **Counter 1** counts the number of pulses from the unknown signal.
4. After the gate time expires, the count value in **TH1:TL1** directly represents the **frequency in Hz** (if gate = 1 second).

**Register Configuration:**
- **TMOD = 61H** (0110 0001):
    - Timer 0: Mode 1, Timer Mode (C/T̄=0, M1=0, M0=1)
    - Timer 1: Mode 1, Counter Mode (C/T̄=1, M1=0, M0=1)
- **TCON**: TR0 and TR1 both set to 1 to start both timers simultaneously.
- Initial values:
    - Timer 0 loaded to overflow after the desired gate period.
    - Counter 1 loaded with 0000H (starts counting from zero).
- After the gate: TR0 and TR1 are cleared, and TH1:TL1 is read as the frequency.

**Advantages:** Hardware-based counting is **highly accurate** (no software polling overhead) and the 8051 can simultaneously process other tasks via interrupts.

---

**(b)** An 8051 system uses **Counter 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to count pulses from a motor encoder. The encoder generates **50 pulses per revolution**. The system must trigger an action every time the motor completes **3 revolutions**. Write the assembly program to achieve this and explain the calculation of the TH1 value. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate total pulses needed.**

$$N = 3 \text{ revolutions} \times 50 \text{ pulses/rev} = 150 \text{ pulses}$$

**Step 2: Choose Mode 2 for auto-reload.**

In Mode 2, the counter is 8-bit, max count = 256. The TH1 holds the reload value and TL1 is the active counter.

**Step 3: Calculate TH1 (reload) value.**

$$TH1 = 256 - 150 = 106 = 6AH$$

So, **TH1 = 6AH** and **TL1 also starts at 6AH** (the first count starts from the loaded value).

**Step 4: Configure TMOD.**

For Counter 1, Mode 2, software start:
- GATE = 0, C/T̄ = 1, M1 = 1, M0 = 0 → upper nibble = 0110
- Timer 0 unused: lower nibble = 0000
- $TMOD = 60H$

**Step 5: Assembly Program.**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H

MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #60H     ; Counter 1, Mode 2, software start
        MOV     TH1, #06AH     ; Auto-reload value (256 - 150 = 106)
        MOV     TL1, #06AH     ; Initial counter value
        SETB    P3.5           ; Configure P3.5 for external input
        SETB    TR1            ; Start Counter 1

WAIT:   JNB     TF1, WAIT      ; Wait for 150 pulses (3 revolutions)
        CLR     TR1            ; Stop counter
        CLR     TF1            ; Clear overflow flag
        ; The action is triggered HERE
        ; (e.g., SETB P1.0 to drive a relay)
        SETB    P1.0
        ACALL   DELAY          ; Optional debounce delay
        CLR     P1.0
        SJMP    MAIN           ; Repeat counting cycle

DELAY:  MOV     R3, #0FFH
D1:     DJNZ    R3, D1
        RET

        END
```

**Explanation:** The counter is loaded with 6AH (= 106). It then increments once per encoder pulse. After **150 pulses**, the counter rolls over from FFH → 00H, setting **TF1 = 1**. The CPU detects this and triggers the action. Since Mode 2 is auto-reload, TH1's value (6AH) is automatically copied back to TL1 on overflow, ready for the next cycle **without any software reload overhead**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Counter Programming Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to set P3.4 or P3.5 to 1**: Many students forget that the **port latch must be set to 1** to allow the external pulse to be read on the shared pin. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing C/T̄ = 0 with C/T̄ = 1**: A very common mistake. **C/T̄ = 1** is for **Counter Mode**, **C/T̄ = 0** is for **Timer Mode**. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **Not clearing TFx after detection**: The TFx flag is **not automatically cleared** — it must be cleared by software. Forgetting this prevents subsequent counts from being detected.
> 4. **Wrong initial value calculation**: For 16-bit mode, the initial value is $2^{16} - N$, not $N$ itself. Students often load the count value directly, causing the counter to overflow after 65,536 - N pulses, not N.
> 5. **Using wrong pin**: Counter 0 uses **P3.4**, Counter 1 uses **P3.5**. Mixing these up costs marks.
> 6. **Confusing Mode 1 and Mode 2 for auto-reload**: Only **Mode 2** provides auto-reload functionality. In Mode 1, the programmer must manually reload THx and TLx after every overflow.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Counter Programming in 8051:**

- **Definition**: A counter counts **external events** on pins **P3.4 (T0)** and **P3.5 (T1)**. It increments on the **falling edge** of the external pulse.
- **Counter vs Timer**:
    - Counter → **C/T̄ = 1**, external clock source.
    - Timer → **C/T̄ = 0**, internal machine cycle clock.
- **TMOD Register** (8-bit, not bit-addressable):
    - Lower nibble → Timer 0 configuration.
    - Upper nibble → Timer 1 configuration.
    - Bits: **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0**.
- **TCON Register** (8-bit, bit-addressable at 88H):
    - **TRx** = 1 → Start; **TRx** = 0 → Stop.
    - **TFx** = 1 → Overflow flag (set on roll-over from max to 0).
- **Modes**:
    - **Mode 0**: 13-bit (8192 max). Rarely used.
    - **Mode 1**: 16-bit (65536 max). **Most commonly used** for counters.
    - **Mode 2**: 8-bit auto-reload (256 max). **Best for continuous counting** applications.
    - **Mode 3**: Split timer (Timer 0 only).
- **Initial Value Formulas**:
    - 16-bit: $THx:TLx = 65{,}536 - N$
    - 8-bit: $THx = 256 - N$
    - 13-bit: $THx:TLx = 8192 - N$ (5 bits in TLx)
- **Pin Configuration**: Always **set P3.4 or P3.5 to 1** (port latch) before starting the counter.
- **Flag Clearing**: **TFx must be cleared by software** after detection.
- **Counter Resolution**: Maximum 1 count per **machine cycle** (12 oscillator periods).
- **GATE Bit Use**: For **hardware-controlled** counting, set GATE = 1 and use the INTx pin as a hardware enable signal.
- **Key Applications**: Frequency counters, RPM measurement (tachometers), event counters (people/product counters), pulse-width measurement, digital speedometers, conveyor belt counters.
- **Frequency Measurement Formula**: $f = N / T_{gate}$, where $T_{gate}$ is the precise time the counter is allowed to run (typically 1 second).
- **Programming Sequence**: Configure TMOD → Set port latch → Load THx:TLx → Set TRx → Monitor TFx (poll or interrupt) → Clear TRx and TFx → Repeat.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Serial Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basics of Serial Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serial Communication** is a method of data transmission in which bits of a data word are transmitted **sequentially over a single communication channel (wire or channel)** one after another, as opposed to parallel communication where multiple bits are sent simultaneously on multiple lines.

In the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** and embedded systems, serial communication is primarily handled by the on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter)** hardware peripheral. It allows the microcontroller to exchange data with external devices like PCs, sensors, GSM modules, GPS modules, Bluetooth modules, and other microcontrollers using standardized protocols.

## 1.2 Key Terminology of the KTU Syllabus

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One-way communication (Data flows only in one direction). Example: Radio broadcast. |
| **Half-Duplex** | Two-way communication, but **not simultaneous** (Each device must wait for the other to finish). Example: Walkie-Talkie. |
| **Full-Duplex** | Two-way communication, **simultaneously** possible in both directions. Example: Mobile phone call. |
| **Synchronous** | A shared **clock signal** is transmitted alongside data to synchronize the receiver. |
| **Asynchronous** | **No shared clock** is used. Data is framed with start/stop bits, and both ends must agree on a common **Baud Rate** beforehand. |
| **Baud Rate** | The **number of signal changes (symbols) per second** transmitted over the channel. Often (but not always) equal to bits per second. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine data transmission as a highway:**
> - **Parallel Communication** is a **multi-lane highway** (8 lanes for an 8-bit word). It is *fast*, but *expensive* and *noisy* over long distances.
> - **Serial Communication** is a **single-lane road**. It is *slower*, but *cheaper*, requires *fewer pins*, and is *immune to clock skew* over long distances — making it ideal for connecting devices that are physically apart (USB, Bluetooth, RS-232).

A second analogy: **Serial = sending a message letter-by-letter** through a postal tube. **Parallel = handing 8 letters to 8 people simultaneously**. The first uses one tube but takes time; the second uses 8 tubes but is fast and resource-heavy.

## 1.4 Why Serial Communication? (Engineering Motivation)

- **Pin Economy**: A microcontroller like the 8051 has a limited number of I/O pins. Serial uses **only 1 line (TX)** to send and **1 line (RX)** to receive, instead of 8 parallel lines.
- **Long-Distance Reliability**: Parallel lines suffer from *clock skew* and *crosstalk* over long cables. Serial lines do not.
- **Standardization**: Universal standards (UART, SPI, I²C, USB, CAN) make inter-device compatibility simple.
- **Clock Independence**: Asynchronous serial allows two devices with different clock sources to communicate reliably.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Asynchronous Serial Transmission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the 8051 UART is **asynchronous**, both the transmitter (TX) and the receiver (RX) must operate at the **exact same Baud Rate**, generated from their own independent crystal oscillators. There is no shared clock line.

The transmission of a single data byte follows this strict sequence:

### Step 1: Idle State
When no data is being transmitted, the UART line is held in the **MARK (Logic HIGH = '1')** state. This is the default resting voltage level.

### Step 2: Start Bit
The transmitter pulls the line **LOW (Logic '0')** for exactly **one bit period**. This falling edge alerts the receiver: *"A new byte is arriving. Start sampling!"*

### Step 3: Data Bits
The actual data byte is transmitted, **LSB (Least Significant Bit) first** by convention. There are usually **8 data bits**, but some systems use 7 or 9. Each bit is held for exactly one bit period.

### Step 4: Parity Bit (Optional)
A single bit used for **error detection**. It can be **Even Parity** (total number of 1s including parity is even) or **Odd Parity** (total is odd). This bit detects single-bit errors.

### Step 5: Stop Bit(s)
The line returns to **HIGH (Logic '1')** for **1, 1.5, or 2 bit periods**. This guarantees a defined idle period before the next start bit, allowing the receiver to reset its sampling logic.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-critical** for KTU Board Examinations. Memorize them thoroughly.

### A. Relationship Between Baud Rate and Bit Period

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$$

- $T_{bit}$ = Time duration of one bit (in seconds)
- Baud Rate is measured in **symbols/second** or **bauds**

### B. Total Frame Time (Time to Transmit One Complete Frame)

$$T_{frame} = N_{bits} \times T_{bit}$$

where $N_{bits}$ is the total number of bits in the frame:

$$N_{bits} = 1_{\text{start}} + N_{data} + N_{parity} + N_{stop}$$

### C. Effective Data Throughput (Bits Per Second of Useful Data)

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{N_{data}}{\text{Total Frame Time}} \times \text{Baud Rate}$$

For the most common configuration (1 start, 8 data, 0 parity, 1 stop → **10 bits per frame**):

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{8}{10} \times \text{Baud Rate} = 0.8 \times \text{Baud Rate}$$

### D. 8051 Timer 1 Reload Value (for Baud Rate Generation)

The 8051 uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** to generate the Baud Rate. The TH1 reload value is given by:

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \left( \frac{f_{osc}}{32 \times \text{Baud Rate} \times 12} \right) \times \text{ERROR\_FACTOR}$$

For standard 11.0592 MHz crystal (the most KTU-favoured value):

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{\text{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{11059200}{12 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

> [!TIP]
> The magic crystal **11.0592 MHz** is chosen because it is an integer multiple of standard Baud Rates (9600, 19200, 57600). This **eliminates fractional timing errors** that would otherwise accumulate over long transmissions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit Period** | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | seconds |
| **Frame Time** | $T_{frame} = N_{bits} \times T_{bit}$ | seconds |
| **Frame Bits** | $N_{bits} = 1 + N_d + N_p + N_s$ | dimensionless |
| **Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{N_d}{N_{bits}} \times \text{BR}$ | bps |
| **8051 Timer Reload** | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$ | Hex/Decimal |
| **Standard Baud Rates** | 1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 57600, 115200 | bps |
| **Parity** | $P = N_d \oplus P_{type}$ | Even/Odd |
| **Error Rate** | $\epsilon = \dfrac{\vert f_{ideal} - f_{actual} \vert}{f_{ideal}} \times 100\%$ | percent |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Serial Communication

- **PC ↔ Microcontroller Interfacing** (Debugging, Data Logging, Firmware Updates)
- **Sensor Networks** (GPS, GSM, RFID, Bluetooth, Wi-Fi modules all use serial)
- **Industrial Automation** (Modbus RTU, RS-485 multi-drop networks)
- **Embedded Internet-of-Things (IoT)** devices

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time to Transmit the Character 'A' at 9600 Baud

**Problem (KTU-Style):** Calculate the total time required to transmit the ASCII character **'A'** (binary: `0100 0001`) using an asynchronous serial link with the following configuration: **9600 Baud, 8 Data bits, No Parity, 1 Stop bit**. Show every step explicitly.

### Step 1 — Identify the Frame Structure

The frame consists of:
- 1 Start bit (always '0')
- 8 Data bits (LSB first)
- 0 Parity bits
- 1 Stop bit (always '1')

**Total bits per frame:**

$$N_{bits} = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Bit Period

Using the inverse of the Baud Rate:

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{9600} = 1.0416\overline{6} \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{bit} = 104.16\overline{6} \ \mu s$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Total Frame Time

Multiplying the bit period by the number of bits:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= N_{bits} \times T_{bit} \\
&= 10 \times \frac{1}{9600} \\
&= \frac{10}{9600} \text{ seconds} \\
&= 1.04166\overline{6} \times 10^{-3} \text{ seconds} \\
&\approx 1.0417 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Express the Complete Transmitted Frame on the Wire

For character **'A' = 0x41 = 0100 0001** (LSB first), the on-wire sequence is:

| Bit Position | 1 (Start) | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 10 (Stop) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Value** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **Meaning** | Start | LSB | | | | | MSB | | | Stop |

> [!NOTE]
> Note carefully: the LSB (bit 0) of 'A' is **1**, so the second bit transmitted is '1'. The receiver reconstructs the byte by collecting all 8 data bits and reversing their order to get `0100 0001`.

### Step 5 — Final Answer

$$\boxed{T_{frame} = 1.0417 \text{ ms per character 'A'}}$$

The effective data rate is therefore:

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{8 \text{ data bits}}{10 \text{ frame bits}} \times 9600 = 7680 \text{ bps}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: 8051 Timer 1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud

**Problem:** Compute the value to be loaded into **TH1** of the 8051 to generate a Baud Rate of **9600 bps** using a **11.0592 MHz crystal** with **SMOD = 0** (default).

### Step 1 — Recall the 8051 Baud Rate Formula

The 8051's UART divides the oscillator frequency by 12 (machine cycle), then further divides by 32 (in Mode 1 with SMOD=0), and Timer 1 overflows to produce the Baud Rate:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Solve for TH1

$$\begin{aligned}
(256 - \text{TH1}) &= \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times \text{Baud Rate}} \\
\text{TH1} &= 256 - \left( \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times \text{Baud Rate}} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Known Values

Given $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz, Baud Rate = 9600, SMOD = 0 (so $2^{\text{SMOD}} = 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
(256 - \text{TH1}) &= \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times 9600} \\
&= \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{115200} \\
&= \frac{1}{32} \times 96 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for TH1

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TH1} &= 256 - 3 = 253 \\
\text{In Hex:} \quad \text{TH1} &= \text{FD}_{H}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Verification

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 12 \times 3} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{1152} = 9600 \text{ bps} \ \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\text{TH1} = \text{FD}_{H} = 253_{D}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Standard Baud Rates for 11.0592 MHz Crystal (SMOD=0)

| Desired Baud Rate (bps) | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | Actual Baud Rate | Error (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1200 | 230 | E6 | 1200 | 0.00 |
| 2400 | 244 | F4 | 2400 | 0.00 |
| 4800 | 250 | FA | 4800 | 0.00 |
| **9600** | **253** | **FD** | **9600** | **0.00** |
| 19200 | — | — | (Fractional, not possible cleanly) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 11.0592 MHz crystal produces **0% error** for 1200, 2400, 4800, and 9600 bps. This is precisely why it is the de-facto standard for 8051-based serial communication projects — and is heavily emphasized in KTU board questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Asynchronous Serial Frame Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Idle Line at Logic HIGH = MARK State] --> B[Transmitter pulls line LOW to send Start Bit - 1 bit period]
    B --> C[Transmit Data Bits LSB first - typically 8 bits]
    C --> D{Parity Enabled?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Transmit Parity Bit - Even or Odd]
    D -- No --> F[Skip Parity Bit]
    E --> G[Transmit Stop Bit s - 1, 1.5, or 2 bit periods]
    F --> G
    G --> H{More Data to Send?}
    H -- Yes --> A
    H -- No --> I[Return to Idle HIGH State and wait for next byte]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Two 8051 Microcontrollers in Full-Duplex Serial Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LeftDevice[8051 Microcontroller Device A]
        CPU_A[CPU Core] --> SBUF_A[SBUF Register 8-bit]
        SBUF_A --> TX_A[UART Transmitter Block]
        RX_A[UART Receiver Block] --> SBUF_A
    end

    subgraph RightDevice[8051 Microcontroller Device B]
        CPU_B[CPU Core] --> SBUF_B[SBUF Register 8-bit]
        SBUF_B --> TX_B[UART Transmitter Block]
        RX_B[UART Receiver Block] --> SBUF_B
    end

    TX_A -- TXD Line P3.1 Pin 11 --> RX_B
    RX_A -- RXD Line P3.0 Pin 10 --> TX_B
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: RS-232 Level Translation Between 8051 and PC

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 uses **TTL levels (0V and 5V)**. The PC's serial port uses **RS-232 levels (+12V and -12V)**. A level translator chip like the **MAX232** is mandatory to avoid hardware damage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[PC Serial Port RS-232 Levels plus minus 12V] -- DB9 Connector --> MAX232Chip[MAX232 Level Translator IC]
    MAX232Chip -- TTL 0V and 5V --> MUC[8051 Microcontroller Pins P3.0 RXD and P3.1 TXD]
    MUC --> VCC[Capacitors C1 to C4 form charge pump voltage doubler inside MAX232]
    VCC --> MAX232Chip
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Timing Diagram: Bit-Level Visualization of 'A' Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T0 Idle HIGH] --> T1[T1 Start Bit LOW]
    T1 --> T2[T2 Data bit 0 = 1 MSB first reversed]
    T2 --> T3[T3 Data bit 1 = 0]
    T3 --> T4[T4 Data bit 2 = 0]
    T4 --> T5[T5 Data bit 3 = 0]
    T5 --> T6[T6 Data bit 4 = 0]
    T6 --> T7[T7 Data bit 5 = 1]
    T7 --> T8[T8 Data bit 6 = 0]
    T8 --> T9[T9 Data bit 7 = 0 LSB]
    T9 --> T10[T10 Stop Bit HIGH]
    T10 --> T11[T11 Return to Idle HIGH]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each T-block above represents **one bit period** ($T_{bit} = 1/\text{Baud Rate}$). The receiver samples each data bit at the **center of the bit period** to maximize noise immunity. For 9600 Baud, each T-block is **104.17 µs wide**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Q: Differentiate between Synchronous and Asynchronous Serial Communication. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock Line** | Shared clock line is required | No clock line; both sides use local clocks |
| **Framing** | Data is sent in continuous blocks | Data is framed with Start and Stop bits |
| **Speed** | Typically higher (no start/stop overhead) | Slower due to overhead bits |
| **Hardware** | More pins (data + clock) | Fewer pins (just TX and RX) |
| **Examples** | SPI, I²C | UART, RS-232 |
| **Use Case** | Short distance, high speed (PCB-level) | Long distance, simple wiring |
| **Overhead** | Minimal | ~20% for 8N1 configuration |

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is Baud Rate? How is it related to Bit Rate? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Baud Rate** is defined as the number of **signal changes (symbols) per second** transmitted over a communication channel. Unit: **bauds (symbols/second)**.
- **Bit Rate** is the number of **bits transmitted per second**. Unit: **bps (bits per second)**.
- **Relationship:**
  - When each symbol carries exactly **1 bit** (binary signaling with two voltage levels), Baud Rate = Bit Rate.
  - When each symbol carries **multiple bits** (e.g., using 4 voltage levels to encode 2 bits per symbol), Bit Rate = Baud Rate × $\log_2(M)$ where $M$ is the number of distinct symbols.
- **For 8051 UART:** Binary signaling is used, so **Baud Rate = Bit Rate**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks Each)

### Question B1A [KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **asynchronous serial data frame format** of the 8051 UART. List all possible frame configurations supported by the 8051. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The 8051 is operating with an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH1** to achieve a **Baud Rate of 4800 bps** with **SMOD = 0**. Show all derivations. Also, calculate the **time to transmit one byte** (8N1 configuration). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The 8051 UART frame format is shown in the diagram below:

```
    +-----+----------+------------+-----+
    |START|  DATA    |  PARITY    |STOP |
    | 0   | 8 BITS   |  OPTIONAL  |  1  |
    +-----+----------+------------+-----+
      1b    8b           0 or 1b    1/1.5/2b
```

**Key Features of 8051 UART Frame:**
1. **Start Bit**: Always **1 bit**, always **Logic LOW (0)**. Signals the beginning of a new frame.
2. **Data Bits**: Usually **8 bits**, transmitted **LSB first** (bit 0 first, bit 7 last).
3. **Parity Bit**: Optional. Can be **Even** or **Odd** (configured via SCON register bit TB8/RB8).
4. **Stop Bit**: **1, 1.5, or 2 bits**, always **Logic HIGH (1)**. Allows the receiver to reset.

**All 8051 Frame Configurations (Mode 1 with parity options):**
- 8 data, no parity, 1 stop (8N1) — 10 bits total
- 8 data, no parity, 2 stop (8N2) — 11 bits total
- 8 data, even parity, 1 stop (8E1) — 11 bits total
- 8 data, odd parity, 1 stop (8O1) — 11 bits total
- 9 data bits (used for multiprocessor communication in Mode 2/3)

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, Baud Rate = 4800, SMOD = 0, 8N1 frame.

**Step 1 — Calculate TH1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{TH1} &= 256 - \left( \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times \text{Baud Rate}} \right) \\
&= 256 - \left( \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times 4800} \right) \\
&= 256 - \left( \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{57600} \right) \\
&= 256 - \left( \frac{1}{32} \times 192 \right) \\
&= 256 - 6 \\
&= 250
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{TH1} = 250_{D} = \text{FA}_{H}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Bit Period:**

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{4800} = 2.0833 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 208.33 \ \mu s$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Time Per Frame (8N1 = 10 bits):**

$$T_{frame} = 10 \times 208.33 \ \mu s = 2083.33 \ \mu s \approx 2.083 \text{ ms}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TH1} = \text{FA}_{H}, \quad T_{frame} = 2.083 \text{ ms}}$$

---

### Question B1B [Alternative Choice - KTU 2024 Scheme Sample]

**(a)** Compare **Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex** communication with neat diagrams. Which mode does the 8051 UART support, and why? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system transmits ASCII characters at **2400 Baud** with **8 data bits, even parity, and 2 stop bits**. Calculate: (i) the number of overhead bits per frame, (ii) total bits per frame, (iii) time to transmit the character **'Z'**, and (iv) effective data throughput in bps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Mode | Direction | Simultaneous? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One direction only | N/A | Radio, TV broadcast |
| **Half-Duplex** | Both directions, but not at the same time | No | Walkie-Talkie |
| **Full-Duplex** | Both directions simultaneously | Yes | Mobile phone |

**Mermaid Sketch of the Three Modes:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SimplexDiagram[Simplex]
        A1[Device A] -- one way data --> B1[Device B]
    end
    subgraph HalfDuplexDiagram[Half Duplex]
        A2[Device A] <-- shared channel --> B2[Device B]
    end
    subgraph FullDuplexDiagram[Full Duplex]
        A3[Device A] -- TX line --> B3[Device B]
        B3 -- RX line --> A3
    end
```

**8051 UART supports Full-Duplex** because it has **separate physical pins for transmission (P3.1 / TXD) and reception (P3.0 / RXD)**, along with **independent hardware buffers (SBUF for TX and SBUF for RX)** that can operate concurrently.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** Baud Rate = 2400, Frame: 8E2 (8 data, Even parity, 2 stop)

**(i) Overhead bits per frame:**

$$\text{Overhead} = 1_{\text{start}} + 1_{\text{parity}} + 2_{\text{stop}} = 4 \text{ bits}$$

**(ii) Total bits per frame:**

$$N_{bits} = 8_{\text{data}} + 4_{\text{overhead}} = 12 \text{ bits}$$

**(iii) Time to transmit 'Z':**

- 'Z' ASCII = 0x5A = `0101 1010`
- Bit Period: $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{2400} = 416.6\overline{6} \ \mu s$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{frame} &= 12 \times 416.6\overline{6} \ \mu s \\
&= 5000 \ \mu s \\
&= 5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Effective Data Throughput:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{8 \text{ data bits}}{12 \text{ total bits}} \times 2400 \\
&= 0.6\overline{6} \times 2400 \\
&= 1600 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Overhead} = 4, \quad N_{bits} = 12, \quad T_{frame} = 5 \text{ ms}, \quad \text{Throughput} = 1600 \text{ bps}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Forgetting the start bit** is the #1 mistake. Always count it. A 8N1 frame has **10 bits**, not 8.
> 2. **Stop bit duration is in BIT PERIODS, not microseconds.** "1 stop bit" = 1 × $T_{bit}$, not a fixed 1 ms.
> 3. **Parity bit calculation**: For **even parity** of 'Z' (binary 01011010, four 1s), parity bit = **0** (to keep total even). Students often compute this wrong.
> 4. **MSB vs LSB order**: UART always sends **LSB first**. If the question asks for the on-wire bit pattern, reverse the binary representation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam — Module 3**

- **Serial vs Parallel**: Serial = 1 bit at a time (cheaper, slower over short, faster over long); Parallel = simultaneous (fast, but pin-hungry, suffers skew).
- **8051 UART Pins**: **P3.0 (RXD)** for receiving, **P3.1 (TXD)** for transmitting.
- **Two independent SBUF registers** exist — one for TX, one for RX — both mapped to the same address (99H). Writing to SBUF loads the TX buffer; reading from SBUF reads the RX buffer.
- **SCON Register (98H)** controls the UART mode. **Mode 1** is the standard 8-bit variable Baud Rate mode used in 99% of KTU problems.
- **Mode 1 Frame**: 1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Stop (plus optional parity via TB8/RB8) = **10 or 11 bits per frame**.
- **Magic Crystal 11.0592 MHz** produces **0% error** for 1200, 2400, 4800, 9600 bps.
- **TH1 Reload Formula**: $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$ (for SMOD=0).
- **Bit Period Formula**: $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$.
- **LSB first transmission** is the universal UART convention — don't get tricked.
- **MAX232 IC** is required to interface 8051 (TTL: 0/5V) with PC (RS-232: ±12V) — never connect them directly.
- **Full-duplex** is the default mode of 8051 UART (separate TX and RX pins, independent SBUF).
- **TI flag** (in SCON) is set by hardware when transmission completes; **RI flag** is set when reception completes. Both must be **cleared by software**.
- **Effective Throughput** = $\dfrac{\text{Data bits}}{\text{Total frame bits}} \times \text{Baud Rate}$. For 8N1: 80% efficiency.
- **Receiver samples bits at the center of each bit period** (e.g., at $T/2, 3T/2, 5T/2, \ldots$ from the start bit falling edge) to maximize noise immunity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 Connections to RS232
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** 8051 Connections to RS232 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232)** is a legacy standard for serial binary data interchange between a *Data Terminal Equipment (DTE)* and a *Data Circuit-terminating Equipment (DCE)*. It defines the electrical signal characteristics, connector pin assignments (typically **DB-9** or DB-25), timing, and a full-duplex asynchronous communication protocol. The **8051 microcontroller** contains an on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** that supports serial communication. Since the 8051 transmits/receives data using **TTL/CMOS voltage levels (0 V and +5 V)** and RS-232 uses much higher bipolar voltages (**+3 V to +25 V for SPACE (0) and −3 V to −25 V for MARK (1)**), a **level-shifting interface IC** (e.g., **MAX232**) is mandatory to interconnect them. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board High-Yield Concept:** The 8051's UART is the *only* on-chip peripheral in the original 8051 that natively supports asynchronous serial communication. The relevant pins are **P3.0 (RXD)** and **P3.1 (TXD)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine two people trying to have a conversation: - **Person A (8051):** Speaks softly, using simple hand signals with **two voltage levels (0 V and 5 V)** — like a *binary whisper*. - **Person B (RS-232 Device like a PC):** Speaks loudly with **negative and positive voltages (±12 V)** — like a *thunderous boom*. Neither can understand the other directly. We insert a **bilingual translator (the MAX232 chip)** that: - **Translates** the soft 0/5 V signals into the loud ±12 V RS-232 signals going *out* of the 8051. - **Translates** the loud ±12 V signals back into soft 0/5 V signals coming *into* the 8051. The **baud rate** is the *speed* at which this conversation happens — measured in **bits per second (bps)**. Both sides must agree on the exact same speed, just like two drummers must keep the same tempo. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Baud Rates:** 1200, 2400, 4800, **9600** (most common), 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200 bps. ### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Constants | Parameter | TTL (8051) | RS-232 Standard | |---|---|---| | Logic 0 (SPACE) | **0 V** | **+3 V to +25 V** (typ. +12 V) | | Logic 1 (MARK) | **+5 V** | **−3 V to −25 V** (typ. −12 V) | | Maximum Cable Length | < 1 m (practical) | **15 meters (50 feet)** | | Connector | — | **DB-9 (DE-9)** — Male on DTE side | | Communication Mode | — | **Full-duplex asynchronous** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 RS-232 DB-9 (DE-9) Pin Assignment — PC (DTE) Side | Pin | Name | Function | Direction (DTE ↔ DCE) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | DCD | Data Carrier Detect | ← from DCE | | 2 | **RXD** | Received Data | ← from DCE | | 3 | **TXD** | Transmitted Data | → to DCE | | 4 | DTR | Data Terminal Ready | → to DCE | | 5 | **GND** | **Signal Ground** | — | | 6 | DSR | Data Set Ready | ← from DCE | | 7 | RTS | Request To Send | → to DCE | | 8 | CTS | Clear To Send | ← from DCE | | 9 | RI | Ring Indicator | ← from DCE | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often wire TXD→TXD. The correct cross-connection is **PC's TXD (Pin 3) → 8051's RXD (P3.0)** and **PC's RXD (Pin 2) → 8051's TXD (P3.1)**. This is called a **NULL MODEM** configuration. ### 2.2 The 8051 UART — Internal Architecture The 8051 UART consists of: 1. **SBUF (Serial Buffer Register, address 99H):** Two physically separate 8-bit registers sharing one address — one for *transmit* (write-only) and one for *receive* (read-only). 2. **SCON (Serial Control Register, address 98H):** Configures mode and flags. 3. **PCON (Power Control Register, address 87H):** The **SMOD** bit (bit 7) doubles the baud rate when set to 1. 4. **Timer 1:** Used as the baud-rate generator (must be configured in **Mode 2 — 8-bit auto-reload**). ### 2.3 SCON Register (98H) — Bit-by-Bit Breakdown | Bit | Name | Function | |---|---|---| | 7 | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 | | 6 | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 | | 5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor communication enable (kept 0 for Mode 1) | | 4 | **REN** | Receive Enable (must be 1 to receive) | | 3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit (used in Mode 2/3) | | 2 | RB8 | 9th received bit (used in Mode 2/3) | | 1 | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag (set by HW, cleared by SW) | | 0 | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag (set by HW, cleared by SW) | **Mode Selection Table:** | SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate Source | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed (Fosc/12) | | 0 | 1 | **1** | **8-bit UART (most common)** | **Timer 1 Overflow** | | 1 | 0 | 2 | 9-bit UART | Fixed (Fosc/32 or /64) | | 1 | 1 | 3 | 9-bit UART | Timer 1 Overflow | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **KTU-board-essential** and must be memorized. | # | Formula / Constant | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \text{Timer 1 Overflow Rate}$ | Mode 1 master formula | bps | | 2 | $\text{Timer 1 Overflow Rate} = \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | For Timer 1 in Mode 2 | Hz | | 3 | $\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | **Combined Master Formula** | bps | | 4 | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}} \times 2^{\text{SMOD}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$ | **TH1 Reload Value** (decimal) | — | | 5 | $F_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ | **Magic Crystal** giving 0% baud error | Hz | | 6 | $\text{Frame Size (Mode 1)} = 1 \text{ Start} + 8 \text{ Data} + 1 \text{ Stop} = 10 \text{ bits}$ | Total bits per frame | bits | | 7 | RS-232 Logic 0 = **+12 V** (SPACE) | Driver output | V | | 8 | RS-232 Logic 1 = **−12 V** (MARK) | Driver output | V | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 11.0592 MHz Crystal?** This frequency is chosen because when divided by 12 (machine cycle) and further divided for baud rate generation, it yields *integer* reload values for TH1 — giving **0% error** for all standard baud rates up to 57600. Using a 12 MHz crystal produces non-integer divisions → communication errors. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Legacy PC COM Ports:** Industrial equipment, CNC machines, GPS modules, GSM modems still use RS-232. - **Embedded Debugging:** Early 8051 development relied entirely on RS-232 for printf-style debugging. - **Sensor Interfacing:** Many sensors (e.g., older GPS, RFID readers) output RS-232 directly. - **Foundation for Modern Protocols:** Understanding RS-232 voltage-level translation is the *gateway* to RS-485, UART-based Bluetooth (HC-05), and USB-UART bridges (CP2102, FT232RL). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: TH1 Value for 9600 Baud @ 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0 **Given:** - $F_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$ - Target Baud Rate $= 9600$ bps - SMOD $= 0$ (standard configuration) **Goal:** Find TH1 (decimal) such that the 8051 UART generates exactly 9600 bps. --- **Step 1 — Start with the combined master formula:** $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ *Logic:* This is the combined formula obtained by substituting the Timer 1 overflow rate into the Mode 1 baud rate equation. --- **Step 2 — Substitute the known values:** $$ 9600 = \frac{2^{0} \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ *Logic:* $2^0 = 1$, so the SMOD bit is effectively "off" (no doubling). --- **Step 3 — Simplify the numerator:** $$ 9600 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ *Logic:* Direct substitution of known constants. --- **Step 4 — Cross-multiply to isolate $(256 - TH1)$:** $$ 9600 \times 384 \times (256 - TH1) = 11{,}059{,}200 $$ *Logic:* Multiply both sides by $384 \times (256 - TH1)$. --- **Step 5 — Calculate the left-hand product:** $$ 3{,}686{,}400 \times (256 - TH1) = 11{,}059{,}200 $$ *Logic:* $9600 \times 384 = 3{,}686{,}400$. --- **Step 6 — Divide both sides by 3,686,400:** $$ (256 - TH1) = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} $$ --- **Step 7 — Perform the division:** $$ (256 - TH1) = 3 $$ *Logic:* $11{,}059{,}200 \div 3{,}686{,}400 = 3$ **exactly**, confirming the 0% error property of 11.0592 MHz crystal. --- **Step 8 — Solve for TH1:** $$ TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 $$ --- **Step 9 — Convert to hexadecimal (assembly convention):** $$ 253_{10} = \text{FD}_{16} $$ --- **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{TH1 = \text{FD}_{H} = 253_{10}} $$ **Verification (back-substitution):** $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1152} = 9600 \text{ bps} \quad \checkmark $$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Standard TH1 Values (F_osc = 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0) | Baud Rate (bps) | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | % Error | |---|---|---|---| | 1200 | 232 | **E8** | 0% | | 2400 | 244 | **F4** | 0% | | 4800 | 250 | **FA** | 0% | | **9600** | **253** | **FD** | **0%** | | 19200 | 253 (with SMOD=1) | **FD** | 0% | | 62500 | 252 (with SMOD=1) | **FC** | 0% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Notice that 19200 baud uses the *same* TH1 = 253 as 9600 baud, but with **SMOD = 1** to *double* the baud rate. This is a frequently asked conceptual question. ### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Why ±12 V, Not 0/5 V? The original RS-232 standard (1960s) was designed for **telephone-grade cabling** and noisy industrial environments. The **±12 V swing** provides: - **Wide noise margin** (≥ 5 V noise immunity). - **Immunity to ground potential differences** between DTE and DCE (can be tens of volts). - **Compatibility with electromechanical relays** of that era. Modern ICs (MAX232, MAX3232) use internal **charge-pump capacitors** to generate ±10 V from a single +5 V supply — a marvel of analog design condensed into a 16-pin DIP. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 8051 ↔ PC (RS-232) Connection ```mermaid flowchart LR A[8051 Microcontroller] -->|TXD P3.1
0 to 5V TTL| B[MAX232 IC
Level Shifter] B -->|T1OUT Pin 14
+12V or -12V RS232| C[DB9 Connector
PC COM Port] C -->|RXD Pin 2 of PC
+12V or -12V RS232| B B -->|R1OUT Pin 13
0 to 5V TTL| A A -->|Timer1 Overflow| D[Baud Rate Generator] D -->|9600 bps| A B -->|+5V Supply| E[Charge Pump
4 x 1uF Caps] E -->|+10V and -10V| B ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Left side (8051):** Operates in TTL domain (0–5 V). - **Center (MAX232):** The translator — contains 2 drivers (TTL→RS232) and 2 receivers (RS232→TTL), plus a charge pump. - **Right side (DB9/PC):** RS-232 voltage domain (±12 V). - **Bidirectional arrows** show full-duplex asynchronous flow. ### 4.2 MAX232 Pin Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POWER["Charge Pump Power Section"] VCC[VCC +5V Pin 16] GND[GND Pin 15] C1P[C1+ Pin 1] C1M[C1- Pin 3] C2P[C2+ Pin 4] C2M[C2- Pin 5] VPLUS[V+ Pin 2 approx +10V] VMINUS[V- Pin 6 approx -10V] end subgraph CH1["Channel 1"] T1IN[T1IN Pin 11 TTL Input] T1OUT[T1OUT Pin 14 RS232 Output] R1IN[R1IN Pin 13 RS232 Input] R1OUT[R1OUT Pin 12 TTL Output] end subgraph CH2["Channel 2"] T2IN[T2IN Pin 10] T2OUT[T2OUT Pin 7] R2IN[R2IN Pin 8] R2OUT[R2OUT Pin 9] end VCC --> C1P C1P --> C1M C1M --> VPLUS VCC --> C2P C2P --> C2M C2M --> VMINUS T1IN --> T1OUT R1IN --> R1OUT ``` **Standard Wiring Connections:** | MAX232 Pin | Signal | Connect To | |---|---|---| | 11 (T1IN) | TTL Input | **8051 TXD (P3.1)** | | 14 (T1OUT) | RS-232 Output | **DB9 Pin 3 (PC RXD)** | | 13 (R1IN) | RS-232 Input | **DB9 Pin 2 (PC TXD)** | | 12 (R1OUT) | TTL Output | **8051 RXD (P3.0)** | | 15 (GND) | Ground | Common GND | | 16 (VCC) | +5V | +5V Supply | | 1, 3 / 4, 6 | Capacitors | **Four 1 µF electrolytic** (charge pump) | ### 4.3 8051 Serial Data Frame (Mode 1) — Timing Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Idle as Idle Line participant Start as Start Bit participant D0 as D0 participant D1 as D1 participant D2 as D2 participant D3 as D3 participant D4 as D4 participant D5 as D5 participant D6 as D6 participant D7 as D7 participant Stop as Stop Bit Idle->>Start: 1 to 0 transition Start->>D0: LSB first D0->>D1: 1 bit time D1->>D2: 1 bit time D2->>D3: 1 bit time D3->>D4: 1 bit time D4->>D5: 1 bit time D5->>D6: 1 bit time D6->>D7: 1 bit time D7->>Stop: MSB last Stop->>Idle: Returns to 1 ``` *Frame structure:* **1 Start bit (0) + 8 Data bits (LSB first) + 1 Stop bit (1) = 10 bits per frame**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Explain the role of the MAX232 IC in interfacing the 8051 with an RS-232 device. Why is it necessary?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The MAX232 is a **dual-driver/receiver IC** that performs **bidirectional voltage-level translation** between the 8051's TTL levels (0 V to +5 V) and the RS-232 standard levels (+3 V to +25 V for logic 0, −3 V to −25 V for logic 1). It contains an internal **charge pump** (using four external 1 µF capacitors) to generate **+10 V and −10 V** from a single +5 V supply. **Why necessary? (1 mark)** The 8051's UART pins (P3.0/RXD, P3.1/TXD) operate at TTL levels. Directly connecting them to a PC's RS-232 port (which swings ±12 V) would **damage the 8051** (absolute maximum input is VCC + 0.5 V) and would not produce valid RS-232 signal levels for the PC. Hence, MAX232 acts as a **protective and signal-conditioning interface**. **Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Internal mechanism (1) + Necessity (1) = **3 marks**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]** *List the signals present on pins 2, 3, and 5 of a DB-9 RS-232 connector. State the direction of data flow for each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Pin | Signal Name | Function | Direction (DTE = PC) | |---|---|---|---| | 2 | **RXD** | Received Data | **Input to DTE (from DCE)** | | 3 | **TXD** | Transmitted Data | **Output from DTE (to DCE)** | | 5 | **GND** | Signal Ground | Common reference | **Mark distribution:** 3 pins × 1 mark each = **3 marks**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 3, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how the 8051 is interfaced to a PC's RS-232 COM port. Discuss the role of the MAX232 IC and the DB-9 connector signals. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH1** of Timer 1 (operating in Mode 2) to generate a baud rate of **4800 bps** with a crystal frequency of **11.0592 MHz** and **SMOD = 0**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):** **Diagram (3 marks):** ``` 8051 MAX232 PC COM Port +-------+ P3.1 TXD ----> T1IN T1OUT ----> Pin3 RXD | | (0/5V TTL) (11)----(14) (+/-12V) | UART | | | P3.0 RXD <---- R1OUT R1IN <---- Pin2 TXD +-------+ (0/5V TTL) (12)----(13) (+/-12V) | | Timer1 +5V Supply (Baud) + 4 x 1uF caps Generator (charge pump) ``` **Explanation (4 marks):** - **P3.1 (TXD)** of 8051 carries TTL-level serial data into **T1IN (Pin 11)** of MAX232, which converts it to RS-232 levels at **T1OUT (Pin 14)**, connected to **DB-9 Pin 3 (PC's RXD)**. - **DB-9 Pin 2 (PC's TXD)** carries RS-232-level data into **R1IN (Pin 13)** of MAX232, which converts it to TTL at **R1OUT (Pin 12)**, fed to **P3.0 (RXD)** of 8051. - **GND (DB-9 Pin 5)** is connected to common ground. - The charge pump (4 capacitors) generates ±10 V internally. - **REN** bit in SCON must be set to 1 to enable reception. --- **(b) TH1 Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** F_osc = 11,059,200 Hz, Baud Rate = 4800, SMOD = 0 **Step 1 — Master Formula:** $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$ 4800 = \frac{2^{0} \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times (256 - TH1)} = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ **Step 3 — Cross-multiply:** $$ 384 \times 4800 \times (256 - TH1) = 11{,}059{,}200 $$ **Step 4 — Compute left product:** $$ 1{,}843{,}200 \times (256 - TH1) = 11{,}059{,}200 $$ **Step 5 — Divide:** $$ (256 - TH1) = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1{,}843{,}200} = 6 $$ **Step 6 — Solve:** $$ TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250 $$ **Step 7 — Convert to Hex:** $$ 250_{10} = \text{FA}_{16} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{TH1 = 250_{10} = \text{FA}_{H}} $$ **Verification:** $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 6} = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{2304} = 4800 \text{ bps} \quad \checkmark $$ **Mark distribution:** Formula (1) + Substitution (1) + Cross-multiplication (1) + Division (1) + TH1 result (1) + Hex conversion (1) + Verification (1) = **7 marks**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the SCON register of the 8051 microcontroller. List the four serial modes with their corresponding baud-rate sources. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to transmit the byte **"K" (ASCII 0x4B)** serially at **9600 baud** continuously. Assume F_osc = 11.0592 MHz. Show the initialization steps. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) SCON Register and Modes (7 marks):** **SCON Register (98H) — bit-addressable (3 marks):** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **SM0** | **SM1** | SM2 | **REN** | TB8 | RB8 | **TI** | **RI** | - **SM0, SM1:** Mode select bits. - **SM2:** Multiprocessor communication enable (used in Modes 2 & 3). - **REN:** Receive Enable (1 = enable serial reception). - **TB8, RB8:** 9th transmitted/received bit (Modes 2 & 3 only). - **TI:** Transmit Interrupt flag — set by hardware, must be cleared by software. - **RI:** Receive Interrupt flag — set by hardware, must be cleared by software. **Four Serial Modes (4 marks):** | Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Baud Rate Source | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed = F_osc / 12 | | 1 | 0 | 1 | 8-bit UART (most common) | Variable = Timer 1 Overflow | | 2 | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART | Fixed = F_osc / 32 (SMOD=0) or /16 (SMOD=1) | | 3 | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART | Variable = Timer 1 Overflow | --- **(b) Assembly Program for 9600 Baud Continuous Transmission (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Timer 1 Configuration (2 marks):** For 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0: - TH1 = 0xFD (253 decimal) - Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) **Step 2 — SCON Configuration (1 mark):** - Mode 1 → SM0=0, SM1=1 **Step 3 — Program Listing (4 marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload) MOV TH1, #0FDH ; Reload value for 9600 baud MOV SCON, #50H ; Mode 1, REN=1 (SM0=0,SM1=1,REN=1) SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 AGAIN: MOV SBUF, #'K' ; Load 'K' (0x4B) into serial buffer HERE: JNB TI, HERE ; Wait until transmission complete CLR TI ; Clear TI flag (MUST be done by SW) SJMP AGAIN ; Transmit 'K' continuously END ``` **Explanation of key instructions (as required by KTU valuators):** - `MOV SCON, #50H` → Binary 0101 0000 → SM0=0, SM1=1 (Mode 1), REN=1, TI=RI=0. - `MOV SBUF, #'K'` → Writing to SBUF starts transmission automatically. - The line `JNB TI, HERE` polls the TI flag, which is set by hardware when transmission finishes. - `CLR TI` is **mandatory** — TI is *not* cleared automatically (this is the most common error). **Mark distribution:** Timer config (2) + SCON config (1) + Program listing (3) + TI clearing logic (1) = **7 marks**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI flags:** These are set by hardware but *never* cleared automatically. If you forget `CLR TI` in a loop, the program *hangs* at `JNB TI`. (Lose up to 2 marks) > 2. **Confusing SBUF read/write:** Reading SBUF accesses the *receive* buffer; writing SBUF loads the *transmit* buffer. Same address (99H), two different physical registers. > 3. **Wrong TXD/RXD cross-connection:** Forgetting the null-modem cross (TXD→RXD, RXD→TXD) is a very common hardware mistake. (Lose up to 2 marks in hardware-based questions) > 4. **Using 12 MHz crystal in exam without checking baud error:** This produces non-integer TH1 values → KTU examiners specifically check this awareness. > 5. **Forgetting to set the SMOD bit explicitly:** SMOD is in the **PCON** register (87H), not SCON. To set SMOD=1, use `MOV PCON, #80H`. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam** - ⭐ **RS-232 voltages:** Logic 0 = +12 V (SPACE), Logic 1 = −12 V (MARK). 8051 uses **0 V and +5 V**. - ⭐ **Level translator:** MAX232 (or MAX3232 for 3.3 V systems) is mandatory. Uses internal charge pump with **4 × 1 µF** caps. - ⭐ **DB-9 essentials:** Pin 2 = RXD (input to DTE), Pin 3 = TXD (output from DTE), Pin 5 = GND. - ⭐ **8051 pins:** P3.0 = RXD, P3.1 = TXD. Cross-connect TXD↔RXD between DTE and DCE. - ⭐ **Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1):** 8-bit UART, variable baud rate from Timer 1. **Most commonly used mode.** - ⭐ **Frame = 10 bits:** 1 Start + 8 Data (LSB first) + 1 Stop. No parity in Mode 1. - ⭐ **Master formula:** Baud Rate = $\frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$ - ⭐ **TH1 = 256 − (F_osc × 2^SMOD) / (384 × Baud Rate)** - ⭐ **Magic crystal = 11.0592 MHz** → gives **0% error** for all standard baud rates. - ⭐ **TH1 values (11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0):** 1200→E8H, 2400→F4H, 4800→FAH, **9600→FDH**. - ⭐ **Timer 1 must be in Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) with `SETB TR1` to start. - ⭐ **REN = 1** in SCON to enable reception. **TI/RI must be cleared by software.** - ⭐ **SMOD bit lives in PCON (87H)**, not SCON. `MOV PCON, #80H` sets SMOD=1 → doubles baud rate. - ⭐ **SBUF (99H)** is *two registers* sharing one address: write=transmit, read=receive. - ⭐ **SBUF writing triggers transmission automatically**; SBUF reading retrieves the most recently received byte.

topics.title: Serial Port Programming in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Serial Port Programming in 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Serial Port Programming in 8051** refers to the systematic process of configuring, initializing, transmitting, and receiving data through the **on-chip Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter (UART)** interface of the Intel 8051 microcontroller family. The 8051 contains a dedicated, full-duplex, hardware-implemented serial communication port that allows byte-oriented data exchange with external peripheral devices such as PCs, sensors, GSM modules, and other microcontrollers using standardized protocols like **RS-232**.

The serial port in 8051 is governed by three primary Special Function Registers (SFRs):

1. **SCON (Serial Control Register, Address 0x98):** Configures the mode of operation, enables reception, and holds the 9th data bit.
2. **SBUF (Serial Data Buffer, Address 0x99):** A dual-register (one for transmit, one for receive) used to hold serial data.
3. **PCON (Power Control Register, Address 0x87):** Contains the **SMOD** bit which doubles the baud rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 serial port supports **4 modes of operation (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)**. However, in industrial and academic applications, **Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud rate)** is the most commonly used and is the primary focus of KTU examination questions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the 8051 microcontroller as a **post office clerk** who speaks very fast but can only deliver mail one character at a time through a narrow pipe (a single wire). However, the receiver on the other end (say, a PC) is very fast too but expects a strict, agreed-upon rhythm.

- **Without Serial Programming:** Both ends talk at random speeds → complete chaos, garbled messages.
- **With Serial Programming:** You set up a "contract" — an agreement on:
  - **How many bits per packet** (typically 8 or 9 bits of data).
  - **How fast to send** (the **baud rate**, measured in bits per second).
  - **When to start and stop** (start bit and stop bit framing).
  - **When to acknowledge** (the TI and RI flags act like "receipt confirmations").

The **Timer 1** acts like a metronome keeping both the sender and receiver in perfect rhythm, while the **SBUF** is the actual mailbox where letters (data bytes) are dropped in or picked up from.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants Used in 8051 Serial Communication:**
> - **Standard Crystal Frequency:** **11.0592 MHz** (chosen because it is an exact multiple of standard baud rates, producing 0% error).
> - **Most Common Baud Rate:** **9600 bps** (followed by 4800, 2400, 1200, 19200).
> - **Default Frame Format (Mode 1):** **1 Start bit + 8 Data bits + 1 Stop bit (10 bits total)**.
> - **SMOD Bit Location:** **PCON.7** (Doubled baud rate when set to 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SCON Register — The Master Control Switch

The **SCON register (0x98)** is bit-addressable and is the central configuration register for the serial port. Each bit plays a distinct role:

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:----|:-----|:---------|
| 7 | **SM0** | Serial Mode selector bit 0 |
| 6 | **SM1** | Serial Mode selector bit 1 |
| 5 | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (used in Modes 2 & 3) |
| 4 | **REN** | **Receive Enable** — must be set to 1 to allow reception |
| 3 | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2 & 3) |
| 2 | **RB8** | 9th received bit (Modes 2 & 3) |
| 1 | **TI** | **Transmit Interrupt Flag** — set by hardware at end of transmission |
| 0 | **RI** | **Receive Interrupt Flag** — set by hardware at end of reception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode Selection Table (SM0, SM1):**
> - **Mode 0:** SM0=0, SM1=0 → 8-bit Shift Register, Fixed Baud = Fosc/12
> - **Mode 1:** SM0=0, SM1=1 → **8-bit UART, Variable Baud (Timer 1)** ← Most Common
> - **Mode 2:** SM0=1, SM1=0 → 9-bit UART, Fixed Baud = Fosc/32 or Fosc/64
> - **Mode 3:** SM0=1, SM1=1 → 9-bit UART, Variable Baud (Timer 1)

## 2.2 The SBUF Register — The Data Mailbox

The **SBUF register (0x99)** is a unique SFR that physically consists of **two separate 8-bit registers** sharing a single address:
- **Writing** to SBUF loads the **Transmit Buffer**.
- **Reading** from SBUF accesses the **Receive Buffer**.

This **double-buffered architecture** allows a byte to be received and held in the receive buffer while another byte is being transmitted, enabling true **full-duplex** communication.

## 2.3 The PCON Register — The Speed Doubler

The **PCON register (0x87)** is not bit-addressable. The most significant bit, **SMOD (PCON.7)**, directly affects the serial port baud rate. When **SMOD = 1**, the baud rate generated by Timer 1 is **doubled**.

> [!NOTE]
> Setting PCON cannot be done bit-by-bit (since it's not bit-addressable). The common KTU-approved idiom is:
> ```c
> PCON = PCON | 0x80;  // Set SMOD bit to double the baud rate
> ```

## 2.4 Timer 1 as the Baud Rate Generator

In **Mode 1** and **Mode 3**, **Timer 1** is repurposed as the baud rate clock generator. The timer must be configured in:
- **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** — the **most common** configuration.
- The auto-reload value is held in **TH1**.

The overflow frequency of Timer 1 is fed into the serial port, and the baud rate is derived from it.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Notes |
|:----------|:----------------|:------|
| **Baud Rate (Mode 1/3, Timer 1)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | General formula |
| **TH1 Reload Value (SMOD=0)** | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ | No doubling |
| **TH1 Reload Value (SMOD=1)** | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times \text{Baud}}$ | Doubled baud |
| **Baud Rate (Mode 2)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{64} \times F_{\text{osc}}$ | Fixed baud, no timer |
| **Baud Rate (Mode 0)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | Fixed baud, shift register mode |
| **Frame Format (Mode 1)** | 1 Start + 8 Data + 1 Stop = **10 bits** | Standard UART |
| **Timer 1 Mode** | **Mode 2** (8-bit Auto-Reload) | Always use this for baud gen |
| **Timer 1 Run Control** | **TR1 = 1** | Starts the baud rate clock |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz Crystal?**
> For 9600 baud with SMOD=0: TH1 = 256 − (11059200 / (384 × 9600)) = 256 − 3 = **253 = 0xFD** (exact integer). At 12 MHz, you get TH1 = 252.75 → **0.16% error** causing communication failures. This is a **favourite KTU question**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Serial port programming is the backbone of countless embedded systems:
- **PC-to-Microcontroller Debugging:** Logging sensor values, debug strings.
- **GPS Modules:** NMEA protocol data reception at 4800/9600 baud.
- **GSM/IoT Modules:** AT command-based communication.
- **Industrial Automation:** MODBUS RTU protocol.
- **Bluetooth Modules (HC-05):** Wireless serial bridging.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the TH1 Reload Value Formula

The 8051 Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** increments on every machine cycle. The 8051's oscillator frequency $F_{\text{osc}}$ is divided by **12** to produce the machine cycle frequency. Therefore, Timer 1 increments at a rate of $\frac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ ticks per second.

If **TH1** is the reload value, the timer overflows every $(256 - \text{TH1})$ ticks. Thus, the **overflow frequency** is:

$$
f_{\text{overflow}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

The serial port divides this overflow frequency by **32** (in Modes 1 and 3) to generate the baud rate. The SMOD bit provides an additional division by 2 when set to 0, effectively multiplying the baud rate by 2 when SMOD=1:

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times f_{\text{overflow}}
$$

Substituting the overflow frequency:

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

Rearranging to solve for TH1:

$$
(256 - \text{TH1}) = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times F_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

$$
256 - \text{TH1} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}}
$$

When **SMOD = 0**, the numerator reduces to $F_{\text{osc}}$, giving the simpler form:

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

When **SMOD = 1**, the numerator becomes $2 \times F_{\text{osc}}$:

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{2 \times F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}} = 256 - \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Standard 9600 Baud Calculation (KTU Favourite)

**Problem:** Calculate the TH1 value for 9600 baud serial communication using an 11.0592 MHz crystal with SMOD = 0.

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- $F_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{Baud Rate} = 9600$
- $\text{SMOD} = 0$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator.

$$
384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400
$$

**Step 4:** Perform the division.

$$
\dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3
$$

**Step 5:** Compute TH1.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to hexadecimal.

$$
253_{10} = \text{FD}_{16}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{TH1} = 0\text{xFD} = 253$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: 2400 Baud with SMOD=1

**Problem:** Calculate TH1 for 2400 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=1.

**Step 1:** Apply the SMOD=1 formula.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{192 \times 2400}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute denominator.

$$
192 \times 2400 = 460{,}800
$$

**Step 3:** Division.

$$
\dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{460{,}800} = 24
$$

**Step 4:** Final value.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 24 = 232 = 0\text{xE8}
$$

## 3.4 Standard TH1 Value Reference Matrix

| Baud Rate | SMOD | Fosc = 11.0592 MHz | Fosc = 12 MHz (with error) |
|:----------|:-----|:-------------------|:--------------------------|
| 1200 | 0 | TH1 = 0xE8 (232) | TH1 = 0xF3 (243) — 0.16% error |
| 2400 | 0 | TH1 = 0xF4 (244) | TH1 = 0xF9 (249) — 0.16% error |
| 4800 | 0 | TH1 = 0xFA (250) | TH1 = 0xFD (253) — 0.16% error |
| 9600 | 0 | TH1 = 0xFD (253) | TH1 = 0xFE (254) — 0.16% error |
| 19200 | 0 | TH1 = 0xFE (254) | Not feasible with 12 MHz |
| 9600 | 1 | TH1 = 0xFE (254) | — |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step 8051 Serial Port Initialization Procedure

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know the **exact sequence** of register configurations. The standard 8051 C code for serial port initialization at 9600 baud with 11.0592 MHz crystal is:

| Step | Register | Operation | Purpose |
|:-----|:---------|:----------|:--------|
| 1 | **TMOD** | `TMOD = 0x20` | Configure Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) |
| 2 | **TH1** | `TH1 = 0xFD` | Load reload value for **9600 baud** |
| 3 | **TR1** | `TR1 = 1` | **Start Timer 1** — begin baud rate generation |
| 4 | **SCON** | `SCON = 0x50` | Set **Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1)**, **REN=1** |
| 5 | **TI/RI** | `TI = 0; RI = 0` | **Clear flags** to ready the system |

> [!NOTE]
> **SCON = 0x50 breakdown:**
> - 0x50 = 0101 0000 in binary
> - SM0 = 0, SM1 = 1 (Mode 1)
> - REN = 1 (Receive enabled)
> - All other bits = 0 (no 9th bit, no multiprocessor mode)

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Transmission and Reception Logic

**Transmitting a Single Byte:**

| Step | Action | Code |
|:-----|:-------|:-----|
| 1 | Load data into SBUF | `SBUF = data;` |
| 2 | Hardware transmits bit-by-bit | (Automatic) |
| 3 | Hardware sets **TI = 1** at end of last stop bit | (Automatic) |
| 4 | Software clears TI (must be done by programmer) | `while(TI == 0); TI = 0;` |

**Receiving a Single Byte:**

| Step | Action | Code |
|:-----|:-------|:-----|
| 1 | Hardware sets **RI = 1** after stop bit received | (Automatic) |
| 2 | Software waits for RI flag | `while(RI == 0);` |
| 3 | Read data from SBUF | `data = SBUF;` |
| 4 | Software clears RI | `RI = 0;` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule:** Both **TI** and **RI** must be **cleared by software**. The 8051 hardware only **sets** them. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8051 Serial Port

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete internal architecture and data flow of the 8051 serial communication subsystem, showing how SBUF, SCON, Timer 1, and the external pins interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Core of 8051] -->|Write Data| B[Transmit SBUF Buffer]
    A -->|Read Data| C[Receive SBUF Buffer]
    D[SCON Register 0x98] -->|Mode Control SM0 SM1| E[Mode Selector MUX]
    D -->|REN Bit| F[Receiver Enable Gate]
    D -->|TI RI Flags| A
    G[PCON Register 0x87] -->|SMOD Bit| H[Baud Rate Doubler]
    I[Timer 1 Mode 2] -->|Overflow Pulse| J[Baud Rate Generator Divider 32]
    H --> J
    J -->|Baud Clock| E
    E -->|Bit Stream Out| B
    E -->|Bit Stream In| C
    F --> C
    B -->|Serial Data Out| K[TXD Pin P3.1]
    C -->|Serial Data In| L[RXD Pin P3.0]
    K -->|To External Device PC GSM GPS| M[External UART Device]
    M -->|Return Data| L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Transmission and Reception Flow

This sequence diagram shows the **temporal interaction** between the CPU, SBUF, and the external device during a single byte transmission.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant SBUFTX as Transmit SBUF
    participant SHIFT as Shift Register
    participant TXPIN as TXD Pin P3.1
    participant RXPIN as RXD Pin P3.0
    participant SBUFRX as Receive SBUF
    participant EXT as External Device PC

    Note over CPU: Initialization Complete SCON=0x50 TH1=0xFD TR1=1
    CPU->>SBUFTX: Write 'A' to SBUF
    SBUFTX->>SHIFT: Load byte into shift register
    SHIFT->>TXPIN: Send Start Bit 0
    SHIFT->>TXPIN: Send 8 Data Bits LSB to MSB
    SHIFT->>TXPIN: Send Stop Bit 1
    SHIFT->>CPU: Hardware sets TI = 1
    CPU->>CPU: Software clears TI = 0
    TXPIN->>EXT: Physical signal via wire

    EXT->>RXPIN: Return byte 'B' with start stop bits
    RXPIN->>SBUFRX: Hardware loads incoming byte
    SBUFRX->>CPU: Hardware sets RI = 1
    CPU->>SBUFRX: Read SBUF to get 'B'
    CPU->>CPU: Software clears RI = 0
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Serial Port Configuration States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> Uninitialized: Power ON
    Uninitialized --> Mode0Config: SM0=0 SM1=0
    Uninitialized --> Mode1Config: SM0=0 SM1=1
    Uninitialized --> Mode2Config: SM0=1 SM1=0
    Uninitialized --> Mode3Config: SM0=1 SM1=1
    Mode0Config --> ShiftRegisterMode: Fixed baud Fosc by 12
    Mode1Config --> UART8Bit: Variable baud Timer 1
    Mode2Config --> UART9Bit: Fixed baud Fosc by 32 or 64
    Mode3Config --> UART9BitVar: Variable baud Timer 1
    UART8Bit --> Transmitting: Write to SBUF
    UART8Bit --> Receiving: REN=1 and RI triggered
    Transmitting --> UART8Bit: TI cleared after transmission
    Receiving --> UART8Bit: RI cleared after reading SBUF
    UART8Bit --> [*]: Power OFF
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Connection Between 8051 and PC via MAX232

The MAX232 is an **essential level-shifting IC** that converts the 8051's **TTL (0V/5V) levels** to **RS-232 (±12V) levels** required by a PC's serial port.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MCU8051[8051 Microcontroller]
        TX1[TXD P3.1] --> SB1[SBUF Logic]
        RX1[RXD P3.0] --> SB1
    end
    SB1 --> TX1
    TX1 -->|TTL 0V to 5V| MAX[Level Shifter MAX232 IC]
    RX1 <--|TTL 0V to 5V| MAX
    MAX -->|RS232 minus 12V to plus 12V| DB9[DB9 Connector on PC]
    DB9 --> PCCOM[PC COM Port Hyperterminal]
    PCCOM --> DB9
    MAX <--|RS232 levels| DB9
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the SBUF register and explain its dual-buffer nature.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **SBUF (Serial Data Buffer) register** is an 8-bit Special Function Register located at internal address **0x99** in the 8051. It is unique because it is physically implemented as **two separate 8-bit registers** that share the same memory address.

- **Write operation** to SBUF (e.g., `SBUF = 'A';`) loads data into the **Transmit Buffer**, which is then shifted out serially through the TXD pin.
- **Read operation** from SBUF (e.g., `data = SBUF;`) accesses the **Receive Buffer**, which contains the most recently received byte from the RXD pin.

This **double-buffered architecture** allows the 8051 to perform **full-duplex communication** — receiving one byte while simultaneously transmitting another — without data corruption.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the REN bit in the SCON register?
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **REN (Receiver Enable) bit** is **bit 4** of the SCON register (0x98). It is used to **enable or disable the serial port receiver** in the 8051.
