- When **REN = 1**, the 8051's receiver is **enabled** and incoming serial data on the RXD pin (P3.0) will be accepted, with the RI flag being set upon reception of a complete frame.
- When **REN = 0**, the receiver is **disabled** and any data on RXD is ignored.

This bit must be set to 1 for any reception to occur. It is commonly set during initialization via `SCON = 0x50`, which sets REN=1 along with Mode 1 configuration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A: Serial Port Programming and Baud Rate Calculation

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the 8051 serial port programming steps to transmit the character 'K' continuously to a PC at 9600 baud using an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Provide the full C program.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate TH1 for 9600 baud.**
- Using the formula: $\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0\text{xFD}$

**Step 2: Understand the initialization sequence.**
- **TMOD = 0x20:** Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
- **TH1 = 0xFD:** Reload value for 9600 baud
- **TR1 = 1:** Start Timer 1
- **SCON = 0x50:** Mode 1 (8-bit UART) + REN enabled

**Step 3: Write the full C program.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Serial_Init(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;    // Timer 1 Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1 = 0xFD;     // 9600 baud rate value
    TR1 = 1;        // Start Timer 1
    SCON = 0x50;    // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN enabled
    TI = 0;         // Clear transmit flag
}

void main(void) {
    Serial_Init();
    while(1) {
        SBUF = 'K';         // Load character into transmit buffer
        while(TI == 0);     // Wait until transmission complete
        TI = 0;             // Clear TI flag (must be done by software)
    }
}
```

**Step 4: Explain the transmission flow.**
- The character 'K' (ASCII 0x4B = 01001011) is loaded into SBUF.
- Hardware shifts out: Start bit (0) → 8 data bits LSB first (1,1,0,1,0,0,1,0) → Stop bit (1).
- TI is set by hardware; software must clear it.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With an 8051 system running at 11.0592 MHz, calculate the TH1 value needed for a baud rate of 4800 bps when SMOD = 1. Show all steps.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $F_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$
- $\text{Baud Rate} = 4800 \text{ bps}$
- $\text{SMOD} = 1$

**Step 1:** Apply the formula for TH1 when SMOD = 1.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times \text{Baud Rate}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{192 \times 4800}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator.

$$
192 \times 4800 = 921{,}600
$$

**Step 4:** Perform the division.

$$
\dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{921{,}600} = 12
$$

**Step 5:** Compute TH1.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 12 = 244
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to hexadecimal.

$$
244_{10} = \text{F4}_{16}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{TH1} = 0\text{xF4} = 244$

---

### Question B: Serial Port Reception and SCON Register Analysis

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete procedure to receive a byte serially in the 8051 from a PC at 2400 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0. Provide the full C program with comments.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate TH1 for 2400 baud, SMOD=0.**

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 2400} = 256 - 12 = 244 = 0\text{xF4}
$$

**Step 2: Write the complete C program with detailed comments.**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char received_data;

void Serial_Init(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;    // Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1 = 0xF4;     // 2400 baud rate reload value
    TR1 = 1;        // Start Timer 1 to generate baud clock
    SCON = 0x50;    // Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN = 1 (enable receiver)
    RI = 0;         // Clear receive interrupt flag
}

unsigned char Serial_Receive(void) {
    while(RI == 0);        // WAIT for hardware to set RI after stop bit
    RI = 0;                // CLEAR RI flag (MUST be done in software)
    return SBUF;           // READ received byte from receive buffer
}

void main(void) {
    Serial_Init();
    while(1) {
        received_data = Serial_Receive();
        P1 = received_data;  // Display received data on Port 1 LEDs
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Explain the reception flow in detail.**
- After initialization, REN=1 enables the receiver.
- The hardware monitors RXD (P3.0) for a falling edge (start bit).
- The hardware samples the incoming bits at the center of each bit period using the Timer 1 baud clock.
- After 8 data bits and the stop bit are received, the byte is transferred to the receive SBUF, and **RI is set by hardware**.
- The software loop `while(RI == 0)` blocks until RI becomes 1.
- Reading `SBUF` retrieves the data; clearing RI prepares the system for the next byte.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With neat bit-level descriptions, explain the SCON register of 8051. Also list all four serial port modes with their characteristics.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**SCON Register (Address 0x98, Bit-Addressable):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:----|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Name | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** |

**Bit Descriptions:**

- **SM0, SM1 (Bits 7, 6):** Mode selection bits. Together they select one of 4 serial modes.
- **SM2 (Bit 5):** Multiprocessor communication enable. In Modes 2 & 3, RI is activated only if the received 9th bit (RB8) is 1. In Mode 1, RI is activated only if a valid stop bit is received.
- **REN (Bit 4):** Receive Enable. Set by software to enable reception; clear to disable.
- **TB8 (Bit 3):** The 9th transmitted bit in Modes 2 & 3. Often used for parity.
- **RB8 (Bit 2):** The 9th received bit in Modes 2 & 3. In Mode 1, it stores the stop bit.
- **TI (Bit 1):** Transmit Interrupt flag. **Set by hardware** at the end of the 8th bit in Mode 0, or at the beginning of the stop bit in Mode 1. **Must be cleared by software.**
- **RI (Bit 0):** Receive Interrupt flag. **Set by hardware** at the end of reception. **Must be cleared by software.**

**Four Serial Port Modes:**

| Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Baud Rate |
|:-----|:----|:----|:------------|:----------|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed = $F_{\text{osc}}/12$ |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | **8-bit UART** (standard) | Variable, set by Timer 1 |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART | Fixed = $F_{\text{osc}}/32$ or $F_{\text{osc}}/64$ |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART | Variable, set by Timer 1 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI:** Both flags must be cleared **by software**. Examiners specifically look for `TI = 0;` and `RI = 0;` statements. Forgetting these is a **2-mark deduction** in most valuation schemes.
> 2. **Wrong TH1 value:** Using a 12 MHz crystal in the formula for 11.0592 MHz questions, or vice versa, is a fatal error. **Always verify the crystal frequency** mentioned in the question.
> 3. **Incorrect SCON initialization:** Students often write `SCON = 0x40` (REN=0), which disables reception. The correct value for "transmit and receive" is `SCON = 0x50`.
> 4. **Forgetting TR1 = 1:** Without starting Timer 1, there is no baud rate clock and the serial port will not function. This is a classic 1-mark cut.
> 5. **Confusing SBUF read/write:** Reading from SBUF accesses the receive buffer; writing to SBUF loads the transmit buffer. Mixing these up will lose marks.
> 6. **Using Mode 1 formula for Mode 2:** The baud rate formulas are different for each mode. Mode 2 does NOT use Timer 1.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Serial Port Programming in 8051:**
> - ✅ The 8051 has a **full-duplex, on-chip UART** controlled by **SCON, SBUF, and PCON** registers.
> - ✅ **SBUF (0x99)** is a double-buffered register: write = transmit, read = receive.
> - ✅ **SCON (0x98)** is bit-addressable and contains **SM0, SM1, SM2, REN, TB8, RB8, TI, RI**.
> - ✅ **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)** is the most common and is used with **Timer 1 in Mode 2** for baud rate generation.
> - ✅ **Baud Rate Formula:** $\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$
> - ✅ **TH1 Formula:** $\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{F_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$ (when SMOD=0)
> - ✅ The **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred because it gives **integer TH1 values** for standard baud rates (0% error).
> - ✅ **TI and RI flags** are **set by hardware** but **must be cleared by software** — this is the most tested KTU concept.
> - ✅ **SMOD bit (PCON.7)** doubles the baud rate when set to 1.
> - ✅ The standard initialization sequence is: **TMOD → TH1 → TR1 → SCON → Clear TI/RI**.
> - ✅ For transmission: write to SBUF, wait for TI, clear TI.
> - ✅ For reception: wait for RI, read SBUF, clear RI.
> - ✅ **MAX232** IC is required to interface 8051 TTL levels (0/5V) with PC RS-232 levels (±12V).
> - ✅ **TXD = P3.1** and **RXD = P3.0** — these pins are shared with Port 3.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 Interrupt Programming in Embedded C: 8051 Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** 8051 Interrupt Programming in Embedded C: 8051 Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **Interrupt** in the 8051 microcontroller is a hardware- or software-triggered asynchronous event that **temporarily halts the main program execution**, forces the CPU to save the current Program Counter (PC) onto the stack, and transfers control to a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR completes, the CPU resumes execution of the main program from the exact point where it was interrupted.

The 8051 architecture provides **six (6) interrupt sources** (5 maskable + 1 non-maskable reset), managed through two Special Function Registers (SFRs):

- **IE** (Interrupt Enable Register) – Address **0xA8**
- **IP** (Interrupt Priority Register) – Address **0xB8**

### 1.2 The Six 8051 Interrupts

| # | Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Flag Bit | Enable Bit | Priority Bit |
|---|-----------|------|----------------|----------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Reset** | – | **0x0000** | – | – | Highest (non-maskable) |
| 2 | **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** | IE0 | **0x0003** | TCON.1 | EX0 (IE.0) | PX0 (IP.0) |
| 3 | **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 | **0x000B** | TCON.5 | ET0 (IE.1) | PT0 (IP.1) |
| 4 | **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** | IE1 | **0x0013** | TCON.3 | EX1 (IE.2) | PX1 (IP.2) |
| 5 | **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 | **0x001B** | TCON.7 | ET1 (IE.3) | PT1 (IP.3) |
| 6 | **Serial Port (TI/RI)** | TI/RI | **0x0023** | SCON.0/1 | ES (IE.4) | PS (IP.4) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector Address:** The fixed ROM address from where the ISR begins. The 8 bytes between consecutive vectors (e.g., 0x0003 to 0x000B) limit the ISR size, requiring `LJMP` instructions for longer routines.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell vs. Waiting by the Door"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Pizza Delivery Analogy 🍕**
> - **Polling Method:** You sit at the door staring outside, checking every 10 seconds "Is the pizza here yet?" — Wastes CPU time, inefficient.
> - **Interrupt Method:** You continue working on your assignment. When the **doorbell rings (interrupt signal)**, you *pause* your work, **save your place (push PC on stack)**, open the door (execute ISR), take the pizza, and **return to your assignment exactly where you left it (RETI instruction)**.
> - **The doorbell button = Interrupt Request (IRQ) pin**
> - **You being willing to answer = Interrupt Enable bit set to 1**
> - **The doorbell being broken = Interrupt disabled (masked)**

### 1.4 Critical Distinction: Polling vs. Interrupt

| Parameter | Polling | Interrupt |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| CPU Utilization | Wasted (busy-wait loop) | **Efficient (CPU free until needed)** |
| Response Time | Deterministic but slow | **Near-instantaneous (3–4 machine cycles)** |
| Code Complexity | Simple loop | Requires ISR + Vector setup |
| Use Case | Single, time-critical, predictable tasks | **Multiple asynchronous real-time events** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — "What Happens When an Interrupt Fires?"

**Step-by-Step Hardware Sequence (the 6-stage interrupt cycle):**

1. **Edge/Level Detection:** External interrupts (INT0, INT1) detect a signal on P3.2 and P3.3 respectively (controlled by TCON bits IT0, IT1).
2. **Flag Setting:** The corresponding interrupt flag (IE0, TF0, IE1, TF1, TI/RI) is set to logic 1 by hardware.
3. **Enable Verification:** The CPU checks the corresponding enable bit in the IE register. If 0 → **masked**, ignored.
4. **Priority Arbitration:** If two interrupts fire simultaneously, the **IP register** decides order (or default natural priority applies).
5. **Vector Fetch:** CPU fetches the ISR address from the Vector Table (e.g., 0x000B for Timer 0).
6. **Context Save & Execute ISR:** Current PC is pushed onto stack, ISR executes.
7. **Return (RETI):** PC is popped from stack, flag is cleared (hardware for external/timer), main program resumes.

### 2.2 The IE Register (Interrupt Enable) — 0xA8

```
  Bit:    IE.7  IE.6  IE.5  IE.4   IE.3   IE.2   IE.1   IE.0
         [ EA   --    --    ES    ET1    EX1    ET0    EX0 ]
Default:  0     0     0     0      0      0      0      0
```

- **EA (IE.7):** Global Enable — must be set to **1** to enable ANY interrupt.
- **ES (IE.4):** Enable Serial Port interrupt.
- **ET1 (IE.3):** Enable Timer 1 overflow interrupt.
- **EX1 (IE.2):** Enable External Interrupt 1.
- **ET0 (IE.1):** Enable Timer 0 overflow interrupt.
- **EX0 (IE.0):** Enable External Interrupt 0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Activation Rule:** An interrupt is serviced ONLY if `EA = 1` AND its individual enable bit = 1.

### 2.3 The IP Register (Interrupt Priority) — 0xB8

```
  Bit:    IP.7  IP.6  IP.5  IP.4   IP.3   IP.2   IP.1   IP.0
         [ --    --    --    PS    PT1    PX1    PT0    PX0 ]
Default:  0     0     0     0      0      0      0      0
```

- Setting any bit to **1** elevates that interrupt to **High Priority**.
- **High priority interrupts can interrupt other high or low priority ISRs** (nested interrupts).
- **Low priority interrupts can ONLY be interrupted by high priority ones.**

### 2.4 TCON Bits Controlling External Interrupts

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| TCON.0 | **IT0** | 0 = INT0 **Level-Triggered (Low)**, 1 = INT0 **Edge-Triggered (Falling)** |
| TCON.1 | **IE0** | External Interrupt 0 flag (set by hardware) |
| TCON.2 | **IT1** | 0 = INT1 **Level-Triggered (Low)**, 1 = INT1 **Edge-Triggered (Falling)** |
| TCON.3 | **IE1** | External Interrupt 1 flag (set by hardware) |
| TCON.4 | **TR0** | Timer 0 Run control (NOT interrupt) |
| TCON.5 | **TF0** | Timer 0 overflow flag |
| TCON.6 | **TR1** | Timer 1 Run control (NOT interrupt) |
| TCON.7 | **TF1** | Timer 1 overflow flag |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Exam Trap:** For **edge-triggered** external interrupts, the flag is **auto-cleared** by hardware when the CPU vectors to the ISR. For **level-triggered** mode, the external source MUST pull the pin low before RETI, otherwise the ISR will re-trigger infinitely.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| Number of Interrupts (8051) | **6** (5 maskable + Reset) | – |
| Default Vector Memory | **0x0000 – 0x0023** | ROM addresses |
| Space per ISR (min) | **8 bytes** | Between vectors |
| Interrupt Response Time | **3 to 8** | Machine cycles |
| Min Pulse Width (Edge Int.) | **1 machine cycle** | Crystal period |
| Min Pulse Width (Level Int.) | **Until RETI executes** | – |
| IE Register Address | **0xA8** | Bit-addressable SFR |
| IP Register Address | **0xB8** | Bit-addressable SFR |
| TCON Address | **0x88** | Bit-addressable SFR |
| INT0 Pin | **P3.2** | Port 3 pin 2 |
| INT1 Pin | **P3.3** | Port 3 pin 3 |
| Stack Pointer Default | **0x07** | Increments from 0x08 |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** ABS sensor triggers external interrupt to instantly apply brakes without CPU polling.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Encoder pulses trigger Timer interrupts for precise motor control.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** UART RX interrupt wakes CPU only when data arrives, conserving power.
- **Medical Devices:** Heart-rate sensor edge-triggered interrupt ensures sub-millisecond response.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Interrupt Programming Workflow

| Step | Action | Embedded C Syntax | Purpose |
|------|--------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Choose Interrupt Source | `#include <reg51.h>` | SFR definitions |
| 2 | Set Trigger Mode | `IT0 = 1;` (edge) / `IT0 = 0;` (level) | Configure TCON |
| 3 | Enable Individual Source | `EX0 = 1;` | Set IE bit |
| 4 | Enable Global Flag | `EA = 1;` | Master switch |
| 5 | Write ISR | `void ext0_isr(void) interrupt 0` | Handler code |
| 6 | Return from ISR | Compiler auto-emits `RETI` | Restore context |

### 3.2 Interrupt Numbers for `interrupt` Keyword (Keil C51)

The Keil compiler uses **sequential interrupt numbers** (not vector addresses):

$$
\text{Interrupt Number} = \frac{\text{Vector Address} - 3}{8}
$$

**Detailed Mapping:**

$$
\text{Reset} \rightarrow 0
$$

$$
\text{EXT0} \rightarrow 1 \quad (0x0003)
$$

$$
\text{T0} \rightarrow 2 \quad (0x000B)
$$

$$
\text{EXT1} \rightarrow 3 \quad (0x0013)
$$

$$
\text{T1} \rightarrow 4 \quad (0x001B)
$$

$$
\text{Serial} \rightarrow 5 \quad (0x0023)
$$

### 3.3 Derivations — Worked Numerical Problems

#### **Problem 1: Calculate Timer 0 Vector Address**

Given Timer 0 is the 3rd interrupt in the 8051 natural priority order.

**Step 1:** Identify Timer 0 position in vector table.

$$
\text{Vector}_{\text{T0}} = 0x0003 + (2 \times 0x0008)
$$

**Step 2:** Compute hex increment.

$$
0x0003 + 0x0010 = 0x0013
$$

> ❌ **Error!** That gives EXT1. Let us recount vector jumps correctly.

**Step 1 (Corrected):** Timer 0 is the 2nd vector after Reset.

$$
\text{Vector}_{\text{T0}} = 0x0003 + (1 \times 0x0008)
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
0x0003 + 0x0008 = 0x000B
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Vector}_{\text{T0}} = 0x000B}
$$

> **Verification:** Memory range 0x000B–0x0012 (8 bytes) belongs to Timer 0 ISR. ✓

#### **Problem 2: Interrupt Response Time Calculation**

8051 with **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate the time taken for the CPU to vector to an ISR after the flag is set.

**Step 1:** Machine cycle frequency.

$$
f_{\text{machine}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \text{ MHz}}{12}
$$

$$
f_{\text{machine}} = 0.9216 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2:** Machine cycle period.

$$
T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{machine}}} = \frac{1}{0.9216 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{\text{machine}} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3:** Worst-case response (8 machine cycles).

$$
t_{\text{response}} = 8 \times T_{\text{machine}} = 8 \times 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{response}} = 8.68 \, \mu s}
$$

#### **Problem 3: Nested Interrupt Feasibility**

System has Timer 0 (low priority) and External 1 (high priority). Timer 0 ISR is executing. Can External 1 interrupt it?

**Step 1:** Check IP register configuration.

$$
P \times 1 = 1 \quad (\text{EX1 is high priority}), \quad PT0 = 0 \quad (\text{Timer 0 is low priority})
$$

**Step 2:** Apply priority rule.

$$
\text{High priority ISR can interrupt Low priority ISR} \rightarrow \text{TRUE}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Yes, External 1 will preempt Timer 0 ISR.}}
$$

#### **Problem 4: Edge-Triggered External Interrupt Programming Flow**

**Problem:** Configure INT0 to fire on falling edge and toggle P1.0 each time the button is pressed.

**Embedded C Model Solution:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;        // LED at P1.0

void ext0_isr(void) interrupt 0   // Vector 0x0003, INT0
{
    LED = ~LED;         // Toggle LED
}

void main(void)
{
    IT0 = 1;            // Configure INT0 for falling-edge trigger
    EX0 = 1;            // Enable External Interrupt 0
    EA  = 1;            // Enable Global Interrupt

    while(1)
    {
        // Main program does other tasks
        // CPU remains free until INT0 fires
    }
}
```

**Detailed Explanation of Each Line:**

- `IT0 = 1;` → Sets TCON.0 = 1, meaning falling-edge trigger.
- `EX0 = 1;` → Sets IE.0 = 1, enabling INT0.
- `EA = 1;` → Sets IE.7 = 1, master enable.
- `interrupt 0` → Keil C51 keyword, places function at vector 0x0003 and generates RETI.

#### **Problem 5: Serial Port Interrupt Programming**

**Problem:** Receive bytes via UART and echo them back. Use interrupt-driven (not polling) method.

**Embedded C Model Solution:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char rx_byte;

void serial_isr(void) interrupt 4
{
    if(RI == 1)              // Check Receive Interrupt flag
    {
        rx_byte = SBUF;      // Read received byte
        SBUF = rx_byte;      // Transmit back (echo)
        RI = 0;              // Clear RI flag manually
    }
    // TI is cleared after checking
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x20;             // Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFD;             // 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
    SCON = 0x50;             // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN enabled
    TR1  = 1;                // Start Timer 1
    ES   = 1;                // Enable Serial Interrupt
    EA   = 1;                // Global enable

    while(1)
    {
        // CPU free, all reception via interrupt
    }
}
```

**Critical Note on Serial Interrupts:**

> [!WARNING]
> Unlike external/timer interrupts, the **Serial Port interrupt (vector 0x0023)** is shared by **two flags**: TI (transmit complete) and RI (receive complete). The ISR MUST check which flag fired and clear it manually.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Interrupt Architecture — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA[External Int 0 P3.2]
    srcB[Timer 0 Overflow]
    srcC[External Int 1 P3.3]
    srcD[Timer 1 Overflow]
    srcE[Serial Port TI or RI]
    srcF[RESET Pin]

    flagReg[TCON Register 0x88]
    flagReg2[SCON Register 0x98]

    enReg[IE Register 0xA8]
    priReg[IP Register 0xB8]

    arb[Priority and Enable Logic]
    cpu[CPU Core]

    isr0[ISR at 0x0003]
    isr1[ISR at 0x000B]
    isr2[ISR at 0x0013]
    isr3[ISR at 0x001B]
    isr4[ISR at 0x0023]
    isrR[Reset Vector 0x0000]

    srcA --> flagReg
    srcB --> flagReg
    srcC --> flagReg
    srcD --> flagReg
    srcE --> flagReg2

    flagReg --> arb
    flagReg2 --> arb
    enReg --> arb
    priReg --> arb

    arb --> cpu

    cpu --> isr0
    cpu --> isr1
    cpu --> isr2
    cpu --> isr3
    cpu --> isr4
    cpu --> isrR

    isr0 --> reti[RETI Return]
    isr1 --> reti
    isr2 --> reti
    isr3 --> reti
    isr4 --> reti
    reti --> cpu
```

### 4.2 Interrupt Vector Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[0x0000 Reset] --> B[0x0003 EXT0 8 bytes]
    B --> C[0x000B Timer 0 8 bytes]
    C --> D[0x0013 EXT1 8 bytes]
    D --> E[0x001B Timer 1 8 bytes]
    E --> F[0x0023 Serial 8 bytes]
    F --> G[0x002B User Program]
```

### 4.3 Interrupt Decision Flow — Inside the CPU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Interrupt Event Occurs]
    check1{Flag Set by Hardware?}
    check2{EA equals 1?}
    check3{Individual Enable equals 1?}
    check4{Priority Check}
    fetch[Fetch Vector Address]
    push[Push PC onto Stack]
    exec[Execute ISR]
    reti[RETI Instruction]
    pop[Pop PC from Stack]
    resume[Resume Main Program]

    start --> check1
    check1 -- No --> end1[Ignored]
    check1 -- Yes --> check2
    check2 -- No --> end1
    check2 -- Yes --> check3
    check3 -- No --> end1
    check3 -- Yes --> check4
    check4 --> fetch
    fetch --> push
    push --> exec
    exec --> reti
    reti --> pop
    pop --> resume
```

### 4.4 Edge-Triggered vs. Level-Triggered Timing Comparison

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pin as P3.2 INT0 Pin
    participant Flag as IE0 Flag
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant ISR as ISR Code

    Note over Pin,CPU: Edge-Triggered Mode IT0 equals 1
    Pin->>Flag: Falling edge detected
    Flag->>Flag: IE0 set to 1 auto-cleared on vector
    Flag->>CPU: Request interrupt
    CPU->>ISR: Vector to 0x0003
    ISR->>CPU: RETI executed
    Note over Pin,CPU: Level-Triggered Mode IT0 equals 0
    Pin->>Flag: Pin held LOW
    Flag->>CPU: IE0 set to 1
    CPU->>ISR: Vector to 0x0003
    ISR->>CPU: RETI executed
    Note over Pin,CPU: WARNING If pin still LOW re-interrupt occurs
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define an interrupt. List the six interrupts of the 8051 microcontroller with their vector addresses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An interrupt is an asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the main program execution and forces the CPU to execute a special routine called ISR. The six 8051 interrupts are:

| Interrupt | Vector Address |
|-----------|----------------|
| Reset | **0x0000** |
| External Interrupt 0 | **0x0003** |
| Timer 0 Overflow | **0x000B** |
| External Interrupt 1 | **0x0013** |
| Timer 1 Overflow | **0x001B** |
| Serial Port | **0x0023** |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks table)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the EA bit in the IE register? What happens if it is 0?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **EA (Enable All)** bit, located at **IE.7**, is the **global master switch** for all 8051 interrupts. **(1 mark)**
- For any interrupt to be serviced, EA must be set to **1** AND its individual enable bit must also be set. **(1 mark)**
- If EA = 0, **all maskable interrupts are disabled** (masked) regardless of their individual enable bits. The CPU continues executing the main program without responding to any interrupt request. **(1 mark)**

---

### **PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a) Explain the 8051 Interrupt Structure with the help of the IE and IP registers. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 supports **6 interrupt sources**: Reset, two external interrupts (INT0, INT1), two timer overflow interrupts (Timer 0, Timer 1), and one serial port interrupt. Each interrupt is managed through dedicated SFRs.

**IE Register (0xA8) — Interrupt Enable Register:**

The IE register controls whether an interrupt is allowed to reach the CPU. Its bit configuration is:

```
IE.7 (EA)   = 1 → Global enable ON
IE.6, IE.5  = Reserved / unused
IE.4 (ES)   = 1 → Enable Serial Port interrupt
IE.3 (ET1)  = 1 → Enable Timer 1 overflow interrupt
IE.2 (EX1)  = 1 → Enable External Interrupt 1
IE.1 (ET0)  = 1 → Enable Timer 0 overflow interrupt
IE.0 (EX0)  = 1 → Enable External Interrupt 0
```

For any interrupt to be serviced, **EA must be 1** AND its individual enable bit must be 1. **(3 marks)**

**IP Register (0xB8) — Interrupt Priority Register:**

The IP register assigns priority levels. Setting a bit to 1 makes that interrupt **high priority**:

```
IP.4 (PS)   = 1 → Serial port high priority
IP.3 (PT1)  = 1 → Timer 1 high priority
IP.2 (PX1)  = 1 → External 1 high priority
IP.1 (PT0)  = 1 → Timer 0 high priority
IP.0 (PX0)  = 1 → External 0 high priority
```

**Priority Rules:**
- A high-priority interrupt **can preempt** a low-priority ISR (nested interrupts).
- A low-priority interrupt **can only be interrupted by a high-priority one**.
- If two interrupts of the same priority fire simultaneously, **default natural priority** applies: INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial. **(4 marks)**

---

**(b) Write an Embedded C program for 8051 to toggle P1.0 continuously using Timer 0 overflow interrupt. Assume 11.0592 MHz crystal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;            // LED at port pin P1.0

void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 2   // Vector 0x000B, Timer 0
{
    LED = ~LED;             // Toggle LED on every overflow
    TF0 = 0;                // Although auto-cleared, can be set
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;            // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x00;            // Reload high byte
    TL0  = 0x00;            // Reload low byte
    TR0  = 1;               // Start Timer 0
    
    ET0  = 1;               // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;               // Global interrupt enable

    while(1)
    {
        // CPU idle, all toggling handled by ISR
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Key Lines (Step-by-Step):**

1. `TMOD = 0x01;` → Configures Timer 0 in **16-bit Mode 1**. **(1 mark)**
2. `TH0 = 0x00; TL0 = 0x00;` → Timer counts from 0000H to FFFFH, then overflows and triggers interrupt. **(1 mark)**
3. `TR0 = 1;` → Starts the timer counting. **(1 mark)**
4. `ET0 = 1;` → Enables Timer 0 interrupt in IE register. **(1 mark)**
5. `EA = 1;` → Master interrupt enable. **(1 mark)**
6. `interrupt 2` → Keil C51 keyword. Places function at vector 0x000B and emits `RETI` on exit. **(1 mark)**
7. `LED = ~LED;` toggles P1.0 every overflow cycle. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Differentiate between Level-Triggered and Edge-Triggered external interrupts. How are they configured using TCON register? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Level-Triggered (ITx = 0) | Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1) |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Activation | Pin held **LOW** | **Falling edge** (HIGH → LOW transition) |
| Flag Behavior | Flag follows pin level | Flag set on edge, auto-cleared by CPU on vector |
| Pulse Width | Must remain LOW **until RETI** | Minimum **1 machine cycle** width |
| Re-triggering | **Risk of infinite re-entry** if pin stays LOW | **No re-trigger** unless new edge arrives |
| Typical Use | Wired-OR interrupt sharing | Push-button events |

**TCON Register Configuration (0x88):**

The two relevant bits in TCON are **IT0 (TCON.0)** and **IT1 (TCON.2)**. These are configured in software:

```c
IT0 = 0;    // INT0 = Level-triggered (low)
IT0 = 1;    // INT0 = Edge-triggered (falling)

IT1 = 0;    // INT1 = Level-triggered (low)
IT1 = 1;    // INT1 = Edge-triggered (falling)
```

After configuration, the flag bits **IE0 (TCON.1)** and **IE1 (TCON.3)** are set by hardware upon detection. **(3 marks configuration + 4 marks comparison table)**

---

**(b) Explain with a diagram the steps involved in executing an Interrupt Service Routine in 8051. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer — The 6-Stage Interrupt Execution Sequence:**

1. **Interrupt Source Activation:** A hardware event sets the corresponding flag (e.g., IE0 for INT0, TF0 for Timer 0 overflow, RI/TI for serial). **(1 mark)**

2. **Enable Check:** The CPU verifies the **EA bit (IE.7)** is 1 AND the corresponding individual enable bit (EX0, ET0, ES, etc.) is 1. If either is 0, the interrupt is **masked** and ignored. **(1 mark)**

3. **Priority Arbitration:** If multiple interrupts are pending, the **IP register** decides the order. High-priority interrupts preempt low-priority ones. If equal priority, the natural order (INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial) applies. **(1 mark)**

4. **Hardware Response — PC Save:** The CPU completes the current instruction, then **pushes the 16-bit PC onto the stack** (high byte first, then low byte). The stack pointer SP increments by 2. **(1 mark)**

5. **Vector Fetch & ISR Execution:** The CPU loads the PC with the **vector address** (e.g., 0x000B for Timer 0) and begins executing the ISR. The flag is auto-cleared for external/timer interrupts; for serial, it must be cleared in software. **(1 mark)**

6. **Return via RETI:** The `RETI` instruction pops the saved PC from the stack, restoring execution to the next instruction in the main program. **(2 marks)**

**Flow Diagram (text-based):**

```
Interrupt Event
      ↓
Set Flag (TCON/SCON)
      ↓
Check EA and Individual Enable
      ↓
Priority Resolution (IP Register)
      ↓
Push PC onto Stack
      ↓
Load Vector Address into PC
      ↓
Execute ISR
      ↓
RETI → Pop PC from Stack
      ↓
Resume Main Program
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set EA = 1:** Many students configure individual interrupt bits but forget the global EA bit. The interrupt will **never fire** — 0 marks for execution questions.
> 2. **Using `return` instead of `RETI`:** In Keil C51, the `interrupt` keyword auto-emits RETI. Never manually use `return` thinking it works the same.
> 3. **Confusing vector numbers with Keil interrupt numbers:** Keil uses sequential numbers (0–5), not vector addresses. Timer 0 = `interrupt 1`? **NO** — it's `interrupt 1` for EXT0, `interrupt 2` for Timer 0.
> 4. **Serial interrupt flag clearing:** The RI/TI flags are **not auto-cleared** by hardware; forgetting to clear them causes infinite re-entry.
> 5. **Mixing IT0/IT1 default values:** On reset, IT0 = IT1 = 0 (level-triggered). Students often write code assuming edge-triggered without explicitly setting it.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

| # | Key Point to Remember |
|---|------------------------|
| 1 | 8051 has **6 interrupts**: Reset + 5 maskable |
| 2 | Vector addresses start at **0x0000** and increment by **8 bytes** |
| 3 | **IE register (0xA8)** controls enable; **IP register (0xB8)** controls priority |
| 4 | **EA = 1** is mandatory for any interrupt to fire |
| 5 | **External interrupts** use P3.2 (INT0) and P3.3 (INT1) |
| 6 | **IT0/IT1** in TCON choose edge (1) vs level (0) trigger |
| 7 | **High priority interrupts** can preempt low priority ISRs (nested) |
| 8 | **Serial interrupt** has TWO flags (RI, TI) — clear manually |
| 9 | **RETI** restores PC; never replace with plain `return` in Keil |
| 10 | Keil `interrupt N` keyword: N=0 Reset, 1=EXT0, 2=T0, 3=EXT1, 4=T1, 5=Serial |
| 11 | **Edge-triggered** auto-clears flag; **level-triggered** does not |
| 12 | Default natural priority: INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial |
| 13 | Interrupt response time: **3 to 8 machine cycles** |
| 14 | ISR must be **short and fast** — avoid long delays inside ISRs |
| 15 | Interrupt enables bit-addressable SFRs (IE, IP, TCON) — use single-bit assignment |

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Hardware and Serial Communication Interrupt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** External Hardware and Serial Communication Interrupt ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **8051 Microcontroller** provides a sophisticated interrupt-driven mechanism that allows the CPU to respond to asynchronous events without continuously polling peripheral devices. In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, two critical categories of interrupts are studied under Module 3: **(A) External Hardware Interrupts** — These are asynchronous interrupts triggered by external hardware signals applied to dedicated pins of the 8051: - **INT0** → Pin **P3.2** (alternate function of Port 3 bit 2), Vector Address: **0003H** - **INT1** → Pin **P3.3** (alternate function of Port 3 bit 3), Vector Address: **0013H** **(B) Serial Communication Interrupt (UART Interrupt)** — This interrupt is generated internally whenever the **TI (Transmit Interrupt)** or **RI (Receive Interrupt)** flag in the **SCON (Serial Control) register** is set. Vector Address: **0023H**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 mandates a clear understanding of how external interrupts are conditioned (level vs edge-triggered), the role of the **IE (Interrupt Enable)** and **TCON (Timer/Counter Control)** registers, and the complete serial port programming model including **Mode 1** (8-bit UART with variable baud rate). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell and Telephone" Model Imagine you are a **chef cooking in a kitchen (the CPU)**. You are busy chopping vegetables (executing the main program). - **External Hardware Interrupt** is like a **doorbell (INT0/INT1)**. A visitor (external sensor/device) presses the doorbell button. No matter how busy you are, you **pause cooking**, go answer the door (execute the ISR — Interrupt Service Routine), and then **return to cooking exactly where you left off**. - **Serial Communication Interrupt** is like a **telephone ringing**. The UART hardware receives a byte over the TX/RX lines. It sets a "flag" (RI = 1) and rings the CPU. The CPU "picks up the phone," reads the data from **SBUF**, and then resumes the main program. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Polling is like checking the door every second to see if someone is there — it wastes CPU time. Interrupts are like a doorbell — they notify the CPU only when an event of interest actually occurs. This is the foundation of **real-time embedded systems**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Description | |---|---|---| | Crystal Frequency ($f_{osc}$) | **11.0592 MHz** (standard for baud rate generation) | Most common crystal used in 8051 labs | | Machine Cycle Period ($T_{MC}$) | **12 / $f_{osc}$** | Time for one machine cycle | | Default External Trigger | **Level-sensitive (Low)** | When IT0/IT1 = 0 | | Edge-Triggered Mode | **Falling edge (H-to-L)** | When IT0/IT1 = 1 | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Interrupt Enable (IE) Register — Address **A8H** (Bit-Addressable) This is the master switchboard. Unless the **EA (Enable All)** bit is set, **NO** interrupt will be serviced. $$\text{IE Register Bit Map (Address A8H)}$$ | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Symbol | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** | | Function | Global Enable | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Port | Timer 1 | External 1 | Timer 0 | External 0 | - **EA = 1** → Globally enables all interrupts. - **EX0 = 1** → Enables external interrupt 0 (INT0). - **EX1 = 1** → Enables external interrupt 1 (INT1). - **ES = 1** → Enables serial port interrupt (TI/RI). ### 2.2 The Timer/Counter Control (TCON) Register — Address **88H** The lower 4 bits of TCON are dedicated to the external interrupts: $$\text{TCON Register — External Interrupt Bits}$$ | Bit | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | TCON.3 | **IE0** | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag (set by hardware on detection) | | TCON.2 | **IT0** | External Interrupt 0 Type Select: **0 = Level (Low), 1 = Edge (Falling)** | | TCON.5 | **IE1** | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag | | TCON.4 | **IT1** | External Interrupt 1 Type Select: **0 = Level, 1 = Edge** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The IE0/IE1 flags must be cleared by software inside the ISR** when operating in **edge-triggered mode**. In level-triggered mode, the external source must remove the low signal before re-arming. ### 2.3 The Serial Control (SCON) Register — Address **98H** $$\text{SCON Register Bit Map (Address 98H — Bit-Addressable)}$$ | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Symbol | **SM0** | **SM1** | **SM2** | **REN** | **TB8** | **RB8** | **TI** | **RI** | - **SM0, SM1**: Serial Mode selection bits (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3). - **REN**: Receive Enable. Must be set to **1** to receive data. - **TI**: Transmit Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of byte transmission. - **RI**: Receive Interrupt flag — set by hardware at end of byte reception. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Critical Point:** Both **TI** and **RI** must be **cleared by software** inside the serial ISR. They are NOT cleared automatically — this is the #1 reason students' serial programs "hang" after the first byte. ### 2.4 The Serial Buffer (SBUF) Register — Address **99H** Physically two registers share address **99H**: - **Writing** to SBUF loads the **Transmit register**. - **Reading** from SBUF accesses the **Receive register**. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Interrupt Vector Addresses $$\text{Interrupt Vector Address Table}$$ | Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Priority (Default) | |---|---|---|---| | Reset | — | **0000H** | Highest | | External 0 (INT0) | IE0 | **0003H** | 1 | | Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **000BH** | 2 | | External 1 (INT1) | IE1 | **0013H** | 3 | | Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **001BH** | 4 | | Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI / RI | **0023H** | Lowest | > Each vector is separated by exactly **8 bytes** (0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H). This 8-byte spacing is a classic KTU question topic. ### 2.6 Baud Rate Generation Formula (Timer 1, Mode 2 — Auto-Reload) $$Baud\;Rate = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ For **SMOD = 0** and **$f_{osc}$ = 11.0592 MHz**, to get a standard baud rate: $$TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times BaudRate}$$ ### 2.7 Interrupt Latency Equation $$T_{latency} = T_{response} + T_{execution}$$ Where: - $T_{response}$ = Time from flag-set to ISR start (typically **3 to 7 machine cycles**). - $T_{execution}$ = ISR code execution time. $$\text{For } f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}, \quad T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \; \mu s$$ --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — External vs. Serial Interrupt | Feature | External Hardware Interrupt | Serial Communication Interrupt | |---|---|---| | **Source** | External pin (P3.2 / P3.3) | Internal UART hardware | | **Trigger Condition** | Low level or falling edge on pin | TI=1 (TX complete) or RI=1 (RX complete) | | **Trigger Control Register** | TCON (IT0, IT1 bits) | SCON (TI, RI bits — automatic) | | **Enable Bit** | EX0 / EX1 in IE | ES in IE | | **Vector Address** | 0003H / 0013H | 0023H | | **Hardware Clearing** | IE0/IE1 in **edge** mode only | **None — software must clear TI/RI** | | **Typical Use Case** | Emergency stop buttons, sensors, real-time events | UART communication with PC/other MCUs | ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Setting Up an External Interrupt (INT0, Edge-Triggered) **Goal:** Configure INT0 to toggle P1.0 every time a falling edge occurs on pin P3.2. **Step 1 — Set the trigger type in TCON** $$IT0 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Falling edge triggered}$$ $$\text{SETB} \; TCON.0 \quad \text{(or equivalently, SETB IT0)}$$ **Step 2 — Enable the specific interrupt in IE** $$EX0 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{External 0 interrupt enabled}$$ **Step 3 — Enable global interrupts** $$EA = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{CPU will respond to interrupts}$$ **Step 4 — Write the ISR at vector 0003H** $$\text{ORG} \; 0003H$$ **Step 5 — Clear the IE0 flag inside the ISR** (critical for edge mode). **Step 6 — Return using RETI** (not RET — this re-enables the interrupt system). ### 3.3 Complete 8051 Assembly Code — External Interrupt (INT0) ```assembly ORG 0000H SJMP MAIN ; Jump over interrupt vector table ORG 0003H ; INT0 vector address ; ---- ISR for INT0 ---- CPL P1.0 ; Toggle P1.0 RETI ; Return and re-enable interrupts ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV SP, #60H ; Initialize stack pointer SETB IT0 ; Edge-triggered (falling) SETB EX0 ; Enable INT0 SETB EA ; Global enable HERE: SJMP HERE ; Idle main loop END ``` ### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation — Serial Interrupt Setup (Mode 1, 9600 Baud) **Step 1 — Choose Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) for baud rate generation** $$\text{Timer 1 Mode 2: } TMOD = 0 \times 20$$ **Step 2 — Calculate TH1 value using the baud rate formula** For 9600 baud, $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, SMOD = 0: $$TH1 = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600}$$ $$TH1 = 256 - \frac{11059200}{3686400}$$ $$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0 \times FD$$ **Step 3 — Configure SCON for Mode 1 (8-bit UART) with reception enabled** $$SCON = 0 \times 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad SM0=0, SM1=1, REN=1$$ **Step 4 — Start Timer 1** $$TR1 = 1$$ **Step 5 — Enable serial interrupt** $$ES = 1, \quad EA = 1$$ **Step 6 — In ISR, check TI vs RI using JNB/JB, service, and clear flag** ### 3.5 Complete 8051 Assembly Code — Serial Interrupt (Transmit "A" on Interrupt) ```assembly ORG 0000H SJMP MAIN ORG 0023H ; Serial interrupt vector SERIAL_ISR: JB TI, TRANSMIT_DONE JB RI, RECEIVE_DONE RETI TRANSMIT_DONE: CLR TI ; MUST clear software RETI RECEIVE_DONE: MOV A, SBUF ; Read received byte CLR RI ; MUST clear software RETI ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV SP, #60H MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1 Mode 2 MOV TH1, #0FDH ; 9600 baud MOV SCON, #50H ; Mode 1, REN=1 SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 SETB ES ; Enable serial interrupt SETB EA ; Global enable MOV SBUF, #'A' ; Kickstart transmission HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` ### 3.6 Interrupt Priority Register (IP) — Address **B8H** For changing default priority order: $$\text{IP Register: } PT2,\; PS,\; PT1,\; PX1,\; PT0,\; PX0$$ Setting a bit to 1 elevates that interrupt to **high priority**. KTU often asks: "How do you give the serial interrupt higher priority than Timer 0?" $$SETB \; PS \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Serial port = High Priority}$$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 Interrupt Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU[CPU Core - 8051] subgraph External_Sources [External Interrupt Sources] INT0Pin[P3.2 - INT0 Pin] INT1Pin[P3.3 - INT1 Pin] end subgraph Internal_Sources [Internal Interrupt Sources] TMR0[Timer 0 Overflow - TF0] TMR1[Timer 1 Overflow - TF1] UART_TX[UART Transmit Complete - TI] UART_RX[UART Receive Complete - RI] end subgraph Control_Registers [Control and Enable Registers] TCON_REG[TCON Register - 88H - IT0 IT1 IE0 IE1] SCON_REG[SCON Register - 98H - TI RI REN] IE_REG[IE Register - A8H - EA ES EX0 EX1 ET0 ET1] IP_REG[IP Register - B8H - Priority Control] end subgraph Vector_Table [Interrupt Vector Table - Code Memory] V_RESET[0000H - Reset] V_INT0[0003H - External 0] V_TMR0[000BH - Timer 0] V_INT1[0013H - External 1] V_TMR1[001BH - Timer 1] V_SER[0023H - Serial Port] end INT0Pin --> TCON_REG INT1Pin --> TCON_REG TMR0 --> TCON_REG TMR1 --> TCON_REG UART_TX --> SCON_REG UART_RX --> SCON_REG TCON_REG --> IP_REG SCON_REG --> IP_REG IE_REG --> IP_REG IP_REG --> CPU CPU --> V_RESET CPU --> V_INT0 CPU --> V_TMR0 CPU --> V_INT1 CPU --> V_TMR1 CPU --> V_SER ``` ### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Interrupt Servicing Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Main as Main Program participant CPU as 8051 CPU participant Source as Interrupt Source participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine Main->>CPU: Execute main loop instruction Note over CPU: Polls interrupt flags every machine cycle Source->>CPU: Trigger signal (INT0 / TI / RI) CPU->>CPU: Detect flag at end of instruction cycle alt If EA AND specific enable bit = 1 CPU->>CPU: Save PC on stack (2 bytes) CPU->>CPU: Clear corresponding flag if hardware-cleared CPU->>ISR: Jump to vector address (LCALL) ISR->>CPU: Execute ISR body Note right of ISR: Push/Pop registers
Clear flags manually
Process event ISR->>CPU: RETI instruction CPU->>CPU: Pop PC from stack CPU->>Main: Resume main program execution else If disabled CPU->>Main: Ignore interrupt, continue main loop end ``` ### 4.3 Pin Configuration Diagram — External Interrupt Pins ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Port3 [Port 3 - Alternate Functions] P30[P3.0 - RXD] P31[P3.1 - TXD] P32[P3.2 - INT0] P33[P3.3 - INT1] P34[P3.4 - T0] P35[P3.5 - T1] end EXT_DEVICE[External Switch or Sensor] PC_SIDE[PC or Another MCU] EXT_DEVICE -- Falling edge or low pulse --> P32 EXT_DEVICE -- Falling edge or low pulse --> P33 PC_SIDE <--> P30 PC_SIDE <--> P31 ``` ### 4.4 Memory Map — Interrupt Vector Locations in Code ROM ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CodeROM [8051 Code ROM - 4KB visible initially] A0000[0000H - Reset Vector - 8 bytes] A0003[0003H - INT0 ISR Entry] A000B[000BH - Timer 0 ISR Entry] A0013[0013H - INT1 ISR Entry] A001B[001BH - Timer 1 ISR Entry] A0023[0023H - Serial ISR Entry] A0030[0030H onwards - Main Program] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Q1.** *Differentiate between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts in 8051. Which bit controls this and in which register is it located?* **Model Answer:** In **level-triggered mode**, the interrupt is requested as long as the pin (INT0/INT1) is held **LOW**. The flag IE0/IE1 is **not** automatically cleared and the source must release the line. In **edge-triggered mode**, the interrupt is requested only on a **falling edge (H → L transition)**; the flag IE0/IE1 is set by hardware and **must be cleared by software** inside the ISR. The control bits are **IT0 (TCON.2)** and **IT1 (TCON.4)** in the **TCON register (88H)**. ITx = 0 selects level-triggered; ITx = 1 selects edge-triggered. --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]** **Q2.** *Why is it necessary to clear the TI and RI flags by software inside the serial interrupt service routine? What happens if you use RET instead of RETI?* **Model Answer:** The 8051 sets **TI (SCON.1)** and **RI (SCON.0)** automatically when a byte is transmitted or received respectively. They are **not cleared by hardware**, so if the programmer does not explicitly clear them (e.g., `CLR TI` and `CLR RI`), the CPU will **re-enter the ISR endlessly**, causing the program to hang. Using **RETI** instead of **RET** is equally critical because **RETI** performs two essential actions: (1) pops the return address from the stack, and (2) **re-arms the interrupt logic** by signaling the interrupt controller that the ISR is complete. Using **RET** would return from the ISR but leave the interrupt system "logically busy," blocking all future interrupts. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) #### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]* **(a)** *Explain with a neat diagram the external interrupt structure of 8051. List the vector addresses of all interrupts. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** The 8051 has **5 interrupt sources** (excluding Reset): 1. **Two External Hardware Interrupts** — INT0 (P3.2) and INT1 (P3.3) 2. **Two Timer Interrupts** — TF0 (Timer 0) and TF1 (Timer 1) 3. **One Serial Port Interrupt** — TI/RI (combined) **Vector Address Table:** $$\text{Reset: } 0000H, \quad INT0: 0003H, \quad T0: 000BH, \quad INT1: 0013H, \quad T1: 001BH, \quad Serial: 0023H$$ The **IE register (A8H)** controls enable/disable, with **EA** as the master switch. The **IP register (B8H)** controls priority. The **TCON register (88H)** contains the IE0/IE1 flag bits and IT0/IT1 type-select bits for external interrupts. When an event occurs, the flag is set, the CPU completes the current instruction, pushes the PC onto the stack, jumps to the vector address (8-byte space), executes the ISR, and returns via RETI. --- **(b)** *Write an 8051 ALP to configure INT1 as a falling-edge triggered interrupt. The ISR should read Port P1, complement it, and send it to Port P2. Assume main program initializes SP and is an infinite loop. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 0013H ; INT1 vector address INT1_ISR: MOV A, P1 ; Read P1 CPL A ; Complement MOV P2, A ; Send to P2 RETI ; Return and re-enable ORG 0030H MAIN: MOV SP, #60H SETB IT1 ; Falling edge on INT1 SETB EX1 ; Enable INT1 SETB EA ; Global enable HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` **Explanation:** - `SETB IT1` configures INT1 for falling-edge detection. - `SETB EX1` enables INT1 specifically. - `SETB EA` is mandatory to globally allow interrupts. - Inside the ISR, no manual clearing of IE1 is strictly required in **edge-triggered** mode because the 8051 auto-clears the edge flag upon entering the ISR. - `RETI` must be used, not `RET`. --- #### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2023]* **(a)** *Explain the SCON register of 8051. Describe Mode 1 of the serial port with the baud rate equation. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **SCON Register (98H, Bit-Addressable):** | Bit | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | 7 | SM0 | Serial Mode bit 0 | | 6 | SM1 | Serial Mode bit 1 | | 5 | SM2 | Multiprocessor communication enable | | 4 | REN | Receive Enable (must be 1 to receive) | | 3 | TB8 | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2, 3) | | 2 | RB8 | 9th received bit (Modes 2, 3) | | 1 | TI | Transmit Interrupt flag | | 0 | RI | Receive Interrupt flag | **Serial Mode 1:** SM0=0, SM1=1 → **8-bit UART** with **variable baud rate** (driven by Timer 1). - **Frame format:** 1 Start bit (0) + 8 Data bits (LSB first) + 1 Stop bit (1) = **10 bits total**. - **Baud rate** determined by Timer 1 overflow rate: $$Baud\;Rate = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ - For **SMOD = 0**, $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, baud = 9600: $$TH1 = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = 253 = 0xFD$$ - **REN** must be set to enable reception. - **TI** is set after the stop bit is transmitted; **RI** is set after the stop bit is received. - Both flags must be **cleared by software**. --- **(b)** *Write an 8051 C program to transmit the string "KTU" repeatedly via the serial port at 9600 baud using interrupts. Use 11.0592 MHz crystal. (7 Marks)* **Model Solution (Embedded C using Keil syntax):** ```c #include unsigned char data i = 0; unsigned char code msg[] = "KTU"; void serial_ISR(void) interrupt 4 { if (TI == 1) { TI = 0; // Clear transmit flag SBUF = msg[i]; // Send next character i++; if (msg[i] == '\0') i = 0; // Loop string } // RI handling omitted (not needed for TX only) } void main(void) { TMOD = 0x20; // Timer 1, Mode 2 TH1 = 0xFD; // 9600 baud SCON = 0x50; // Mode 1, REN=1 TR1 = 1; // Start Timer 1 ES = 1; // Enable serial interrupt EA = 1; // Global enable SBUF = msg[0]; // Kickstart transmission while (1); // Idle main loop } ``` **Explanation:** - Timer 1 in Mode 2 provides the baud rate clock. - TH1 = 0xFD generates 9600 baud exactly because the crystal is 11.0592 MHz (an integer divisor). - The interrupt is declared with `interrupt 4` (the C51 keyword maps to vector 0023H). - Each time TI becomes 1 (end of transmission), the ISR sends the next character. - The string loops back to start when the null terminator is encountered. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 3 Interrupts):** > 1. **Forgetting `SETB EA`** — Without EA = 1, NO interrupt works. Examiners specifically deduct for this. > 2. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** — RET does not re-enable the interrupt logic; program hangs after first ISR. > 3. **Failing to clear TI/RI in serial ISR** — Causes continuous re-entry into the ISR. Loss of 2–3 marks. > 4. **Wrong vector address** — Writing the ISR at the wrong address (e.g., 0008H instead of 0003H). The 8-byte spacing is a classic trap. > 5. **Using wrong TH1 value for non-standard crystal** — If the crystal is not 11.0592 MHz, baud rate will be inaccurate. Always show the **formula calculation**, not just the value. > 6. **Confusing SBUF read/write** — Writing SBUF loads TX; reading SBUF accesses RX. Examiners test this distinction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ⭐ **Two External Interrupts**: INT0 (P3.2, vector 0003H), INT1 (P3.3, vector 0013H). - ⭐ **Trigger Types**: `ITx = 0` → Low-level sensitive; `ITx = 1` → Falling-edge sensitive. - ⭐ **TCON Register (88H)** holds IE0, IT0, IE1, IT1; **IE Register (A8H)** holds EA, EX0, EX1, ES, ET0, ET1. - ⭐ **Serial Interrupt** is triggered by **TI or RI flags** in **SCON (98H)**; vector is **0023H**. - ⭐ **SBUF (99H)** is a shared register — write to transmit, read to receive. - ⭐ **TI and RI must be cleared by software** — most-failed KTU concept. - ⭐ **Use `RETI`, never `RET`** — RETI pops PC and re-enables interrupt logic. - ⭐ **Baud rate formula (Timer 1, Mode 2)**: $Baud = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12(256-TH1)}$. - ⭐ **Standard TH1 values for 11.0592 MHz crystal**: - 9600 baud → TH1 = 0xFD - 4800 baud → TH1 = 0xFA - 2400 baud → TH1 = 0xF4 - ⭐ **Vector addresses are 8 bytes apart** — ISR code must fit or use `LJMP`/`SJMP` to actual code elsewhere. - ⭐ **IP register (B8H)** controls priority; setting PS = 1 makes serial interrupt high priority. - ⭐ **Default priority order**: INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial. - ⭐ **Reset vector is 0000H** — main program usually starts with `ORG 0000H; LJMP MAIN`. ---

topics.title: Interrupt Priority in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Priority in 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, *Interrupt Priority* is a deterministic hardware-controlled arbitration mechanism that defines the **execution hierarchy** among multiple interrupt sources requesting service at the same instant. The 8051 supports a **two-level fixed priority structure** (a natural hardware priority chain) combined with a **programmable two-tier priority** (high and low) configurable through the **IP (Interrupt Priority) Special Function Register (SFR) at address 0xB8**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 8051 has **6 interrupt sources** (in 8052/8032 variants), with the original 8051 having **5 user-accessible sources** plus the **non-maskable Reset**. Priority is resolved in **two stages**: (1) Programmable priority level via the IP register, and (2) Fixed polling sequence for same-priority conflicts.

### 1.2 The Six Interrupt Sources of the 8051

| Vector Address | Interrupt Source | Flag Bit | Priority Register Bit |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 0x0000 | Reset (Non-maskable) | — | — |
| 0x0003 | External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 (TCON.1) | PX0 (IP.0) |
| 0x000B | Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 (TCON.5) | PT0 (IP.1) |
| 0x0013 | External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 (TCON.3) | PX1 (IP.2) |
| 0x001B | Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 (TCON.7) | PT1 (IP.3) |
| 0x0023 | Serial Port (TI or RI) | TI/RI (SCON) | PS (IP.4) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine a hospital Emergency Department (ED) with patients arriving simultaneously.
> - The **Triage Nurse** = **Programmable Priority (IP register)**: She tags each incoming case as "**Critical (High)**" or "**Standard (Low)**".
> - The **Hospital's Standing Rule** = **Fixed Polling Sequence**: When two patients have the *same* triage level, the doctor sees them in a pre-defined order (e.g., cardiac → trauma → pediatric).
> - **Critical (High-priority)** cases can *interrupt* the treatment of **Standard (Low-priority)** patients — this is **Nested Interrupting**.
> - The **Reset** is the "**Code Blue / System Collapse**" — it overrides *everything* unconditionally.

### 1.4 Reset — The Supreme Authority

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Rule:** The **Reset** is a **non-maskable, highest-priority** event. It cannot be disabled by software, cannot be assigned a low priority, and **Vector Address 0x0000** always wins over every other interrupt, regardless of the IP register contents.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Stage Priority Resolution Model

The 8051 resolves interrupt conflicts in **two deterministic stages** every machine cycle:

**Stage 1 — Programmable Level Priority (Software Configured via IP):**
- Each interrupt source has one bit in the **IP register**.
- Logic **1** = **High Priority** ; Logic **0** = **Low Priority** (default on reset).
- A high-priority ISR can preempt (nest into) a low-priority ISR at any instruction boundary.

**Stage 2 — Fixed Polling Sequence (Hardware-Defined Tie-Breaker):**
- When **two or more interrupts of the same priority level** are activated simultaneously, the 8051 uses a **fixed polling order**:

$$\text{Polling Order: } \overline{\text{INT0}} \rightarrow \text{TF0} \rightarrow \overline{\text{INT1}} \rightarrow \text{TF1} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port}$$

### 2.2 The IP (Interrupt Priority) Register — Bit Layout

The **IP register** is a bit-addressable SFR at internal RAM address **0xB8**:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Symbol | — | — | **PT2** | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| Reset Value | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> - **PT2** exists only in the 8052/8032 (Timer 2). In standard 8051, IP.5 must be left as 0.
> - On reset, **all bits = 0**, meaning every interrupt is **low priority** by default.
> - Setting any bit to **1** elevates the corresponding source to **high priority**.

### 2.3 The IE (Interrupt Enable) Register — The Gatekeeper

Before priority is even considered, the interrupt must be **enabled**. The **IE register at 0xA8** controls this:

| Bit | Symbol | Function | Default |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| IE.7 | **EA** | Global Enable (must be 1 for any interrupt) | 0 |
| IE.4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port | 0 |
| IE.3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 | 0 |
| IE.2 | **EX1** | Enable External 1 | 0 |
| IE.1 | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 | 0 |
| IE.0 | **EX0** | Enable External 0 | 0 |

### 2.4 Why and How — The Underlying Logic

- **Why two stages?** The 8051 designer trade-off: hardware is *simple and fast* (polling is just a sequence of bit-tests), but software can *override* the natural order for application-specific needs.
- **How nesting works:** When a high-priority interrupt fires, the **hardware automatically** pushes the current PC onto the stack and jumps to the high-priority ISR. The low-priority ISR is *suspended* — it resumes only after the high-priority ISR executes `RETI`.
- **The Trap:** A low-priority interrupt **cannot** interrupt another low-priority interrupt, but a high-priority interrupt **can** interrupt a low-priority one. Two high-priority interrupts cannot interrupt each other (they follow polling order).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Engineering Implication |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Default Polling Order | $\overline{\text{INT0}} > \text{TF0} > \overline{\text{INT1}} > \text{TF1} > \text{Serial}$ | Used when IP bits are all 0 (reset condition) |
| High-Priority Nesting | If $IP.x = 1$, ISR can preempt any $IP.y = 0$ ISR | Critical for real-time response |
| Same-Level Conflict | Resolve by **polling sequence** above | Deterministic but inflexible |
| Reset Dominance | $\text{Priority}(\text{Reset}) > \forall$ others unconditionally | Used for system recovery |
| Global Enable | $EA = 1 \land EX_x = 1$ required for INT_x | Two-step enabling prevents accidental interrupts |
| ISR Latency | $Latency = N_{cycles} \times T_{osc} \times 12$ | Max **13 machine cycles** to first ISR instruction |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interrupt priority in 8051 is the cornerstone of **real-time embedded design**:
- **Automotive ECUs:** Anti-lock braking system (high priority) overriding HVAC display updates (low priority).
- **Industrial PLCs:** Emergency stop interrupt (high) preempting routine sensor polling.
- **Consumer Electronics:** TV remote IR signal (high) preempting channel scan (low).
- **Medical Devices:** Heart-rate alarm (high) preempting LCD refresh (low).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Effective Priority Function

The 8051's interrupt arbitration can be expressed as a **composite priority function** $P_{eff}$ for any two simultaneous interrupts $I_a$ and $I_b$:

$$P_{eff}(I_a, I_b) = \begin{cases} \text{If } IP_a \neq IP_b: \quad \max(IP_a, IP_b) \text{ wins} \\ \text{If } IP_a = IP_b: \quad \text{PollingOrder}(I_a, I_b) \text{ wins} \\ \text{Always: } \text{Reset} \succ \text{all} \end{cases}$$

**Derivation Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Hardware samples all active interrupt flags every machine cycle.**
The 8051 internally maintains a 6-bit "pending request" register, latched from the IE0, TF0, IE1, TF1, TI, and RI flags.

**Step 2 — Apply the IP mask to filter into two priority buckets.**
For each pending request $i$, the effective level $L_i$ is:

$$L_i = IP_i \cdot 1 + (1 - IP_i) \cdot 0 = IP_i$$

**Step 3 — Check the high-priority bucket first.**
The hardware identifies the set $H = \{i : IP_i = 1\}$. If $H \neq \emptyset$, it serves one interrupt from $H$ according to the polling order.

**Step 4 — If $H = \emptyset$, fall through to the low-priority bucket $L = \{i : IP_i = 0\}$.**
The polling order resolves conflicts within $L$.

**Step 5 — Reset overrides all logic.**
The Reset pin is sampled independently and is **unaffected by IP or IE**.

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Priority Assignment

> **Problem:** Configure the 8051 so that **Timer 1 overflow** has the **highest priority** among all enabled interrupts, while **External Interrupt 0** remains at the **lowest possible position**. All other interrupts should remain at their natural default level.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify which IP bits to manipulate.
- To elevate Timer 1: set **PT1 (IP.3) = 1**
- To ensure INT0 stays lowest: leave **PX0 (IP.0) = 0**
- Leave all other IP bits at default (0).

**Step 2:** Construct the IP register byte.

$$\begin{aligned}
IP &= 0 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 0 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 \\
IP &= 0x08
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the resulting priority hierarchy.

- **PT1 = 1** (Timer 1) → enters high-priority bucket $H$.
- All others remain in low-priority bucket $L$.
- Within $L$, default polling order applies: INT0 → TF0 → INT1 → Serial.

**Step 4:** Final priority order from highest to lowest:
$$\text{Timer 1} \rightarrow \text{INT0} \rightarrow \text{TF0} \rightarrow \text{INT1} \rightarrow \text{Serial Port}$$

**Step 5:** Note that INT0 retains its natural *highest position within the low-priority bucket*, but Timer 1 — being the only high-priority source — will **always preempt** it.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use High Priority

| Use Case | Recommended Priority | Justification |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Power failure / brown-out | **High** | Must preempt all to save state |
| Emergency stop button | **High** | Safety-critical, < 1 cycle latency |
| Real-time data acquisition | **High** | Cannot miss a sample |
| UART communication | **Medium** | Buffer absorbs short delays |
| LED display refresh | **Low** | Tolerable jitter |
| Keypad scanning | **Low** | Human reaction time >> ISR latency |
| Timer tick (RTOS) | **Low–Medium** | Depends on tick rate |

### 3.4 Verification — What Happens During Simultaneous Trigger?

Consider a scenario at $t = 0$:
- **TF0** flag sets (Timer 0 overflow)
- **IE1** flag sets (External INT1 trigger)
- **IP register = 0x00** (reset default — all low priority)

**Hardware Arbitration Trace:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Hardware samples pending} &\rightarrow \{TF0, IE1\} \\
\text{Step 2: Apply IP mask (all zeros)} &\rightarrow H = \emptyset, L = \{TF0, IE1\} \\
\text{Step 3: Polling order in } L &\rightarrow \text{TF0 comes before INT1} \\
\text{Step 4: Service TF0 first} &\rightarrow \text{Vector } 0x000B \\
\text{Step 5: After RETI, service IE1} &\rightarrow \text{Vector } 0x0013
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** Timer 0 ISR executes first despite simultaneous arrival — the polling sequence is **deterministic and non-configurable** for same-level conflicts.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Stage Interrupt Arbitration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Interrupt Sources Activated] --> B{Reset Pin Asserted?}
    B -- Yes --> Z1[Serve Reset Vector 0x0000]
    B -- No --> C[Sample IP Register Bits]
    C --> D{High Priority Bucket Nonempty?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Select High Priority Source by Polling]
    E --> F[Serve High Priority ISR with Nesting Allowed]
    D -- No --> G[Apply Polling Order to Low Priority Bucket]
    G --> H[Serve Low Priority ISR No Nesting]
    F --> I[Execute RETI]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Return to Main Program]
    Z1 --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — IP Register Bit Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A0[IP bit 7: Reserved] --> A1[IP bit 6: Reserved]
    A1 --> A2[IP bit 5: PT2 Timer 2]
    A2 --> A3[IP bit 4: PS Serial Port]
    A3 --> A4[IP bit 3: PT1 Timer 1]
    A4 --> A5[IP bit 2: PX1 External INT1]
    A5 --> A6[IP bit 1: PT0 Timer 0]
    A6 --> A7[IP bit 0: PX0 External INT0]
    A7 --> A8[Value 1 means HIGH Priority]
    A7 --> A9[Value 0 means LOW Priority]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Interrupt Enable & Priority Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EA Global Enable] --> B{Is EA Set?}
    B -- No --> C[All Interrupts Blocked]
    B -- Yes --> D[Check Individual Enable Bits EX ET]
    D --> E[Interrupt Eligible to Compete]
    E --> F[Check IP Priority Level]
    F --> G{IP bit 1?}
    G -- Yes --> H[High Priority Bucket]
    G -- No --> I[Low Priority Bucket]
    H --> J[Polling Tiebreaker if Multiple]
    I --> J
    J --> K[ISR Vector Served]
```

### 4.4 Priority Hierarchy Flowchart (Default Reset Condition)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All 8051 Interrupts After Reset] --> B[INT0 External 0]
    B --> C[TF0 Timer 0]
    C --> D[INT1 External 1]
    D --> E[TF1 Timer 1]
    E --> F[Serial Port TI RI]
    F --> G[Lowest Natural Priority]
    A --> H[Reset Highest Always]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid — Nested Interrupt Scenario

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Program
    participant LowISR as Low Priority ISR TF0
    participant HighISR as High Priority ISR INT1
    Main->>LowISR: TF0 fires, IP0, jumps to 0x000B
    LowISR->>LowISR: Executing low priority code
    LowISR->>HighISR: INT1 fires, IP2, hardware preempts
    HighISR->>HighISR: Executes high priority ISR
    HighISR->>LowISR: RETI returns to suspended low ISR
    LowISR->>Main: RETI returns to main program
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the interrupt sources of the 8051 microcontroller in their default order of priority.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Upon reset, the 8051 interrupt priority order is:
1. **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** — highest default
2. **Timer 0 Overflow (TF0)**
3. **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)**
4. **Timer 1 Overflow (TF1)**
5. **Serial Port (TI/RI)** — lowest default
   *(Plus Reset — unconditionally highest)*

> *Awarding logic: 1 mark for naming all 5, 1 mark for correct order, 1 mark for mentioning Reset.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the IP register in 8051 interrupt handling? Name its address.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **IP (Interrupt Priority) register** at SFR address **0xB8** assigns one of **two priority levels** (high or low) to each interrupt source. A bit value of **1** marks the corresponding interrupt as **high priority**, allowing it to **preempt (nest into)** any low-priority ISR. A value of **0** marks it as **low priority**, which executes only after all high-priority ISRs are serviced, with conflicts resolved by the fixed polling sequence.

> *Awarding logic: 1 mark for address, 1 mark for two-level purpose, 1 mark for nesting behavior.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**
*(a)* Explain the interrupt priority structure of the 8051 microcontroller. Describe the role of the IP register and the IE register with their respective addresses. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* With a neat diagram, explain the execution sequence when two interrupts of the same priority level are triggered simultaneously. Show how the IP register can be programmed to alter the natural priority. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Interrupt Priority Structure of 8051 (3 marks):**
The 8051 implements a **two-tier priority system**:
- **Hardware-fixed natural priority** (polling sequence): INT0 → TF0 → INT1 → TF1 → Serial.
- **Programmable priority** (IP register bits): high vs. low.
- The **Reset** always has absolute highest priority and is non-maskable.

**Role of IP Register (2 marks):**
- SFR at **address 0xB8**, bit-addressable.
- Bits PX0, PT0, PX1, PT1, PS, PT2 (in 8052) assign priority.
- Logic 1 = high priority (can nest); Logic 0 = low priority (default).

**Role of IE Register (2 marks):**
- SFR at **address 0xA8**, bit-addressable.
- **EA (IE.7)** is the global enable; individual bits **EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES** enable specific sources.
- A disabled interrupt is **completely ignored** by the hardware, regardless of priority setting.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Same-Priority Simultaneous Trigger Scenario (3 marks):**
When two interrupts of equal priority fire simultaneously, the 8051 hardware executes them in the **fixed polling sequence**: first INT0, then TF0, INT1, TF1, and finally Serial. The hardware tests each flag bit in this exact order every machine cycle and services the first match found.

**Sequence Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Main:        [PC executing main]
       |
   (IE0 set, IE1 set, IP = 0x00)
       |
       v
  0x0003: External 0 ISR
       |
   (RETI)
       |
       v
  0x0013: External 1 ISR
       |
   (RETI)
       |
       v
Main:        [Resumes]
```

**Programming the IP Register (2 marks):**
Writing **0x08** to IP sets **PT1 = 1** (Timer 1 high priority), leaving all others low. Writing **0x04** sets **PX1 = 1** (External 1 high). Multiple bits can be set simultaneously to create a custom high-priority group, e.g., `IP = 0x0C` makes **both External 1 and Timer 1 high priority** (polling order between them: Timer 1 still wins because TF1 poll occurs after IE1... correction: **IE1 poll occurs before TF1 poll**, so **External 1 wins**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:**
> Students often confuse the **polling order direction**. The polling sequence checks **from left to right** (highest to lowest in the natural list). If PT1 = PX1 = 1, then in the high-priority bucket, **IE1 is polled BEFORE TF1**, so External Interrupt 1 wins — *not* Timer 1. This costs 2 marks if missed.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]**
*(a)* Differentiate between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts in 8051. How is the trigger mode selected? **(7 marks)**

*(b)* An 8051 system uses a **stepper motor controller** (Timer 0) and a **UART receiver** (Serial Port). Design an interrupt priority scheme so the stepper motor timing is never missed, and the UART can be interrupted by the stepper if both fire simultaneously. Show the IE and IP register values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Level-Triggered Mode (3 marks):**
- The external interrupt flag **IE0/IE1 is set as long as the pin is held low**.
- The flag **does not need to be cleared by software** — it follows the pin.
- Suitable for **emergency signals** and **wired-OR interrupt sharing**.
- Problem: If the pin stays low when `RETI` executes, **the interrupt re-fires immediately**, causing a loop.

**Edge-Triggered Mode (3 marks):**
- The flag is latched on the **falling edge (high-to-low transition)** of the pin.
- Once set, the flag **must be cleared by software** inside the ISR (it is *not* automatically cleared like in level mode).
- Suitable for **fast transient events** like key presses, sensor pulses.
- Prevents re-triggering loops.

**Trigger Selection (1 mark):**
Controlled by the **IT0 (TCON.0)** and **IT1 (TCON.2)** bits:
- `IT0 = 0` → INT0 level-triggered
- `IT0 = 1` → INT0 edge-triggered (falling edge)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Design Requirements:**
- **Timer 0 (stepper)**: Must never miss → **HIGH priority**.
- **Serial Port (UART)**: Lower priority but must be enabled → **LOW priority**.

**Step 1: Configure IP register (2 marks):**
Set **PT0 (IP.1) = 1**, leave **PS (IP.4) = 0**.

$$\begin{aligned}
IP &= 0 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 0 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 \\
IP &= 0x02
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Configure IE register (2 marks):**
- **EA = 1** (global enable)
- **ET0 = 1** (Timer 0 enable)
- **ES = 1** (Serial enable)
- All others = 0

$$\begin{aligned}
IE &= 1 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 0 \cdot 2^5 + 1 \cdot 2^4 + 0 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0 \\
IE &= 0x92
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Initialize Timer 0 (1 mark):**
- `TMOD = 0x01` (Timer 0, Mode 1 — 16-bit)
- `TR0 = 1` to start

**Step 4: Serial Port Setup (1 mark):**
- `SCON = 0x50` (Mode 1, REN enabled)
- `PCON = 0x00` (Standard baud rate)
- `TR1 = 1` for baud generator

**Step 5: Behavior Verification (1 mark):**
When both Timer 0 and Serial fire simultaneously, Timer 0 (high) **immediately preempts** the Serial ISR. The Serial ISR resumes only after Timer 0's `RETI`. This guarantees the stepper pulse timing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake #2:**
> Students often set **EA = 0** by accident or forget to set the individual enable bits, causing the system to fail silently. Also, **forgetting `TR0 = 1`** in Timer setup is a top cause of "interrupt never fires" lab failures. **Always double-check IE and the timer/counter start bit together.** This costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Interrupt Priority in 8051**

- ⭐ The 8051 has **5 user-interrupts** (6 in 8052), plus the **unconditional Reset**.
- ⭐ The **IP register (0xB8)** controls priority: **1 = High, 0 = Low**.
- ⭐ The **IE register (0xA8)** controls enable: must set **EA + individual bit**.
- ⭐ **Default polling order**: INT0 → TF0 → INT1 → TF1 → Serial.
- ⭐ **Reset always wins** — non-maskable, vector 0x0000.
- ⭐ **High-priority ISR can nest** into low-priority ISR (hardware auto-stacks PC).
- ⭐ **Two same-priority ISRs cannot nest** each other — polling order resolves.
- ⭐ **Edge-triggered mode** requires **manual flag clearing** in the ISR; level-triggered does not.
- ⭐ **IT0/IT1 in TCON** select trigger mode (0 = level, 1 = falling edge).
- ⭐ **Vector addresses are fixed**: 0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023 (8 bytes apart).
- ⭐ **Maximum interrupt latency** = **13 machine cycles** (one full instruction + housekeeping).
- ⭐ **8052 only**: IP.5 (PT2) controls Timer 2 priority — leave at 0 on standard 8051.
- ⭐ **All IP bits are 0 on reset** → all interrupts low priority by default.

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw the **IP register byte** with bit positions labeled — examiners award 1–2 marks for *visual clarity* in priority questions. Memorize the polling order as **"I-T-I-T-S"** (INT0, TF0, INT1, TF1, Serial) for quick recall.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Programming in C
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
