> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Programming in C

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Interrupt** is a hardware- or software-triggered asynchronous event that temporarily halts the main execution flow of the CPU, forcing it to branch to a predefined routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. After the ISR completes, the CPU resumes execution from the exact instruction where it was interrupted.

In the **Intel 8051 family of microcontrollers**, the interrupt system is a critical feature of the **Programmable Interrupt Controller (PIC)** block embedded within the CPU. The 8051 supports **six (6) interrupt sources**, with **two priority levels** (High and Low), and uses a fixed **vectored addressing** scheme to jump to the corresponding ISR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 supports **6 interrupts** — **Reset**, **External Hardware Interrupts (INT0 and INT1)**, **Timer Interrupts (TF0 and TF1)**, and **Serial Port Interrupt (TI/RI)**. Of these, **Reset is non-maskable**, while the remaining **5 are maskable** (can be enabled/disabled via software).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef and the Doorbell" Model

Imagine you are a chef cooking a complex meal (your **main program**). Each step in the recipe is sequential and important.

- **Polling Method:** You keep stopping to check the door every 30 seconds. This wastes time and slows down cooking. → CPU wastes cycles continuously checking a flag.
- **Interrupt Method:** You focus entirely on cooking. The moment the **doorbell rings** (an **interrupt signal**), you **pause** the current step, **remember your place** in the recipe (the **stack push** of the Program Counter), **answer the door** (execute the **ISR**), and then **return** to cooking exactly where you left off (the **RETI** instruction).

This is the essence of interrupt-driven programming: **efficiency, responsiveness, and real-time event handling**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Constants

| Parameter | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| **8051 Clock Source (typical)** | **11.0592 MHz** crystal (chosen for clean **9600 baud** generation) |
| **Number of Interrupt Sources** | **6** (Reset + 5 maskable) |
| **Interrupt Priority Levels** | **2** (High / Low) |
| **Default Priority Order (high → low)** | Reset > TF0 > INT0 > TF1 > INT1 > Serial (TI/RI) |
| **Interrupt Vector Table Location** | **Code memory addresses 0000H to 002BH** |
| **RETI Instruction Length** | **1 byte** (opcode 0x32) |
| **RETI vs RET** | **RETI** restores the interrupt enable state; **RET** does not (use RETI in ISRs!) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** This frequency is a multiple of the standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200). The Timer 1 reload value for **9600 baud** in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) is **0xFD = 253** → TH1 = 0xFD, giving **0% error**.

## 1.4 Why We Need Interrupts in Embedded C

> [!TIP]
> Without interrupts, the CPU must use **polling** — a wasteful busy-wait loop. Interrupts enable **event-driven programming**, which is foundational for **RTOS (Real-Time Operating Systems)**, **UART communication**, **timing-critical control**, and **low-power embedded design**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Interrupt Architecture — Six Sources Explained

### A. Reset (Address 0000H)
- **Highest priority**, non-maskable, activated at power-on or via RESET pin.
- Initializes all SFRs to known default states; PC is loaded with **0000H**.

### B. External Hardware Interrupts — INT0 and INT1 (Addresses 0003H, 0013H)
- Triggered by a **level (low)** or **edge (falling)** signal on **P3.2 (INT0)** or **P3.3 (INT1)** pin.
- The trigger mode is selected by bits **IT0** and **IT1** in the **TCON** register:
  - **ITx = 0** → Low-level triggered (must be held low until CPU services).
  - **ITx = 1** → Falling-edge triggered (latched, edge-sensitive).

### C. Timer Interrupts — TF0 and TF1 (Addresses 000BH, 001BH)
- Generated when the **Timer/Counter overflow flag (TF0 or TF1)** is set in the **TCON** register.
- **TF0** → Timer 0 overflow, **TF1** → Timer 1 overflow.

### D. Serial Port Interrupt — TI or RI (Address 0023H)
- Generated when either the **Transmit Interrupt (TI)** flag or the **Receive Interrupt (RI)** flag is set in the **SCON** register.
- Both flags must be **cleared by software** inside the ISR (8051 does NOT auto-clear them).

## 2.2 The Two Key SFRs for Interrupt Programming

### Register 1: IE — Interrupt Enable Register (Address 0xA8, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Function | Global Enable | — | — | Serial | Timer 1 | INT1 | Timer 0 | INT0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **EA (Enable All) = 1** → Globally enable all maskable interrupts. If EA = 0, **all interrupts are disabled** regardless of individual bits.
> - Each interrupt bit must be set to **1** to enable that specific interrupt.
> - **EA = 0** is the **default after reset** — interrupts are OFF until the programmer enables them.

### Register 2: IP — Interrupt Priority Register (Address 0xB8, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| Function | — | — | — | Serial Pri. | Timer 1 Pri. | INT1 Pri. | Timer 0 Pri. | INT0 Pri. |

- **Px = 1** → Assigns **High priority**; **Px = 0** → **Low priority** (default).
- A **High-priority** interrupt can preempt a **Low-priority** ISR (nested interrupts).
- Two interrupts of the same priority are resolved by the **default hardware polling order**.

## 2.3 Interrupt Vector Address Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | SFR to Clear |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reset** | — | **0000H** | — |
| **External 0 (INT0)** | IE0 | **0003H** | (auto-cleared if edge-triggered) |
| **Timer 0** | TF0 | **000BH** | (auto-cleared) |
| **External 1 (INT1)** | IE1 | **0013H** | (auto-cleared if edge-triggered) |
| **Timer 1** | TF1 | **001BH** | (auto-cleared) |
| **Serial Port** | TI \vert RI | **0023H** | **Must clear in software!** |

> [!WARNING]
> The vector addresses are spaced exactly **8 bytes apart** (0003H, 000BH, 0013H …). This is **insufficient** for most real ISRs! We must place an **LJMP** instruction at each vector address to redirect to the actual ISR located elsewhere in code memory.

## 2.4 Steps to Program Interrupts in C (Keil C51 Convention)

> [!TIP]
> **Six Mandatory Steps in Interrupt Programming in C:**

1. **Configure the Interrupt Type** (e.g., for edge/level trigger in TCON).
2. **Write the ISR** using the `interrupt` keyword with the correct vector number.
3. **Configure registers** (e.g., TMOD for timer mode, SCON for serial).
4. **Start the source** (e.g., `TR0 = 1` to start Timer 0).
5. **Enable the specific interrupt** (e.g., `ET0 = 1`).
6. **Enable the global flag** (`EA = 1`).

## 2.5 The `interrupt` and `using` Keywords in Keil C51

The Keil C51 compiler provides **two special keywords** to declare an ISR:

### Keyword 1: `interrupt <vector_number>`

| Vector Number | Interrupt Source |
|---|---|
| 0 | External Interrupt 0 |
| 1 | Timer 0 Overflow |
| 2 | External Interrupt 1 |
| 3 | Timer 1 Overflow |
| 4 | Serial Port (TI/RI) |

> The `interrupt` keyword instructs the compiler to:
> - Save the current state of registers (ACC, B, PSW, DPTR) onto the stack.
> - Place the function's start address in the **interrupt vector table**.
> - Generate a `RETI` instruction at the end of the function.

### Keyword 2: `using <register_bank_number>`

- Selects which **Register Bank (0, 1, 2, or 3)** to use for the ISR.
- This is a **performance optimization** — switching register banks avoids the overhead of pushing/popping R0–R7.
- Default is `using 0` (Register Bank 0).

### Canonical ISR Syntax

```c
void ISR_Name(void) interrupt <vector>  using <bank>
{
    // ISR body
}
```

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The interrupt system in 8051 is largely **register-based** rather than equation-based. However, a few derived metrics are tested:

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Timer Overflow Time** | $T = (2^n - \text{initial value}) \times T_{machine}$ | seconds, where $n$ = timer bit-width |
| **Machine Cycle Period** | $T_{machine} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | **12 clocks per machine cycle** for classic 8051 |
| **Baud Rate (Timer 1, Mode 2)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | **SMOD = 0** is the power-on default |
| **Vector Address Calculation** | $\text{Addr} = 0x0003 + 8 \times (\text{vector\_num} - 1)$ | For interrupts 1 through 4 |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Typically **3 to 7** machine cycles for the 8051 | Includes call latency |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** Engine control units use timer interrupts for **fuel injection timing** (every few ms).
- **Industrial PLCs:** External interrupts for **emergency stop buttons** (INT0, edge-triggered).
- **IoT Devices:** UART serial interrupts for **sensor data acquisition** without CPU busy-wait.
- **Medical Devices:** Real-time patient monitoring requires **deterministic interrupt response**.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machines, microwave ovens use timer interrupts for **delayed operations**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Machine Cycle Period for 8051

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 internally. This means one **machine cycle** consumes 12 oscillator periods.

$$
T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}
$$

Therefore, one machine cycle period is:

$$
T_{machine} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

**Derivation logic:** The internal clocking of the 8051 increments the program counter (and instruction phases) once every 12 crystal oscillations. This was an architectural choice to keep the internal datapath simple and to provide a clean integer division for common baud rates when $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.

$$
T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Practical implication:** Each instruction takes 1 to 4 machine cycles → 1.085 µs to 4.34 µs per instruction at 11.0592 MHz.

## 3.2 Derivation: Timer 1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud (SMOD = 0)

Starting from the canonical 8051 baud rate equation for **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**:

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute known values: Baud = 9600, SMOD = 0, $f_{osc}$ = 11.0592 MHz.

$$
9600 = \frac{2^0}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify $2^0 = 1$.

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by 32.

$$
9600 \times 32 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

$$
307200 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})$.

$$
307200 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1}) = 11.0592 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 5:** Compute $307200 \times 12$.

$$
3.6864 \times 10^6 \times (256 - \text{TH1}) = 11.0592 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 6:** Divide both sides by $3.6864 \times 10^6$.

$$
256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3.6864 \times 10^6} = 3
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for TH1.

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**Result:** $\text{TH1} = 0xFD$ (decimal 253) → **0% baud rate error**, the magic of 11.0592 MHz.

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Timer 0 Interrupt — Toggle P1.0 Every 50 ms (Mode 1)

### Step 1 — Compute the Timer Reload Value

With **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**, the machine cycle is:

$$
T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

In **Mode 1**, Timer 0 is a **16-bit timer**. For 50 ms total delay:

$$
\text{Counts required} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46080
$$

Since 16-bit max is 65536:

$$
\text{Initial value} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456 = 0x4C00
$$

Therefore: **TH0 = 0x4C**, **TL0 = 0x00**.

### Step 2 — Full Keil C51 Program

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;   // Define LED on P1.0

void timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0x4C;     // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x00;     // Reload low byte
    LED = ~LED;     // Toggle LED
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;    // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x4C;    // Initial high byte
    TL0  = 0x00;    // Initial low byte
    TR0  = 1;       // Start Timer 0
    ET0  = 1;       // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;       // Global enable
    
    while(1)         // Main loop does nothing
    {
        // CPU is free; LED is toggled by ISR
    }
}
```

### Step 3 — Explanation of Each Line

- `interrupt 1` → Maps this function to **vector 1** (Timer 0 at address 000BH).
- `TH0 = 0x4C; TL0 = 0x00;` → Must be reloaded manually because Mode 1 is **NOT auto-reload**.
- `LED = ~LED;` → Standard 8051 C does not have a `^=` operator for SFR bits; bit-toggle is done by **read-modify-write**.
- `while(1)` → Idle main loop — the **CPU is free** to do other tasks (this is the power of interrupts).

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: External Interrupt 0 (INT0) — Falling-Edge Triggered

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit SENSOR = P3^2;   // INT0 pin (also used as sensor input)
sbit ALARM  = P1^7;

void ext0_ISR(void) interrupt 0
{
    ALARM = 1;   // Activate alarm
    // Debouncing delay (simple software delay)
    unsigned int i;
    for(i = 0; i < 30000; i++);
    ALARM = 0;   // Deactivate alarm
}

void main(void)
{
    IT0 = 1;     // INT0: Falling-edge triggered
    EX0 = 1;     // Enable External Interrupt 0
    EA  = 1;     // Global enable
    
    ALARM = 0;   // Initialize alarm OFF
    SENSOR = 1;  // Make P3.2 high (for proper edge detection)
    
    while(1)
    {
        // Main program — can do other work
    }
}
```

### Analytical Notes:

- `IT0 = 1` → Configure **TCON.0** for **falling-edge** detection. This is written in the **TCON** register (address 0x88, bit-addressable).
- `SENSOR = 1` → Writing 1 to a port pin configures it as input (8051 quirk) and enables the internal pull-up.
- The ISR is mapped to **vector 0** (address 0003H).
- Inside the ISR, the **IE0 flag is automatically cleared by hardware** for edge-triggered interrupts.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Serial Port Interrupt — UART Echo

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void serial_ISR(void) interrupt 4
{
    if (TI == 1)        // Transmit complete?
    {
        TI = 0;         // MUST clear TI manually
    }
    else if (RI == 1)   // Receive complete?
    {
        char c = SBUF;  // Read received byte
        RI = 0;         // MUST clear RI manually
        SBUF = c;       // Echo back the received byte
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    SCON = 0x50;        // Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN enabled
    TMOD = 0x20;        // Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFD;        // 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
    TR1  = 1;           // Start Timer 1
    ES   = 1;           // Enable serial interrupt
    EA   = 1;           // Global enable
    
    while(1)
    {
        // Main loop idle — all UART handled by ISR
    }
}
```

### Why This Is Superior to Polling:

- The CPU **does not spend a single cycle** checking RI or TI.
- The program is **fully responsive** to incoming serial data.
- Power consumption is reduced (CPU can be put in **idle/power-down mode** between interrupts).

## 3.6 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix: Interrupt Programming Decisions

| Programming Decision | Option A | Option B | KTU Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger Mode (External)** | Level-triggered (ITx = 0) | Edge-triggered (ITx = 1) | **Edge-triggered** is preferred (auto-clears flag) |
| **Timer Mode** | Mode 1 (16-bit, manual reload) | Mode 2 (8-bit, auto-reload) | **Mode 2** for periodic tasks; **Mode 1** for long delays |
| **Register Bank** | using 0 (default) | using 1/2/3 | **using 1** is common for high-frequency ISRs |
| **Priority Assignment** | All default (low) | Mixed high/low | Assign **high priority** to time-critical interrupts (e.g., INT0 for emergency stop) |
| **Interrupt vs Polling** | Interrupt-driven | Polling (while loop) | **Interrupt-driven** for events; polling only for ultra-fast signals |

## 3.7 Common Pitfalls in Interrupt Programming (KTU Examiner Focus)

> [!WARNING]
> **Forgetting to clear TI/RI** in serial ISR — only these two flags require **manual clearing**!
> **Using `RET` instead of `RETI`** at the end of an ISR in assembly — in Keil C, the compiler handles this, but **do not write a custom `RETI`** in the function body.
> **Reload not done in Mode 1** — Timer overflows back to 0, not to your desired value!
> **Stack overflow** — ISRs push many registers; deep nesting or large local arrays may overflow the 8051's **128-byte internal RAM**.
> **Forgetting `EA = 1`** — Individual enable bits are useless without the **global enable**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Interrupt Processing Sequence in 8051

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Fetches Next Instruction] --> B{Interrupt Pending?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Complete Current Instruction]
    C --> D[Detect Interrupt by Hardware]
    D --> E[Push PC onto Stack]
    E --> F[Load PC from Vector Address]
    F --> G[Execute ISR Body]
    G --> H{More Interrupts Pending?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Service Higher Priority Interrupt]
    I --> G
    H -- No --> J[Execute RETI Instruction]
    J --> K[Pop PC from Stack]
    K --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The CPU checks for interrupts at the **end of every instruction** (not in the middle).
- The **current instruction always completes** before interrupt servicing.
- **RETI** (not RET) is the only valid way to exit an ISR — it signals the hardware to allow further interrupts.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: 8051 Interrupt Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC [Interrupt Sources]
        S1[INT0 P3.2]
        S2[Timer 0 TF0]
        S3[INT1 P3.3]
        S4[Timer 1 TF1]
        S5[Serial TI RI]
        S6[RESET Pin]
    end
    subgraph CTRL [Control Logic]
        REG1[TCON: IT0 IT1 IE0 IE1 TF0 TF1 TR0 TR1]
        REG2[IE: EA ES ET1 EX1 ET0 EX0]
        REG3[IP: PS PT1 PX1 PT0 PX0]
    end
    subgraph VEC [Vector Table in Code Memory]
        V0[0000H Reset]
        V1[0003H INT0]
        V2[000BH TF0]
        V3[0013H INT1]
        V4[001BH TF1]
        V5[0023H Serial]
    end
    S1 --> REG1
    S2 --> REG1
    S3 --> REG1
    S4 --> REG1
    S5 --> REG1
    REG1 --> REG2
    REG2 --> REG3
    REG3 --> VEC
    VEC --> CPU[CPU Execution Unit]
```

**Key insight from the diagram:**
- The **TCON** register captures the **raw event flags** (TF0, TF1, IE0, IE1) and the **trigger mode bits** (IT0, IT1).
- The **IE** register acts as the **gate** — no interrupt reaches the CPU unless both its individual bit AND the global EA bit are set.
- The **IP** register is the **arbiter** — it decides which interrupt wins when two are pending simultaneously.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Timer Interrupt Lifecycle (C Code)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU
    participant T0 as Timer 0 Hardware
    participant ISR as timer0_ISR interrupt 1
    participant LED as P1.0 LED
    CPU->>T0: TR0 = 1 (Start Timer)
    Note over CPU: main() enters while(1) loop
    loop Every 50 ms
        T0->>T0: Counter increments
        T0->>T0: Overflow from 0xFFFF to 0x0000
        T0-->>CPU: TF0 flag set, Interrupt Request
        CPU->>CPU: Finish current instruction
        CPU->>CPU: Push PC onto stack
        CPU->>ISR: Jump to vector 0x000B
        ISR->>T0: TH0 = 0x4C, TL0 = 0x00 (Reload)
        ISR->>LED: LED = ~LED (Toggle)
        ISR-->>CPU: RETI (Pop PC, return)
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Interrupt Enable/Disable Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Reset
    Reset --> AllDisabled: Default state EA = 0
    AllDisabled --> GlobalEnabled: EA = 1
    GlobalEnabled --> SpecificEnabled: Ex = 1
    GlobalEnabled --> SpecificEnabled: ETx = 1
    GlobalEnabled --> SpecificEnabled: ES = 1
    SpecificEnabled --> ISRExecuting: Interrupt event
    ISRExecuting --> SpecificEnabled: RETI
    SpecificEnabled --> AllDisabled: EA = 0
    SpecificEnabled --> AllDisabled: Individual bit = 0
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Q1. What is an interrupt? List the six interrupts of the 8051 microcontroller. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

An interrupt is an asynchronous event that temporarily halts the main program execution, causing the CPU to execute a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR finishes (via `RETI`), the CPU resumes the main program from the point of interruption.

The six interrupts of the 8051 are:
1. **Reset** (vector 0000H, non-maskable)
2. **External Interrupt 0 — INT0** (vector 0003H, P3.2)
3. **Timer 0 Overflow — TF0** (vector 000BH)
4. **External Interrupt 1 — INT1** (vector 0013H, P3.3)
5. **Timer 1 Overflow — TF1** (vector 001BH)
6. **Serial Port Interrupt — TI/RI** (vector 0023H)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Q2. Differentiate between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Level-Triggered (ITx = 0) | Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1) |
|---|---|---|
| **Activation** | Held **low** until serviced | **Falling edge** (high-to-low transition) |
| **Flag clearing** | **NOT auto-cleared** by hardware | **Auto-cleared** by hardware on ISR entry |
| **Re-triggering** | Can re-enter if pin is still low | Latched — only one event per edge |
| **Use case** | Wired-OR interrupt sharing | Clean event notification (e.g., button press) |
| **TCON bit** | IT0 = 0 (for INT0) | IT0 = 1 (for INT0) |

Edge-triggered is generally preferred in embedded design because it avoids the spurious double-interrupt problem common in level-triggered mode.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**a)** Explain the **Interrupt Enable (IE)** and **Interrupt Priority (IP)** registers of the 8051 with bit diagrams. Discuss the role of the **EA** bit. (7 marks)

**b)** Write an 8051 C program to generate a **square wave of 1 kHz** on pin **P1.0** using **Timer 0 interrupt** in **Mode 1**. Assume crystal frequency = **11.0592 MHz**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The **IE (Interrupt Enable) register** is located at SFR address **0xA8** and is bit-addressable:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Default | 0 | x | x | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- **EA (Enable All, bit 7):** The **global master switch**. If EA = 0, **all maskable interrupts are disabled** irrespective of the individual bits. After reset, EA = 0; the programmer must set EA = 1 to allow any interrupt.
- **ES (bit 4):** Enables the **serial port interrupt** (TI or RI).
- **ET1 (bit 3):** Enables **Timer 1 overflow** interrupt.
- **EX1 (bit 2):** Enables **External Interrupt 1** (P3.3).
- **ET0 (bit 1):** Enables **Timer 0 overflow** interrupt.
- **EX0 (bit 0):** Enables **External Interrupt 0** (P3.2).
- Bits 5 and 6 are **reserved** (undefined in classic 8051).

The **IP (Interrupt Priority) register** is at address **0xB8**, also bit-addressable:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |

- Setting a bit to **1** assigns **high priority**; **0** assigns **low priority** (default).
- A high-priority ISR can **preempt** (nest into) a low-priority ISR.
- Within the same priority level, the **default polling order** applies: INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial.

**Role of EA in nested interrupts:** Even if a high-priority interrupt is configured, it will not be recognized during a low-priority ISR unless EA = 1. The `RETI` instruction restores the interrupt enable state to allow nesting.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Frequency Analysis:**

For a 1 kHz square wave, the **period T = 1 ms**. Since a full period requires **two toggles** (high-to-low and low-to-high), the toggle interval is **T/2 = 0.5 ms = 500 µs**.

**Step 2 — Compute Timer Reload Value:**

Machine cycle period:

$$
T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

Counts required for 500 µs:

$$
N = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46080
$$

Since 46080 < 65536 (16-bit max), **Mode 1 is valid**.

Initial timer value:

$$
\text{Init} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456 = 0x4C00
$$

Therefore: **TH0 = 0x4C**, **TL0 = 0x00**.

**Step 3 — Complete C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit WAVE = P1^0;

void timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0x4C;     // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x00;     // Reload low byte
    WAVE = ~WAVE;   // Toggle P1.0
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;    // T0 in Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x4C;    // Load initial value
    TL0  = 0x00;
    TR0  = 1;       // Start Timer 0
    ET0  = 1;       // Enable T0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;       // Global interrupt enable
    
    while(1);       // Idle main loop
}
```

**Output verification:** ISR fires every 500 µs → toggles P1.0 → full period = 1 ms → **f = 1 kHz** ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the **six interrupt sources** of the 8051 with their **vector addresses**. List the **SFR bits** that must be configured to enable and prioritize each. (7 marks)

**b)** Write an 8051 C program to **receive a byte** through the **serial port** (9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz) and **echo it back** using **serial port interrupt**. The received byte should also be displayed on **Port P2** via LEDs. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**The Six Interrupt Sources of 8051:**

| # | Interrupt | Source | Vector Address | Flag Bit | Enable Bit | Priority Bit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reset** | RESET pin / Power-on | **0000H** | — | (non-maskable) | Always highest |
| 2 | **External 0 (INT0)** | P3.2 pin | **0003H** | IE0 (TCON.1) | EX0 (IE.0) | PX0 (IP.0) |
| 3 | **Timer 0** | TF0 overflow | **000BH** | TF0 (TCON.5) | ET0 (IE.1) | PT0 (IP.1) |
| 4 | **External 1 (INT1)** | P3.3 pin | **0013H** | IE1 (TCON.3) | EX1 (IE.2) | PX1 (IP.2) |
| 5 | **Timer 1** | TF1 overflow | **001BH** | TF1 (TCON.7) | ET1 (IE.3) | PT1 (IP.3) |
| 6 | **Serial Port** | TI or RI set | **0023H** | TI/RI (SCON) | ES (IE.4) | PS (IP.4) |

**Key Configuration Notes:**
- For **external interrupts**, the trigger mode (level vs edge) is set by **IT0** and **IT1** bits in TCON.
- The **global enable bit EA** (IE.7) must be set for any of the five maskable interrupts to be recognized.
- For the **serial interrupt**, both TI and RI share the same vector; the ISR must check which one caused the interrupt and clear the corresponding flag **manually**.
- The vector addresses are spaced **8 bytes** apart, which is often insufficient for real ISRs — typically, a `LJMP` instruction is placed at each vector to redirect to the actual ISR.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void serial_ISR(void) interrupt 4
{
    if (RI == 1)
    {
        unsigned char received = SBUF;  // Read received byte
        P2    = received;               // Display on Port 2 LEDs
        SBUF  = received;               // Echo back to sender
        RI    = 0;                      // Clear RI flag
        // Note: TI will be set after SBUF write;
        // clear it after echo transmission completes
    }
    if (TI == 1)
    {
        TI = 0;    // Clear TI after transmission
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    SCON = 0x50;    // Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN = 1
    TMOD = 0x20;    // Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFD;    // 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz, SMOD = 0
    TR1  = 1;       // Start Timer 1
    
    ES   = 1;       // Enable serial interrupt
    EA   = 1;       // Global enable
    
    while(1)
    {
        // Main loop free for other tasks
    }
}
```

**Verification:** When a byte is sent from a PC (or another 8051) over UART, the RI flag is set → CPU jumps to `serial_ISR` at vector 0023H → the byte is read from `SBUF`, displayed on P2, and echoed back via `SBUF`. The main `while(1)` loop remains free.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in Interrupt Programming Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to clear RI/TI manually** — the 8051 hardware clears TF0, TF1, IE0, IE1 automatically, but **TI and RI MUST be cleared by software**. (-2 marks if missed)
> 
> 2. **Not reloading TH0/TL0 in Mode 1** — Mode 1 is **not auto-reload**. Students often forget to write `TH0 = …; TL0 = …;` at the START of the ISR, causing wrong timing. (-2 to -3 marks)
> 
> 3. **Forgetting `EA = 1`** — The most common mistake. Individual enables (EX0, ET0, etc.) are useless without the **global enable**. (-1 mark)
> 
> 4. **Wrong vector number** — The Keil C51 `interrupt` keyword uses **0-based numbering** (0 = INT0, 1 = TF0, 2 = INT1, 3 = TF1, 4 = Serial). Students confuse this with the **hardware priority order** (which is also 0-based but indexed differently). (-2 marks)
> 
> 5. **Using `RET` instead of `RETI` in assembly-based ISRs** — `RET` does not signal the interrupt controller to allow further interrupts, leading to a **locked-up system** after the first interrupt. In Keil C, the compiler auto-inserts `RETI`, so students should not manually write it. (-2 marks)
> 
> 6. **Incorrect baud rate reload value** — Using `TH1 = 0xF3` (for 12 MHz crystal) in a program that assumes `11.0592 MHz` will produce **2.1% baud error**, which may fail communication. (-1 to -2 marks)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist — Interrupt Programming in C (8051)**

- [x] The 8051 has **6 interrupts**; 5 are maskable, 1 (Reset) is non-maskable.
- [x] Default vector table starts at **0000H**; each interrupt vector is **8 bytes** apart.
- [x] Three critical SFRs: **TCON** (flags + trigger mode), **IE** (enables), **IP** (priorities).
- [x] **EA = 1** is **mandatory** for any maskable interrupt to be serviced.
- [x] Keil C51 ISR syntax: `void isr(void) interrupt <0..4> using <0..3>`.
- [x] **Vector numbers in Keil C**: 0=INT0, 1=TF0, 2=INT1, 3=TF1, 4=Serial.
- [x] **TI and RI must be cleared manually** in the serial ISR.
- [x] **Mode 1 timers need manual reload**; **Mode 2 timers auto-reload** from THx.
- [x] External interrupts: **ITx = 1** → falling-edge (preferred), **ITx = 0** → low-level.
- [x] High-priority interrupt can **preempt** a low-priority ISR (nested interrupts).
- [x] `RETI` restores the interrupt enable state; `RET` does **not** — use only `RETI`.
- [x] Default priority order: **Reset > INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial**.
- [x] Crystal **11.0592 MHz** + **TH1 = 0xFD** → **9600 baud with 0% error**.
- [x] The CPU checks for interrupts only at **instruction boundaries**, not mid-instruction.
- [x] Interrupts save **ACC, B, PSW, DPTR** automatically; the ISR need not preserve them.
- [x] Stack size is limited to **128 bytes** of internal RAM — avoid large local arrays in ISRs.

> **End of Module 3 — Interrupt Programming in C Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing: LCD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfacing: LCD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
An **LCD (Liquid Crystal Display)** in embedded systems is a flat-panel electronic visual display that uses the light-modulating properties of liquid crystals, controlled by a dedicated on-board controller (most commonly the **Hitachi HD44780** or its equivalent). In KTU 8051/ARM microcontroller architecture, LCD interfacing refers to the hardware connection and software routines required to map the microcontroller's parallel port pins to the LCD's data bus, control lines, and power terminals, enabling the display of alphanumeric characters, custom symbols, and system status.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 16x2 character LCD is the *de facto* standard display peripheral used in KTU lab examinations. The HD44780 controller is **8-bit compatible but supports a 4-bit mode** to save microcontroller I/O pins — a frequent KTU Part-B question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Bulletin Board"**
> Think of the LCD as a digital **bulletin board in a school corridor**. The **DDRAM (Display Data RAM)** is the actual board surface where notices are pinned. The **microcontroller** is the school office deciding *what* to write. The **RS (Register Select) pin** is like a sign saying *"To the Principal's Desk"* (Command) or *"To the Notice Board"* (Data). The **Enable (E) pin** is the *moment the office boy presses 'Send'* — without that pulse, nothing moves. The **VEE pin** is the *light dimmer* controlling how dark the ink appears. Just as the office must follow a strict protocol (write notice, get approval, post it), the MCU must follow a strict initialization sequence before any character appears.

### 1.3 Standard Physical/Display Metrics
- **Operating Voltage (VDD):** **+5V DC** (±5% tolerance)
- **Logic Low (VIL):** **0.0 V to 0.8 V**
- **Logic High (VIH):** **2.2 V to VDD**
- **Standard Display Sizes:** 16x2, 20x4 (characters × rows)
- **Character Font:** 5x7 dot matrix (with 5x8 option)
- **DDRAM Capacity:** 80 bytes (addresses 0x00 to 0x4F)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Always remember — the second line of a 16x2 LCD does **NOT** start at address 0x20. It starts at **0x40** for a 16x2 module, and **0x14** for a 20x4 module. This is a **classic KTU 2-mark trap!**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HD44780 Pin Architecture
The LCD module has **16 pins** (standard single-line header):

| Pin | Symbol | Direction | Function |
|:----|:-------|:----------|:---------|
| 1 | VSS | — | Ground (0V) |
| 2 | VDD | — | Power Supply (**+5V**) |
| 3 | VEE | — | Contrast Adjustment (via potentiometer) |
| 4 | **RS** | Input | Register Select (0=Command, 1=Data) |
| 5 | **RW** | Input | Read/Write (0=Write, 1=Read) |
| 6 | **E** | Input | Enable (active HIGH data latch) |
| 7–14 | D0–D7 | Bidirectional | 8-bit Data Bus |
| 15 | A | — | Anode (Backlight +5V) |
| 16 | K | — | Cathode (Backlight GND) |

### 2.2 Control Signal Logic Truth Table
The HD44780 is controlled by the combination of **RS** and **R/W** lines:

| RS | R/W | Operation | Description |
|:---|:----|:----------|:------------|
| 0 | 0 | **Command Write** | MCU sends instruction (Clear, Cursor, Function Set) |
| 0 | 1 | **Status Read** | Read Busy Flag (BF) and Address Counter |
| 1 | 0 | **Data Write** | Write ASCII character to DDRAM/CGRAM |
| 1 | 1 | **Data Read** | Read content of DDRAM/CGRAM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Enable Pulse matters:** The HD44780 uses an *edge-triggered* latch. Data and commands are read on the **FALLING edge of E** (HIGH → LOW transition). The E pin must be held HIGH for a minimum duration (Enable Pulse Width, **tW ≥ 450 ns** for standard HD44780) before being pulled low.

### 2.3 LCD Command Set (High-Yield for KTU)
The following table is the **single most important table** for KTU LCD questions:

| Hex Code | Command | Execution Time |
|:---------|:--------|:---------------|
| **0x01** | Clear Display | 1.64 ms |
| **0x02** | Return Cursor Home | 1.64 ms |
| **0x04** | Entry Mode Set — Decrement cursor, No shift | 40 µs |
| **0x06** | Entry Mode Set — Increment cursor, No shift | 40 µs |
| **0x0E** | Display ON, Cursor ON, Blink OFF | 40 µs |
| **0x0C** | Display ON, Cursor OFF | 40 µs |
| **0x0F** | Display ON, Cursor ON, Blink ON | 40 µs |
| **0x10** | Shift Cursor Left | 40 µs |
| **0x14** | Shift Cursor Right | 40 µs |
| **0x18** | Shift Entire Display Left | 40 µs |
| **0x1C** | Shift Entire Display Right | 40 µs |
| **0x20** | 4-bit, 1 line, 5x7 dots | 40 µs |
| **0x28** | **4-bit, 2 lines, 5x7 dots** | 40 µs |
| **0x30** | 8-bit, 1 line, 5x7 dots | 40 µs |
| **0x38** | **8-bit, 2 lines, 5x7 dots** | 40 µs |
| **0x80** | Set DDRAM Address 0x00 (Line 1 start) | 40 µs |
| **0xC0** | Set DDRAM Address 0x40 (Line 2 start) | 40 µs |

### 2.4 Interfacing Topologies
**(A) 8-bit Mode:**
- Uses all 8 data lines (D0–D7) plus RS, RW, E = **11 MCU pins total**.
- Faster data throughput.
- Preferred when MCU pin count is not a constraint.

**(B) 4-bit Mode:**
- Uses only D4–D7 plus RS, RW, E = **7 MCU pins total**.
- Each byte is sent as **two nibbles** (HIGH nibble first, then LOW).
- Saves **4 I/O pins** for other peripherals — crucial in KTU mini-projects.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Engineering Application |
|:----------|:----------------|:------------------------|
| Total pins used (8-bit mode) | $N_{8} = 8 + 3 = 11$ | GPIO budget planning |
| Total pins used (4-bit mode) | $N_{4} = 4 + 3 = 7$ | GPIO budget planning |
| LCD Total Characters | $C_{total} = R \times L$ | Where R = rows, L = line length |
| Initialization delay (Power-on) | $t_{POR} \geq 15$ ms | After VDD reaches 4.5V |
| Function Set command delay | $t_{FS} \geq 4.1$ ms (first), 100 µs (subsequent) | Reset sequence |
| Enable Pulse Width | $t_{W} \geq 450$ ns | Write timing |
| Enable Cycle Time | $t_{C} \geq 1$ µs | Write/Read timing |
| Data Setup Time | $t_{DSU} \geq 195$ ns | Before E falling edge |
| Data Hold Time | $t_{DH} \geq 10$ ns | After E falling edge |
| Address Counter Increment | $AC_{new} = AC_{old} + 1$ (with 0x06 command) | Auto-increment in entry mode |
| Display shift offset | $S = \pm 1$ per shift command | For scrolling effect |
| Line 2 DDRAM base address | $\text{Addr}_{L2} = 0x40$ | **KTU trap address** |
| Line 3 DDRAM base (20x4) | $\text{Addr}_{L3} = 0x14$ | 20x4 module only |
| Line 4 DDRAM base (20x4) | $\text{Addr}_{L4} = 0x54$ | 20x4 module only |
| CGRAM address | $0x00 \leq \text{Addr} \leq 0x3F$ | Custom character storage |
| ASCII 'A' hex value | $0x41$ | Data write example |
| Busy Flag check (polling) | $BF = 1 \Rightarrow$ busy, wait | Replaces fixed delay |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility
LCD interfacing is foundational to **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** design in:
- Industrial control panels (PLCs, VFD drives)
- Medical devices (glucometers, infusion pumps)
- Consumer electronics (microwave ovens, washing machines)
- Automotive dashboards and instrument clusters
- IoT edge devices with local status display
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pin Count Savings in 4-bit Mode
The motivation for 4-bit mode is GPIO conservation. Let us derive the savings quantitatively.

**Step 1:** Count control pins (always required, regardless of mode)
The LCD requires three mandatory control signals: RS, R/W, and E.
Therefore, the control pin count is:
$$
N_{ctrl} = 3
$$

**Step 2:** Count data pins in 8-bit mode
In 8-bit mode, the full data bus D0 through D7 is used:
$$
N_{data,8} = 8
$$

**Step 3:** Count data pins in 4-bit mode
In 4-bit mode, only D4 through D7 are connected:
$$
N_{data,4} = 8 - 4 = 4
$$

**Step 4:** Total pins in each mode
For 8-bit mode:
$$
N_{total,8} = N_{ctrl} + N_{data,8} = 3 + 8 = 11 \text{ pins}
$$
For 4-bit mode:
$$
N_{total,4} = N_{ctrl} + N_{data,4} = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ pins}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the savings
The pin savings $S$ is given by the difference:
$$
S = N_{total,8} - N_{total,4} = 11 - 7 = 4 \text{ pins}
$$

**Step 6:** Express as a percentage
$$
\%\text{Savings} = \frac{S}{N_{total,8}} \times 100 = \frac{4}{11} \times 100 \approx 36.36\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** The 4-bit interface frees **4 GPIO pins (36.36% savings)** at the cost of doubling the transmission time per byte (two nibble transfers instead of one byte).

### 3.2 Derivation: DDRAM Address Mapping for 16x2 LCD
The HD44780 has 80 bytes of DDRAM (addresses 0x00 to 0x4F). However, only 32 are visible on a 16x2 display (16 chars × 2 lines).

**Step 1:** Identify visible address range for Line 1
The first visible character of Line 1 corresponds to DDRAM address 0x00. The last visible character of Line 1 corresponds to address:
$$
\text{Addr}_{L1,end} = 0x00 + (16 - 1) = 0x0F
$$

**Step 2:** Identify base address of Line 2
Although the DDRAM is contiguous, the HD44780 internally skips the range 0x10 to 0x3F and routes the second line to start at 0x40. The base address is:
$$
\text{Addr}_{L2,base} = 0x40
$$

**Step 3:** Identify last visible address of Line 2
$$
\text{Addr}_{L2,end} = 0x40 + (16 - 1) = 0x4F
$$

**Step 4:** Address command for Line 1 start
To position the cursor at the first character of Line 1, the command word is formed by setting DB7 = 1 and adding the address:
$$
\text{Command}_{L1} = 0x80 \text{ OR } \text{Addr}_{L1,base} = 0x80 \text{ OR } 0x00 = 0x80
$$

**Step 5:** Address command for Line 2 start
$$
\text{Command}_{L2} = 0x80 \text{ OR } \text{Addr}_{L2,base} = 0x80 \text{ OR } 0x40 = 0xC0
$$

**Step 6:** General address command
For any cursor position $A$ in the visible range $[0x00, 0x4F]$:
$$
\text{Command}_{addr} = 0x80 \text{ OR } A
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Transmission Time Penalty in 4-bit Mode
Each character requires **one byte** to transmit. In 8-bit mode, this is a single transfer. In 4-bit mode, it is split into two nibble transfers.

**Step 1:** Time per transfer
Let $t_{cycle}$ be the time for one enable cycle. From the datasheet:
$$
t_{cycle} \geq 1 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 2:** Time to send 1 character in 8-bit mode
$$
T_{8} = 1 \times t_{cycle} = 1 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3:** Time to send 1 character in 4-bit mode
$$
T_{4} = 2 \times t_{cycle} = 2 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 4:** Overhead percentage
$$
\text{Overhead} = \frac{T_{4} - T_{8}}{T_{8}} \times 100 = \frac{2 - 1}{1} \times 100 = 100\%
$$

**Conclusion:** 4-bit mode takes **twice the time** of 8-bit mode per character, which is acceptable for human-readable text but problematic for high-speed data displays.

### 3.4 Standard Initialization Sequence (Analytical Matrix)
The HD44780 requires a strict power-on initialization. Below is the **canonical KTU-mandated sequence** for 8-bit mode:

| Step | Action | Command/Data | Delay After | Purpose |
|:----:|:-------|:-------------|:------------|:--------|
| 1 | Power ON | — | $\geq 15$ ms | VDD rise to 4.5V |
| 2 | Function Set | 0x30 | 4.1 ms | Wake up in 8-bit mode |
| 3 | Function Set | 0x30 | 100 µs | Confirm 8-bit mode |
| 4 | Function Set | 0x30 | 40 µs | Confirm 8-bit mode |
| 5 | Function Set | 0x38 | 40 µs | Set 2 lines, 5x7 font |
| 6 | Display OFF | 0x08 | 40 µs | Clear display state |
| 7 | Clear Display | 0x01 | 1.64 ms | Clear DDRAM |
| 8 | Entry Mode | 0x06 | 40 µs | Auto-increment cursor |
| 9 | Display ON | 0x0C | 40 µs | Enable display |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LCD-to-Microcontroller Interfacing Block Diagram (8-bit Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[8051 Microcontroller] -- "P0.0-P0.7" --> LCDDATA[LCD Data Bus D0-D7]
    MCU -- "P1.0" --> LCDRS[LCD RS Pin]
    MCU -- "P1.1" --> LCDRW[LCD RW Pin]
    MCU -- "P1.2" --> LCDE[LCD E Pin]
    LCDVCC[+5V Supply] --> LCDVDD[LCD VDD]
    LCDGND[Ground] --> LCDVSS[LCD VSS]
    LCDPOT[10K Potentiometer] --> LCDVEE[LCD VEE Contrast]
    LCDVCC --> LBLA[LCD A Backlight Anode]
    LCDGND --> LBLK[LCD K Backlight Cathode]
    LCDDATA --> HD44780[HD44780 Controller]
    LCDRS --> HD44780
    LCDRW --> HD44780
    LCDE --> HD44780
    HD44780 --> DDDRAM[80-byte DDRAM]
    HD44780 --> CGGRAM[64-byte CGRAM]
    HD44780 --> CGROM[Character Generator ROM 192 chars]
```

### 4.2 HD44780 Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT [Input Interface]
        DBUS[D0-D7 Data Bus]
        RSIN[RS Pin]
        RWIN[RW Pin]
        EIN[E Pin]
    end
    subgraph CTRL [Control Block]
        IR[Instruction Register]
        DR[Data Register]
        DECODE[Instruction Decoder]
        TGEN[Timing Generator]
    end
    subgraph MEM [Memory Blocks]
        DD[80-byte DDRAM]
        CG[64-byte CGRAM]
        CHR[192-char CGROM]
    end
    subgraph DRV [Output Driver]
        SSEG[Segment Driver 40]
        COMD[Common Driver 16]
        LQP[Liquid Crystal Panel 16x2]
    end
    DBUS --> IR
    DBUS --> DR
    RSIN --> DECODE
    RWIN --> DECODE
    EIN --> TGEN
    IR --> DECODE
    DECODE --> DD
    DECODE --> CG
    DECODE --> CHR
    DR --> DD
    DR --> CG
    DD --> SSEG
    CG --> SSEG
    CHR --> SSEG
    SSEG --> LQP
    COMD --> LQP
    TGEN --> COMD
    TGEN --> SSEG
```

### 4.3 Command vs Data Write Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start MCU Action] --> SETRS{Set RS Pin Level}
    SETRS -- "RS = 0" --> CMD[Command Mode]
    SETRS -- "RS = 1" --> DAT[Data Mode]
    CMD --> SETRW1[Set RW = 0 for Write]
    DAT --> SETRW2[Set RW = 0 for Write]
    SETRW1 --> PUTCMD[Place Command Byte on D0-D7]
    SETRW2 --> PUTASC[Place ASCII Byte on D0-D7]
    PUTCMD --> PULSE1[Pulse E HIGH then LOW]
    PUTASC --> PULSE2[Pulse E HIGH then LOW]
    PULSE1 --> LCD1[LCD Latches Command into IR]
    PULSE2 --> LCD2[LCD Writes to DDRAM at Address Counter]
    LCD1 --> UPD1[Update Address Counter or Display]
    LCD2 --> UPD2[Increment Address Counter by 1]
    UPD1 --> END[End]
    UPD2 --> END
```

### 4.4 4-bit Mode Nibble Transmission Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as Microcontroller
    participant LCD as HD44780 LCD
    Note over MCU,LCD: Send Byte 0x28 (Function Set: 4-bit, 2 lines)
    MCU->>LCD: Set RS=0, RW=0
    MCU->>LCD: Place HIGH NIBBLE 0x2 on D4-D7
    MCU->>LCD: Pulse E HIGH then LOW
    LCD-->>LCD: Latch HIGH nibble into internal register
    MCU->>LCD: Place LOW NIBBLE 0x8 on D4-D7
    MCU->>LCD: Pulse E HIGH then LOW
    LCD-->>LCD: Combine nibbles, execute 0x28
    Note over MCU,LCD: All subsequent bytes follow same pattern
    MCU->>LCD: Send data byte as two consecutive nibbles
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Explain the function of the **RS** and **E** pins of an HD44780 LCD module. Why is the Enable pin said to be edge-triggered?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **RS (Register Select) pin** — Logic **0** selects the **Instruction Register (IR)** for command words (Clear, Cursor Position, Function Set). Logic **1** selects the **Data Register (DR)** for writing ASCII characters to DDRAM. **(1 mark)**

- **E (Enable) pin** — Acts as the **active-high data latch signal**. The LCD samples the data bus (D0–D7) and the RS/RW lines only on the **falling edge** (HIGH→LOW transition) of E. This is why it is called edge-triggered. **(1 mark)**

- **Timing requirement:** The HIGH duration of E (Enable Pulse Width, $t_W$) must be at least **450 ns**, and the full enable cycle $t_C \geq 1$ µs, before another write can be initiated. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
List the DDRAM base addresses for Line 1 and Line 2 of a 16x2 LCD. Write the command bytes to position the cursor at column 5 of Line 1 and column 10 of Line 2.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Line 1 base address:** 0x00, **Line 2 base address:** 0x40. **(1 mark)**

- **Cursor at Line 1, Column 5:** Address = 0x00 + (5−1) = 0x04. Command byte = **0x80 OR 0x04 = 0x84**. **(1 mark)**

- **Cursor at Line 2, Column 10:** Address = 0x40 + (10−1) = 0x49. Command byte = **0x80 OR 0x49 = 0xC9**. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *14-mark Module-3 Internal Choice Question*

**Question A:**
(a) Draw the interfacing circuit diagram of a 16x2 HD44780 LCD with the 8051 microcontroller in **8-bit mode**. List the pin connections clearly. **(7 marks)**

(b) Explain the **initialization sequence** of the HD44780 LCD in 8-bit mode. Why are the first three Function Set commands followed by different delays? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Interfacing Circuit (7 marks):**

The connections are tabulated below. **Port P1** of the 8051 is used for control, and **Port P0** is used for the data bus.

| 8051 Pin | LCD Pin | Function |
|:---------|:--------|:---------|
| P0.0 – P0.7 | D0 – D7 | Data Bus (8-bit) |
| P1.0 | RS | Register Select |
| P1.1 | RW | Read/Write (tied to GND for write-only) |
| P1.2 | E | Enable |
| VCC (+5V) | VDD, A | Power and Backlight Anode |
| GND | VSS, K | Ground and Backlight Cathode |
| 10 KΩ POT | VEE | Contrast Adjustment |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[8051 Port P0] -- "P0.0-P0.7" --> BUS[LCD D0-D7]
    P1[8051 Port P1] -- "P1.0" --> RS[RS]
    P1 -- "P1.1" --> RW[RW]
    P1 -- "P1.2" --> EN[E]
    BUS --> LCD[HD44780 16x2 LCD Module]
    RS --> LCD
    RW --> LCD
    EN --> LCD
```

**(4 marks for correct table + 3 marks for diagram)**

---

**(b) Initialization Sequence (7 marks):**

| Step | Command | Hex | Delay | Reason |
|:----:|:--------|:----|:------|:-------|
| 1 | Wait after power ON | — | **15 ms** | VDD must stabilize at $\geq 4.5$V **(1 mark)** |
| 2 | Function Set | 0x30 | **4.1 ms** | First command always 0x30; long delay because the LCD may not have completed internal reset **(2 marks)** |
| 3 | Function Set | 0x30 | **100 µs** | Confirms 8-bit mode; shorter delay acceptable as LCD is now responsive **(1 mark)** |
| 4 | Function Set | 0x30 | **40 µs** | Final confirmation; LCD now in known state **(0.5 marks)** |
| 5 | Function Set | 0x38 | 40 µs | Sets **2 lines, 5x7 font, 8-bit interface** **(1 mark)** |
| 6 | Display OFF | 0x08 | 40 µs | Clears any prior display state |
| 7 | Clear Display | 0x01 | **1.64 ms** | Wipes DDRAM; long delay as all 80 bytes are reset |
| 8 | Entry Mode | 0x06 | 40 µs | Auto-increment cursor |
| 9 | Display ON | 0x0C | 40 µs | Final display enable |

**Why different delays for the first three 0x30 commands:** The HD44780 is guaranteed to recognize commands only after VDD has stabilized. The very first command must be sent after **15 ms** because the internal reset circuit of the LCD may still be in progress. The first 0x30 must wait **4.1 ms** because the LCD's reset oscillator may still be running. Subsequent commands can use shorter delays (100 µs, 40 µs) because the LCD is now synchronized. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Alternative 14-mark Internal Choice*

**Question B:**
(a) With a neat diagram, explain the **internal block diagram** of the HD44780 LCD controller. What are the roles of DDRAM, CGRAM, and CGROM? **(7 marks)**

(b) Compare **8-bit and 4-bit** interfacing modes of the LCD with the 8051. Mention the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HD44780 Internal Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The HD44780 consists of the following functional blocks: **(Refer Section 4.2 diagram above)**

| Block | Size | Role |
|:------|:-----|:-----|
| **DDRAM** (Display Data RAM) | **80 bytes** (0x00–0x4F) | Stores the ASCII codes of characters currently displayed. Only 32 bytes are visible at a time on a 16x2 LCD. **(2 marks)** |
| **CGRAM** (Character Generator RAM) | **64 bytes** (0x00–0x3F) | Stores user-defined custom characters (max **8 custom 5x8 characters** at addresses 0x00–0x3F). **(2 marks)** |
| **CGROM** (Character Generator ROM) | **192 characters** | Pre-programmed ROM containing standard ASCII, Japanese katakana, and some European symbols. **(1 mark)** |
| Instruction Register (IR) | 8-bit | Latches command words from the data bus. |
| Data Register (DR) | 8-bit | Temporarily holds data to be written to DDRAM/CGRAM. **(1 mark)** |
| Address Counter (AC) | 7-bit | Points to current DDRAM/CGRAM address; auto-increments/decrements per Entry Mode setting. **(1 mark)** |
| Busy Flag (BF) | 1-bit | Indicates internal operation; BF=1 means busy, BF=0 means ready. |

---

**(b) 8-bit vs 4-bit Interfacing Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | 8-bit Mode | 4-bit Mode |
|:----------|:-----------|:-----------|
| **Data Lines Used** | D0 – D7 (8 lines) | D4 – D7 (4 lines) |
| **Total MCU Pins Required** | 11 (8 data + RS + RW + E) | 7 (4 data + RS + RW + E) |
| **GPIO Savings** | None (baseline) | **4 pins saved (≈ 36.36%)** |
| **Data Transfer per Character** | 1 transfer (full byte) | 2 transfers (nibbles) |
| **Transmission Time** | $1 \times t_{cycle}$ = 1 µs | $2 \times t_{cycle}$ = 2 µs |
| **Speed** | Faster | **2× slower per byte** |
| **Software Complexity** | Simpler (single write) | Higher (nibble packing) **(2 marks)** |
| **Power Consumption** | Slightly higher (more I/O toggling) | Slightly lower |
| **Use Case** | High-speed display updates, abundant I/O | I/O-constrained designs, KTU mini-projects |
| **Initialization Complexity** | Standard | **More complex** (must switch to 4-bit via 0x20 command) **(1 mark)** |
| **Noise Immunity** | Better (more lines = better averaging) | Lower |

**Advantages of 4-bit mode:** Saves 4 GPIO pins, ideal for AT89C51/52 (which has only 32 I/O lines). **(1 mark)**

**Limitations of 4-bit mode:** Slower throughput, more complex code, requires careful timing of nibble transmission. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Line-2 address trap (–1 mark):** Students often write `0x20` for Line 2 start address. The correct value is **`0x40`**. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in KTU LCD questions.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to set RS for data write (–1 mark):** If RS is left at 0, the ASCII character will be misinterpreted as a command. Students forget that **RS = 1 is mandatory** for data writes.
>
> 3. **Missing delays in initialization (–2 marks):** The 15 ms, 4.1 ms, and 1.64 ms delays are **not optional**. KTU examiners specifically check for these in the initialization table.
>
> 4. **Confusing 4-bit and 8-bit Function Set commands (–1 mark):** `0x28` is for **4-bit, 2-line, 5x7**; `0x38` is for **8-bit, 2-line, 5x7**. Students often swap them.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to OR with 0x80 for address commands (–1 mark):** The "Set DDRAM Address" command requires the MSB (DB7) to be 1, so the byte must be `0x80 OR address`.
>
> 6. **Not specifying RW = 0 explicitly (–0.5 mark):** Always tie RW to GND for write-only operation, or drive it from MCU. Leaving it floating is a hardware bug.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ HD44780 is the **universal LCD controller** for 16x2 / 20x4 character LCDs.
- ✅ **16 pins** total: 8 data, 3 control, 2 power, 2 backlight, 1 VEE.
- ✅ RS = 0 → **Command**, RS = 1 → **Data**.
- ✅ E pin is **edge-triggered on the falling edge**; $t_W \geq 450$ ns.
- ✅ **8-bit mode** uses **11 MCU pins**; **4-bit mode** uses **7 MCU pins**.
- ✅ Line 1 starts at DDRAM address **0x00**; Line 2 starts at **0x40**.
- ✅ Address command format: **0x80 OR Address**.
- ✅ Function Set for 8-bit/2-line/5x7 is **0x38**; for 4-bit/2-line/5x7 is **0x28**.
- ✅ Display ON with cursor OFF is **0x0C**; with cursor ON and blink is **0x0F**.
- ✅ Auto-increment entry mode is **0x06**.
- ✅ Clear display is **0x01** (takes 1.64 ms — longest delay).
- ✅ Initialization sequence: **15 ms → 0x30 (4.1 ms) → 0x30 (100 µs) → 0x30 (40 µs) → 0x38 → 0x08 → 0x01 → 0x06 → 0x0C**.
- ✅ DDRAM = 80 bytes (visible = 32 for 16x2).
- ✅ CGRAM = 64 bytes → can store **8 custom 5x8 characters** (8 bytes each).
- ✅ CGROM = 192 pre-programmed characters.
- ✅ Busy Flag (BF) polling is **faster** than fixed delays and is preferred in production code.
- ✅ 4-bit mode is **2× slower** per character but saves **36.36% of GPIO pins**.
- ✅ ASCII 'A' = **0x41**, ASCII '0' = **0x30**, ASCII space = **0x20**.
- ✅ VEE pin is connected to a **potentiometer wiper** for contrast adjustment — never leave it floating.

> **End of Module 3 – LCD Interfacing Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC & DAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** ADC & DAC # ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a mixed-signal electronic circuit that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code (typically a binary word of *n* bits) into a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog voltage or current output proportional to the digital input. ### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy: The "Volume Knob vs. Light Switch" Model Imagine you want to control the brightness of a room bulb using a digital switch panel. - A **digital-only** system is like a simple ON/OFF light switch — there are only **2 levels** (bright or dark). - A **DAC** is like a **dimmer knob** with infinite smooth positions between dark and bright. The DAC takes a binary number (say `10110101`) and produces an exact analog voltage that is **proportional** to that number. In an embedded system (e.g., 8051 or ARM), the microcontroller outputs digital data, but real-world actuators (motors, speakers, heaters) require analog signals — so the DAC bridges this **digital-to-physical** gap. --- ## 1.2 What is an ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is a mixed-signal circuit that samples a continuous analog input signal at discrete time intervals and quantizes each sample into a finite-resolution binary code. The output is an *n*-bit digital word representing the magnitude of the input voltage at that instant. ### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy: The "Thermometer Reading" Model Consider an analog mercury thermometer (continuous mercury column) and a digital thermometer (displays numbers like 36.6°C). - The **analog signal** = the smooth mercury column (infinitely many possible levels). - The **ADC** = the digital thermometer's internal circuit — it **samples** the temperature at regular intervals (say every 1 second) and **rounds off** the value to the nearest representable digital step (e.g., 36.5°C or 36.6°C). This rounding-off step is called **quantization**, and the smallest distinguishable step is the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** or **resolution**. --- ## 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (Syllabus Highlights) > [!NOTE] > The following metrics are **must-know** for any KTU question on ADC/DAC: | Parameter | Definition | Typical Value | |-----------|------------|---------------| | **Resolution** | Smallest analog change distinguishable per LSB = $V_{ref} / 2^n$ | e.g., for 8-bit, $V_{ref}=5V \Rightarrow 19.53\,mV$ | | **% Resolution** | Percentage of full-scale range per LSB = $(1/2^n) \times 100$ | For 8-bit = **0.3906 %** | | **Quantization Error** | Maximum deviation from true value = $\pm \frac{1}{2} LSB$ | e.g., $\pm 9.77\,mV$ for 8-bit/5V | | **Conversion Time** | Time taken to complete one A/D or D/A conversion | 1 µs – 100 µs (typical) | | **Sampling Rate ($f_s$)** | Samples per second (Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$) | Depends on application | # 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — The Big Picture The analog world is **continuous** in both time and amplitude. The digital world is **discrete** in both. Conversion in either direction requires three sub-processes: **For ADC (Analog → Digital):** 1. **Sampling** — Capture the analog signal at discrete time instants. 2. **Holding** — Freeze the sample on a capacitor (Sample & Hold circuit). 3. **Quantization** — Map the held voltage to the nearest digital code. 4. **Encoding** — Convert to binary (Unipolar: Natural Binary; Bipolar: Offset Binary / 2's Complement). **For DAC (Digital → Analog):** 1. **Decoding** — Interpret the binary word. 2. **Summing** — Combine weighted analog contributions of each bit. 3. **Filtering** — Smooth the resulting "staircase" output. --- ## 2.2 Types of DAC (KTU-Favorite Block) ### A. Weighted-Resistor DAC - Each bit drives a resistor whose value is **halved** for the next lower bit: $R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$. - The output is the sum of currents through an **op-amp summing amplifier**. - **Drawback:** Resistor values span an enormous range for high *n* (for 12-bit, ratio = 4096:1) → impractical to fabricate accurately. ### B. R-2R Ladder DAC ⭐ (Most Important) - Uses only **two resistor values: R and 2R**. - Each bit switches between ground and $V_{ref}$ at the ladder node. - Binary weighting is achieved through successive **current division** at each node. - **Advantage:** Easy to manufacture in IC form → used in most modern 8/10/12-bit DACs (e.g., DAC0808). ### C. Other (Brief) - **Inverted R-2R Ladder**, **Monolithic Current-Steering DAC**, **Sigma-Delta DAC** (used in audio). --- ## 2.3 Types of ADC (KTU-Favorite Block) ### A. Flash (Parallel) ADC ⚡ (Fastest) - Uses $2^n - 1$ comparators and a priority encoder. - Conversion in **one clock cycle** → ultra-fast. - **Drawback:** Hardware explodes with resolution (8-bit → 255 comparators). ### B. Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC ⭐ (Most Common) - Uses a single comparator + DAC + SAR logic. - Performs a **binary search** over *n* clock cycles. - Excellent trade-off of speed, accuracy, and cost → used in 8051's ADC and ARM Cortex-M ADCs. ### C. Dual-Slope (Integrating) ADC - Integrates input for fixed time, then de-integrates with reference. - **Excellent noise rejection** → used in digital voltmeters. - **Slow** (typically 10–100 conversions/sec). ### D. Counter-Type / Tracking ADC - Uses an up/down counter tracking the input. - Slow but simple; rarely used in modern MCUs. --- ## 2.4 🧮 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize these equations — they appear in 70% of KTU numericals on this topic.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Step size / Resolution | Volts | | 2 | $\% \text{Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$ | Relative resolution | % | | 3 | $V_{out}(\text{DAC}) = V_{ref} \times \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | Output for digital input *D* | Volts | | 4 | $Q_e = \pm \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Quantization error | Volts | | 5 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling theorem | Hz | | 6 | $V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB}$ | ADC digital output *D* | Volts | | 7 | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$ | ADC digital code | integer | | 8 | $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Systems (CD, MP3):** 16-bit DACs reconstruct audio from PCM data. - **Automotive ECUs:** SAR ADCs read throttle position, temperature sensors. - **Process Control:** 4–20 mA DAC outputs drive industrial valves. - **Medical Instruments (ECG, EEG):** High-resolution (24-bit) sigma-delta ADCs capture microvolt biosignals. - **8051/ARM Labs:** Students interface ADC0804 (8-bit) and DAC0808 to study mixed-signal design. # 🧠 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: DAC Output Voltage for R-2R Ladder **Setup:** An *n*-bit R-2R ladder DAC receives digital word $D = b_{n-1}2^{n-1} + b_{n-2}2^{n-2} + \ldots + b_0 2^0$, where each bit $b_i \in \{0, 1\}$ switches the corresponding ladder leg between ground (0) and $V_{ref}$ (1). **Op-amp Configuration:** The ladder output feeds the inverting input of a summing op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f = R$. **Current contribution from the MSB leg ($b_{n-1}$):** $$I_{n-1} = \frac{b_{n-1} \cdot V_{ref}}{2R}$$ **Current contribution from the next bit leg ($b_{n-2}$):** Because of ladder current division, each successive bit contributes half the previous current. Therefore: $$I_{n-2} = \frac{b_{n-2} \cdot V_{ref}}{4R}$$ **General form for bit $b_k$ (where $k$ counts from 0 = LSB):** $$I_k = \frac{b_k \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{n-k} \cdot 2R}$$ **Total ladder current at the summing node:** $$I_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} I_k = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_k}{2^{n-k-1}}$$ **Convert summation to standard binary form** (with $i = k$ and $b_{n-1}$ as MSB): $$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \frac{D}{2^{n-1}} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot D}{2^n \cdot R}$$ **Op-amp output voltage (inverting summing amplifier):** $$V_{out} = -I_{total} \cdot R_f = -\frac{V_{ref} \cdot D}{2^n}$$ **Final DAC Equation:** $$\boxed{V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}}$$ The negative sign is from the inverting amplifier; if a non-inverting buffer is added, the polarity flips. **This equation is the most-tested formula in KTU ESE on DACs.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Resolution and % Resolution **Starting Point:** Resolution is defined as the change in output when the digital input changes by 1 LSB. $$\Delta V_{LSB} = V_{out}(D+1) - V_{out}(D)$$ **Substitute the DAC equation:** $$\Delta V_{LSB} = -\frac{V_{ref}(D+1)}{2^n} - \left(-\frac{V_{ref} \cdot D}{2^n}\right)$$ **Simplify term by term:** $$\Delta V_{LSB} = -\frac{V_{ref} D}{2^n} - \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} + \frac{V_{ref} D}{2^n}$$ **The $-V_{ref}D/2^n$ and $+V_{ref}D/2^n$ cancel:** $$\Delta V_{LSB} = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$ **Take magnitude (step size is positive):** $$\boxed{V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}}$$ **Percentage Resolution:** $$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{V_{ref,FS}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Theorem **Statement (proved by Shannon, 1949):** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ **Intuitive Proof Outline (Anti-Aliasing Argument):** 1. Sampling multiplies $x(t)$ by an impulse train $\sum_k \delta(t - kT_s)$. 2. In the frequency domain, this **convolves** $X(f)$ with another impulse train, producing **replicas** of $X(f)$ centered at $\pm k f_s$. 3. To prevent overlap (aliasing), the gap between adjacent replicas must be $\geq$ bandwidth $f_{max}$: 4. The gap equals $f_s - f_{max} - (-f_{max}) = f_s - 2f_{max}$. 5. For non-overlap: $f_s - 2 f_{max} \geq 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{f_s \geq 2 f_{max}}$. **Example:** Human voice has $f_{max} \approx 4$ kHz → telephone sampling rate = **8 kHz** (Nyquist minimum). CD audio uses **44.1 kHz** for $f_{max} = 20$ kHz. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical (KTU Typical — 7 Mark) > **Problem:** A 10-bit DAC has a reference voltage of 5.08 V. Find: (a) the resolution in volts, (b) the % resolution, (c) the output voltage for input `1101011001`, (d) the quantization error. **Given:** $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5.08\,V$. **Solution (a): Resolution** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.08}{1024}$$ $$V_{LSB} = 4.96 \times 10^{-3}\,V = 4.96\,mV$$ **Solution (b): % Resolution** $$\% \text{Res} = \frac{1}{1024} \times 100 = 0.0977\,\%$$ **Solution (c): Output Voltage** First, convert `1101011001` to decimal: $$D = 1 \cdot 512 + 1 \cdot 256 + 0 \cdot 128 + 1 \cdot 64 + 0 \cdot 32 + 1 \cdot 16 + 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1$$ $$D = 512 + 256 + 64 + 16 + 8 + 1 = 857$$ Now apply the DAC equation: $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = 5.08 \times \frac{857}{1024}$$ $$V_{out} = 5.08 \times 0.8369 = 4.251\,V$$ **Solution (d): Quantization Error** $$Q_e = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} = \pm \frac{5.08}{2048} = \pm 2.48\,mV$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} = 4.251\,V \pm 2.48\,mV}$$ # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — ADC & DAC in an Embedded Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AnalogWorld["🌐 ANALOG WORLD (Continuous)"] S1[Physical Sensor
e.g. Temperature LM35] ACT[Actuator
e.g. DC Motor / Speaker] end subgraph Conditioning["📡 Signal Conditioning"] AMP[Amplifier / Filter] SH[Sample and Hold Circuit] end subgraph DigitalCore["💻 DIGITAL CORE (8051 / ARM)"] MCU[Microcontroller
CPU + Memory] end subgraph Converters["🔄 Mixed-Signal Converters"] ADC[ADC
e.g. ADC0804 SAR] DAC[DAC
e.g. DAC0808 R-2R] end S1 -->|Analog mV| AMP AMP -->|0-5V Analog| SH SH -->|Held Voltage| ADC ADC -->|8-bit Digital Code| MCU MCU -->|Processed Digital Data| DAC DAC -->|Analog Voltage / Current| ACT style S1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style ACT fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 style ADC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style DAC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style MCU fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style SH fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style AMP fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 R-2R Ladder DAC — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BitInputs["Digital Input Word (n bits)"] B3[b3 MSB] B2[b2] B1[b1] B0[b0 LSB] end subgraph Ladder["R-2R Resistor Network"] N3[Node 3] N2[Node 2] N1[Node 1] N0[Node 0 Ground] end subgraph Switches["CMOS Switches"] S3[SW3] S2[SW2] S1[SW1] end subgraph OpAmp["Summing Op-Amp"] OP[Op-Amp
Feedback Rf = R] end B3 --> S3 B2 --> S2 B1 --> S1 S3 --> N3 S2 --> N2 S1 --> N1 N3 -->|2R| N2 N2 -->|2R| N1 N1 -->|2R| N0 N3 -->|R| VREF N2 -->|R| VREF N1 -->|R| VREF N3 --> OP N2 --> OP N1 --> OP OP --> VOUT[Analog Vout] style VREF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style VOUT fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style OP fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 style Ladder fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 SAR ADC — Binary Search Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Start Conversion]) --> SETMSB[Set MSB = 1
Trial Code = 1000...0] SETMSB --> DAC1[DAC produces Vdac] DAC1 --> COMP{Comparator:
Vin > Vdac?} COMP -->|YES| KEEP1[Keep MSB = 1] COMP -->|NO| CLR1[Clear MSB = 0] KEEP1 --> NEXT1 CLR1 --> NEXT1[Test next bit] NEXT1 --> DECISION{All n bits tested?} DECISION -->|NO| SETMSB DECISION -->|YES| OUTPUT[Final Digital Output D] OUTPUT --> END([End]) style START fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style END fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 style COMP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style DECISION fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 style DAC1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 style OUTPUT fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` # 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]** > > **Q1.** Define the term **resolution** of a DAC. An 8-bit DAC has a full-scale output of 5 V. Calculate its resolution in volts and as a percentage. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Resolution of a DAC is the smallest change in the analog output that can be distinguished as the digital input is incremented by 1 LSB. It is given by $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^n$. - **Calculation (2 marks):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{5}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256} = 19.53\,mV$$ $$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{256} \times 100 = 0.3906\,\%$$ > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]** > > **Q2.** State the **Nyquist sampling theorem**. Why is it important in ADC design? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Statement (1 mark):** The Nyquist sampling theorem states that to faithfully reconstruct a bandlimited analog signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling frequency must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. - **Importance (2 marks):** 1. If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency components fold back as false low-frequency signals, causing irreversible information loss. 2. It defines the **minimum ADC sampling rate** specification; otherwise, the digital output will be a distorted, unusable representation of the input. --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B Question – Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / ESE Module 3 Pattern]** ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the working of an **R-2R ladder DAC** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for its output voltage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 12-bit ADC operates on a 0–10 V range. Calculate: **(i)** Resolution in mV, **(ii)** % resolution, **(iii)** digital output code when the analog input is 6.43 V, **(iv)** quantization error in mV. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A **(a) R-2R Ladder DAC Working + Derivation (7 marks)** **Working (4 marks):** - The R-2R ladder uses only **two resistor values** (R and 2R), making it ideal for IC fabrication. - Each bit $b_k$ controls a switch that connects the corresponding ladder node to either $V_{ref}$ (logic 1) or ground (logic 0). - At each ladder node, the current is **halved** by the successive 2R shunt resistors, producing binary-weighted currents. - The summed current feeds an inverting op-amp that converts it into the output voltage. **Derivation (3 marks):** The output voltage expression (as derived in Section 3.1) is: $$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$ where $D = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k$ is the decimal equivalent of the digital input word. **(b) 12-bit ADC Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $n = 12$, $V_{ref} = 10\,V$, $V_{in} = 6.43\,V$. **(i) Resolution in mV (2 marks):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3}\,V = 2.441\,mV$$ **(ii) % Resolution (1 mark):** $$\% \text{Res} = \frac{1}{4096} \times 100 = 0.0244\,\%$$ **(iii) Digital Output Code (2 marks):** $$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{6.43}{0.0024414} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 2633.79 \rfloor = 2633$$ Converting 2633 to 12-bit binary: $$2633 = 2048 + 512 + 64 + 8 + 1 = 2^{11} + 2^9 + 2^6 + 2^3 + 2^0$$ $$D = 101001001001_2$$ **(iv) Quantization Error (2 marks):** $$Q_e = \pm \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n+1}} = \pm \frac{10}{8192} = \pm 1.2207\,mV$$ --- ### 🔹 Question B (Alternative Choice — 14 Marks) **(a)** With a functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC**. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A temperature sensor outputs 0–5 V for 0°C to 150°C. It is connected to an 8-bit ADC. Find: **(i)** the temperature corresponding to a digital output of `10010110`, **(ii)** the smallest temperature change that can be detected. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B **(a) SAR ADC Explanation (7 marks)** **Working (5 marks):** - A SAR ADC contains: **1 Comparator, 1 DAC, 1 Successive Approximation Register, 1 Control/Output Register**. - On a START pulse, the SAR sets the MSB of the trial register to 1 and all other bits to 0. - The trial digital code is fed to the internal DAC, producing $V_{dac}$. - The comparator checks: $V_{in} > V_{dac}$? - If **YES**, the bit is kept as 1. - If **NO**, the bit is reset to 0. - The process repeats for the next bit, performing a **binary search**. - After *n* clock cycles, the SAR holds the final digital code, which is loaded into the output register. **Advantages (1 mark):** - High resolution with moderate speed (typical 1–100 µs). - Low power; uses only 1 comparator → cheaper than Flash. **Disadvantages (1 mark):** - Slower than Flash ADC. - Requires an accurate internal DAC. **(b) Temperature Sensor Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $V_{in}$ range = 0–5 V, Temperature range = 0–150 °C, $n = 8$. **(i) Temperature for code `10010110` (4 marks):** Convert binary to decimal: $$D = 128 + 16 + 4 + 2 = 150$$ Compute voltage equivalent: $$V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB} = 150 \times \frac{5}{256} = 2.9297\,V$$ Convert voltage to temperature (linear scaling): $$T = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{FS}} \times T_{FS} = \frac{2.9297}{5} \times 150$$ $$\boxed{T = 87.89\,°C}$$ **(ii) Smallest detectable temperature change (3 marks):** $$\Delta T_{min} = \frac{T_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{150}{256} = 0.586\,°C$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each:** > > 1. **Sign error in DAC output equation:** Forgetting the negative sign (inverting op-amp) when the question expects a positive output. Always state the assumption explicitly. > 2. **Confusing $2^n$ with $2^{n-1}$:** Resolution uses $2^n$; quantization error uses $2^{n+1}$. Mixing these up is the #1 reason for lost marks. > 3. **Binary-to-Decimal conversion errors:** Students often misplace bits when converting ADC codes. Always write out powers explicitly (e.g., $2^7 + 2^4 + 2^2 + 2^1$) before summing. > 4. **Forgetting units in final answer:** "10" is meaningless; write "10 mV" or "10 °C". > 5. **Aliasing trap:** If asked "minimum sampling rate for a 22 kHz audio signal", students sometimes answer 22 kHz. The correct answer is **44 kHz** (Nyquist). > 6. **R-2R vs. Weighted Resistor confusion:** Both use binary weighting, but weighted resistor uses $R, 2R, 4R, ...$ (impractical), while R-2R uses only **R and 2R** (practical). Examiners test this distinction. --- ## 5.4 🏁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):** ### 🔑 Core Concepts - ✅ **DAC = Digital → Analog; ADC = Analog → Digital** (basic conversion direction). - ✅ DAC output equation: $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ (non-inverting) or with minus sign (inverting). - ✅ Resolution = $V_{ref}/2^n$ — **always memorize first**. - ✅ % Resolution = $\dfrac{100}{2^n}$ — depends only on *n*, not $V_{ref}$. - ✅ Quantization error = $\pm V_{ref}/2^{n+1}$ — half of LSB on either side. ### 🔑 Converter Types - ✅ **Weighted Resistor DAC:** Simple but impractical (large resistor spread). - ✅ **R-2R Ladder DAC:** Industry standard; uses only R and 2R. - ✅ **Flash ADC:** Fastest ($2^n - 1$ comparators) but expensive. - ✅ **SAR ADC:** Most common in MCUs; binary search; *n* clock cycles. - ✅ **Dual-Slope ADC:** Slowest but highest noise immunity (DVMs). ### 🔑 Key Numbers to Remember - ✅ 8-bit → $2^8 = 256$ → $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/256$. - ✅ 10-bit → $2^{10} = 1024$. - ✅ 12-bit → $2^{12} = 4096$ → common in industrial ADCs. - ✅ 16-bit → $2^{16} = 65536$ → audio CD quality. ### 🔑 Nyquist Theorem - ✅ **$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$** — any lower causes aliasing. - ✅ Telephone: 8 kHz sampling (4 kHz voice). - ✅ Audio CD: 44.1 kHz sampling (20 kHz audio). ### 🔑 SNR Rule of Thumb - ✅ Each additional bit adds **~6 dB** to SNR: $\text{SNR} \approx 6.02n + 1.76$ dB. ### 🔑 Exam Day Tip - ✅ Always **draw the block diagram** even if not explicitly asked — KTU examiners award **1–2 marks** for neat diagrams. ---

topics.title: Motor Control: Relays and Stepper Motor Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motor Control: Relays and Stepper Motor Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electromechanical Relays

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
An **electromechanical relay (EMR)** is an electrically operated switch that uses an electromagnet (a coil of wire) to mechanically control one or more sets of contacts.
