When a control voltage is applied across the coil, the resulting magnetic field attracts a movable armature, which in turn opens or closes the contacts, allowing a separate (often high-power) circuit to be switched ON or OFF while remaining electrically isolated from the low-power control circuit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Isolation Property:** A relay provides *galvanic isolation* between the low-voltage microcontroller side (typically **5 V DC**, a few milliamps) and the high-voltage/high-current load side (e.g., **230 V AC**, up to several **Amperes**). This is the single most important reason why relays are taught in embedded systems.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Bicep and the Broom":**
Imagine you want to flip a heavy industrial wall switch, but the switch is on the ceiling and your hand is small. You cannot directly touch it. So you tie a string to a small, easy-to-pull lever on a table. When you pull the string (apply a small signal), a hidden mechanical linkage lifts your arm and pushes the heavy switch. The **string is the coil current**, the **hidden linkage is the armature + spring mechanism**, and the **heavy switch is the contact** that handles the real load. The microcontroller "pulls the string" — it never touches the heavy switch.

**Essential Contact Terminology (must memorize for KTU viva):**

| Contact Type | Symbol | Behaviour When Coil is OFF | Behaviour When Coil is ON |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normally Open (NO)** | `NO` | Open circuit — no current flows to load | Closes — load gets supply |
| **Normally Closed (NC)** | `NC` | Closed — load is connected to supply | Opens — load is disconnected |
| **Change-Over (CO / SPDT)** | `CO` | Common pin is connected to NC pin | Common pin flips over to NO pin |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ratings you must know:** Coil voltage (e.g., **5 V**, **12 V**, **24 V**), coil current (e.g., **30 mA–100 mA**), contact rating (e.g., **230 V AC, 7 A** or **30 V DC, 10 A**). The *coil side* is what the microcontroller drives; the *contact side* is what the load uses.

---

## 1.2 Stepper Motors

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
A **stepper motor** is a *brushless, synchronous* electric motor that converts a train of digital input pulses into discrete angular displacements (steps). The shaft rotates in **fixed angular increments** for each input pulse, making it an *open-loop* positioning device. The motor's position can be precisely controlled without any feedback sensor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open-Loop Positioning:** Unlike a DC servo motor (which needs an encoder), a stepper assumes the shaft has moved exactly one step for every pulse sent. If the load torque exceeds the motor's holding torque, the rotor will *skip steps* and the position error is **permanent** until reset.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Compass and Four Magnets":**
Place a free-spinning compass needle in the centre of four bar magnets pointing inward (North, South, East, West). Switch ON only the North magnet: the needle swings to North. Now switch OFF North and switch ON the East magnet: the needle swings to East (90°). Switch to South, then West — the needle has taken four discrete steps of 90° each. A stepper works exactly this way: each *phase* (stator winding) acts like one of the four magnets, and the digital pulse sequence "rotates" the magnetic field, dragging the rotor (compass) along in precise steps.

**The Three Families of Stepper Motors:**

1. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper** — Rotor is a *soft-iron toothed wheel* with no permanent magnet. The rotor aligns with the stator phase that offers the *lowest magnetic reluctance* (path of least resistance to magnetic flux). It rotates only when the energised phase is "mismatched" with the rotor teeth.
2. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper** — Rotor is a *permanent magnet* (typically axially magnetised). It locks to the stator field and has higher holding torque than VR but typically coarser step angles (commonly **7.5°** or **15°**).
3. **Hybrid Stepper** — Combines both principles: a *toothed rotor with axially magnetised end caps*. This gives the finest step angles (commonly **0.9°, 1.8°**, i.e., 200 or 400 steps/revolution) and the highest torque density. This is the type most commonly used in CNC machines, 3D printers, and robotics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Stepper" and not "Servo" for KTU labs?** A stepper gives *exact, repeatable, no-feedback positioning* at low cost. A servo is closed-loop and more accurate under varying load, but needs an encoder, a PID controller, and is overkill for the simple "rotate N steps" task asked in 8051/ARM lab exams.

---

## 1.3 Why Microcontroller Interfacing is Non-Trivial

Two glaring mismatches exist between a microcontroller pin and a motor — these are the heart of KTU Module 3:

| Mismatch | Microcontroller Pin | Motor Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Current** | GPIO can source/sink ~10–20 mA (e.g., 8051 P1 port) | Stepper coils need **200 mA – 2 A**; relay coil needs **30–100 mA** |
| **Voltage** | Logic levels **0 V / 5 V (or 3.3 V)** | Steppers may need **12 V, 24 V, or bipolar ±12 V**; relays often **12 V** coils |
| **Back-EMM** | Pin is sensitive, easily destroyed | Stepper/relay coils generate high-voltage spikes when switched OFF |

**Solution:** Insert a *driver* (transistor / Darlington array / H-bridge IC) **between** the microcontroller and the motor. For steppers, the most common KTU-lab devices are **ULN2003** (unipolar, 7-channel Darlington) and **L293D** (bipolar H-bridge, dual channel).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Relay — Operating Theory Step-by-Step

1. **De-energised state:** A spring holds the armature against the *NC* contact. The *NO* contact is open. Current through the load (if wired to NO) is zero.
2. **Energise coil:** Apply rated DC voltage across the coil (e.g., **+5 V**). Current flows, building a magnetic flux.
3. **Pull-in:** When the magnetic force exceeds the spring tension, the armature snaps toward the *core*. The *NC* contact opens; the *NO* contact closes. The load is now connected to its supply.
4. **De-energise:** Remove the coil voltage. The magnetic field collapses. The spring pulls the armature back, restoring the original contact state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pull-in voltage** (minimum voltage to actuate) is typically **60–80 %** of nominal. **Drop-out voltage** (minimum voltage to release) is typically **20–40 %** of nominal. This *hysteresis* prevents chattering near the threshold.

### 2.1.1 Microcontroller → Relay Interfacing (Transistor Driver)

A single bipolar junction transistor (BJT) — typically an **NPN BC547** or **Darlington TIP122** — is wired as a *low-side switch*. The relay coil sits between **Vcc** (e.g., +12 V) and the *collector*; the *emitter* goes to ground; the *base* receives a current-limiting resistor (R_B) from the microcontroller pin.

- **Microcontroller output HIGH** → Base current flows → Transistor saturates → Collector voltage ≈ 0.2 V → Relay coil energised.
- **Microcontroller output LOW** → Transistor in cutoff → No collector current → Relay de-energised.

**Base resistor calculation** (high-yield KTU 2-mark problem):
$$I_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{R_B}, \quad \text{choose } I_B \geq \frac{I_C}{h_{FE(\min)}}$$

### 2.1.2 The Flyback / Freewheeling Diode — *Why KTU loves this topic*

When the transistor switches OFF, the relay coil's magnetic flux collapses. A coil *opposes any change in current* (Lenz's law), so it generates a huge reverse-polarity voltage spike of **hundreds of volts** for a few microseconds. This spike can:
- Punch through the transistor's collector-base junction (destroying it).
- Reset the microcontroller via the supply rail.

**Solution:** Place a **1N4007** (or any fast-recovery diode) *reverse-biased* across the coil — **cathode at Vcc, anode at collector**. When the spike occurs, the diode becomes forward-biased and safely circulates the inductive current until it dies out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always reverse-bias the flyback diode during normal operation.** If you accidentally place it the other way, you will short-circuit Vcc through the diode the moment the transistor turns ON — instant magic-smoke failure.

---

## 2.2 Stepper Motor — Operating Theory Step-by-Step

Consider a 4-phase unipolar stepper (most common in KTU labs, e.g., a **28BYJ-48** driven by **ULN2003**). It has **5** or **6** wires: four coil-centre-tap wires + one common.

1. **Rotor:** A permanent magnet (PM stepper) with a known number of pole pairs.
2. **Stator:** Four wound pole pieces, A, B, C, D, spaced 90° apart. Each has a centre-tap connected to Vcc (often **+5 V** or **+12 V**).
3. **Sequencing:** By switching each coil's lower-end to ground (via the driver) in a specific order, the stator field rotates. The rotor (permanent magnet) chases the field, taking discrete steps.

### 2.2.1 The Three Stepping Modes

Let `A, B, C, D` be the four coils. The ULN2003's input pins are pulled LOW to activate a coil.

| Mode | Sequence (one full electrical cycle) | Steps / Cycle | Torque | Smoothness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wave Drive (1-phase ON)** | A → B → C → D → A → … | 4 | Lowest | Jerky |
| **Full Step / Normal Drive (2-phase ON)** | AB → BC → CD → DA → AB → … | 4 | Highest | Smooth |
| **Half-Step Drive** | A → AB → B → BC → C → CD → D → DA → A → … | 8 | Intermediate | Smoothest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Direction reversal:** Reverse the sequence (A → D → C → B → A) and the rotor spins the other way. This is the standard answer to "How do you change the direction of a stepper motor?"

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All of the formulas below have been asked in past KTU University Exam papers. Memorise the table.

**Table 2.3 — Master Formula Reference**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step angle (β) | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N}$ | degrees per step | $N$ = number of steps per *complete mechanical revolution* |
| 2 | Steps per revolution (N) | $N = \dfrac{360^\circ}{\beta}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of (1). For 1.8° motor, N = 200 |
| 3 | Shaft speed (n) in RPM | $n = \dfrac{f \cdot 60}{N}$ | RPM | $f$ = pulse frequency in pulses per second (Hz) |
| 4 | Shaft speed (n) in RPS | $n = \dfrac{f}{N}$ | revolutions per second | Divide RPM by 60 |
| 5 | Angular displacement (θ) | $\theta = N_{steps} \cdot \beta$ | degrees | $N_{steps}$ = total number of pulses sent |
| 6 | Time for one revolution | $T_{rev} = \dfrac{N}{f}$ | seconds | Also = 60/n |
| 7 | Time delay between pulses | $T_{step} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | seconds | Sets the *stepping rate* in software |
| 8 | Required timer reload (8051) | $T_{delay} = \dfrac{12 \cdot (256 - X)}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$ | seconds | For crystal = 11.0592 MHz, machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods |
| 9 | Relay coil current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | Amps | Use measured coil resistance |
| 10 | BJT base resistor | $R_B = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$ | Ohms | $I_B \geq I_C \div h_{FE(min)}$ |
| 11 | Hybrid stepper step angle | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r \cdot m}$ | degrees | $N_r$ = rotor teeth, $m$ = number of phases (typically 2) |
| 12 | VR stepper step angle | $\beta = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_r}$ | degrees | $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth (one tooth pitch per step) |
| 13 | Stepper holding torque (empirical) | $T_h \propto N \cdot \phi \cdot I$ | N·m | More phases, more flux, more current = more holding torque |
| 14 | Resolution of a stepper | $R = \beta$ | degrees | Smaller β ⇒ finer resolution |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Relays** are still the *de-facto* choice in industrial control panels, home automation, and automotive ECUs for switching AC mains (lights, heaters, solenoids) where *isolation* is a legal/safety requirement. Solid-state relays (SSRs) replace mechanical contacts in high-cycle applications.
- **Stepper motors** are the workhorse of *open-loop positioning* — 3D printers, CNC routers, camera autofocus lenses, disk-drive head positioners, textile machines, and the *tiny* vibrator motor inside your phone (LRAs are a stepper cousin). They dominate where the cost of an encoder is unjustified.
- **Hybrid steppers** with **microstepping** (e.g., 1/32 step) are used in modern CNC machines to achieve sub-arcminute positioning smoothness without a closed loop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Relationship Between Step Angle, Pulse Rate, and RPM

> **Starting facts (KTU-given or measured):**
> - A stepper motor has $N$ steps per mechanical revolution.
> - Input pulse frequency to the driver = $f$ pulses per second.
> - Each pulse advances the rotor by **one** step angle $\beta$.

**Step 1:** One mechanical revolution requires exactly $N$ pulses.

$$N = \frac{360^\circ}{\beta}$$

**Step 2:** The number of revolutions completed in one second is therefore $f \div N$ (revolutions per second).

$$n_{RPS} = \frac{f}{N}$$

**Step 3:** Convert RPS to RPM (revolutions per minute) by multiplying by 60.

$$\boxed{n_{RPM} = \frac{f \cdot 60}{N} = f \cdot \frac{60 \cdot \beta}{360^\circ} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{6}}$$

**Step 4:** Inverting, the time to complete one revolution is:

$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{n_{RPM}} = \frac{N}{f} = \frac{360^\circ}{\beta \cdot f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The boxed formula $n = f \cdot \beta / 6$ is the **fastest one-line answer** KTU examiners expect when asked "find the RPM given f and step angle." Memorise it.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Standard Type

> **Problem (KTU University Exam, May 2018 style):** A stepper motor has a step angle of **1.8°**. The applied pulse frequency is **400 Hz**. Calculate: (a) the steps per revolution, (b) the shaft speed in RPM, (c) the time for one revolution, (d) the angular displacement after 250 pulses.

### (a) Steps per revolution
$$N = \frac{360^\circ}{\beta} = \frac{360^\circ}{1.8^\circ} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$

### (b) Shaft speed in RPM
$$n_{RPM} = \frac{f \cdot \beta}{6} = \frac{400 \times 1.8}{6} = \frac{720}{6} = 120 \text{ RPM}$$

### (c) Time per revolution
$$T_{rev} = \frac{60}{n_{RPM}} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5 \text{ seconds}$$

### (d) Angular displacement for 250 pulses
$$\theta = N_{steps} \cdot \beta = 250 \times 1.8^\circ = 450^\circ = 1.25 \text{ full revolutions}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Templates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five templates below cover ~90 % of the stepper numericals you will see in KTU ESE. Practice each one.

### Template 1 — Given β, find N
Use $N = 360°/\beta$.
*Sample:* β = 7.5° → N = **48** steps/rev. β = 0.9° → N = **400** steps/rev.

### Template 2 — Given f and N, find RPM
Use $n = f \cdot 60 / N$.
*Sample:* f = 1000 Hz, N = 200 → n = **300 RPM**.

### Template 3 — Given RPM and N, find f
Rearrange: $f = n \cdot N / 60$.
*Sample:* n = 60 RPM, N = 200 → f = **200 Hz**.

### Template 4 — Given desired RPM and N, find timer delay
First find $f = n \cdot N / 60$. Then $T_{delay} = 1/f$. Convert to timer ticks for 8051:
$$\text{Timer ticks} = T_{delay} \cdot \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = T_{delay} \cdot 921600$$

*Sample:* Want 100 RPM at 200 steps/rev → f = 333.33 Hz → T_delay = 3 ms → ≈ 2765 timer ticks → split into 2 reloads or use Timer-1 in mode 1 (16-bit).

### Template 5 — Given N, β, and number of pulses, find total angle
$\theta_{total} = N_{pulses} \cdot \beta$; reduce modulo 360° to express in revolutions.
*Sample:* 530 pulses at 1.8° → 530 × 1.8° = 954° = 2 rev + **234°**.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Relay Base-Resistor Sizing

> **Problem:** Interface a **+5 V relay** (coil resistance $R_{coil} = 200 \, \Omega$, pull-in current = **30 mA**) to an 8051 pin using a **BC547** NPN transistor ($V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7 \, V$, $h_{FE(min)} = 100$). Vcc for the relay = **+5 V**, $V_{OH}$ of 8051 = **2.4 V** (worst-case TTL high). Find the minimum base resistor $R_B$.

**Step 1 — Coil current (sanity check):**
$$I_{coil} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{coil(actual)}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{200 \, \Omega} = 25 \text{ mA}$$
This is less than the rated 30 mA pull-in, so the relay will energise. (If it were less than the *drop-out* current, the relay might not release cleanly — a KTU viva trap.)

**Step 2 — Required collector current (saturated switch):**
The transistor must pass at least the coil current when ON.
$$I_C = I_{coil} = 25 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Required base current for saturation:**
$$I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_C}{h_{FE(min)}} = \frac{25 \text{ mA}}{100} = 0.25 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Choose a safety factor.** Designers use 2× to ensure hard saturation:
$$I_{B(design)} = 2 \times I_{B(min)} = 0.5 \text{ mA} = 500 \, \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_B$:**
$$R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \frac{2.4 - 0.7}{0.5 \text{ mA}} = \frac{1.7 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ mA}} = 3.4 \, k\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Choose the next standard E12 value:** $R_B = 3.3 \, k\Omega$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Always include the flyback diode** in the final circuit (1N4007 across the coil, cathode to +5 V). Without it, the collapsing field will spike the collector to **>50 V** and destroy the BC547.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison — Relays vs. Stepper Drivers

| Aspect | Relay Driver (BJT) | Stepper Driver (ULN2003 / L293D) |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | Single low-side switch | Darlington array (ULN2003) or H-bridge (L293D) |
| Current per channel | ~100–500 mA | 500 mA (ULN2003) / 600 mA continuous, 1.2 A peak (L293D) |
| Voltage rating | Vcc of the coil (e.g., 5 V, 12 V, 24 V) | Up to **50 V** (ULN2003) / 36 V (L293D) |
| Built-in flyback diodes | NO — must add 1N4007 externally | YES — ULN2003 has 7 internal flyback diodes; L293D has internal clamp diodes |
| Number of channels | 1 per transistor | 7 (ULN2003) / 2 H-bridges = 4 channels (L293D) |
| KTU exam wiring | Relay coil between Vcc and collector | Stepper phases A, B, C, D to OUT1–OUT4 of ULN2003 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Microcontroller to Relay to AC Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU["8051 GPIO PIN P1.0"] --> RB["BASE RESISTOR RB 3.3K"]
    RB --> BASE["BJT BASE BC547"]
    BASE --> Q1["NPN TRANSISTOR LOW SIDE SWITCH"]
    Q1 --> COIL["RELAY COIL 5V 200 OHM"]
    COIL --> VCC["VCC PLUS 5V"]
    COIL -. in parallel .-> DIODE["FLYBACK DIODE 1N4007"]
    Q1 --> GND1["DIGITAL GROUND"]
    COIL --> CONTACT["RELAY COMMON PIN"]
    CONTACT --> NOC["NORMALLY OPEN CONTACT"]
    CONTACT --> NCC["NORMALLY CLOSED CONTACT"]
    NOC --> LOAD["AC LOAD 230V LAMP"]
    LOAD --> MAINS["AC MAINS 230V 50 HZ"]
    NCC --> MAINS

    classDef mcu fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef pwr fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef iso fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000

    class MCU mcu
    class RB,BASE,Q1,COIL,DIODE drv
    class VCC,GND1,MAINS pwr
    class CONTACT,NOC,NCC,LOAD iso
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The blue zone (8051 pin) and the red zone (230 V AC mains) are **galvanically isolated** by the relay's coil-to-contact barrier. The yellow zone is the *driver* that bridges them. A KTU full-mark answer must explicitly mention the isolation.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — 8051 to 4-Phase Unipolar Stepper via ULN2003

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P10["8051 PIN P1.0"] --> IN1["ULN2003 IN1"]
    P11["8051 PIN P1.1"] --> IN2["ULN2003 IN2"]
    P12["8051 PIN P1.2"] --> IN3["ULN2003 IN3"]
    P13["8051 PIN P1.3"] --> IN4["ULN2003 IN4"]
    P14["8051 PIN P1.4"] --> BUZZER["ULN2003 IN5 BUZZER OPTIONAL"]

    IN1 --> OUT1["ULN OUT1 OPEN COLLECTOR"]
    IN2 --> OUT2["ULN OUT2"]
    IN3 --> OUT3["ULN OUT3"]
    IN4 --> OUT4["ULN OUT4"]

    OUT1 --> PHASEA["STEPPER PHASE A"]
    OUT2 --> PHASEB["STEPPER PHASE B"]
    OUT3 --> PHASEC["STEPPER PHASE C"]
    OUT4 --> PHASED["STEPPER PHASE D"]

    PHASEA --> CT["CENTRE TAP PLUS 5V OR 12V"]
    PHASEB --> CT
    PHASEC --> CT
    PHASED --> CT

    CT --> POWER["EXTERNAL VCC MOTOR SUPPLY"]
    OUT1 --> GNDD["GROUND"]
    OUT2 --> GNDD
    OUT3 --> GNDD
    OUT4 --> GNDD

    TIMER["8051 TIMER0 ISR"] --> SEQ["STEP SEQUENCE LOOKUP TABLE"]
    SEQ --> P10
    SEQ --> P11
    SEQ --> P12
    SEQ --> P13

    classDef mcu fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef mot fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef sw fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2d5016,color:#000

    class P10,P11,P12,P13,P14,TIMER,SEQ mcu
    class IN1,IN2,IN3,IN4,IN5,OUT1,OUT2,OUT3,OUT4 drv
    class PHASEA,PHASEB,PHASEC,PHASED,CT,MOTOR1,POWER mot
    class GNDD sw
```

---

## 4.3 Stepping-Sequence State Machine (Full-Step Normal Drive)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_IDLE
    S0_IDLE --> S1_AB : START COMMAND
    S1_AB : S1 PHASES AB ON
    S1_AB --> S2_BC : NEXT PULSE
    S2_BC : S2 PHASES BC ON
    S2_BC --> S3_CD : NEXT PULSE
    S3_CD : S3 PHASES CD ON
    S3_CD --> S4_DA : NEXT PULSE
    S4_DA : S4 PHASES DA ON
    S4_DA --> S1_AB : NEXT PULSE FORWARD
    S4_DA --> S3_CD : DIRECTION REVERSE
    S1_AB --> S0_IDLE : STOP COMMAND
    S2_BC --> S0_IDLE : STOP COMMAND
    S3_CD --> S0_IDLE : STOP COMMAND
    S4_DA --> S0_IDLE : STOP COMMAND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse the chain** for opposite rotation: `S1_AB → S4_DA → S3_CD → S2_BC → S1_AB`. This is implemented in 8051 C as a *lookup table* traversed forward or backward using an index that increments or decrements modulo 4 (or modulo 8 for half-step).

---

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — One Full-Step Cycle (Wave Drive)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T["TIMING AXIS"]
        direction LR
        T1["T0"] --> T2["T1"] --> T3["T2"] --> T4["T3"] --> T5["T4 CYCLE RESTART"]
    end

    subgraph SIG["COIL SIGNALS"]
        direction LR
        A1["A HIGH"] --> A2["A LOW"] --> A3["A LOW"] --> A4["A LOW"] --> A5["A HIGH"]
        B1["B LOW"] --> B2["B HIGH"] --> B3["B LOW"] --> B4["B LOW"] --> B5["B LOW"]
        C1["C LOW"] --> C2["C LOW"] --> C3["C HIGH"] --> C4["C LOW"] --> C5["C LOW"]
        D1["D LOW"] --> D2["D LOW"] --> D3["D LOW"] --> D4["D HIGH"] --> D5["D LOW"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Differentiate between Normally Open (NO) and Normally Closed (NC) relay contacts with neat diagrams.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Normally Open (NO)** contact is in the *open* state when the relay coil is de-energised; it *closes* only when the coil is energised. It is drawn as two parallel lines with a diagonal line that is *lifted away* from them.

A **Normally Closed (NC)** contact is in the *closed* state when the coil is de-energised; it *opens* when the coil is energised. It is drawn as two parallel lines with a diagonal line that is *touching* them, and it lifts off on actuation.

The change-over (CO) contact is a combination of both — a *common* terminal that flips between NO and NC. (1 mark for diagrams, 2 marks for explanation.)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> *List the three types of stepper motors and state one distinguishing feature of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Variable Reluctance (VR)** — Rotor is a *plain soft-iron toothed wheel* with no magnet; steps occur as the rotor aligns to minimise magnetic reluctance. (1 mark)
2. **Permanent Magnet (PM)** — Rotor carries a *permanent magnet*; coarser step angles (7.5°/15°), higher holding torque than VR. (1 mark)
3. **Hybrid** — Combines a *toothed rotor with axially magnetised end caps*; finest step angles (0.9°/1.8°), highest torque density, used in CNC/3D printers. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Relay Interfacing
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2022, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** Draw the interface circuit to switch a **230 V, 60 W lamp** using an 8051 microcontroller pin, a +5 V relay, and a BC547 driver transistor. Clearly label the **flyback diode**, **base resistor**, and **coil supply**. Explain the role of each component. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** The relay coil draws **70 mA at 5 V**. The driving BC547 has $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7$ V and $h_{FE(min)} = 120$. The 8051 worst-case $V_{OH} = 2.4$ V. Compute the minimum base resistor $R_B$ for *hard saturation* (use a safety factor of 2). (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Circuit diagram & explanation (7 marks):**

The circuit has four sections (the student must sketch and label all):

1. **Microcontroller side:** 8051 pin P1.0 drives the base resistor $R_B$.
2. **Driver stage:** NPN BC547 in *common-emitter, low-side switch* configuration. Collector connects to one end of the relay coil; emitter goes to digital ground.
3. **Relay coil:** Other end of coil connects to **+5 V** supply. A 1N4007 flyback diode is wired *cathode to +5 V, anode to collector* (reverse-biased during normal operation).
4. **Contact side (isolated):** The relay's *common* pin is wired to **230 V AC live**; the *NO* pin is wired to the lamp; the lamp's other terminal goes to **AC neutral**. The contact rating must be ≥ 230 V AC, ≥ 1 A (since 60 W / 230 V ≈ 260 mA).

| Component | Role | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| BC547 | Low-side switch; current amplifier from ~0.5 mA base to 70 mA coil | 2 |
| $R_B$ | Limits base current; sized to saturate transistor | 1 |
| 1N4007 | Suppresses back-EMF spike when coil de-energises | 2 |
| Relay | Provides **galvanic isolation** between 5 V DC and 230 V AC domains | 2 |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
$I_C = 70$ mA, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $h_{FE(min)} = 120$, $V_{OH} = 2.4$ V.

**Step 2 — Minimum base current for saturation (no safety factor):**
$$I_{B(min)} = \frac{I_C}{h_{FE(min)}} = \frac{70 \text{ mA}}{120} = 0.583 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor of 2 (as stated in the problem):**
$$I_{B(design)} = 2 \times 0.583 = 1.167 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $R_B$ using KVL on the base loop:**
$$R_B = \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_B} = \frac{2.4 - 0.7}{1.167 \text{ mA}} = \frac{1.7}{1.167 \times 10^{-3}} = 1457 \, \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Standard value:** Choose the next E12 value: $R_B = 1.5 \, k\Omega$ (or $1.2 \, k\Omega$ for slightly harder saturation). **(7 marks distributed across steps 1–5.)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Relay Questions:**
> - Students often **omit the flyback diode** in the diagram and lose **2 marks** outright.
> - Students frequently **forget the safety factor** in base-resistor problems and get a value that barely saturates the transistor (unreliable in production).
> - Students often connect the diode **the wrong way round** (forward-biased during normal operation), which would short-circuit Vcc and destroy the transistor the moment the relay turns ON. Always state *"cathode to Vcc, anode to collector"* explicitly.
> - Do NOT forget to state the **galvanic isolation** property of the relay — it is a 1-mark freebie that most students skip.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Stepper Motor Interfacing
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a **4-phase unipolar stepper motor** to an 8051 using a **ULN2003** driver IC. Describe the **full-step normal drive** sequence and the **half-step** sequence. How is the direction of rotation reversed? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A stepper motor has a step angle of **7.5°** and is driven by a pulse train of **200 Hz**. Calculate: (i) the number of steps per revolution, (ii) the shaft speed in RPM, (iii) the time delay between consecutive pulses, and (iv) the total angular displacement when **640 pulses** are applied. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Interface & sequences (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (3 marks):** The student must draw:
- 8051 port P1 (P1.0–P1.3) → ULN2003 inputs IN1–IN4.
- ULN2003 outputs OUT1–OUT4 → stepper phases A, B, C, D.
- Stepper centre-tap → +5 V (or +12 V) supply.
- ULN2003 GND → common ground.
- A freewheeling diode bank (built-in inside ULN2003) across each output.

**Full-step normal-drive sequence (2 marks):**
| Step | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ON** | **ON** | OFF | OFF |
| 2 | OFF | **ON** | **ON** | OFF |
| 3 | OFF | OFF | **ON** | **ON** |
| 4 | **ON** | OFF | OFF | **ON** |

**Half-step sequence (1 mark):**
`A → AB → B → BC → C → CD → D → DA → A` (8 steps per cycle; each step is half of the full-step angle).

**Direction reversal (1 mark):** Reverse the lookup-table traversal: instead of stepping *forward* through the table, step *backward*. In C, replace `index = (index + 1) % 4` with `index = (index + 3) % 4` (equivalent to `index = (index - 1 + 4) % 4`).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Steps per revolution:**
$$N = \frac{360^\circ}{\beta} = \frac{360^\circ}{7.5^\circ} = 48 \text{ steps/rev} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Shaft speed in RPM:**
$$n = \frac{f \cdot 60}{N} = \frac{200 \times 60}{48} = \frac{12000}{48} = 250 \text{ RPM} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Time delay between pulses:**
$$T_{step} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{200} = 5 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iv) Angular displacement for 640 pulses:**
$$\theta = 640 \times 7.5^\circ = 4800^\circ$$
$$\theta_{reduced} = 4800^\circ \mod 360^\circ = 4800 - 13 \times 360 = 4800 - 4680 = 120^\circ$$
Equivalent to $13.33$ revolutions or **13 full revolutions + 120°**. **(1.5 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Stepper Questions:**
> - **Unit confusion:** Some students write $n = f \cdot \beta$ (forgetting the *60* and the */360°*), giving an answer in degrees-per-minute and losing 2 marks.
> - **Modulo reduction:** In (iv), if the answer 4800° is *not* reduced modulo 360°, the examiner deducts **1 mark** for failing to express the angle in the conventional form.
> - **Direction reversal:** Students often say "reverse the current" — wrong for a unipolar stepper. The correct answer is *"reverse the sequence order of phase energisation."*
> - **Confusing step modes:** Many students mix up wave drive (1-phase ON, lowest torque) with normal drive (2-phase ON, highest torque). Memorise the trade-off.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Last-minute revision — go through this list 5 minutes before the exam.**

**Relay Essentials**

- ☐ Relay = **electromagnetic switch** providing **galvanic isolation** between control and load circuits.
- ☐ Two contact types: **NO** (open when OFF) and **NC** (closed when OFF). **CO/SPDT** has both.
- ☐ **Always add a flyback diode (1N4007)** across the relay coil — cathode to Vcc, anode to collector.
- ☐ BJT driver: compute $I_B$ via $I_C / h_{FE(min)}$; apply a **safety factor of 2** for hard saturation.
- ☐ Coil current formula: $I_{coil} = V_{cc} / R_{coil}$ (when fully ON).
- ☐ Typical relay driver ICs: **ULN2003** (Darlington array, 7 channels, internal flyback diodes), **TLP281** (opto-isolated), or discrete BJT/MOSFET.

**Stepper Motor Essentials**

- ☐ Stepper is **brushless, synchronous, open-loop** — converts *digital pulses* into *discrete angular steps*.
- ☐ Three types: **VR** (no magnet, toothed iron rotor), **PM** (magnet rotor, coarser steps), **Hybrid** (best of both — most common in labs).
- ☐ Master formula: $n_{RPM} = f \cdot \beta / 6$ (uses β in degrees, f in Hz).
- ☐ Steps per revolution: $N = 360° / \beta$. For 1.8° → 200 steps/rev. For 7.5° → 48 steps/rev.
- ☐ Three drive modes:
  - **Wave drive (1-phase ON):** 4 steps/cycle, lowest torque.
  - **Normal drive (2-phase ON):** 4 steps/cycle, highest torque.
  - **Half-step:** 8 steps/cycle, smoothest, intermediate torque.
- ☐ Direction reversal = **reverse the phase sequence order**.
- ☐ Common driver ICs: **ULN2003** (unipolar, 500 mA/channel, internal flyback) and **L293D** (bipolar H-bridge, 600 mA/channel).
- ☐ "**Skip step**" = motor torque exceeded → permanent position error → no closed-loop correction.

**Numerical Templates to Drill**

- ☐ Given β → find N.
- ☐ Given f, N → find RPM.
- ☐ Given desired RPM, N → find f → find timer delay.
- ☐ Given N pulses and β → find total angle (reduce mod 360°).
- ☐ Given relay $I_{coil}$, $h_{FE}$, $V_{OH}$ → find $R_B$ (with safety factor 2).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor Interfacing and PWM using 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC Motor Interfacing and PWM using 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 DC Motor — Formal Definition

A **DC (Direct Current) Motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts electrical energy in the form of DC into rotational mechanical energy, based on the principle that a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field experiences a force (Lorentz force, $F = BIL$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 microcontroller I/O pins can source/sink only ~**10–20 mA** of current at logic levels of **0 V (LOW)** and **+5 V (HIGH)**. However, even a small DC motor requires currents in the range of **200 mA to 2 A**. Therefore, **motor interfacing driver circuits (like L293D)** are mandatory between the 8051 and the motor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are controlling the speed of a ceiling fan using a **regulator (dimmer)** at home:
- The regulator knob doesn't physically limit the electricity — it rapidly **switches the power ON and OFF** many times per second.
- The longer the "ON" time in each cycle, the **faster** the fan spins.
- This rapid ON–OFF switching technique is exactly what is called **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)**.

| Component | Real-World Analogy | 8051 Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Regulator Knob | Controls fan speed | PWM duty cycle from 8051 |
| Fan Motor | Rotating load | DC Motor |
| Heavy Switch | ON/OFF power to motor | H-Bridge Driver (L293D) |
| Timer-based switching | Mechanical oscillator | 8051 Timer/Counter |

## 1.3 PWM — Formal Definition

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a digital modulation technique in which the **width of the pulse** is varied in proportion to an analog input signal, while keeping the **frequency (period) constant**. It is the standard method used to control the **average power** delivered to a DC motor using only digital logic outputs.

## 1.4 H-Bridge — Formal Definition

An **H-Bridge** is an electronic circuit (typically built using 4 switching elements arranged in an "H" configuration) that allows a voltage to be applied across a DC motor in **either direction**, enabling **bidirectional rotation** (clockwise / counter-clockwise) from a single DC supply.

> [!NOTE]
> The name "H-Bridge" comes from the typical circuit drawing which resembles the letter **"H"** — with the motor forming the horizontal bar and the four switches forming the vertical strokes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why 8051 Cannot Directly Drive a DC Motor

The 8051 family of microcontrollers (e.g., AT89C51, P89V51RD2) has GPIO pins with the following electrical limits:
- **Maximum source/sink current per pin ≈ 10–20 mA**
- **Maximum total current for entire port ≈ 80–100 mA**
- **Logic voltage levels: 0 V and +5 V**

A typical small DC motor (e.g., 6 V, 300 mA) draws currents **15–30×** higher than what a single 8051 pin can deliver. Attempting this directly will **burn out the port pin** permanently.

**Solution:** Use a **motor driver IC** like the **L293D** or **L298N** between the 8051 and the motor.

## 2.2 The L293D Motor Driver IC

The **L293D** is a quadruple high-current half-H driver IC designed to provide bidirectional drive currents of up to **600 mA per channel** (1.2 A peak) at voltages from **4.5 V to 36 V**.

### Pin Configuration & Functions

| Pin | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EN1,2 (Enable)** | Enables channels 1 & 2 (logic HIGH = active) |
| 2 | **1A (Input A)** | Control input for Motor 1 (from 8051) |
| 3 | **1Y (Output A)** | Output to Motor 1 terminal 1 |
| 4, 5 | **GND, GND** | Ground pins (heat dissipation) |
| 6 | **2Y (Output B)** | Output to Motor 2 terminal 1 |
| 7 | **2A (Input B)** | Control input for Motor 2 (from 8051) |
| 8 | **Vcc2 (Motor Supply)** | Motor power supply (4.5 V – 36 V) |
| 9 | **EN3,4** | Enables channels 3 & 4 |
| 10–15 | **3A, 3Y, 4Y, 4A, GND, Vcc1** | Channel 3, 4 + Logic supply (5 V) |
| 16 | **Vcc1 (Logic Supply)** | 8051 logic supply (+5 V) |

## 2.3 H-Bridge Truth Table (Using L293D Channel 1)

Let `EN1,2 = 1` (Enable active). The motor's rotation is determined by inputs `1A` and `2A`:

| Input 1A | Input 2A | Motor 1Y | Motor 2Y | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | **0** | LOW | LOW | **STOP (Brake)** — both terminals grounded |
| **0** | **1** | LOW | HIGH | **ROTATE Clockwise (CW)** |
| **1** | **0** | HIGH | LOW | **ROTATE Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** |
| **1** | **1** | HIGH | HIGH | **STOP (Brake)** — both terminals at Vcc2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Setting both inputs to **opposite (1,0) → (0,1)** simultaneously (called "shoot-through") will short-circuit the supply and destroy the H-bridge. Always ensure a **dead time** or use the EN pin for safe transitions.

## 2.4 PWM — Detailed Theory

A PWM signal has two key parameters:
- **$T_{ON}$** → Time for which the pulse is HIGH
- **$T_{OFF}$** → Time for which the pulse is LOW
- **$T = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$** → Total period
- **$f = \dfrac{1}{T}$** → Frequency of PWM

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| PWM Period | $T = T_{ON} + T_{OFF}$ | seconds (s) | One complete cycle |
| PWM Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Kept constant |
| **Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$ | % | Varies from **0% to 100%** |
| Average Motor Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{max}$ | Volts (V) | $V_{max}$ = motor supply |
| Motor Speed Relation | $N \propto V_{avg} \propto D$ | RPM | Direct proportionality |
| Timer Count Value | $TC = 2^n - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{f_{PWM}}$ | integer | $n$ = timer bit length |
| Timer Reload Value | $Reload = 65536 - TC$ | integer hex | For 8051 Mode 1 (16-bit) |
| 8051 Machine Cycle | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds | 1 MC = 12 oscillator periods |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Fan Speed Control** in CPU coolers, exhaust fans
- **Robotic Wheel Drive** control in mobile robots
- **Servo Motor Angle Control** (servo uses PWM at 50 Hz)
- **LED Dimming** in smart lighting systems
- **Power Inverters & SMPS** (Switched Mode Power Supplies)
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) Throttle Control** — Tesla, Tata Nexon EV use PWM for motor control

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Voltage Across DC Motor

A DC motor responds to the **average DC voltage** applied to its terminals. When a PWM signal is applied, the average voltage can be calculated as the area under the PWM waveform divided by the total period.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a single PWM cycle with HIGH for $T_{ON}$ seconds and LOW for $T_{OFF}$ seconds.

**Step 2:** The total area under the waveform in one cycle (which gives the average voltage when divided by period) is:

$$
A_{total} = V_{max} \times T_{ON} + 0 \times T_{OFF}
$$

> Here, the LOW portion contributes zero area because voltage = 0 V during $T_{OFF}$.

**Step 3:** Simplify the expression:

$$
A_{total} = V_{max} \times T_{ON}
$$

**Step 4:** The average voltage is the area divided by the total period $T$:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{A_{total}}{T} = \frac{V_{max} \times T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}
$$

**Step 5:** Define the duty cycle $D$:

$$
D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $D$ into the voltage equation to obtain the final compact form:

$$
\boxed{V_{avg} = D \times V_{max}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 12 V DC motor is driven by a PWM signal with $T_{ON} = 4 \text{ ms}$ and $T_{OFF} = 6 \text{ ms}$. Calculate the average voltage and motor speed percentage.

**Step 1:** Total period:

$$
T = T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = 4 \text{ ms} + 6 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate duty cycle:

$$
D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} = \frac{4 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} = 0.4 = 40\%
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate average voltage:

$$
V_{avg} = D \times V_{max} = 0.4 \times 12 \text{ V} = 4.8 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4:** Speed percentage (assuming linear relation):

$$
\% \text{Speed} = D \times 100 = 40\% \text{ of rated speed}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Timer Reload Value for 8051 PWM Generation

The 8051 uses **Timer/Counter** units to generate precise time delays. To generate PWM, we configure the timer to overflow at regular intervals, and toggle the output pin in software (or using interrupts).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the 8051's clock input. Standard 8051 uses an external crystal oscillator.

**Step 2:** The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by **12** internally to create the **machine cycle** clock.

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12}
$$

**Step 3:** For **Timer Mode 1** (16-bit), the timer counts from the reload value up to $65536$ ($2^{16}$) and overflows.

**Step 4:** The time for one timer count is the **machine cycle period**:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5:** To generate a PWM with frequency $f_{PWM}$, the timer must overflow every half-period (or full period depending on algorithm). The number of timer counts needed is:

$$
TC = \frac{f_{OSC}}{f_{PWM} \times 12 \times m}
$$

> where $m = 1$ for one full period, or $m = 2$ if we toggle output at each overflow.

**Step 6:** The value to be loaded into the timer registers (TH0 and TL0) is:

$$
\boxed{Reload = 2^{16} - TC = 65536 - TC}
$$

**Step 7:** The reload value is split between TH0 (high byte) and TL0 (low byte):

$$
TH0 = \frac{Reload}{256}, \quad TL0 = (Reload) \mod 256
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Generate a **1 kHz PWM** signal using 8051 Timer 0 in Mode 1 with a **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate the timer reload value for full-period generation.

**Step 1:** Calculate machine cycle frequency:

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921600 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2:** Time per machine cycle (per timer count):

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{1}{921600} = 1.085 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3:** Timer counts required for 1 kHz PWM (one full period = 1 ms):

$$
TC = f_{MC} \times T_{PWM} = 921600 \times \frac{1}{1000} = 921.6 \approx 922
$$

**Step 4:** Reload value:

$$
Reload = 65536 - 922 = 64614
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hexadecimal:

$$
64614 \div 256 = 252 \text{ (integer division)} \rightarrow \text{0xFC}
$$

$$
64614 \mod 256 = 102 \rightarrow \text{0x66}
$$

**Step 6:** Final timer register values:

$$
\boxed{TH0 = 0xFC, \quad TL0 = 0x66}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **11.0592 MHz** crystal is the most commonly used frequency in 8051 systems because it produces **exact integer machine cycle periods** (1.085 μs) which aligns perfectly with standard baud rate generation (9600, 19200, etc.).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Motor Control Strategies

| Strategy | Switching Frequency | Torque Ripple | Audible Noise | Efficiency | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear (Analog) Control** | N/A (continuous) | Very Low | Silent | Low (heat loss) | Rarely used (wasteful) |
| **Low-Frequency PWM (<1 kHz)** | 100–1000 Hz | High | Loud (whining) | High | Educational, basic demos |
| **High-Frequency PWM (>20 kHz)** | 20–200 kHz | Very Low | Silent (above human ear) | Very High | Industrial / EV motors |
| **8051 Software PWM** | 100 Hz – few kHz | Moderate | Moderate | High (CPU dependent) | KTU Lab experiments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 H-Bridge Circuit — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PWR["DC Power Supply Vcc"]
        V1["+Vcc (Top Rail)"]
    end
    subgraph GND["Ground Reference"]
        V2["GND (Bottom Rail)"]
    end
    V1 --- SW1["Switch S1 Top-Left"]
    V1 --- SW2["Switch S2 Top-Right"]
    V2 --- SW3["Switch S3 Bottom-Left"]
    V2 --- SW4["Switch S4 Bottom-Right"]
    SW1 --- M1["Motor Terminal A"]
    SW2 --- M2["Motor Terminal B"]
    SW3 --- M1
    SW4 --- M2
    M1 --- MOTOR["DC MOTOR"]
    M2 --- MOTOR
    CTRL["8051 Logic Pins"] --> SW1
    CTRL --> SW2
    CTRL --> SW3
    CTRL --> SW4
    CTRL -.Enable.-> EN["EN1,2 Pin"]
```

> **Functional Flow Explanation:** The four switches (S1, S2, S3, S4) form the four "legs" of the H. When S1 and S4 are ON (S2, S3 OFF), current flows from Vcc through S1 → Motor → S4 → GND, rotating the motor in one direction. Reversing S2 and S3 reverses current flow → opposite rotation.

## 4.2 8051 ↔ L293D ↔ DC Motor Interfacing Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MCU["8051 Microcontroller Unit"]
        P1["Port 1.0 PWM Output"]
        P2["Port 1.1 Direction Bit A"]
        P3["Port 1.2 Direction Bit B"]
        TM["Timer 0 Overflow Interrupt"]
    end
    subgraph DRV["L293D Motor Driver IC"]
        EN["EN1,2 Enable Pin"]
        IA["Input 1A Logic"]
        IB["Input 2A Logic"]
        OY["Output 1Y to Motor"]
        OZ["Output 2Y to Motor"]
        VCC2["Vcc2 Motor Supply 12V"]
        VCC1["Vcc1 Logic Supply 5V"]
    end
    subgraph LOAD["Output Load"]
        MOT["DC Motor 12V"]
        D1["Flyback Diode D1"]
        D2["Flyback Diode D2"]
    end
    P1 --> IA
    P2 --> IA
    P3 --> IB
    TM --> P1
    EN --> PWMEN["PWM Frequency Gate"]
    PWMEN --> IA
    IA --> OY
    IB --> OZ
    OY --> MOT
    OZ --> MOT
    VCC2 --> OY
    VCC1 --> VCC2
    D1 -.-> OY
    D2 -.-> OZ
```

> **Architectural Notes:** The L293D has **internal flyback diodes** (D1, D2) to protect the IC from the back-EMF generated by the motor's inductive windings when the current is switched OFF. The **Enable pin (EN1,2)** is modulated by the PWM signal from 8051 to control motor speed, while **1A and 2A** control the direction.

## 4.3 PWM Waveform — Duty Cycle Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DC50["Duty Cycle 50 percent"]
        A1["Pulse ON 5ms"] --> A2["Pulse OFF 5ms"] --> A3["Pulse ON 5ms"] --> A4["Pulse OFF 5ms"]
    end
    subgraph DC25["Duty Cycle 25 percent"]
        B1["Pulse ON 2.5ms"] --> B2["Pulse OFF 7.5ms"] --> B3["Pulse ON 2.5ms"] --> B4["Pulse OFF 7.5ms"]
    end
    subgraph DC75["Duty Cycle 75 percent"]
        C1["Pulse ON 7.5ms"] --> C2["Pulse OFF 2.5ms"] --> C3["Pulse ON 7.5ms"] --> C4["Pulse OFF 2.5ms"]
    end
    DC50 --> AVG1["Vavg equals 6.0V for Vmax 12V"]
    DC25 --> AVG2["Vavg equals 3.0V for Vmax 12V"]
    DC75 --> AVG3["Vavg equals 9.0V for Vmax 12V"]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice how the period $T$ remains **constant at 10 ms** in all three cases, but the **ON time increases** as the duty cycle goes from 25% → 50% → 75%. The average voltage $V_{avg}$ is directly proportional to this ON time, which directly controls motor RPM.

## 4.4 Software PWM Generation Flow (8051 Algorithm)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START["Start Program"] --> INIT["Initialize Timer0 in Mode1 16bit"]
    INIT --> LOAD["Load TH0 and TL0 with Reload Value"]
    LOAD --> TR0["Start Timer0 TR0 equals 1"]
    TR0 --> WAIT["Wait for TF0 equals 1 Overflow Flag"]
    WAIT --> CLEAR["Clear TF0 and Reload Timer"]
    CLEAR --> TOGGLE["Toggle PWM Pin P1.0"]
    TOGGLE --> DUTY{"Duty Cycle Reached?"}
    DUTY -->|No| WAIT
    DUTY -->|Yes| END["Stop Motor P1.0 equals 0"]
```
