# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define PWM. Why is it preferred over analog DC voltage control for DC motor speed regulation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a digital technique in which the width of a rectangular pulse is varied proportionally to control the average power delivered to a load, while keeping the switching frequency constant.

**Why PWM is preferred over analog control:**
1. **High Efficiency:** PWM switches the MOSFET/Transistor between fully ON (saturation, low $V_{CE}$) and fully OFF (no current). Power dissipation $P = I^2R$ is very small compared to linear (analog) control, which dissipates the excess voltage as heat.
2. **No Heat Sinks Required:** Lower heat generation → smaller, cheaper circuits.
3. **Digital Compatibility:** PWM is a purely digital signal → directly compatible with microcontroller outputs (like 8051).
4. **Precise Control:** Duty cycle can be varied from 0% to 100% in fine steps → high resolution speed control.
5. **Compact & Reliable:** Modern motor driver ICs integrate the switching circuit, reducing component count.

> **3-Mark Distribution:** Definition (1) + 3 key advantages with technical reasoning (2).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three reasons why the 8051 microcontroller cannot directly drive a DC motor. What is the role of the L293D IC?**

**Model Answer:**

**Reasons 8051 cannot directly drive a DC motor:**

1. **Current Limitation:** 8051 GPIO pins can source/sink only **~10–20 mA** of current, whereas DC motors require **200 mA to 2 A** (or more). Drawing higher current will permanently burn out the port pin.
2. **Voltage Mismatch:** 8051 outputs logic levels of **0 V or +5 V**, but DC motors typically operate at **6 V, 9 V, 12 V, or 24 V** — far beyond the 8051's safe output range.
3. **Back-EMF Protection:** DC motors are inductive loads that generate high voltage spikes (back-EMF) when switched off, which can destroy the microcontroller. The 8051 has no built-in protection diodes for such inductive kickback.

**Role of L293D IC:**
The **L293D** is a dual H-bridge motor driver IC that acts as a **current/voltage amplifier** and **bidirectional switch** between the 8051 and the motor. It accepts **low-current TTL logic inputs (from 8051)** and provides **high-current, high-voltage outputs (up to 600 mA, 36 V)** to drive the motor. It also contains **internal flyback protection diodes** and supports **bidirectional rotation** via direction control pins.

> **3-Mark Distribution:** 3 reasons (1.5) + Role of L293D (1.5).

---

## **PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)**

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question has internal choice (either OR). The question consists of two sub-parts: (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### **🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a DC motor to the 8051 microcontroller using the **L293D motor driver IC**. Discuss the role of the **Enable pin** in PWM-based speed control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Design a PWM signal of frequency **2 kHz** with a **50% duty cycle** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Calculate the timer reload value and write the 8051 assembly program to generate this PWM continuously on pin **P1.0**. **(7 marks)**

### **📘 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A (a)**

**Block Diagram of Interfacing:**

```
        +5V (Vcc1)
          |
    8051  |         L293D IC           DC Motor (12V)
  +-------+-----+   +----------+      +-----------+
  |  P1.0|-----EN |          |      |           |
  |  P1.1|-----1A|  Enable  1Y|-----+ M1       M2 +-----+
  |  P1.2|-----2A|  (1,2)    2Y|     |           |     |
  |      |      |  +----------+     +-----------+     |
  |      |      |  +Vcc2 (12V) <---- Motor Supply     |
  |      |      |  +-------------+                   |
  |      |      |  | Internal    |                   |
  |      |      |  | Flyback Diodes                   |
  |      |      |  +-------------+                   |
  +------+------+                                      |
         GND ------------------------------------- GND
```

**Working Explanation:**

1. **8051 Port Pins (P1.0, P1.1, P1.2)** are configured as outputs.
2. **P1.0** is connected to the **EN1,2 (Enable) pin** of L293D.
3. **P1.1 and P1.2** are connected to the **1A and 2A direction control inputs**.
4. The **L293D output pins 1Y and 2Y** are connected to the **two terminals of the DC motor**.
5. **Vcc1 = +5 V** is the logic supply for L293D (shared with 8051).
6. **Vcc2 = +12 V** is the motor supply (separate, higher current source).
7. The L293D's internal diodes protect against the motor's back-EMF.

**Role of Enable Pin in PWM Speed Control:**

- The **EN1,2 pin** acts as a master gate. When **EN = HIGH**, the motor is energized based on direction inputs (1A, 2A). When **EN = LOW**, the motor is disconnected (OFF) regardless of direction pins.
- By **rapidly toggling EN at a high frequency (PWM)**, the motor receives pulses of power. The **average power** is controlled by the **duty cycle** of this PWM signal.
- **100% duty cycle on EN** → motor runs at full speed. **50% duty cycle** → motor runs at half speed. **0% duty cycle** → motor is stopped.
- This technique preserves the direction logic on 1A/2A while only modulating speed through the Enable pin.

> **7-Mark Distribution:** Block diagram (3) + Pin connections explained (2) + Enable role in PWM (2).

---

### **📘 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A (b)**

**Step 1: Calculate the machine cycle frequency.**

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921600 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the timer counts required for 2 kHz PWM (period = 1/2000 = 500 μs).**

$$
TC = \frac{f_{MC}}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{921600}{2000} = 460.8 \approx 461
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the timer reload value.**

$$
Reload = 65536 - 461 = 65075
$$

**Step 4: Convert to hexadecimal.**

$$
65075 \div 256 = 254 \rightarrow \text{0xFE}
$$

$$
65075 \mod 256 = 51 \rightarrow \text{0x33}
$$

$$
\boxed{TH0 = 0xFE, \quad TL0 = 0x33}
$$

**Step 5: 8051 Assembly Program for 2 kHz, 50% Duty Cycle PWM on P1.0.**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 000BH           ; Timer 0 ISR address
RELOAD_T0:
        CPL P1.0            ; Toggle P1.0 (create PWM)
        MOV TH0, #0FEH      ; Reload TH0
        MOV TL0, #033H      ; Reload TL0
        RETI                ; Return from interrupt

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:
        MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV TH0, #0FEH      ; Load initial value (high byte)
        MOV TL0, #033H      ; Load initial value (low byte)
        SETB ET0            ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA             ; Enable global interrupt
        SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   SJMP HERE           ; Idle loop (PWM generated in ISR)

        END
```

**Program Logic Explanation:**

- **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is chosen for flexibility and large count range.
- Timer counts from **65075 to 65535** (= 460 counts) and overflows → causes Timer 0 ISR.
- The ISR **toggles P1.0**, producing a square wave of 2 kHz (since the ISR fires twice per period, once for HIGH and once for LOW).
- For variable duty cycle, the software must count a specific number of ON cycles vs OFF cycles before toggling.

> **7-Mark Distribution:** Timer calculations with all steps (3) + Hex conversion (1) + Assembly program with comments (3).

---

### **🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **H-Bridge circuit** in detail with a diagram. Show how it can be used to control the **direction of rotation** of a DC motor. Provide the complete **truth table** for all four switching combinations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 24 V DC motor is controlled by a PWM signal of frequency **500 Hz** with a duty cycle of **30%**. Calculate:
- (i) The **ON time** and **OFF time** of the PWM signal.
- (ii) The **average voltage** applied to the motor.
- (iii) The **percentage of full speed** at which the motor runs. **(7 marks)**

### **📗 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B (a)**

**H-Bridge Detailed Explanation:**

The H-bridge is named after its characteristic **"H"-shaped** topology, consisting of **four switches** (S1, S2, S3, S4 — typically BJTs, MOSFETs, or IGBTs) arranged around a central DC motor. The motor forms the crossbar of the H, and the switches form the four vertical legs.

**Operating Modes:**

**Mode 1 — S1 ON, S4 ON (S2, S3 OFF):**
- Current path: $Vcc \rightarrow S1 \rightarrow \text{Motor (A to B)} \rightarrow S4 \rightarrow GND$
- Motor rotates in **Direction 1 (say, Clockwise)**.

**Mode 2 — S2 ON, S3 ON (S1, S4 OFF):**
- Current path: $Vcc \rightarrow S2 \rightarrow \text{Motor (B to A)} \rightarrow S3 \rightarrow GND$
- Current through motor is **reversed** → motor rotates in **Direction 2 (Counter-Clockwise)**.

**Mode 3 — All switches OFF:**
- Motor is **floating (coast)** — it continues to spin due to inertia and gradually slows down.

**Mode 4 — S1, S2 ON (S3, S4 OFF) OR S3, S4 ON (S1, S2 OFF):**
- Both motor terminals connected to Vcc (or both to GND) — **dynamic braking** — motor stops quickly.

**Complete Truth Table:**

| S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor Action | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **Coast** | All OFF, motor freewheels |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **CW Rotation** | Current flows A→B |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | **CCW Rotation** | Current flows B→A |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **Brake (HIGH)** | Both terminals at Vcc |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **Brake (LOW)** | Both terminals at GND |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | **SHORT — FORBIDDEN** | Direct Vcc to GND — destroys switches |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **SHORT — FORBIDDEN** | Direct Vcc to GND — destroys switches |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Illegal (Shoot-through)** | Causes excessive current |

> **7-Mark Distribution:** Diagram (2) + Direction control explanation with current paths (3) + Truth table (2).

---

### **📗 MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B (b)**

**Given Data:**
- $V_{max} = 24 \text{ V}$
- $f_{PWM} = 500 \text{ Hz}$
- $D = 30\% = 0.30$

**Step (i): Calculate ON time and OFF time.**

Total period:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{500} = 0.002 \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

ON time:

$$
T_{ON} = D \times T = 0.30 \times 2 \text{ ms} = 0.6 \text{ ms} = 600 \text{ μs}
$$

OFF time:

$$
T_{OFF} = T - T_{ON} = 2 \text{ ms} - 0.6 \text{ ms} = 1.4 \text{ ms} = 1400 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step (ii): Average voltage applied to motor.**

$$
V_{avg} = D \times V_{max} = 0.30 \times 24 = 7.2 \text{ V}
$$

**Step (iii): Percentage of full speed.**

Since motor speed is approximately proportional to average voltage (assuming linear region):

$$
\% \text{ Full Speed} = D \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Final Answers Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $T_{ON}$ | 0.6 ms (600 μs) |
| $T_{OFF}$ | 1.4 ms (1400 μs) |
| $V_{avg}$ | 7.2 V |
| % Full Speed | 30% |

> **7-Mark Distribution:** Step (i) = 2 marks, Step (ii) = 2 marks, Step (iii) = 1 mark, Formula derivation + final summary table = 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 12** in machine cycle calculation: Many students write $T_{MC} = 1/f_{OSC}$ instead of $T_{MC} = 12/f_{OSC}$. This causes a 12× error in reload value.
> 2. **Wrong register split:** Writing $TH0 = 0xFE$ and $TL0 = 0x33$ without verifying: $254 \times 256 + 51 = 65075$ ✓. Always cross-check.
> 3. **Duty cycle in percentage vs decimal:** Duty cycle of 30% in formula must be used as **0.30**, not 30. A common error is to multiply by 30 instead of 0.30.
> 4. **H-Bridge shoot-through state:** Students often forget to mention that states (1,0,1,0) and (0,1,0,1) are **destructive** and must be **prohibited by software interlocks**.
> 5. **Direction control via L293D:** Both direction pins (1A, 2A) must NOT be changed simultaneously without a small delay, or current spike can damage the IC.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **DC Motor** converts DC electrical energy into mechanical rotation; needs high current/voltage.

✅ **8051 GPIO pins** are limited to ~10–20 mA — **CANNOT directly drive motors**.

✅ **L293D** is a dual H-bridge motor driver: **600 mA per channel, 4.5–36 V** motor supply.

✅ **L293D Pin Roles:** **EN** = Enable/Speed, **1A/2A** = Direction, **1Y/2Y** = Motor terminals.

✅ **H-Bridge** uses 4 switches in "H" topology for **bidirectional motor control**.

✅ **Forbidden H-Bridge states:** S1 & S3 ON together (or S2 & S4 ON) = **shoot-through → short circuit**.

✅ **PWM = Pulse Width Modulation:** Vary pulse width at **constant frequency** to control average power.

✅ **Duty Cycle** $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$ — controls average voltage & speed.

✅ **Average voltage** $V_{avg} = D \times V_{max}$ — DC motor speed is proportional to $V_{avg}$.

✅ **8051 Timer** in **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is preferred for flexible, wide-range PWM generation.

✅ **Reload value formula:** $Reload = 65536 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times f_{PWM}}$.

✅ **TH0 = Reload >> 8** (high byte) and **TL0 = Reload & 0xFF** (low byte).

✅ **PWM Frequency rule of thumb:** Use **>20 kHz** for silent operation (above human hearing range); use **<1 kHz** for educational demonstrations with audible tone.

✅ **Flyback Diodes** in L293D protect the IC from **back-EMF** generated by the motor's inductance.

✅ **Vcc1 = +5 V** (logic), **Vcc2 = motor supply** (e.g., 12 V, 24 V) — **two separate supplies** for L293D.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to ARM Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to ARM Processors # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **ARM** stands for **Advanced RISC Machines** (originally **Acorn RISC Machine**). It is a family of Reduced Instruction Set Computing (RISC) processor architectures designed by **ARM Holdings (now owned by SoftBank/ARM Ltd.)** and licensed to a vast ecosystem of chip manufacturers who integrate them into custom System-on-Chip (SoC) designs. The architecture is the most widely deployed instruction set architecture (ISA) in the world, powering billions of embedded and mobile devices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **ARM is NOT a chip manufacturer**. ARM Holdings only designs the *architecture* (the ISA and core IP). Companies like Qualcomm, Samsung, Apple, NXP, and STMicroelectronics manufacture the actual silicon chips based on ARM's designs. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blueprint vs. Building" Metaphor Imagine ARM as a **master architectural blueprint** for a high-performance engine. A blueprint specifies the precise dimensions of pistons, the fuel flow paths, and the ignition sequence. However, the blueprint is not an engine itself. - **ARM Holdings** = The architect (draws blueprints of the engine) - **Licensee Companies (Qualcomm, Apple, NXP, etc.)** = The factory that fabricates the engine using the blueprint, often customizing it for a specific car - **Final SoC (e.g., Qualcomm Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, STM32F407)** = The complete, driveable vehicle built around that custom engine This licensing model is the key reason ARM dominates: it allows hundreds of manufacturers to create specialized chips all while sharing a common software ecosystem. ## 1.3 The Origin Story ARM was conceived in **1983** at **Acorn Computers Ltd.** (Cambridge, UK) by **Sophie Wilson** and **Steve Furber**. The first chip, ARM1, was delivered in **1985**. It was revolutionary because it was designed from a clean slate using the RISC philosophy (in contrast to the dominant CISC processors like Intel 8086 and Motorola 68000). The partnership with Apple (ARM = Advanced *RISC* *M*achines, with the "A" honoring Acorn and the rest honoring Apple) in **1990** gave birth to ARM Holdings, which has since become the backbone of mobile and embedded computing. ## 1.4 The Three Pillars of ARM's Design Philosophy > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Three Defining Features of ARM:** > 1. **Load/Store Architecture** — Data processing operations only operate on registers (no direct memory operands). > 2. **Fixed Instruction Length** — All ARM instructions are exactly **32 bits** wide (Thumb mode is 16 bits), simplifying the fetch/decode pipeline. > 3. **Large Uniform Register File** — **37 registers** (in user mode) including banked registers for fast exception handling. ## 1.5 Real-World Deployment Statistics | Metric | Value (Approx.) | | :--- | :--- | | Chips shipped containing ARM cores | **> 250 Billion** (cumulative, since inception) | | Share in mobile/smartphone SoCs | **> 99 %** | | Share in IoT/MCU market | **> 50 %** | | Global market capitalization (ARM Ltd.) | **> USD 150 Billion** | | Active licensees | **> 500** companies | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 ARM vs. RISC vs. CISC — The Foundational Comparison | Feature | ARM (RISC) | Traditional CISC (e.g., x86) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Design philosophy** | Reduced Instruction Set | Complex Instruction Set | | **Instruction length** | Fixed (32-bit, or 16-bit Thumb) | Variable (1 to 15 bytes in x86) | | **Instruction format** | Highly regular, uniform | Irregular and complex | | **Memory access** | Only via `LDR`/`STR` (Load/Store) | Can directly access memory operands | | **Register count** | Large (37 visible in ARM) | Few (8 in legacy x86) | | **Pipelining** | Deeply pipelined from day one | Added later, retroactively | | **Code density** | Lower (mitigated by Thumb-2) | Higher (compact variable encoding) | | **Power efficiency** | Excellent (used in phones/IoT) | Lower (used in desktops/servers) | | **Clock per instruction** | Typically **1 CPI** (single-cycle) | Variable, often multi-cycle | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Question Pattern**: Examiners frequently ask students to compare RISC and CISC. Memorize the table above; it is a guaranteed 7-mark question when it appears. ## 2.2 ARM Processor Profiles (Cortex Family) The ARM Cortex family is split into three distinct profiles, each targeting a different application domain: ### 2.2.1 Cortex-M Profile — Microcontrollers (Our Focus Area) | Sub-Family | Typical Use Case | | :--- | :--- | | **Cortex-M0 / M0+** | Ultra-low-power IoT, wearables, sensors | | **Cortex-M3** | General-purpose MCUs, motor control, automation | | **Cortex-M4** | DSP, motor control (with FPU + SIMD instructions) | | **Cortex-M7** | High-performance DSP, audio processing | | **Cortex-M23 / M33** | Modern baseline with TrustZone security | | **Cortex-M55 / M85** | Machine learning at the edge (Helium vector extension) | ### 2.2.2 Cortex-A Profile — Application Processors High-end processors used in smartphones (Snapdragon, Apple A-series, Exynos), tablets, smart TVs, and servers. Supports full operating systems (Linux, Android, iOS). Features out-of-order execution, branch prediction, and large caches. ### 2.2.3 Cortex-R Profile — Real-Time Processors Designed for hard-real-time applications such as automotive braking systems, storage controllers, and 5G base stations. Offers deterministic interrupt latency and high reliability (often used in safety-critical ASIL-D systems). ## 2.3 The ARM Instruction Set Evolution | ISA Version | Year | Key Addition | Bit Width | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | ARMv1 | 1985 | First ARM1 chip | 32-bit | | ARMv2 | 1987 | Multiply instruction | 32-bit | | ARMv3 | 1992 | 32-bit address space (virtual) | 32-bit | | ARMv4 | 1994 | Thumb (16-bit compressed) | 32-bit | | ARMv5 | 1997 | Enhanced DSP, CLZ | 32-bit | | ARMv6 | 2001 | SIMD media instructions | 32-bit | | ARMv7 | 2004 | Cortex profile separation (M/A/R) | 32-bit | | **ARMv7-M** | 2004 | **Cortex-M3 baseline** | **32-bit** | | ARMv8-A | 2011 | **64-bit (AArch64) introduced** | 64-bit | | ARMv8.1-M | 2017 | M-profile with 64-bit, TrustZone | 32-bit | | ARMv9 | 2021 | SVE2, Confidential Compute | 64-bit | > [!NOTE] > For the KTU course, **ARMv7-M (Cortex-M3/M4)** is the canonical reference. This is what the STM32F407 / LPC1768 / Tiva-C boards used in your lab are based on. ## 2.4 Pipeline Architecture of ARM The classic ARM7TDMI used a **3-stage pipeline**: $$ \text{Fetch} \longrightarrow \text{Decode} \longrightarrow \text{Execute} $$ Modern Cortex-M processors use a **3-stage pipeline** (M0/M0+/M3) or a **5-stage pipeline** (M4/M7) for higher clock frequencies. | Stage | 3-Stage Function | 5-Stage Extension | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1** | Fetch the instruction | Fetch the instruction | | **2** | Decode + read registers | Decode + read registers | | **3** | Execute + write back | ALU shift / MAC operation | | **4** | (combined) | Memory access (load/store) | | **5** | (combined) | Write back to register file | The pipeline throughput ideally reaches **1 instruction per cycle** after the initial fill, giving an effective **CPI = 1**. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The ARM architecture is the silent workhorse of modern engineering: - **Automotive (AUTOSAR)**: Engine control units (ECUs), ADAS — uses Cortex-R - **Medical Devices**: Pacemakers, infusion pumps, MRI controllers — uses Cortex-M - **Industrial IoT**: PLCs, smart sensors, motor drives — uses Cortex-M4 - **Consumer Electronics**: Smart TVs, smart speakers, washing machines — uses Cortex-A/M - **Aerospace & Defense**: Flight controllers, satellites (radiation-hardened ARM cores) — uses Cortex-R > [!TIP] > The most widely deployed computer in history is an ARM Cortex-M — there is one inside essentially every electronic device you own, including the BLE chip in your wireless earphones. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Architecture Walk-Through ## 3.1 The ARM Register Set — Exhaustive Breakdown The ARM Cortex architecture exposes **37 registers** in user mode and an additional set of banked registers for exception handling, totaling **up to 47 physical 32-bit registers**. ### 3.1.1 Categorization of Registers The 37 user-visible registers are partitioned into: $$ \text{Total Registers} = \underbrace{13}_{\text{General Purpose (R0–R12)}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{Stack Pointer (R13/SP)}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{LR (R14)}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{PC (R15)}} + \underbrace{1}_{\text{CPSR}} + \underbrace{7}_{\text{Banked SPSRs (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, etc.)}} $$ > Total = 13 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 7 = **24** directly visible per mode, but switching modes swaps the banked set, making **37 total unique registers visible to the programmer** in the user/system mode. ### 3.1.2 Detailed Role of Each Register | Register | Alias | Role | Banked? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **R0 – R12** | — | General-purpose data registers. **R0–R3** hold function arguments and return values (AAPCS calling convention); **R4–R11** are callee-saved. | No | | **R13** | **SP** | **Stack Pointer** — points to the top of the active stack (MSP/PSP in Cortex-M). | **Yes** (per mode) | | **R14** | **LR** | **Link Register** — stores the return address when a function (BL/BLX) is called. | **Yes** (per mode) | | **R15** | **PC** | **Program Counter** — holds the address of the current instruction + 2 (pipeline offset in ARM state). | No (unique) | | **CPSR** | — | **Current Program Status Register** — flags (N, Z, C, V), interrupt disables (I, F), mode bits (M[4:0]), Thumb state (T). | No | | **SPSR** | — | **Saved Program Status Register** — saves CPSR on exception entry. | **Yes** (per privileged mode) | ### 3.1.3 Mathematical Derivation of "PC + 8 / PC + 12" Offset ARM's pipeline pre-fetches instructions. When the CPU is executing an instruction at address `A`, the PC holds `A + 8` (in ARM state) due to two prefetched instructions sitting in the pipeline: $$ \text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{CurrentInstructionAddress} + 8 $$ **Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let current instruction address} &= A \\ \text{Cycle 1: Fetch instruction at } & A \\ \text{Cycle 2: Decode instruction at } & A \\ \text{Cycle 3: Execute instruction at } & A \\ \text{While executing } A \text{, the pipeline has already fetched instructions at } & (A+4) \text{ and } (A+8) \\ \text{Hence, when the CPU reads PC, it returns} &= A + 8 \end{aligned} $$ For Thumb state (where instructions are 16-bit), the pipeline half-words cause a different offset: $$ \text{PC}_{\text{read in Thumb state}} = A + 4 $$ ## 3.2 CPSR / SPSR Flag Bit Layout (Cortex-M3/M4) The CPSR is a **32-bit** status register. Its bit layout is critical for KTU board questions: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CPSR} &= [31:28] \;\;|\;\; [27:25] \;\;|\;\; [24] \;\;|\;\; [23:20] \;\;|\;\; [19:16] \;\;|\;\; [15:8] \;\;|\;\; [7:0] \\ &= \underbrace{\text{N Z C V}}_{Condition Flags} \;\;|\;\; \text{Q IT[1:0]} \;\;|\;\; \underbrace{J}_{Jazelle} \;\;|\;\; \text{Reserved} \;\;|\;\; \text{GE[3:0]} \;\;|\;\; \text{IT[7:2]} \;\;|\;\; \underbrace{\text{E A I F T}}_{Control Bits} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.1 Condition Flag Definitions | Flag | Bit | Name | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **N** | 31 | **Negative** | Set if the result is negative (MSB = 1) | | **Z** | 30 | **Zero** | Set if the result is zero | | **C** | 29 | **Carry** | Set if an unsigned overflow occurred (or borrow did NOT occur) | | **V** | 28 | **Overflow** | Set if a signed overflow occurred | | **Q** | 27 | **Sticky Overflow** | Set by DSP/saturation arithmetic; never cleared by MSU | | **I** | 7 | **IRQ disable** | 1 = Mask IRQ exceptions | | **F** | 6 | **FIQ disable** | 1 = Mask FIQ exceptions | | **T** | 5 | **Thumb state** | 1 = executing Thumb instructions | | **M[4:0]** | 4:0 | **Mode field** | Selects processor mode | > [!NOTE] > In **Cortex-M3/M4**, the mode field is restricted to Thread and Handler modes only (privileged/unprivileged). The full legacy ARM mode decoding (FIQ/IRQ/SVC/Abort/Undef/System/User) applies to classic ARM cores and Cortex-A/R. ### 3.2.2 APSR / IPSR / EPSR — The Cortex-M Split Cortex-M3/M4 implements the legacy CPSR as **three logically distinct registers** at the same address, to support atomic bit-band access and atomicity for interrupt handlers: $$ \text{PROGRAM STATUS} = \begin{cases} \text{APSR} & \text{(Application PSR — N, Z, C, V, Q, GE)} \\ \text{IPSR} & \text{(Interrupt PSR — holds the active exception number)} \\ \text{EPSR} & \text{(Execution PSR — holds the IT/ICI state, Thumb bit)} \end{cases} $$ **Derivation of Interrupt Entry Behaviour:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{On exception entry:} & \\ 1.\;\; \text{Push} \; & (R0,R1,\ldots,R3,R12,LR) \text{ onto stack} \\ 2.\;\; \text{Set } & \text{LR} = \text{EXC\_RETURN} \text{ magic value} \\ 3.\;\; \text{Read } & \text{vector address from Vector Table} \\ 4.\;\; \text{Save } & \text{CPSR} \rightarrow \text{SPSR\_} \\ 5.\;\; \text{Switch} & \text{to Handler mode, update IPSR} \\ 6.\;\; \text{Jump} & \text{to ISR vector} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Memory Map of a Typical ARM Cortex-M SoC (e.g., STM32F407) | Address Range | Size | Region | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **0x0000 0000 – 0x1FFF FFFF** | 512 MB | Code — Flash / ROM alias | | **0x2000 0000 – 0x3FFF FFFF** | 512 MB | SRAM — main RAM | | **0x4000 0000 – 0x5FFF FFFF** | 512 MB | Peripheral registers | | **0x6000 0000 – 0x7FFF FFFF** | 512 MB | External RAM (FSMC) | | **0x8000 0000 – 0x9FFF FFFF** | 512 MB | External Device | | **0xA000 0000 – 0xDFFF FFFF** | 512 MB | External Device | | **0xE000 0000 – 0xE00F FFFF** | 1 MB | **System Control Space** (NVIC, MPU, SysTick) | | **0xE010 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF** | — | Reserved | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **System Control Space at 0xE000_0000** is reserved by ARM and is identical across all Cortex-M3/M4 implementations. It contains the **NVIC** (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller), **MPU** (Memory Protection Unit), **SysTick** timer, and **debug interfaces (DWT, BPU, ITM)**. This standardization is a major advantage over proprietary MCUs like the legacy 8051 or PIC16, where peripherals are vendor-defined. ## 3.4 Exception Vector Table Layout (Cortex-M3/M4) On reset, the Cortex-M3 reads the vector from address **0x0000_0004**. The full table layout is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Offset} &\rightarrow \text{Vector} \\ 0x00 &\rightarrow \text{Initial MSP value} \\ 0x04 &\rightarrow \text{Reset Vector (Entry point)} \\ 0x08 &\rightarrow \text{NMI Handler} \\ 0x0C &\rightarrow \text{HardFault Handler} \\ 0x10 &\rightarrow \text{MemManage Handler} \\ 0x14 &\rightarrow \text{BusFault Handler} \\ 0x18 &\rightarrow \text{UsageFault Handler} \\ 0x1C \text{– } 0x28 &\rightarrow \text{Reserved} \\ 0x2C &\rightarrow \text{SVCall Handler} \\ 0x30 &\rightarrow \text{Debug Monitor Handler} \\ 0x34 &\rightarrow \text{Reserved} \\ 0x38 &\rightarrow \text{PendSV Handler} \\ 0x3C &\rightarrow \text{SysTick Handler} \\ 0x40+ &\rightarrow \text{IRQ[0], IRQ[1], \ldots} \\ \end{aligned} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ARM Holdings Business Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ARM Holdings PLC
Architects the ISA & Core IP] -->|Licenses IP| B[Qualcomm] A -->|Licenses IP| C[Apple Inc.] A -->|Licenses IP| D[STMicroelectronics] A -->|Licenses IP| E[NXP Semiconductors] A -->|Licenses IP| F[Samsung] A -->|Licenses IP| G[MediaTek] B --> B1[Snapdragon 8 Gen 3
Cortex-X4 + A720] C --> C1[Apple M3 Chip
Cortex-A inspired] D --> D1[STM32F407 Discovery
Cortex-M4] E --> E1[LPC1768 Board
Cortex-M3] F --> F1[Exynos 2400
Cortex-X4 + A720] G --> G1[Dimensity 9300
Cortex-X4] style A fill:#0091BD,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#3253DC,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#A2AAAD,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#03234B,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style F fill:#1428A0,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#FF6600,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 ARM Cortex Family Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB ARM[ARM Architecture Family] --> M[Cortex-M Profile
Microcontrollers] ARM --> A[Cortex-A Profile
Application Processors] ARM --> R[Cortex-R Profile
Real Time Systems] M --> M0[Cortex-M0/M0+
Ultra Low Power] M --> M3[Cortex-M3
Mainstream MCU] M --> M4[Cortex-M4
MCU with DSP FPU] M --> M7[Cortex-M7
High Perf DSP] M --> M33[Cortex-M33
TrustZone Security] A --> A53[Cortex-A53
Efficiency Cores] A --> A72[Cortex-A72
Performance Cores] A --> X1[Cortex-X1/X2/X3
Premium Mobile] A --> X4[Cortex-X4
Latest Flagship] R --> R5[Cortex-R5
Dual Core Lockstep] R --> R8[Cortex-R8
High Throughput] R --> R52[Cortex-R52
ASIL-D Functional Safety] style ARM fill:#003D7A,stroke:#000,color:#fff style M fill:#00873E,stroke:#000,color:#fff style A fill:#C8102E,stroke:#000,color:#fff style R fill:#FF6F00,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Internal Architecture of a Cortex-M3 Core ```mermaid flowchart LR CPU[Cortex-M3 Core] --> FET[Instruction Fetch Unit
Prefetch Buffer 64 bit] CPU --> DEC[Instruction Decoder
Decodes Thumb 2] CPU --> EXE[Execution Unit
ALU + Barrel Shifter] CPU --> REG[Register Bank
R0 to R15 plus Banked] CPU --> LDST[Load Store Unit
Single Cycle Access] CPU --> CTRL[Control Unit
Branch and Pipeline] FET --> DEC DEC --> EXE EXE --> REG EXE --> LDST CTRL --> FET REG --> EXE LDST --> EXE style CPU fill:#0091BD,stroke:#000,color:#fff style FET fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000 style DEC fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000 style EXE fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000 style REG fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#000 style LDST fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#000 style CTRL fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 ARM 3-Stage Pipeline Operation Timeline ```mermaid gantt title Classic ARM 3-Stage Pipeline Operation dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Instruction 1 Fetch Instruction 1 :a1, 0, 1 Decode Instruction 1 :a2, 1, 1 Execute Instruction 1 :a3, 2, 1 section Instruction 2 Fetch Instruction 2 :b1, 1, 1 Decode Instruction 2 :b2, 2, 1 Execute Instruction 2 :b3, 3, 1 section Instruction 3 Fetch Instruction 3 :c1, 2, 1 Decode Instruction 3 :c2, 3, 1 Execute Instruction 3 :c3, 4, 1 section Instruction 4 Fetch Instruction 4 :d1, 3, 1 Decode Instruction 4 :d2, 4, 1 Execute Instruction 4 :d3, 5, 1 ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram of an ARM-Based SoC (e.g., STM32F407) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SOC[ARM Cortex M4 SoC Example STM32F407] direction TB CORE[Cortex M4 Core
168 MHz FPU DSP] NVIC[Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller] SYST[SysTick Timer 24 bit] MPU[Memory Protection Unit] ROM[Flash 1 MB] RAM[SRAM 192 KB] DMA[General Purpose DMA] BUS[AHB APB Matrix Bus] subgraph PERIPHERALS[On Chip Peripherals] GPIO[GPIO Ports A to I] UART[USART 1 2 3 6] SPI[SPI 1 2 3 I2S] I2C[I2C 1 2 3] CAN[CAN 1 2] USB[USB OTG Full Speed] ETH[Ethernet MAC] ADC[ADC 12 bit] TIM[Timers PWM] end CORE <--> BUS NVIC <--> CORE SYST <--> CORE MPU <--> CORE BUS <--> ROM BUS <--> RAM BUS <--> DMA BUS <--> PERIPHERALS end style CORE fill:#0091BD,stroke:#000,color:#fff style NVIC fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#000 style SYST fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#000 style MPU fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#000 style ROM fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#000 style RAM fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#000 style DMA fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000 style PERIPHERALS fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#666 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define ARM processor. List any four features of the ARM architecture.** **Model Answer:** ARM stands for **Advanced RISC Machine**. It is a family of 32-bit/64-bit RISC processor architectures designed by ARM Holdings and licensed to chip manufacturers for integration into embedded SoCs. **Four key features:** 1. **Load/Store RISC architecture** — Data processing instructions operate only on registers; memory is accessed only through `LDR` and `STR` instructions. 2. **Fixed 32-bit instruction length** — Simplifies instruction fetch and decode; enables deterministic pipelining. 3. **Large register file** — 37 visible registers including banked SPSRs and stack pointers for fast context switching. 4. **Excellent power efficiency** — Ideal for battery-operated embedded and IoT applications. --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between ARM Cortex-M, Cortex-A, and Cortex-R profiles.** **Model Answer:** | Profile | Target Application | Typical Clock | OS Support | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Cortex-M** | Microcontrollers, IoT nodes, sensors | Up to ~200 MHz (M7) | Bare-metal, RTOS (FreeRTOS) | | **Cortex-A** | Smartphones, tablets, laptops, servers | Up to ~3.5 GHz (Cortex-X4) | Linux, Android, iOS, Windows | | **Cortex-R** | Real-time safety systems (ABS, 5G) | Up to ~1 GHz (R8) | RTOS, AUTOSAR | The three profiles share the base ARM ISA but differ in memory model, privilege levels, cache hierarchy, and pipeline depth. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU Examination Rule**: "Internal Choice permitted between sub-questions (a) and (b) only, OR between two complete alternative questions (full 14 marks)." ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Internal Choice — Module 4]` **(a)** Explain the **register organization of the ARM Cortex-M processor** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the special registers R13, R14, and R15 in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **CPSR and SPSR registers** of the ARM processor. Explain the significance of each condition flag (N, Z, C, V) with an example instruction. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A (a) — Register Organization The ARM Cortex-M3/M4 processor has a uniform **register file of 37 32-bit registers** visible in user/system mode: | Register Range | Quantity | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **R0 – R12** | 13 | General-purpose data registers | | **R13 (SP)** | 1 + banked | Stack Pointer (MSP & PSP) | | **R14 (LR)** | 1 + banked | Link Register | | **R15 (PC)** | 1 | Program Counter | | **CPSR** | 1 | Current Program Status Register | | **SPSR** | 6 | Saved PSR per privileged mode | **Role of Special Registers:** 1. **R13 — Stack Pointer (SP):** In Cortex-M3, the SP is banked into **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** for privileged/handler mode and **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)** for unprivileged thread mode. SP always points to the last item pushed onto the stack (full-descending model). After reset, MSP is loaded from address 0x0000_0000. 2. **R14 — Link Register (LR):** Stores the return address automatically when a subroutine is invoked via `BL` or `BLX`. For example: ``` 0x00001000 BL delay_ms ; LR = 0x00001004 (PC+4) 0x00001004 MOV PC, LR ; Function returns here ``` The lower bits of LR encode the **EXC_RETURN** value on exception entry, used by the hardware to return from an ISR. 3. **R15 — Program Counter (PC):** Holds the address of the currently executing instruction. Due to the 3-stage pipeline, reading PC returns `current_address + 4` (in Thumb mode). Writing to PC causes a branch — this is how `B`, `BL`, `BX`, `POP {PC}`, and exception returns are implemented. --- ### Model Solution for Question A (b) — CPSR and SPSR The **CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** is a 32-bit register containing: - **Condition flags** (N, Z, C, V) — set by arithmetic/logic operations - **Control bits** (I, F, T) — interrupt masks and instruction set state - **Mode bits** (M[4:0]) — current processor mode The **SPSR (Saved PSR)** is a banked register, one per privileged mode, that automatically saves the CPSR when an exception is taken. On exception return (`SUBS PC, LR, #4` or `POP {PC}` with EXC_RETURN), the SPSR is restored into CPSR atomically. **Significance of Condition Flags:** | Flag | Set When | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **N (Negative)** | Result MSB is 1 (signed negative) | `SUBS R0, R1, R2` where R1 < R2 | | **Z (Zero)** | Result equals 0 | `CMP R0, #0` then `BEQ` taken if R0 was 0 | | **C (Carry)** | Unsigned overflow (or no borrow in subtraction) | `ADDS R0, R1, R2` if result > 2³² | | **V (Overflow)** | Signed overflow occurred | `ADDS R0, #0x7FFFFFFF, #1` → overflow | **Example:** ``` MOVS R0, #0x7FFFFFFF ; R0 = 2^31 - 1 (max positive int32) ADDS R0, R0, #1 ; Result = 0x80000000 = -2^31 (negative!) ; N flag = 1, V flag = 1, C flag = 0, Z flag = 0 ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 4 Internal Choice]` **(a)** Explain the **ARM 3-stage pipeline** with a timing diagram. Discuss how branch instructions cause pipeline stalls and how modern Cortex-M cores mitigate this. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of an ARM Cortex-M3 core**. List the major on-chip peripherals that are typically integrated alongside it in a modern SoC. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 3-Stage Pipeline The classic ARM7TDMI pipeline has three stages: $$ \text{Stage 1 (Fetch)} \longrightarrow \text{Stage 2 (Decode)} \longrightarrow \text{Stage 3 (Execute)} $$ **Timing Table** (showing 5 cycles for 4 instructions): | Cycle | Instruction 1 | Instruction 2 | Instruction 3 | Instruction 4 | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **C1** | Fetch | — | — | — | | **C2** | Decode | Fetch | — | — | | **C3** | Execute | Decode | Fetch | — | | **C4** | — | Execute | Decode | Fetch | | **C5** | — | — | Execute | Decode | After cycle 3, one instruction completes per cycle — achieving the theoretical **CPI = 1**. **Branch Penalty:** When a `B` or `BL` is decoded in stage 2, the next two instructions already in the pipeline (fetched in the wrong path) must be flushed. This results in a **2-cycle penalty** per branch. **Mitigation in Cortex-M3:** - **Branch speculation** — Hardware assumes forward branches are not taken and backward are taken. - **Branch target forwarding** — The branch target address is computed in decode stage and forwarded to the fetch unit immediately. - **Prefetch buffer** — The 64-bit instruction prefetch buffer smooths out flash memory access latency. --- ### Model Solution for Question B (b) — Cortex-M3 Internal Architecture The Cortex-M3 core consists of the following functional blocks (see mermaid diagram in Section 4.3): 1. **Instruction Fetch Unit (with Prefetch Buffer)** — Fetches 32-bit aligned instructions from flash/RAM; supports single-cycle fetch via AHB-Lite. 2. **Instruction Decoder** — Decodes 16-bit Thumb-2 instructions into micro-operations. 3. **Execution Unit** — Contains the ALU, barrel shifter, and multiplier. Performs data processing in a single cycle. 4. **Load/Store Unit** — Executes `LDR`, `STR`, `LDM`, `STM` instructions; handles unaligned transfers. 5. **Register Bank** — 37 visible 32-bit registers, each accessible in one cycle via dual-port RAM. 6. **Control Unit** — Manages pipeline flow, exceptions, and debug events. **Typical SoC Peripherals Integrated:** | Peripheral | Function | | :--- | :--- | | **NVIC** | Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (up to 240 IRQs) | | **SysTick** | 24-bit down-counter for RTOS tick | | **MPU** | Memory Protection Unit (8 regions on Cortex-M4) | | **Flash + SRAM** | Code storage and runtime memory | | **GPIO** | General-purpose I/O pins | | **UART/USART** | Serial communication | | **SPI / I²C** | Synchronous serial buses | | **CAN / USB / Ethernet** | Networking interfaces | | **ADC / DAC** | Analog I/O | | **PWM Timers** | Motor control and waveform generation | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in ARM-Related Questions:** > > 1. **PC offset confusion** — Many students write that PC reads as `current_address + 8`. This is correct for **ARM state (32-bit)** but in **Thumb state (used by Cortex-M)**, the offset is `current_address + 4`. Examiners are strict about this distinction. > > 2. **Confusing R13 with MSP only** — On Cortex-M3, R13 is banked into MSP *and* PSP. Simply writing "R13 is the Stack Pointer" loses marks. Specify the dual-pointer mechanism. > > 3. **Confusing ARM Profiles** — Writing that "Cortex-M is used in smartphones" is **wrong**. Smartphones use Cortex-A. Cortex-M is for microcontrollers. This single error can cost up to 2 marks. > > 4. **Forgetting that ARM Holdings does not manufacture chips** — A surprising number of students incorrectly write that "ARM manufactures microcontrollers". Always clarify the licensing model. > > 5. **Misspelling "CPSR" as "CSPR" or "SPSR" as "CPSR"** — Trivial typo, but auto-evaluators and strict examiners deduct 0.5 mark. > > 6. **Confusing SPSR with a CPSR backup register visible in user mode** — SPSR is **only** visible in privileged (handler) mode, and there is **one SPSR per privileged mode** (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, Abort, Undef, Mon). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **ARM** = Advanced RISC Machine — a *licensable* ISA, not a chip. - [ ] ARM Holdings licenses IP to **Qualcomm, Apple, NXP, ST, Samsung**, etc. - [ ] Three Cortex profiles: **M** (MCU), **A** (Application), **R** (Real-time). - [ ] ARM is **Load/Store RISC**, fixed 32-bit instructions, large register file. - [ ] **37 user-visible registers**: R0–R12 + R13 (SP) + R14 (LR) + R15 (PC) + CPSR + 7 banked SPSRs. - [ ] **R13 (SP)** is banked: **MSP** (handler/privileged) and **PSP** (unprivileged thread). - [ ] **R14 (LR)** holds return address; on exceptions, holds **EXC_RETURN**. - [ ] **R15 (PC)** reads as `current + 4` in Thumb, `current + 8` in ARM state. - [ ] **CPSR** flags: **N, Z, C, V** (condition) + **I, F** (interrupt mask) + **T** (Thumb) + **M[4:0]** (mode). - [ ] Cortex-M3 uses a **3-stage pipeline**; CPI ≈ **1** after fill. - [ ] Branch penalty is **2 cycles** in classic ARM7; mitigated by speculation in Cortex-M. - [ ] Vector table at **0x0000_0000**; first entry is **MSP**, second is **Reset Handler**. - [ ] ARMv7-M = the ISA used by Cortex-M3/M4; ARMv8-M = modern with TrustZone. - [ ] Thumb-2 instructions in Cortex-M are a mix of **16-bit** and **32-bit** encodings — improved code density without losing performance. - [ ] System Control Space at **0xE000_0000** contains NVIC, MPU, SysTick (ARM-standard across vendors). - [ ] ARM chips dominate **> 99 % of smartphones** and **> 50 % of IoT/MCU** market.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Core

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the ARM Core?

The **ARM Core** refers to the central processing unit (CPU) core designed by **ARM Holdings (now Arm Ltd.)** based on the **Advanced RISC Machine (ARM)** architecture. It is the heart of billions of embedded systems, smartphones, IoT devices, and microcontrollers. In the context of the KTU syllabus (PCEOT501), the focus is on the **ARM7TDMI** core, a 32-bit RISC processor widely used in LPC2148 microcontrollers and other embedded platforms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The ARM core is a **32-bit RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) processor core** that uses a **Load-Store architecture**, where data processing operations operate only on registers (not memory), and memory is accessed exclusively through explicit Load and Store instructions. The ARM7TDMI implements the **ARMv4T** instruction set architecture.

### 1.2 Decoding the Name "ARM7TDMI"

The naming convention of ARM cores follows a strict taxonomy that students **must** memorize for KTU exams:

| Suffix | Meaning | Technical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **T** | **Thumb** | Supports 16-bit compressed Thumb instruction set alongside 32-bit ARM instructions |
| **D** | **Debug** | Contains hardware debug extensions (JTAG-based boundary scan) |
| **M** | **Multiplier** | Has enhanced hardware multiplier (supports 32×32→64 bit in single cycle) |
| **I** | **EmbeddedICE** | Includes EmbeddedICE macrocell for on-chip breakpoint/watchpoint debug logic |

> [!NOTE]
> The bare "ARM7" (without suffixes) supports only the 32-bit ARM instruction set and lacks debug and multiplier enhancements. The **ARM7TDMI** is the version most frequently referenced in KTU board examination questions.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Efficient Workshop Manager:**
> Imagine a master carpenter (the ARM core) managing a workshop. He has **31 workbenches** (general-purpose registers R0–R15) right next to him where he keeps his tools (data). He only goes to the **storage warehouse** (external memory) when he absolutely must — either to fetch a new tool (**Load**) or to put one away (**Store**). He never tries to work on tools directly in the warehouse. This is precisely the **Load-Store architecture** of ARM: data manipulation happens *only* in registers, never in memory.

### 1.4 Core Architectural Philosophy

The ARM core is engineered around four foundational design principles:

1. **Simplicity favors regularity** — Fixed 32-bit instruction word simplifies decoding.
2. **Smaller is faster** — Small, highly optimized register file.
3. **Make the common case fast** — Conditional execution eliminates many branches.
4. **Good design demands good compromises** — Tradeoffs between performance, power, and silicon area.

### 1.5 Key Distinguishing Features (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Five" ARM Core Features:**
> - **32-bit RISC architecture** with **Load-Store design philosophy**
> - **37 register bank** (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers) — physically only 16–18 visible at a time
> - **Uniform 32-bit fixed-length instructions** (ARM mode) for fast pipelined decoding
> - **Most instructions execute in a single cycle** (with the 3-stage pipeline)
> - **Built-in hardware multiplier and barrel shifter** integrated into the data path

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ARM Core Architectural Classification

The ARM7TDMI core belongs to the **Von Neumann architecture** family, where instructions and data share a **single 32-bit data bus**. This contrasts with ARM9 (Harvard architecture) which uses separate instruction and data buses. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

| Parameter | Von Neumann (ARM7) | Harvard (ARM9 onwards) |
|---|---|---|
| Bus Structure | Single shared bus | Separate I-bus and D-bus |
| Clock per Fetch | 1 fetch = 1 cycle, cannot fetch data and instruction simultaneously | Simultaneous fetch possible |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher |
| KTU Board Frequency | **Very High** | Medium |

## 2.2 The 3-Stage Pipeline (ARM7TDMI)

The ARM7TDMI uses a classic **3-stage pipeline** to overlap instruction execution and increase throughput:

| Stage | Name | Function | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1** | **Fetch (F)** | Instruction is fetched from memory and loaded into the instruction pipeline | 1 clock cycle |
| **Stage 2** | **Decode (D)** | Instruction is decoded; register operands read from register file; barrel shifter evaluated | 1 clock cycle |
| **Stage 3** | **Execute (E)** | ALU operation performed; memory address calculated; branch target computed; result written back | 1 clock cycle |

### 2.2.1 Why a Pipeline Matters — Throughput Logic

In a non-pipelined processor, each instruction takes **3 cycles** to complete. With pipelining, after the pipeline fills (3 cycles), **one instruction completes per clock cycle**. The speedup can be expressed as:

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{non-pipeline}}}{T_{\text{pipeline}}} = \frac{N \times k}{k + (N - 1)}
$$

where **$N$** is the number of instructions, **$k$** is the number of pipeline stages (**$k = 3$** for ARM7TDMI). As $N \to \infty$, the theoretical speedup approaches **$k = 3$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline Hazards:** While the ARM7TDMI pipeline is straightforward, the **branch instruction** causes a "flush" of 2 cycles. ARM mitigates this by allowing **conditional execution** of most instructions (using the `COND` field in bits [31:28] of every instruction), which avoids branches entirely for simple if-then logic.

## 2.3 ARM Core Dataflow Model

The ARM core's data path consists of interconnected functional units operating under control of the instruction decoder. The primary functional units are:

- **Register Bank** — 31 × 32-bit general-purpose registers + 6 status registers
- **Barrel Shifter** — Performs pre-processing on the second operand of every data-processing instruction (logical/arithmetic shift, rotate, etc.) **for free**, in a single cycle, before the ALU sees the data
- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** — Performs 32-bit arithmetic and logic operations
- **Address Register & Incrementer** — Holds memory addresses and auto-increments for sequential access
- **Multiplier** — Enhanced 32×8-bit (early ARM) or 32×32→64-bit (ARM7TDMI) hardware multiplier
- **Instruction Decoder & Control Logic** — Decodes the 32-bit instruction word and asserts control signals

## 2.4 Register Set of the ARM7TDMI Core

The ARM7TDMI exposes **16 general-purpose registers (R0–R15)** plus **1 CPSR (Current Program Status Register)** in any given processor mode, though the underlying physical register file contains **37 registers** (banked for each privileged mode).

| Register | Alias / Role | Special Function |
|---|---|---|
| **R0–R12** | General Purpose | Identical roles in all modes; used for data and address manipulation |
| **R13** | **SP — Stack Pointer** | Each privileged mode has its own banked R13 |
| **R14** | **LR — Link Register** | Holds return address when subroutine is called (BL/BLX); banked per mode |
| **R15** | **PC — Program Counter** | Reads as current instruction address + 8 (ARM mode) or +4 (Thumb mode) |
| **CPSR** | Current Program Status Register | Contains condition flags, interrupt disable bits, mode bits, Thumb state |
| **SPSR** | Saved PSR | Saves CPSR on exception entry; banked per privileged mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** Reading R15 (PC) returns the address of the **current instruction + 8** in ARM state (because the PC is always 2 instructions ahead in a 3-stage pipeline: instruction being fetched and instruction being executed). In Thumb state, it returns **current + 4**.

## 2.5 The CPSR — Current Program Status Register (32 bits)

The CPSR is the **most important status register** and is frequently tested in KTU exams:

| Bits [31:28] | Bits [27:25] | Bits [24] | Bits [23:20] | Bits [19:16] | Bits [15:10] | Bits [9:8] | Bits [7:6] | Bits [5:0] |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Condition Flags | Reserved | J (Jazelle) | Reserved | GE flags | IT [1:0], Reserved | Reserved | Reserved | Mode + Control |

### 2.5.1 Condition Flags (Bits [31:28])

| Flag | Bit | Meaning | Set When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **N** | [31] | **Negative / Less than** | Result bit[31] = 1 (signed overflow) |
| **Z** | [30] | **Zero** | Result equals zero |
| **C** | [29] | **Carry / Borrow / Extend** | Unsigned overflow; for non-arithmetic ops, carries the shifted-out bit |
| **V** | [28] | **Overflow** | Signed overflow occurred in arithmetic operation |

> [!NOTE]
> The C (Carry) flag has multiple meanings. After an **arithmetic** operation, it indicates unsigned carry/borrow. After a **shift/rotate** operation, it holds the *last bit shifted out*. This dual meaning is a classic KTU question.

### 2.5.2 Mode Bits (Bits [4:0])

The 5 mode bits encode the current processor mode:

| Mode Bits | Mode | Privilege | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `10000` | **User (usr)** | Unprivileged | Normal application code |
| `10001` | **FIQ (fiq)** | Privileged | Fast interrupt |
| `10010` | **IRQ (irq)** | Privileged | Normal interrupt |
| `10011` | **Supervisor (svc)** | Privileged | Reset / SWI (software interrupt) |
| `10111` | **Abort (abt)** | Privileged | Memory access failure |
| `11011` | **Undefined (und)** | Privileged | Undefined instruction |
| `11111` | **System (sys)** | Privileged | Privileged User mode |

## 2.6 The ARM Instruction Pipeline — Step-by-Step Walk

Consider three sequential instructions: `I1`, `I2`, `I3` being executed on the ARM7TDMI 3-stage pipeline.

| Clock Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **I1** | Fetch | Decode | Execute | — | — | — |
| **I2** | — | Fetch | Decode | Execute | — | — |
| **I3** | — | — | Fetch | Decode | Execute | — |

- **Cycle 1:** I1 enters Fetch stage.
- **Cycle 2:** I1 moves to Decode; I2 enters Fetch. (Pipeline overlap begins)
- **Cycle 3:** I1 in Execute (last result written back), I2 in Decode, I3 in Fetch. **Pipeline is now full.**
- **Cycles 4, 5, 6, ...:** One instruction completes per cycle.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** This 3-stage pipeline is the same design philosophy used in early MIPS, SPARC, and the original ARM. Modern processors (Cortex-A series) extend this to **13–15+ stages** (deep pipelining) and add **superscalar**, **out-of-order**, and **speculative execution** features — but the foundational concept of overlapping Fetch/Decode/Execute remains.

## 2.7 ARM vs. Thumb — Two States, One Core

The ARM7TDMI can operate in two instruction set states, controlled by bit **T (bit 5) of CPSR**:

| State | Instruction Width | Code Density | Performance | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ARM** | 32 bits | Lower | Higher (more expressive) | Performance-critical inner loops |
| **Thumb** | 16 bits | **~30% higher** | Slightly lower | Memory-constrained embedded systems |

Switching between states is done via the `BX` (Branch and Exchange) instruction. **R0–R7** are directly accessible in Thumb mode; R8–R12 are *not* accessible in Thumb-1 state — a critical detail for KTU questions on Thumb programming.

## 2.8 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Definition | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Pipeline Speedup | $S_{\max} = k$ (number of stages) | $S_{\max} = 3$ (for ARM7TDMI) |
| Actual Speedup | $S = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + (N-1)}$ | Approaches 3 as $N \to \infty$ |
| Pipeline Throughput | $T = \dfrac{N}{k + (N-1)}$ instructions/cycle | Approaches 1 inst/cycle |
| PC value in ARM state | $\text{PC} = \text{Address of current instr.} + 8$ | Pipeline offset = 8 |
| PC value in Thumb state | $\text{PC} = \text{Address of current instr.} + 4$ | Pipeline offset = 4 |
| Word size | $W = 32$ bits | $4$ bytes per word |
| Address bus width | $A = 32$ bits | $2^{32} = 4$ GB address space |
| Branch penalty | $P_{\text{branch}} = 2$ cycles (for non-conditional) | Mitigated by conditional execution |
| ARM instruction size | 32 bits = 4 bytes | Fixed width |
| Thumb instruction size | 16 bits = 2 bytes | Compressed for density |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Speedup of the ARM7TDMI Pipeline

This derivation is **critical** for the 14-mark questions in KTU ESE — examiners love to ask "derive the maximum speedup of the 3-stage pipeline."

### Step 1 — Define Non-Pipelined Execution Time

In a **non-pipelined (single-cycle)** architecture, each instruction must complete all three stages sequentially before the next instruction can begin. Thus, for one instruction:

$$
T_{\text{non-pipeline, 1 inst}} = k \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}
$$

where $k = 3$ (number of pipeline stages for ARM7TDMI) and $t_{\text{cycle}}$ is the duration of one clock cycle.

### Step 2 — Total Time for N Instructions (Non-Pipelined)

For $N$ instructions executed sequentially without pipelining:

$$
T_{\text{non-pipeline}} = N \cdot k \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} = 3N \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** Because every instruction must fully finish its 3 stages before the next can start, we simply multiply the per-instruction time by the instruction count. For example, 10 instructions in a non-pipelined design require $3 \times 10 = 30$ clock cycles.

### Step 3 — Total Time for N Instructions (Pipelined)

In a pipelined design, the first instruction takes $k$ cycles to complete (pipeline fill), but every subsequent instruction completes in just **1 additional cycle**:

$$
T_{\text{pipeline}} = \left[ k + (N - 1) \right] \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} = \left[ 3 + (N - 1) \right] \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The pipeline must be filled once (costing $k = 3$ cycles), after which instructions "fall off the end" of the pipeline at a rate of one per cycle. For 10 instructions: $3 + (10 - 1) = 12$ cycles total.

### Step 4 — Compute the Speedup Ratio

Speedup is defined as the ratio of non-pipelined to pipelined execution times:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{\text{non-pipeline}}}{T_{\text{pipeline}}} = \frac{N \cdot k \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}}{\left[ k + (N - 1) \right] \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}} \\
  &= \frac{N \cdot k}{k + (N - 1)} \\
  &= \frac{3N}{3 + (N - 1)} \\
  &= \frac{3N}{N + 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** The clock period $t_{\text{cycle}}$ cancels out (assuming both designs use the same clock). We are left with a clean ratio in terms of $N$ and $k$.

### Step 5 — Take the Limit as N → ∞

The **maximum theoretical speedup** is achieved in the limit of infinite instructions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{3N}{N + 2} \\
         &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{3N}{N \left( 1 + \frac{2}{N} \right)} \\
         &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{3}{1 + \frac{2}{N}} \\
         &= \frac{3}{1 + 0} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Textual Explanation:** As $N$ becomes very large, the $\frac{2}{N}$ term becomes negligible, and the speedup approaches the number of pipeline stages $k = 3$. This is the **fundamental limit** of a $k$-stage pipeline.

### Step 6 — Numerical Sanity Check

Let us verify with small $N$:

| $N$ | Non-pipeline Cycles | Pipeline Cycles | Speedup $S = 3N/(N+2)$ | % of $S_{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 3 | 1.00 | 33.3% |
| 5 | 15 | 7 | 2.14 | 71.4% |
| 10 | 30 | 12 | 2.50 | 83.3% |
| 100 | 300 | 102 | 2.94 | 98.0% |
| 1000 | 3000 | 1002 | 2.99 | 99.7% |

> **Textual Conclusion:** Even with 1000 instructions, the speedup reaches **99.7%** of the theoretical maximum. This confirms the pipeline is highly effective for real workloads.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Reading R15 in ARM State Returns "PC + 8"

This is a KTU "favorite" question. The 3-stage pipeline shifts each instruction two positions ahead of where it logically "is" in the program counter sequence.

### Step 1 — Identify the Three Pipeline Registers

As instruction `I1` flows through the pipeline at clock cycles 1, 2, 3, the **PC** that points to it in program order is logically at:

- Cycle 1 (Fetch): The address placed on the memory bus is `A`
- Cycle 2 (Decode): `I1` is now in the decode stage, but the **PC has been incremented** to `A + 4` (next instruction in ARM state)
- Cycle 3 (Execute): `I1` is in execute, the PC is now `A + 8`

### Step 2 — Formal Statement

When the **Decode stage** of an instruction reads R15, the value returned is the address of the instruction **two instructions ahead**:

$$
\text{R15}_{\text{read}} = \text{Address}(I_{\text{current}}) + 8 \quad \text{(ARM state)}
$$

### Step 3 — Why +4 in Thumb

In Thumb state, instructions are 16 bits (2 bytes) wide, so the address of the next instruction is only +2. The pipeline offset is **2 instructions ahead**:

$$
\text{R15}_{\text{read}} = \text{Address}(I_{\text{current}}) + 4 \quad \text{(Thumb state)}
$$

because $2 \text{ instructions} \times 2 \text{ bytes/instruction} = 4 \text{ bytes}$.

### Step 4 — Implication for Programmer

When writing position-independent code (e.g., `ADR` pseudo-instruction), the assembler automatically compensates for this offset. A student writing raw `MOV R0, PC` in ARM state gets R0 = `CurrentAddress + 8` — this is **not** a bug; it is a deliberate pipeline visibility feature.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ARM Core Architectural Properties

The following decision matrix summarizes all architectural properties of the ARM7TDMI core as tested in KTU exams:

| Property | Value / Description | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture Type | **Von Neumann** | Single 32-bit bus for I and D |
| ISA Class | **RISC** | Reduced Instruction Set Computer |
| Instruction Set | **ARMv4T** | Includes both ARM and Thumb |
| Word Size | **32 bits** | ALU operates on 32-bit operands |
| Address Space | **$2^{32}$ bytes = 4 GB** | 32-bit address bus |
| Pipeline Stages | **3** | Fetch, Decode, Execute |
| Register File | **31 × 32-bit GPR + 6 SPSRs** | Physically; 16 + 1 CPSR visible at a time |
| Big-Endian / Little-Endian | Configurable (B-bit in CP15) | ARM7TDMI supports both |
| Branch Penalty | **2 cycles** (without prediction) | Flush of Fetch + Decode |
| Interrupt Latency | **~3 cycles** (FIQ); IRQ: ~4 cycles | Plus vector fetch |
| MIPS Rating (at 50 MHz) | ~45 MIPS (Dhrystone 1.1) | Typical for ARM7TDMI |
| Power Dissipation | **~0.6 mW/MHz** (0.18 µm) | Low-power design hallmark |

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: ARM Core vs. Classical CISC (e.g., 8051)

This comparison is a **favorite KTU question** (often worth 7 marks).

| Feature | ARM7TDMI (RISC) | 8051 (CISC) |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Width | Fixed 32 bits (ARM) | Variable 1–3 bytes |
| Addressing Modes | Few (3–4 main modes) | Many (~11 modes) |
| Data Processing | **Registers only** | Registers + Memory (Accumulator-based) |
| Instruction Count | ~30 base instructions | ~111 instructions |
| Clock per Instruction (CPI) | ~1 (pipelined) | 1–4 (variable) |
| Pipeline | 3-stage | None (original) |
| Power | Low | Moderate |
| Performance/MHz | High | Lower |
| Suitability | Modern embedded, mobile | Legacy 8-bit control |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM7TDMI Core Block Diagram (Mermaid Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    addrBus["ADDRESS BUS 32 BIT"] --> addrReg["ADDRESS REGISTER AND INCREMENTER"]
    addrReg --> mem["EXTERNAL MEMORY SINGLE VON NEUMANN BUS"]
    mem --> dataBus["DATA BUS 32 BIT"]
    dataBus --> writeBus["WRITE DATA BUS TO REGISTER BANK OR MEMORY"]
    mem --> instrBus["INSTRUCTION BUS"]
    instrBus --> decoder["INSTRUCTION DECODER AND CONTROL LOGIC"]
    decoder --> regBank["REGISTER BANK 31 GP REGISTERS PLUS 6 SPSR CPSR"]
    decoder --> shifter["BARREL SHIFTER OPERATES ON SECOND OPERAND"]
    shifter --> alu["ALU 32 BIT ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT"]
    regBank --> alu
    alu --> writeBus
    regBank --> cpsrReg["CPSR AND SPSR REGISTERS"]
    decoder --> cpsrReg
    decoder --> mulUnit["ENHANCED HARDWARE MULTIPLIER 32 BY 32 TO 64 BIT"]
    alu --> writeBus
    writeBus --> regBank
    cpsrReg --> condCheck["CONDITION CODE FLAGS N Z C V"]
    condCheck --> decoder
```

> **Diagram Reading Note:** The **Barrel Shifter** is a unique feature of the ARM core — it processes one of the ALU operands *before* the ALU sees it, in a single cycle, with no extra latency. This is why ARM instructions can embed shift operations "for free."

## 4.4 ARM7TDMI Register Bank — Banking Across Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user["USER MODE 10000"]:::user
    fiq["FIQ MODE 10001"]:::priv
    irq["IRQ MODE 10010"]:::priv
    svc["SUPERVISOR MODE 10011"]:::priv
    abt["ABORT MODE 10111"]:::priv
    und["UNDEFINED MODE 11011"]:::priv
    sys["SYSTEM MODE 11111"]:::priv

    user --> r0_12["R0 TO R12 UNBANKED SHARED"]:::shared
    user --> r13_usr["R13 USER STACK POINTER"]:::bank
    user --> r14_usr["R14 USER LINK REGISTER"]:::bank

    fiq --> r0_12
    fiq --> r8_14_fiq["R8 TO R14 BANKED FIQ PRIVATE"]:::bank
    fiq --> spsr_fiq["SPSR FIQ"]:::status

    irq --> r0_12
    irq --> r13_irq["R13 IRQ BANKED"]:::bank
    irq --> r14_irq["R14 IRQ BANKED"]:::bank
    irq --> spsr_irq["SPSR IRQ"]:::status

    svc --> r0_12
    svc --> r13_svc["R13 SVC BANKED"]:::bank
    svc --> r14_svc["R14 SVC BANKED"]:::bank
    svc --> spsr_svc["SPSR SVC"]:::status

    classDef user fill:#C8E6C9,color:#000,stroke:#1B5E20
    classDef priv fill:#FFCDD2,color:#000,stroke:#B71C1C
    classDef shared fill:#FFF9C4,color:#000,stroke:#F57F17
    classDef bank fill:#FFE0B2,color:#000,stroke:#E65100
    classDef status fill:#E1BEE7,color:#000,stroke:#4A148C
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** R0–R7 are **never banked** — they are shared across all modes. R8–R12 are also unbanked, **except** in FIQ mode where R8–R12 are banked to enable ultra-fast context switching during fast interrupt handling. R13 (SP), R14 (LR), and SPSR are banked for **all** privileged modes.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

### Q1. List any six features of the ARM core architecture.
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **32-bit RISC processor** with a Load-Store architecture
2. **37-physical-register file** (31 GPR + 6 status), with 16 user-visible at a time
3. **Fixed 32-bit instruction encoding** (in ARM state) for fast pipelined decoding
4. **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch–Decode–Execute) enabling single-cycle instruction throughput
5. **Integrated barrel shifter** providing free shift/rotate operations in a single cycle
6. **Two operating states** — ARM (32-bit) and Thumb (16-bit compressed) — selected by the T-bit of CPSR
7. **Seven processor modes** (User, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, System) with banked registers for fast exception handling

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

### Q2. What is the significance of the T-bit in the CPSR register of the ARM7TDMI core?
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **T-bit (bit 5) of the CPSR** is the **Thumb state selector**:
- When **T = 0** → Processor is in **ARM state**, executing 32-bit ARM instructions
- When **T = 1** → Processor is in **Thumb state**, executing 16-bit Thumb instructions

The state is switched dynamically by the **`BX` (Branch and Exchange)** instruction. Thumb state is used to achieve **higher code density** (~30% reduction) for memory-constrained embedded systems, while ARM state is used for performance-critical code where the full instruction set is needed.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

### Question A(14): ARM Core Architecture

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the Von Neumann architecture of the ARM7TDMI core with a neat block diagram. List the major functional units of the core.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the 3-stage pipeline of the ARM7TDMI with a timing diagram. Derive the maximum speedup achievable.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition:** The ARM7TDMI core uses the **Von Neumann architecture** in which a **single 32-bit system bus** carries both instructions and data to/from a unified memory system. This is in contrast to the Harvard architecture (used in ARM9) which employs separate buses.

**Major Functional Units:**

1. **Register Bank:** Contains 31 general-purpose 32-bit registers (R0–R12, R13, R14, R15) plus 6 status registers (CPSR + 5 SPSRs). Only 16 GPRs + CPSR are visible at a time in any given mode.
2. **Barrel Shifter:** Performs pre-processing on the second operand of data-processing instructions (LSL, LSR, ASR, ROR, RRX) **for free in one cycle**, before passing it to the ALU.
3. **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit):** Performs 32-bit arithmetic (ADD, SUB, CMP) and logic (AND, OR, XOR) operations.
4. **Address Register & Incrementer:** Generates and increments memory addresses for load/store multiple instructions.
5. **Enhanced Hardware Multiplier:** Computes 32×32 = 64-bit product in a single cycle (this is the "M" in ARM7TDMI).
6. **Instruction Decoder & Control Logic:** Decodes the 32-bit opcode and asserts the appropriate control signals across the data path.
7. **EmbeddedICE & JTAG Tap:** The "D" and "I" suffixes — provides on-chip debug and breakpoint/watchpoint logic accessible via the JTAG interface.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram — re-draw neatly on answer sheet)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**The 3-Stage Pipeline Stages:**
1. **Fetch (F):** The instruction addressed by the PC is read from memory and latched into the instruction pipeline register.
2. **Decode (D):** The instruction is decoded; up to 3 source operands are read from the register file; the barrel shifter pre-processes Operand 2.
3. **Execute (E):** The ALU performs the operation; the result is written back to the destination register; branch targets are computed and the PC updated; load/store addresses are generated.

**Timing Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.2 — re-draw as a three-row cycle-by-cycle table)

**Derivation of Maximum Speedup:**

The detailed derivation is provided in Section 3.1. The summary result:

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N \cdot k}{k + (N-1)} = k = 3
$$

Thus, the **maximum theoretical speedup of the ARM7TDMI's 3-stage pipeline is 3×** compared to a non-pipelined implementation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Speedup Question:**
> Students frequently make these errors and lose 2–3 marks:
> 1. **Forgetting the pipeline fill cost** of $k$ cycles for the first instruction
> 2. **Assuming speedup is always $k$** — it is the *limit*, not the value for any finite $N$
> 3. **Ignoring the clock period** — both pipelined and non-pipelined designs are compared at the *same* clock frequency in standard analysis
> 4. **Not specifying units clearly** — say "instructions per cycle" or "cycles per instruction" explicitly

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

### Question B(14): ARM Core Registers & Pipeline Behavior

**(a) (7 Marks)** Describe the register organization of the ARM7TDMI core. Explain with a diagram how registers are banked across the seven processor modes.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A program executes 100 ARM instructions on an ARM7TDMI core. Compute (i) the total number of cycles required with the 3-stage pipeline and (ii) the speedup achieved compared to a non-pipelined implementation.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Register Organization:** The ARM7TDMI contains **37 physical 32-bit registers**: 31 general-purpose registers (R0–R14 + R15 = PC) and 6 status registers (1 CPSR + 5 SPSR). At any instant, the programmer sees only **16 GPRs (R0–R15) + 1 CPSR** depending on the current mode.

**Register Roles:**
- **R0–R12:** General-purpose data/address registers, unbanked across all modes
- **R13 (SP):** Stack Pointer, **banked** per privileged mode (separate R13 for each of FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, USR, SYS)
- **R14 (LR):** Link Register, **banked** per mode; holds subroutine return address
- **R15 (PC):** Program Counter, points to current instruction + 8 (ARM state)
- **CPSR:** Current Program Status Register, single physical copy
- **SPSR:** Saved Program Status Register, **banked** per privileged mode

**Special Note on FIQ:** In FIQ mode, registers R8–R14 are all banked (instead of just R13–R14), providing **7 private registers** for ultra-fast interrupt service routine execution without needing to push registers to the stack.

**Banking Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Number of instructions: $N = 100$
- Pipeline stages: $k = 3$ (Fetch, Decode, Execute)

**(i) Total Cycles with 3-Stage Pipeline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{pipeline}} &= \left[ k + (N - 1) \right] \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} \\
                    &= \left[ 3 + (100 - 1) \right] \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} \\
                    &= \left[ 3 + 99 \right] \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} \\
                    &= 102 \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The pipelined execution of 100 instructions takes **102 clock cycles**.

**(ii) Speedup Compared to Non-Pipelined:**

Non-pipelined cycles for 100 instructions:

$$
T_{\text{non-pipeline}} = N \cdot k \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} = 100 \times 3 \cdot t_{\text{cycle}} = 300 \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}
$$

Speedup:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_{\text{non-pipeline}}}{T_{\text{pipeline}}} = \frac{300 \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}}{102 \cdot t_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{300}{102} \\
  &= 2.941
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
S \approx 2.94
$$

> **Result:** The achieved speedup is **2.94×**, which is **98.0%** of the theoretical maximum of 3×. The efficiency is very high because 100 is a sufficiently large workload for the pipeline to reach near-peak performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Pipeline Calculation:**
> 1. **Do not compute cycles as simply $N$** — students forget the initial pipeline fill cost of $k$ cycles
> 2. **Always state your variables** ($N$, $k$) before substituting values
> 3. **Show all intermediate arithmetic steps** — KTU expects step-by-step working, not just a final number
> 4. **Mention the unit explicitly** (cycles, not "time") unless the clock period is given

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ARM Core Module:**

- ✅ **ARM = Advanced RISC Machine** — 32-bit RISC, Load-Store architecture
- ✅ **ARM7TDMI suffixes:** T (Thumb), D (Debug), M (Multiplier), I (EmbeddedICE)
- ✅ **Architecture:** Von Neumann for ARM7 (single shared bus); Harvard for ARM9 onwards
- ✅ **3-stage pipeline:** Fetch → Decode → Execute; theoretical max speedup = 3
- ✅ **Pipeline formulas:** $T_{\text{pipe}} = k + (N-1)$; $T_{\text{non-pipe}} = N \cdot k$; $S_{\max} = k$
- ✅ **Register file:** 37 physical (31 GPR + 6 status); 16 GPR + 1 CPSR visible at a time
- ✅ **R15 (PC) offset:** +8 in ARM state, +4 in Thumb state
- ✅ **R13 (SP) and R14 (LR) are banked** per privileged mode
- ✅ **FIQ banks R8–R14** (7 private registers) for ultra-fast interrupt handling
- ✅ **CPSR condition flags:** N (bit 31), Z (bit 30), C (bit 29), V (bit 28)
- ✅ **C flag dual meaning:** unsigned carry (arithmetic) / last bit shifted out (shift/rotate)
- ✅ **Thumb bit T (bit 5 of CPSR):** 0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state
- ✅ **Barrel shifter is "free":** Performs shifts/rotates on Operand 2 in 0 extra cycles
- ✅ **7 processor modes:** User, FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND, SYS — mode bits in CPSR[4:0]
- ✅ **Branch penalty:** 2 cycles in ARM7TDMI (mitigated by conditional execution via COND field [31:28])
- ✅ **Branch instruction is `BX`** to switch between ARM and Thumb states
- ✅ **Endianness:** Configurable (Big or Little) via SETEND instruction or CP15
- ✅ **Address space:** $2^{32}$ bytes = 4 GB (32-bit address bus)
- ✅ **Typical clock:** 50–100 MHz for ARM7TDMI in LPC2148

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the ARM core" in a 14-mark question, **always** include: (1) the architectural classification (RISC, Load-Store, Von Neumann), (2) the register file size and banking, (3) the CPSR and condition flags, (4) the 3-stage pipeline with a timing diagram, and (5) a numerical speedup derivation. This 5-point structure covers virtually every KTU question variant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Microcontroller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM Microcontroller (Advanced RISC Machine):** An ARM Microcontroller is a 32-bit/64-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC) based processor core integrated with memory, peripherals, and I/O on a single chip. In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, the term specifically refers to the **ARM7TDMI-S** core embedded within the **NXP LPC2148** microcontroller — a widely adopted 32-bit MCU for embedded systems education and industrial applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes the architecture of the ARM7TDMI core, the feature set of the LPC2148, register organization, memory map, and the GPIO/peripheral programming model. These constitute high-weightage ESE (End Semester Evaluation) areas.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **ARM Microcontroller** as a **highly disciplined, multi-skilled workshop supervisor** in a factory:

- The **ARM7TDMI core** is the **"supervisor's brain"** — it reads work orders (instructions), thinks quickly, and delegates tasks to workers (peripherals).
- The **Register Bank (R0–R15)** is the **"supervisor's personal notebook"** — a tiny ultra-fast scratchpad where intermediate calculations are jotted down.
- The **Flash + RAM memory** is the **"factory's filing cabinet + workbench"** — the cabinet (Flash) stores permanent instructions, the workbench (RAM) holds the items being actively assembled.
- The **Peripherals (UART, ADC, Timers, GPIO, SPI, I²C)** are the **"specialized workers"** — each worker has a unique skill set, and the supervisor hands them precise jobs via shared memory (registers).
- The **Pipeline (3-stage)** is the **"assembly line mentality"** — while the supervisor is delivering one finished product, the next is being packaged, and the one after is being built.

### 1.3 Key Architectural Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Architecture type:** **32-bit RISC (Load-Store architecture)**
- **Operating Voltage:** **3.0 V – 3.6 V** (typical **3.3 V**)
- **Max CPU Clock:** **Up to 60 MHz** (LPC2148)
- **ARM7TDMI-S data width:** **32 bits**
- **Register file size:** **37 × 32-bit registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status)
- **Pipeline stages:** **3-stage (Fetch → Decode → Execute)**
- **On-chip Flash:** **512 KB** (LPC2148)
- **On-chip SRAM:** **32 KB + 8 KB USB RAM** (LPC2148)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU:** "**A**dvanced **R**ISC **M**achines" — 32-bit, 3-stage pipeline, 37 registers, Load-Store, **T**humb, **D**ebug, **M**ultiplier, **I**ce (the seven features of the ARM7TDMI core).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
