\text{Tag bits} &= A - \log_2(S) - \log_2(B) \\
                &= 32 - 10 - 4 \\
                &= 18 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $18 + 10 + 4 = 32$ ✓

**Final Answer Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of Sets | **1024** |
| Offset Bits | **4** |
| Index Bits | **10** |
| Tag Bits | **18** |

---

### **Question 1B — [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **AMBA bus architecture** in detail. Compare AHB, APB, and AXI buses with their features and use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A processor has a **base CPI = 1.0, instruction count = 1 billion, clock = 500 MHz, miss rate = 2%, miss penalty = 40 cycles**. Calculate the **effective CPI, CPU execution time, and MIPS rating**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question 1B (a) [7 Marks]**

**AMBA Overview (2 marks):**

AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture) is ARM's open-standard on-chip interconnect protocol that defines how functional blocks (CPU, memory, DMA, peripherals) communicate inside an SoC. Modern AMBA specifications include APB, AHB, AXI, CHI, and ACE.

**AMBA Bus Comparison (5 marks):**

| Bus | **APB** (Advanced Peripheral Bus) | **AHB** (Advanced High-performance Bus) | **AXI** (Advanced eXtensible Interface) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Width** | 8/16/32-bit | 32/64/128-bit | 64/128-bit |
| **Speed** | Low (50 MHz) | High (150 MHz) | Very High (1+ GHz) |
| **Pipelining** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Burst Transfers** | No | Yes (INCR, WRAP4/8/16) | Full burst with strobes |
| **Channels** | Single shared | Single shared | 5 independent (AW, W, B, AR, R) |
| **Out-of-Order** | No | No | Yes |
| **Use Case** | UART, GPIO, Timers, Keypad | SRAM, DMA, high-speed peripherals | DDR memory, GPU, multi-core SoC |
| **Bridge** | Connected via AHB-to-APB bridge | Master of APB bridge | Coherent interconnect for cores |

**Why AXI is Preferred for Modern Multi-Core SoCs:**

- Multiple outstanding transactions allow core A to issue a memory read while core B is still computing.
- Independent write and read channels prevent bus contention.
- Quality of Service (QoS) signals allow prioritization for real-time cores.

---

### **Model Answer — Question 1B (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $CPI_{base} = 1.0$
- $I = 1 \times 10^9$
- $f_{clk} = 500$ MHz $= 5 \times 10^8$ Hz
- Miss rate $= 0.02$
- Miss penalty $= 40$ cycles

**Step 1: Effective CPI (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI_{eff} &= CPI_{base} + (\text{miss rate} \times \text{miss penalty}) \\
          &= 1.0 + (0.02 \times 40) \\
          &= 1.0 + 0.8 \\
          &= 1.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: CPU Execution Time (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CPU} &= \frac{I \times CPI_{eff}}{f_{clk}} \\
        &= \frac{1 \times 10^9 \times 1.8}{5 \times 10^8} \\
        &= \frac{1.8 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^8} \\
        &= 3.6 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: MIPS Rating (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MIPS &= \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{eff} \times 10^6} \\
     &= \frac{5 \times 10^8}{1.8 \times 10^6} \\
     &= 277.78 \text{ MIPS}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer Table

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Effective CPI | **1.8** |
| CPU Time | **3.6 seconds** |
| MIPS | **~277.78** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Cache bit calculation errors:** Forgetting that address bits = Tag + Index + Offset. Always cross-verify the sum equals **32 bits** (or 36 for LPAE).
> 2. **MPU alignment:** The base address **must** be aligned to the region size. Marks are deducted if this is not stated.
> 3. **AMBA confusion:** Mixing AHB and AXI features. Remember: **AXI has 5 channels; AHB has 1 shared**.
> 4. **NEON lane count:** Students often misstate NEON as "8 lanes" — correct count depends on data width. **128-bit register / element size = lane count**.
> 5. **CPI derivation:** Forgetting to multiply by **miss penalty**, not add. Effective CPI = base + (rate × penalty).
> 6. **Coprocessor register naming:** CP15 registers are written as **c0, c1, c2...** — students often write "C0, C1" which is grammatically incorrect.
> 7. **AMAT formula:** Some students write $AMAT = h \times t_h + (1-h) \times t_m$ — this is **correct** and equivalent to $AMAT = t_h + (1-h) \times t_m$ only if $t_m$ already includes $t_h$. Clarify in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Advanced Features of ARM (Module 4)**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Remember

- **MPU:** 8 regions in Cortex-M3/M4; base must align to size; higher region number priority.
- **MMU:** Uses page tables; performs virtual → physical translation; present in Cortex-A, not Cortex-M.
- **Cache types:** Direct-mapped, N-way set associative, fully associative; write-through vs write-back.
- **Cache formula:** $S = C / (B \times N)$; Tag + Index + Offset = Address bits.
- **AMAT:** $AMAT = t_h + (1 - h) \times t_m$.
- **Write Buffer:** FIFO, typically 4–16 entries, decouples CPU from memory write latency.
- **Coprocessors:** CP14 → Debug; CP15 → System control (cache, MPU, MMU, TCM).
- **AMBA:** APB (slow peripherals), AHB (medium speed, burst), AXI (high speed, multi-channel).
- **NEON:** SIMD engine, 128-bit registers, parallel processing of 16×8-bit or 8×16-bit data.
- **Jazelle:** Direct Java bytecode execution (deprecated in ARMv8).
- **Thumb-2:** Mixed 16/32-bit ISA; ~30% code size reduction vs full ARM.
- **Power Management:** Clock gating, power gating, DVFS, WFI/WFE.

### ✅ Formula Quick-Fire

| Formula | Use |
|---|---|
| $S = C / (B \times N)$ | Cache sets |
| $\text{Tag} = A - \log_2(S) - \log_2(B)$ | Tag bits |
| $AMAT = t_h + (1 - h) \times t_m$ | Avg memory time |
| $CPI_{eff} = CPI_{base} + mr \times mp$ | Effective CPI |
| $T_{CPU} = I \times CPI / f_{clk}$ | CPU time |
| $MIPS = f / (CPI \times 10^6)$ | Throughput rating |
| $S_{SIMD} = \text{Lane count}$ | SIMD speedup |
| $P_d = \alpha C V^2 f$ | Dynamic power |

### ✅ Common Mistakes to Avoid

- ❌ Confusing **MPU** (regions) with **MMU** (pages).
- ❌ Forgetting that **higher region number wins** in MPU overlap.
- ❌ Stating AXI has only one bus channel.
- ❌ Using the pipe symbol `\|` for absolute value inside a table (breaks markdown).
- ❌ Confusing **write-through** vs **write-back** cache policies.
- ❌ Mixing up **c1** (Control) and **c2** (TTBR) CP15 registers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Advanced Features of ARM**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, PCEOT501 — Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems.*

topics.title: Architecture Versions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Architecture Versions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of microcontrollers and embedded systems, **Architecture Versions** refers to the systematic, generational evolution of processor and microcontroller instruction set architectures (ISAs) over time. Each version introduces refinements in **data width**, **addressing modes**, **pipelining depth**, **power management**, **memory protection**, and **peripheral integration** while maintaining backward compatibility for software portability. For the **Intel 8051 family**, architecture versions trace from the original **MCS-51 (1980)** through modern derivatives like the **8052, 8xC51Fx, and C8051Fxxx (Silicon Labs)**. For the **ARM family**, architecture versions are formally designated as **ARMv1 through ARMv9**, with the Cortex-M series (used in embedded systems) primarily based on **ARMv6-M, ARMv7-M, and ARMv8-M**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Architecture versions are critical for understanding **scalability**, **code reusability**, and **hardware-software co-design** in real-time embedded applications. You must distinguish between **Architecture Version** (ISA specification, e.g., ARMv7-M) and **Architecture Name** (e.g., Cortex-M3). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine architecture versions as **generations of automobiles**: - **Generation 1 (ARMv1 / Original 8051):** Basic sedan — gets you from point A to B, no power steering, no airbags. - **Generation 2 (ARMv4 / 8052):** Adds power steering and basic safety — still no infotainment. - **Generation 3 (ARMv7-M / Cortex-M3):** Modern car with ABS, airbags, fuel injection — efficient, safe, and feature-rich. - **Generation 4 (ARMv8-M / Cortex-M33):** Electric vehicle with autopilot, regenerative braking, and IoT connectivity. Each generation **inherits** the DNA of the previous (backward compatibility) but **extends** capabilities. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics for Architecture Versions:** > - **Data Bus Width:** 8-bit (8051) | 32-bit (ARM Cortex-M) | 64-bit (ARMv8-A) > - **Address Bus Width:** 16-bit (8051) | 32-bit (ARM Cortex-M) > - **Clock Speed Range:** 12 MHz (original 8051) to 200+ MHz (modern Cortex-M7) > - **Pipeline Stages:** 1 (8051) → 3 (Cortex-M0/M0+) → 3 (Cortex-M3/M4) → 6 (Cortex-M7) > - **Power Consumption:** ~mW (8051) to ~µW/MHz (Cortex-M0+) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Intel 8051 Architecture Evolution The 8051 architecture has evolved through several distinct versions, each addressing specific limitations of the original: ### 2.1.1 Original MCS-51 (1980) - **Harvard architecture** with separate code and data memory - 8-bit ALU, 16-bit address bus - **64 KB** program memory, **64 KB** external data memory - **128 bytes** internal RAM + SFR space - **4 KB** on-chip ROM (mask-programmed) - Two 16-bit timers, full-duplex UART, 5 interrupt sources ### 2.1.2 8052 (Enhanced 8051) - **256 bytes** internal RAM (doubled) - **8 KB** on-chip ROM - **Three** 16-bit timers - **6 interrupt** sources - **256 bytes** of internal data memory ### 2.1.3 8xC51Fx (Philips/Signetics Family) | Version | Key Enhancement | |---------|-----------------| | **80C51FA** | Programmable Counter Array (PCA) | | **80C51FB** | Up to 64 KB program memory | | **80C51FC** | Enhanced serial interface, dual data pointers | | **80C51FX+** | I²C bus, A/D converter integration | ### 2.1.4 Modern Derivatives - **Silicon Labs C8051Fxxx:** Up to **100 MIPS**, 12-bit ADC, 100+ MHz clock - **Dallas 89C4x0:** Real-time clock, NV RAM, watchdog timer - **Atmel (Microchip) AT89C51:** Flash memory variant, ISP support ## 2.2 ARM Architecture Versions — The Core Topic for KTU The **ARM architecture** is the industry-standard for 32-bit embedded systems. Each version is formally documented in an **Architecture Reference Manual (ARM ARM)**. ### 2.2.1 Historical ARM Architecture Timeline | Version | Year | Key Innovation | Target Application | |---------|------|----------------|-------------------| | **ARMv1** | 1985 | First ARM processor (ARM1) | Test chip | | **ARMv2** | 1987 | Multiply-accumulate (MUL/MLA) | ARM2/ARM3 | | **ARMv3** | 1992 | 32-bit address space, CPSR/SPSR | ARM6, ARM7 | | **ARMv4** | 1994 | Halfword/sign-extended loads | ARM7TDMI, ARM9 | | **ARMv4T** | 1995 | **Thumb instruction set** (16-bit) | ARM7TDMI | | **ARMv5TE** | 1999 | Enhanced DSP, CLZ instruction | ARM9E, ARM10 | | **ARMv6** | 2001 | SIMD media instructions, Thumb-2 precursor | ARM11 | | **ARMv6-M** | 2004 | **Cortex-M0/M0+/M1** baseline | Ultra-low-power MCU | | **ARMv7-M** | 2004 | **Cortex-M3/M4/M7** | Mainstream embedded MCU | | **ARMv7-R** | 2004 | Cortex-R4/R5/R7 | Real-time systems | | **ARMv7-A** | 2004 | Cortex-A5/A8/A9/A15 | Application processors | | **ARMv8-A** | 2011 | **64-bit (AArch64)** | Smartphones, servers | | **ARMv8-M** | 2015 | Cortex-M23/M33 (with TrustZone) | Secure IoT | | **ARMv8.1-M** | 2017 | Cortex-M55, M85 (Helium/MVE) | ML/AI at edge | | **ARMv9** | 2021 | SVE2, Confidential Compute | AI, 5G, automotive | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula/Rule | Unit/Value | |---------|-------------|------------| | **Memory addressing (8051)** | $\text{Addressable Memory} = 2^n$ bytes | $n$ = address bus width | | **8051 Max Program Memory** | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | **64 KB** | | **ARMv7-M Word size** | $W = 32$ | **bits** | | **Thumb-2 Code Density** | $\text{Density Improvement} \approx 26\%$ vs ARM | — | | **Pipeline CPI (Cortex-M3)** | $CPI_{avg} = 1.25$ | cycles/instruction | | **Dhrystone Score (Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz)** | $DMIPS = \frac{168}{1.25} \approx 134.4$ | DMIPS | | **Power Efficiency (Cortex-M0+)** | $\approx 11 \mu W/MHz$ | watts | | **TrustZone Memory Split** | $\text{Secure} : \text{Non-Secure} = 1 : 1$ configurable | ratio | | **Von Neumann → Harvard** | $H \neq N$ buses for code vs data | boolean | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why Architecture Versions Matter in Engineering:** > 1. **Code Portability:** A firmware written for ARMv7-M can theoretically run on any Cortex-M3/M4/M7 device with minor changes. > 2. **Obsolescence Planning:** Industrial embedded systems (e.g., automotive ECUs) require 10-20 year support — selecting the right architecture version is critical. > 3. **Security:** ARMv8-M's TrustZone is essential for IoT devices handling sensitive data (medical, financial). > 4. **Power Budgeting:** Choosing Cortex-M0+ (ARMv6-M) for battery-operated devices saves significant energy over Cortex-M4. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: 8051 Memory Addressing Capability The 8051 uses a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** and **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)**. We derive the maximum addressable memory: $$ \text{Max Memory} = 2^{n} $$ where $n$ is the number of address lines. For the 8051, $n = 16$. **Step 1:** Identify address bus width. $$ n = 16 $$ **Step 2:** Apply the memory addressing formula. $$ \text{Max Memory} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the power of 2. $$ 2^{16} = 2^{10} \times 2^{6} = 1024 \times 64 = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 4:** Convert to kilobytes. $$ \text{Max Memory} = \frac{65{,}536}{1024} = 64 \text{ KB} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** The factor $2^{10} = 1024$ is used for KB conversion because $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} \text{ bytes}$ in binary addressing systems (standard for memory architectures). ## 3.2 Derivation: ARM Cortex-M3 Performance Calculation To compute the **Dhrystone MIPS (DMIPS)** rating of a Cortex-M3: **Step 1:** Identify core clock frequency. $$ f_{CLK} = 168 \text{ MHz} \quad \text{(typical for STM32F4)} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) for Cortex-M3. $$ CPI_{avg} = 1.25 $$ **Step 3:** Compute MIPS rating. $$ \text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{CPI \times 10^6} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute values. $$ \text{MIPS} = \frac{168 \times 10^6}{1.25 \times 10^6} $$ **Step 5:** Evaluate. $$ \text{MIPS} = \frac{168}{1.25} = 134.4 \text{ MIPS} \approx 134 \text{ DMIPS} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** $1 \text{ DMIPS} = 1 \text{ VAX MIPS}$ of throughput on the Dhrystone benchmark. The Cortex-M3 achieves 1.25 DMIPS/MHz, hence the calculation. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Architecture Version Comparison | Feature | 8051 (MCS-51) | ARMv6-M (M0/M0+) | ARMv7-M (M3/M4) | ARMv8-M (M33) | |---------|---------------|------------------|-----------------|----------------| | **Data Width** | 8-bit | 32-bit | 32-bit | 32-bit | | **Pipeline Stages** | 1 (CISC) | 2-3 | 3 | 3 | | **Thumb-2 Support** | No | Thumb only | Full Thumb-2 | Full Thumb-2 | | **DSP Extensions** | No | No | M4: Yes | Yes | | **FPU** | No | No | M4F: Single-precision | Double-precision | | **TrustZone** | No | No | No | Yes | | **Interrupt Latency** | ~7 cycles | 16 cycles | **12 cycles** | 12 cycles | | **Typical Clock** | 12 MHz | 32-50 MHz | 72-200 MHz | 50-180 MHz | | **DMIPS/MHz** | 0.1 | 0.9 | 1.25 | 1.5 | | **Power** | ~50 mW | 11 µW/MHz | 32 µW/MHz | 30 µW/MHz | ## 3.4 Derivation: Code Size Reduction via Thumb Instruction Set **Step 1:** Define the average instruction size in ARM state (32-bit). $$ S_{ARM} = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2:** Define the average instruction size in Thumb state (16-bit). $$ S_{Thumb} = 16 \text{ bits} = 2 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the size reduction ratio. $$ R = \frac{S_{ARM} - S_{Thumb}}{S_{ARM}} = \frac{32 - 16}{32} = 0.5 = 50\% $$ **Step 4:** Apply empirical correction (Thumb-2 mix gives ~26% reduction over pure ARM). $$ R_{empirical} = 0.26 \quad \text{(mixed code)} $$ **Step 5:** For 64 KB of ARM code, equivalent Thumb-2 size: $$ S_{Thumb2} = 64 \text{ KB} \times (1 - 0.26) = 64 \times 0.74 = 47.36 \text{ KB} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Thumb-2 introduces 32-bit instructions for complex operations, so the reduction is not the theoretical maximum of 50%, but empirically ~26%. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ARM Architecture Version Evolution Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[ARM Architecture 1985] --> B[ARMv1 1985] B --> C[ARMv2 1987] C --> D[ARMv3 1992] D --> E[ARMv4 1994] E --> F[ARMv4T 1995
Thumb Added] F --> G[ARMv5TE 1999
DSP Extensions] G --> H[ARMv6 2001
SIMD] H --> I[ARMv6-M 2004
Cortex M0] H --> J[ARMv7-M 2004
Cortex M3 M4 M7] H --> K[ARMv7-R 2004
Cortex R4 R5] H --> L[ARMv7-A 2004
Cortex A8 A9] L --> M[ARMv8-A 2011
64 BIT AArch64] J --> N[ARMv8-M 2015
TrustZone M33] N --> O[ARMv8 1-M 2017
Helium MVE] M --> P[ARMv9 2021
SVE2 AI] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: 8051 vs ARM Cortex-M3 ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[8051 Original MCS51] S1A[8 BIT ALU] --> S1B[128 BYTE RAM] S1A --> S1C[4 KB ROM] S1A --> S1D[2 x 16 BIT TIMER] S1A --> S1E[UART] end subgraph S2[ARM Cortex M3 ARMv7M] S2A[32 BIT ALU] --> S2B[THUMB 2 DECODER] S2A --> S2C[NVIC] S2A --> S2D[MPU] S2A --> S2E[3 STAGE PIPELINE] S2F[AHB BUS MATRIX] --> S2A end S1A -.EVOLUTION.-> S2A S1B -.SCALED.-> S2B S1D -.ENHANCED.-> S2E ``` ## 4.3 Architecture Version Selection Flowchart for Embedded Design ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[New Embedded Project] --> Q1{Battery Powered?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Processing Needs?} Q1 -->|No| Q3{Real Time Control?} Q2 -->|Simple IO| M0[ARMv6M Cortex M0+] Q2 -->|DSP Math| M4[ARMv7M Cortex M4] Q3 -->|Hard RT| M3[ARMv7M Cortex M3] Q3 -->|Cryptography| M33[ARMv8M Cortex M33] M0 --> End[Selected Architecture Version] M4 --> End M3 --> End M33 --> End ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is meant by "architecture version" in the context of ARM processors? Why is it different from the "architecture name"? **Model Answer:** An **architecture version** (e.g., ARMv7-M) is the formal ISA specification defining instruction set, registers, exception model, and memory model. The **architecture name** (e.g., Cortex-M3) is a specific implementation/brand. The architecture version ensures **compatibility** across multiple processor implementations, while the name refers to a specific product design by ARM Holdings. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** List any **three major enhancements** of the 8052 over the original 8051. **Model Answer:** 1. **256 bytes** of internal RAM (compared to 128 bytes in 8051). 2. **Three** 16-bit timers/counters (8051 has two). 3. **6 interrupt sources** (8051 has 5) and an enhanced UART with auto-address recognition capability. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4 — Choice A]** **(a)** With the help of a clear diagram, explain the **ARM architecture evolution** from ARMv4 to ARMv8-M. Highlight the **Thumb instruction set** introduction. (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare the **ARMv6-M (Cortex-M0+)** and **ARMv7-M (Cortex-M3)** architectures in terms of pipeline, performance (DMIPS/MHz), and interrupt handling. (7 Marks) **Model Solution for (a):** The ARM architecture has undergone continuous evolution: 1. **ARMv4 (1994):** Introduced in ARM7 and ARM9. Featured 32-bit ARM and 16-bit Thumb support was absent initially. Supported load/store architecture with barrel shifter. 2. **ARMv4T (1995):** A landmark version. **Thumb instruction set was introduced** — a 16-bit compressed instruction set that improves code density by ~30%. Processors: ARM7TDMI. 3. **ARMv5TE (1999):** Added enhanced DSP instructions (multiplies, saturating arithmetic) for signal processing. 4. **ARMv6 (2001):** Introduced SIMD media instructions for multimedia, and improved exception handling. Precursor to Cortex family. 5. **ARMv6-M (2004):** Optimized for microcontroller applications. Introduced **Cortex-M0, M0+, M1** cores. Uses only Thumb-2 subset for ultra-low power. 6. **ARMv7-M (2004):** Introduced **Cortex-M3, M4, M7** cores. Full Thumb-2 support, hardware divide, deterministic interrupt handling via **NVIC** (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller). 7. **ARMv8-M (2015):** Introduced **TrustZone security extension**, used in Cortex-M23 and M33. Adds secure/non-secure world separation for IoT security. ``` Evolution: ARMv4 → ARMv4T (Thumb) → ARMv5TE → ARMv6 → ARMv6-M → ARMv7-M → ARMv8-M ``` **Model Solution for (b):** | Parameter | ARMv6-M (Cortex-M0+) | ARMv7-M (Cortex-M3) | |-----------|---------------------|---------------------| | **Pipeline** | 2-stage (von Neumann) | 3-stage (Harvard) | | **DMIPS/MHz** | 0.9 | 1.25 | | **CoreMark/MHz** | 2.46 | 3.34 | | **Interrupt Latency** | 16 cycles | **12 cycles** | | **NVIC** | Yes (basic) | Yes (full) | | **Wake-up Interrupt Controller** | Yes | Yes | | **Bit-banding** | Optional | Yes | | **MPU** | No (in M0+) | Optional 8-region | | **Instruction Set** | Subset of Thumb-2 | Full Thumb-2 | | **Target** | Ultra-low power IoT | Mainstream MCU | **Key Engineering Insight:** Cortex-M3 offers **~40% higher performance** (1.25 vs 0.9 DMIPS/MHz) and a more deterministic interrupt model, making it preferred for hard real-time systems like motor control. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4 — Choice B]** **(a)** Explain the **evolution of the 8051 architecture** with a comparison between **MCS-51, 8052, 8xC51Fx, and modern derivatives (C8051Fxxx)**. (7 Marks) **(b)** Derive the maximum **addressable memory** of the 8051 microcontroller. Show the steps clearly. (7 Marks) **Model Solution for (a):** **MCS-51 (1980):** Original Intel design. 4 KB ROM, 128 bytes RAM, 2 timers, 5 interrupts, 64 KB max program memory. **8052 (Enhanced):** Doubled RAM to 256 bytes, added third timer, expanded interrupt sources to 6. Backward compatible with 8051. **8xC51Fx (Philips):** Introduced **Programmable Counter Array (PCA)**, dual data pointers, I²C bus interface, and integrated A/D converters. Some variants supported up to 64 KB program memory with banking. **C8051Fxxx (Silicon Labs):** Modern derivative achieving **up to 100 MIPS**, 12-bit 200 kSPS ADC, integrated voltage reference, multiple UARTs, and flash memory up to 128 KB. Pipelined CIP-51 core retains 8051 instruction set compatibility. | Version | Year | RAM | ROM | Timers | Special Feature | |---------|------|-----|-----|--------|----------------| | MCS-51 | 1980 | 128 B | 4 KB | 2 | Original | | 8052 | — | 256 B | 8 KB | 3 | Enhanced | | 8xC51Fx | 1990s | 256 B+ | 8-32 KB | 3+ | PCA, I²C, ADC | | C8051Fxxx | 2000s+ | 8 KB+ | 64-128 KB | 5+ | 100 MIPS, Flash | **Model Solution for (b):** Given: 8051 has a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)**. **Step 1:** The number of unique addresses possible: $$ N_{addresses} = 2^n $$ where $n = 16$. **Step 2:** Substituting: $$ N = 2^{16} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluating: $$ N = 65{,}536 \text{ locations} $$ **Step 4:** Each location holds 1 byte (8 bits): $$ \text{Total Memory} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 5:** Converting to kilobytes: $$ \text{Memory} = \frac{65{,}536}{1024} = 64 \text{ KB} $$ **Result:** The 8051 can address a maximum of **64 KB** of program memory and **64 KB** of external data memory separately (Harvard architecture). With bank switching in 8xC51Fx, this can be extended up to **8 MB** using additional I/O lines. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing Architecture Version with Processor Name:** Many students write "Cortex-M3 is an architecture" — INCORRECT. Cortex-M3 is a *processor design*; its architecture version is **ARMv7-M**. > 2. **Forgetting the Harvard vs Von Neumann distinction:** 8051 is Harvard, Cortex-M3 is also Harvard, but Cortex-M0+ is Von Neumann. Marks deducted if confused. > 3. **Miscalculating 2¹⁶:** Students often write 2¹⁶ = 65,000 (round figure). The exact value **65,536 bytes = 64 KB** is required. > 4. **Missing Thumb in ARMv4T:** The "T" in ARMv4T specifically denotes Thumb support — examiners explicitly test this. > 5. **Skipping pipeline stages:** When asked to compare, always mention **pipeline depth, CPI, and DMIPS/MHz together**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Architecture Versions** - **8051 Evolution Path:** MCS-51 → 8052 → 8xC51Fx → C8051Fxxx (Silicon Labs) - **Key 8051 Versions:** Original 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM | 8052: 256 B RAM, 3 timers - **ARM Architecture:** 9 major versions (v1 to v9); embedded uses **v6-M, v7-M, v8-M** - **Thumb Instruction Set:** Introduced in **ARMv4T (1995)** — 16-bit compressed ISA - **Thumb-2:** Full mix of 16/32-bit instructions in **ARMv7-M** (Cortex-M3) - **Cortex Family Mapping:** - M0/M0+ → **ARMv6-M** (ultra-low power) - M3/M4/M7 → **ARMv7-M** (mainstream) - M23/M33 → **ARMv8-M** (with TrustZone security) - M55/M85 → **ARMv8.1-M** (Helium/ML extensions) - **Pipeline Stages:** 8051 = 1 | M0+ = 2-3 | M3 = 3 | M7 = 6 - **DMIPS/MHz:** 8051 ≈ 0.1 | M0+ = 0.9 | M3 = 1.25 | M4 = 1.25 | M33 = 1.5 - **Memory Math:** $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes $= 64$ KB - **Harvard Architecture:** Separate code & data buses (8051, Cortex-M3) - **TrustZone (ARMv8-M):** Hardware-enforced secure/non-secure memory isolation for IoT security - **Key Formula:** $\text{MIPS} = f_{CLK} / (CPI \times 10^6)$ - **Thumb-2 Code Density:** ~26% reduction vs pure ARM code - **Modern Derivatives:** C8051Fxxx achieves 100 MIPS with 8051 ISA — proves scalability > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"T-2-7-8"** = **T**humb (v4T) → **2**-stage M0+ (v6-M) → **7**-M (Cortex-M3/4) → **8**-M (TrustZone M33). ---

topics.title: ARM Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ARM Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of ARM Architecture

**ARM** stands for **Advanced RISC Machine** (originally **Acorn RISC Machine**). It is a family of **32-bit (and 64-bit) Reduced Instruction Set Computing (RISC) processor architectures** developed by **ARM Holdings (now Arm Ltd.)**, which licenses its designs to a vast ecosystem of manufacturers (Qualcomm, Samsung, Apple, NXP, STMicroelectronics, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **ARM Architecture** refers to the underlying instruction set, register organization, memory model, exception handling mechanism, and pipeline structure that define how an ARM processor fetches, decodes, executes, and writes back instructions. It is a **load-store architecture** (data processing operates only on registers, never directly on memory).

The architecture is characterised by:
- A large uniform register file of **37 registers** (31 general-purpose + 6 status registers).
- **Load/Store architecture** where data-processing instructions do not access memory directly.
- **Fixed-length instructions** of 32 bits (ARM mode) or 16 bits (Thumb mode).
- A **von Neumann** bus interface (ARM7) or **modified Harvard** architecture (ARM9, Cortex).
- Conditional execution of almost every instruction, eliminating many branches.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Chef's Kitchen

> [!NOTE] Plain-English Intuition
> Imagine a **professional kitchen**:
>
> - The **chef (CPU core)** can only mix ingredients that are already in **bowls on the counter (registers)**. The chef never reaches into the **fridge (memory)** during cooking.
> - The **counter (register file)** holds 37 bowls — most recipes only need a few, but the extra bowls keep frequently-used items handy (reducing fridge trips).
> - Every **recipe step (instruction)** is exactly **one card (32 bits)** — no variable-length cards to confuse the chef.
> - Most recipe cards have a small **checkbox (condition field)** — the chef only follows the card *if* a certain condition is true (conditional execution).
>
> This is exactly how ARM works: data stays in registers, instructions are uniform length, and conditional execution makes code dense and fast.

## 1.3 Key Architectural Metrics for KTU

| Metric | ARM Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Word size | **32 bits** |
| Instruction length | **32 bits** (ARM), **16 bits** (Thumb) |
| General-purpose registers | **31 × 32-bit** |
| Processor modes | **7 modes** |
| Endianness | **Bi-endian** (configurable) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The ARM Architectural Family Tree

ARM Holdings licenses *processor cores* (IP cores) to chipmakers. These cores are organised into three contemporary families (as of the KTU 2024 syllabus):

| Family | Typical Use Case | Pipeline Depth | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ARM7TDMI** | Legacy embedded, LPC2148 | **3-stage** | Classical ARM, Thumb support |
| **ARM9 / ARM11** | Mobile, networking | **5-stage** | Separate instruction & data buses (modified Harvard) |
| **Cortex-M / Cortex-R / Cortex-A** | Microcontrollers, real-time, applications | Variable | Thumb-2, optional FPU/MPU |

> [!IMPORTANT] For KTU Board Exams
> The syllabus primarily focuses on the **ARM7TDMI** core. Know its pipeline, register set, modes, and exceptions thoroughly.

## 2.2 ARM Processor Operating Modes (7 Modes)

ARM has **7 operating modes**, of which **6 are privileged modes** and **1 is the normal user mode**. Mode changes occur due to exceptions or explicit software writes to the CPSR.

| Mode | Abbrev. | Privilege | Trigger / Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User** | `usr` | Non-privileged | Normal program execution |
| **Fast Interrupt Request** | `fiq` | Privileged | High-speed data transfer / interrupt |
| **Interrupt Request** | `irq` | Privileged | General-purpose interrupt |
| **Supervisor** | `svc` | Privileged | Called by SWI instruction / reset |
| **Abort** | `abt` | Privileged | Memory access failure (instruction or data) |
| **Undefined** | `und` | Privileged | Undefined instruction encountered |
| **System** | `sys` | Privileged | Operating system privileged tasks (uses User registers) |

> [!NOTE] Why privileged modes?
> They allow the processor to access protected memory regions and control system resources. Critical for an RTOS kernel.

## 2.3 The Register File (37 Registers × 32 bits)

At any instant, **exactly 16 general-purpose registers (R0–R15)** and **1 or 2 status registers** are visible. The full 37-register physical set is **banked** across the privileged modes.

| Register | Role | Special Function |
|---|---|---|
| **R0 – R12** | General-purpose data registers | None |
| **R13** | **Stack Pointer (SP)** | One banked copy per privileged mode |
| **R14** | **Link Register (LR)** | Holds return address after subroutine call / exception |
| **R15** | **Program Counter (PC)** | In ARM mode, PC = current instruction + 8 |
| **CPSR** | Current Program Status Register | Flags + mode + control bits |
| **SPSR** | Saved Program Status Register | Saves CPSR on exception entry |

### 2.3.1 CPSR Condition Flags

| Flag | Full Name | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **N** | Negative | Result bit 31 = 1 (negative number) |
| **Z** | Zero | Result = 0 |
| **C** | Carry | Unsigned overflow / extend |
| **V** | oVerflow | Signed overflow |
| **I** | IRQ disable | 1 = disables normal IRQs |
| **F** | FIQ disable | 1 = disables fast IRQs |
| **T** | Thumb | 1 = processor in Thumb state |
| **M[4:0]** | Mode | Current operating mode |

## 2.4 ARM Pipeline Architecture

### 2.4.1 The ARM7TDMI 3-Stage Pipeline

```
   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐   ┌─────────┐
   │  FETCH  │ → │ DECODE  │ → │ EXECUTE │
   └─────────┘   └─────────┘   └─────────┘
```

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| **Fetch** | Instruction fetched from memory via the address held in PC |
| **Decode** | Instruction decoded; operands read from register bank; register bypass logic evaluated |
| **Execute** | ALU operation, register write-back, memory address calculated |

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Insight — The PC Offset
> Because of pipelining, when an instruction is being **executed**, the PC is actually **2 instructions ahead** (in ARM state). This is why the ARM manual defines `PC = Address of current instruction + 8`.

### 2.4.2 The ARM9 5-Stage Pipeline

```
   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐   ┌─────┐
   │FETCH│ → │DECODE│ → │EXEC │ → │MEM/WB│ → │WRITE│
   └─────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘   └─────┘
```

The 5-stage pipeline allows a **higher clock frequency** because each stage does less work. The separate Memory stage allows memory loads/stores to overlap with ALU operations.

## 2.5 ARM Data Processing & Instruction Classes

| Class | Examples | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Data processing** | `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `ORR`, `MOV`, `CMP` | Operands in registers only |
| **Data transfer (load/store)** | `LDR`, `STR`, `LDM`, `STM` | Only instructions that touch memory |
| **Branch** | `B`, `BL`, `BX` | Change PC; `BL` saves return address in LR |
| **Multiply** | `MUL`, `MLA` | Reserved opcodes; pipelined differently |
| **Exception-generating** | `SWI` (now `SVC`) | Supervisor call |

## 2.6 The Von-Neumann vs Modified-Harvard Issue

| Core | Bus Architecture | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **ARM7TDMI** | **Von Neumann** | Single 32-bit bus for instructions & data; simpler, cheaper, but shared bandwidth |
| **ARM9 / Cortex** | **Modified Harvard** | Separate I-cache & D-cache; simultaneous instruction fetch + data access |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| PC value during execution | $PC = \text{CurrInstAddr} + 8$ | In **ARM** state (2-stage ahead) |
| PC value during execution | $PC = \text{CurrInstAddr} + 4$ | In **Thumb** state |
| Pipeline CPI (ideal) | $CPI = 1$ | One instruction completes per cycle once pipeline is full |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI}}$ | In instructions per second |
| Branch penalty | $P = (\text{stages flushed})$ | Flush occurs when branch reaches Execute |
| Memory offset (byte addressing) | $\text{Addr} = \text{Base} \pm \text{Offset}$ | Offset can be immediate, register, or scaled register |

> [!NOTE] Do **NOT** confuse with mathematical formulas — these are **engineering performance equations** commonly tested in KTU problems on pipeline efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why PC = Current Instruction Address + 8 in ARM State

Consider three consecutive instructions at addresses $A$, $A+4$, $A+8$. The pipeline contains:

| Cycle | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inst at $A$ | F | D | **E** |
| Inst at $A+4$ | – | F | D |
| Inst at $A+8$ | – | – | F |

During **cycle 3**, instruction at address $A$ is in the **Execute** stage. The PC hardware points to the instruction currently being **fetched**, which is at $A+8$.

Therefore, when an ARM instruction reads R15:

$$
PC \;=\; \text{Address of instruction currently in EXECUTE} \;+\; 8
$$

because the executed instruction is *two* stages behind the fetch stage, each separated by a 32-bit (4-byte) word.

$$
PC = A + 8
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] For Thumb state (16-bit instructions, 2-byte words), the offset is **+4** instead of **+8**, because the PC must skip only two instructions ahead.

## 3.2 Derivation: Calculating Pipeline Speedup

Let:
- $N$ = total number of instructions
- $k$ = number of pipeline stages
- $T_c$ = clock cycle time
- $T_s$ = time for a single non-pipelined instruction

**Without pipeline (sequential):**

$$
T_{\text{seq}} = N \cdot k \cdot T_c
$$

**With pipeline (ideal, no stalls):**

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = (k + N - 1) \cdot T_c
$$

**Speedup:**

$$
S = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{pipe}}} = \frac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1}
$$

For large $N$, the constant $k-1$ becomes negligible:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = k
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] Theoretical maximum speedup = number of pipeline stages.

### 3.2.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Type)

**Problem:** A processor has a 3-stage pipeline. A program has 100 instructions. Branch instructions (20% of total) cause a 1-cycle penalty each. Find the average CPI.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Step 1 — Identify branches:

$$
N_{\text{branch}} = 0.20 \times 100 = 20
$$

Step 2 — Total cycles without stalls:

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{ideal}} = 3 + (100 - 1) = 102 \quad (\text{first fill} + \text{remaining instructions})
$$

Step 3 — Add stall cycles:

$$
\text{Stall cycles} = 20 \times 1 = 20
$$

Step 4 — Total cycles:

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{total}} = 102 + 20 = 122
$$

Step 5 — Average CPI:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\text{Cycles}_{\text{total}}}{N} = \frac{122}{100} = 1.22
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Effective Throughput With Clock Frequency

If $f_{\text{clk}} = 60\,\text{MHz}$ (common on LPC2148), the throughput is:

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{\text{CPI}_{\text{avg}}} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{1.22} \approx 49.18\,\text{MIPS}
$$

(Million Instructions Per Second.)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Exception Vector Table

Every ARM core has a fixed vector table at address $\mathbf{0x00000000}$. The KTU syllabus often asks to map the table.

| Vector Offset | Exception | Mode Entered |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00` | Reset | Supervisor |
| `0x04` | Undefined Instruction | Undefined |
| `0x08` | Software Interrupt (SWI/SVC) | Supervisor |
| `0x0C` | Prefetch Abort | Abort |
| `0x10` | Data Abort | Abort |
| `0x18` | IRQ | IRQ |
| `0x1C` | FIQ | FIQ |

> [!NOTE] Note that `0x14` is reserved (skipped).

## 3.5 Exception Entry Sequence (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. R14\_mode = address of next instruction (PC + 4 or +8 depending on exception)
2. SPSR\_mode = CPSR
3. CPSR[4:0] = mode bits of new mode
4. CPSR[5] = 0 (enter ARM state, even from Thumb)
5. CPSR[7] = 1 (disable further IRQs by default for FIQ/SVC/ABT/UND; IRQ disables itself)
6. PC = vector address

The handler retrieves `CPSR` from `SPSR`, and `PC` from `LR` to return cleanly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM7TDMI Functional Block Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["External Memory Bus"] --> B["Address Register and Incrementer"]
    B --> C["32-bit Data Bus"]
    C --> D["Instruction Decode and Control Logic"]
    D --> E["Register Bank 37 x 32-bit"]
    E --> F["Barrel Shifter"]
    F --> G["ALU 32-bit"]
    G --> H["Write Back Bus"]
    H --> E
    G --> I["Multiplier Hardware"]
    I --> E
    D --> J["PC Increment and Branch Logic"]
    J --> B
    D --> K["Exception and Mode Controller"]
    K --> E
```

> [!NOTE] Reading guide: arrows show data/control flow. The barrel shifter is what allows every operand to be shifted/rotated for free before entering the ALU — a unique ARM feature that reduces the need for separate shift instructions.

## 4.2 Pipeline Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Cycle1\nFETCH instA"] --> F2["Cycle2\nFETCH instB\nDECODE instA"]
    F2 --> F3["Cycle3\nFETCH instC\nDECODE instB\nEXECUTE instA"]
    F3 --> F4["Cycle4\nFETCH instD\nDECODE instC\nEXECUTE instB\nWriteBack instA"]
    F4 --> F5["Cycle5\n..."]
```

## 4.3 ARM Operating Mode State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> User
    User --> Supervisor : SWI / SVC instruction
    User --> IRQ : External IRQ interrupt
    User --> FIQ : External FIQ interrupt
    User --> Undefined : Undefined opcode
    User --> Abort : Memory access fault
    Supervisor --> User : Return via MOVS PC, LR
    IRQ --> User : Return via SUBS PC, LR, 4
    FIQ --> User : Return via SUBS PC, LR, 4
    Abort --> User : Return via SUBS PC, LR, 8
    Undefined --> User : Return via MOVS PC, LR
```

## 4.4 ARM7TDMI Bus Architecture (Von Neumann)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU["ARM7TDMI Core"] <--> BUS["Single 32-bit Shared Bus"]
    BUS --> ROM["ROM"]
    BUS --> RAM["RAM"]
    BUS --> PER["Peripherals"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT] Contrast with ARM9 which uses **separate** instruction and data buses feeding separate caches.

## 4.5 Exception Vector Map (Linear Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V0["0x00  Reset_Handler"] --> V1["0x04  Undefined_Handler"]
    V1 --> V2["0x08  SWI_Handler"]
    V2 --> V3["0x0C  PrefetchAbort_Handler"]
    V3 --> V4["0x10  DataAbort_Handler"]
    V4 --> V5["0x14  RESERVED"]
    V5 --> V6["0x18  IRQ_Handler"]
    V6 --> V7["0x1C  FIQ_Handler"]
```

## 4.6 Register Banking Across Modes (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserAndSystem["User / System Mode"]
        R0U["R0..R12"]
        R13U["R13_user (SP)"]
        R14U["R14_user (LR)"]
    end
    subgraph FIQBank["FIQ Mode (banked)"]
        R8F["R8_fiq..R14_fiq"]
    end
    subgraph SVCBank["Supervisor Mode"]
        R13S["R13_svc"]
        R14S["R14_svc"]
    end
    subgraph IRQBank["IRQ Mode"]
        R13I["R13_irq"]
        R14I["R14_irq"]
    end
    CPSR["CPSR (shared)"]
    SPSR["SPSR_mode (one per privileged mode)"]
    CPSR -.-> UserAndSystem
    CPSR -.-> FIQBank
    CPSR -.-> SVCBank
    CPSR -.-> IRQBank
```

> [!NOTE] The FIQ mode has **more** banked registers (R8–R14) specifically to enable fast context save without writing to user-mode registers — a performance optimisation for high-speed interrupts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** List the seven operating modes of the ARM processor and state the privileged modes.

**Model Answer:**

ARM has **7 operating modes**:

1. **User (usr)** — non-privileged, normal execution
2. **Fast Interrupt Request (fiq)** — privileged
3. **Interrupt Request (irq)** — privileged
4. **Supervisor (svc)** — privileged
5. **Abort (abt)** — privileged
6. **Undefined (und)** — privileged
7. **System (sys)** — privileged

**Privileged modes:** fiq, irq, svc, abt, und, sys (all modes **except User** are privileged).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Differentiate between ARM state and Thumb state.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ARM State | Thumb State |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction size | **32 bits** | **16 bits** |
| Code density | Lower (more memory used) | Higher (~30–40% smaller) |
| Performance | Higher (more functionality per instruction) | Lower (often needs extra instructions) |
| PC offset (during execution) | `+8` | `+4` |
| Indicated by | `CPSR[T] = 0` | `CPSR[T] = 1` |
| Entered via | `BX/BLX` with bit-0 = 0 | `BX/BLX` with bit-0 = 1 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall
> **Common mistakes students make and lose marks on:**
> 1. Confusing `PC + 4` with `PC + 8` — remember Thumb vs ARM offset!
> 2. Forgetting that CPSR's `I` and `F` bits disable interrupts, **not** exceptions like Reset or FIQ if `F` is set.
> 3. Drawing a 5-stage pipeline when the question asks specifically for **ARM7TDMI** (3-stage).
> 4. Writing exception names as "SWI" — modern ARM documentation calls it **SVC** (Supervisor Call).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the 3-stage pipeline of the ARM7TDMI processor. Show the contents of the pipeline at each cycle for the sequence `ADD`, `SUB`, `MOV`.**

**Model Answer:**

The ARM7TDMI uses a **3-stage pipeline** as shown:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["FETCH\nInstruction from memory using PC"] --> S2["DECODE\nDecode opcode, read registers,\ncontrol signals generated"]
    S2 --> S3["EXECUTE\nALU operation, shift,\nregister write-back,\nmemory address calculation"]
```

**Pipeline contents (instruction sequence ADD → SUB → MOV):**

| Cycle | FETCH | DECODE | EXECUTE |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADD | – | – |
| 2 | SUB | ADD | – |
| 3 | MOV | SUB | ADD |
| 4 | – | MOV | SUB |
| 5 | – | – | MOV |

**Observations:**
- After cycle 3, the pipeline is **full** and one instruction completes per cycle.
- During cycle 3, the ADD is in EXECUTE while its address is `A`; PC = `A + 8` (fetching the next-after-next).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 3-stage pipelined processor executes 500 instructions. 30% of them are branches that incur a 2-cycle penalty each. The clock period is 20 ns. Calculate:**
**(i) Total execution time, (ii) Effective CPI, (iii) Speedup compared to a non-pipelined processor.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Number of branches:**

$$
N_{\text{branch}} = 0.30 \times 500 = 150
$$

**Step 2 — Stall cycles:**

$$
\text{Stall cycles} = 150 \times 2 = 300
$$

**Step 3 — Total cycles (pipeline):**

$$
\text{Cycles}_{\text{pipe}} = 3 + (500 - 1) + 300 = 802
$$

**Step 4 — Total execution time:**

$$
T_{\text{pipe}} = 802 \times 20\,\text{ns} = 16\,040\,\text{ns} \approx 16.04\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective CPI:**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \frac{802}{500} = 1.604
$$

**Step 6 — Non-pipelined execution time:**

$$
T_{\text{seq}} = 500 \times 3 \times 20\,\text{ns} = 30\,000\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 7 — Speedup:**

$$
S = \frac{30\,000}{16\,040} \approx 1.87
$$

The pipelined version is **~1.87× faster** than the non-pipelined implementation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the ARM processor register organisation. With a diagram, show how registers are banked across different operating modes.**

**Model Answer:**

The ARM processor has **37 physical 32-bit registers**, but at any instant only **18** are visible: **R0–R15 + CPSR** (and one SPSR in privileged modes).

**Register classes:**

| Class | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| General-purpose | 31 (R0–R30) | Used for data and address operations |
| Stack Pointer (R13) | 6 banked copies | One per privileged mode + user |
| Link Register (R14) | 6 banked copies | Saves return addresses |
| Program Counter (R15) | 1 (shared) | Always visible |
| CPSR | 1 (shared) | Current status |
| SPSR | 5 banked copies | One per privileged exception mode |

**Banking logic:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Shared["Always Visible (all modes)"]
        R0["R0..R7 (unbanked, common)"]
        R15["R15 PC"]
        CPSR["CPSR"]
    end
    subgraph UserMode["User / System"]
        R8U["R8..R12"]
        R13U["R13_usr / SP"]
        R14U["R14_usr / LR"]
    end
    subgraph FIQBank["FIQ (extra banking)"]
        R8F["R8_fiq..R12_fiq"]
        R13F["R13_fiq / SP"]
        R14F["R14_fiq / LR"]
        SPSRF["SPSR_fiq"]
    end
    subgraph SVCMode["Supervisor"]
        R13S["R13_svc"]
        R14S["R14_svc"]
        SPSRS["SPSR_svc"]
    end
```

**Key points for KTU:**
- R0–R7 are **never banked** — shared across all modes.
- R8–R12 are banked **only** for FIQ (to support fast interrupt context saving).
- R13 (SP) and R14 (LR) are **banked for every privileged mode**.
- Each privileged mode (except System) has its **own SPSR**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the ARM exception handling mechanism. Describe the actions taken by the processor when an IRQ occurs, and how the interrupt service routine returns.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Exception Vector Table:** Each exception type has a fixed memory address (vector) at the start of memory (typically `0x00000000`).

**Step 2 — Actions on IRQ Entry:**

1. The processor saves the **return address** in `R14_irq` (LR_irq):
   $$
   LR_{\text{irq}} = PC + 4
   $$
   (because IRQ is asynchronous and PC has already moved ahead by 2 instructions).

2. The processor saves a copy of CPSR into `SPSR_irq`:
   $$
   SPSR_{\text{irq}} = CPSR
   $$

3. CPSR is modified:
   - Mode bits `M[4:0] = 10010` (binary for IRQ mode = `0b10010`).
   - `I` bit set to **1** → further IRQs disabled.
   - `F` bit unchanged.
   - `T` bit cleared → forces ARM state.

4. PC is forced to vector address `0x00000018`.

**Step 3 — Inside the ISR:**
The handler saves additional registers (`STMFD SP_irq!, {R0-R12, LR}`), services the device, and clears the interrupt source.

**Step 4 — Return from ISR:**

The standard return sequence uses the instruction:

```
SUBS PC, LR, #4
```

This atomically:
- Loads `PC = LR - 4` (the original next-instruction address).
- Restores CPSR from SPSR_irq (because `S` suffix updates flags → in privileged mode, restores CPSR).

**Exception priorities (highest to lowest):** Reset → Data Abort → FIQ → IRQ → Prefetch Abort → Undefined → SWI.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist (Save This Section)

### Architecture Fundamentals
- ARM = **Advanced RISC Machine**, 32-bit load-store architecture.
- Designed for **high code density, low power, and high performance per Watt**.
- Licensable IP — no fabrication done by Arm Ltd.

### Pipeline
- ARM7TDMI = **3 stages** (Fetch, Decode, Execute).
- ARM9 / Cortex = **5 stages** for higher clock.
- In ARM state: **PC = current instruction + 8**.
- In Thumb state: **PC = current instruction + 4**.

### Registers
- **37 physical** registers, **18 visible** at any moment.
- R0–R7 unbanked; R8–R12 banked only for FIQ; R13/R14 banked everywhere.
- R13 = SP, R14 = LR, R15 = PC.

### Modes (7)
- User (non-priv), System (priv, uses User regs), SVC, FIQ, IRQ, ABT, UND.

### CPSR Flags
- **N, Z, C, V** = condition flags.
- **I = 1** disables IRQ, **F = 1** disables FIQ, **T = 1** means Thumb.

### Exceptions
- Vector table at `0x00000000` (size = 8 words × 4 bytes = 32 bytes).
- Each exception saves CPSR → SPSR, sets new mode, jumps to vector.
- Return via `SUBS PC, LR, #N` where N depends on exception (4 for IRQ/FIQ, 8 for Data Abort, etc.).

### Performance Equations
- $CPI_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cycles}}{\text{Total Instructions}}$
- $T_{\text{exec}} = N_{\text{cycles}} \times T_{\text{clk}}$
- $S = \dfrac{N \cdot k}{k + N - 1 + \text{stalls}}$

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- Always specify **which pipeline** the question is about (3-stage vs 5-stage).
- **Conditional execution** on every instruction eliminates many branches — a unique ARM feature.
- ARM7 is **Von Neumann**; ARM9+ is **Modified Harvard**.
- "**SWI**" in old ARM = "**SVC**" in ARMv7+.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Set Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** is the **abstract model** of a computer (or microcontroller) that defines how the CPU is controlled by the software. It acts as a **contractual interface** between the hardware and the lowest-level software (firmware, assembly language, compiler machine code). 

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PCEOT501**, the ISA of a microcontroller (such as the **8051** or **ARM Cortex-M**) specifies the complete set of native instructions, their formats, data types, registers, addressing modes, memory model, interrupt and exception handling model, and the I/O mechanism that the processor can directly execute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT503/PCEOT501 Module 4):**
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to master the ISA of the **8051 microcontroller** (8-bit) and have a working understanding of the **ARM Cortex-M ISA (Thumb-2)**. The ISA is the "soul" of the microcontroller — without it, no embedded firmware can be written.

The ISA is often described as a **layered specification** comprising:

1. **Instruction Set** — The complete list of machine-level operations (opcodes) the processor can execute.
2. **Instruction Format** — The binary layout of an instruction (opcode + operands).
3. **Data Types** — The sizes and representations (bit, byte, word) the hardware natively supports.
4. **Registers** — The programmer-visible register set (ACC, B, PSW, R0–R7, DPTR, PC, SP, etc.).
5. **Addressing Modes** — How operands are located (Immediate, Register, Direct, Indirect, Indexed).
6. **Memory Architecture** — Harvard vs Von Neumann, address space layout.
7. **Interrupt & Exception Model** — Hardware-triggered control transfers.
8. **I/O Model** — Memory-mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The ISA is the Grammar of the Hardware"**

Imagine you hire a **robot chef** for your restaurant. Before the chef can cook, you must hand him a **recipe book written in a language he understands**. The recipe book is your **software**, and the **grammar/vocabulary of that book** is the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**.

- The **chef's brain** = the CPU
- The **recipe book** = the program (firmware)
- The **grammar of the book** = the ISA
- The **actual kitchen utensils** = the microarchitecture (hardware implementation)

If you change the grammar (switch from English recipes to Japanese recipes), the chef can no longer understand you — even though his brain (hardware) is identical. **The ISA defines the boundary between "what the user can say" and "what the machine can execute."**

> [!TIP]
> **A simpler analogy for the classroom:** Think of the ISA as a **TV remote control**. The remote has a fixed set of buttons (Power, Volume+, Channel−, etc.). Each button is an *instruction*. The arrangement and meaning of buttons is the *architecture*. You can press any button in any order, but the TV's hardware is designed to recognize only those specific buttons.

## 1.3 The Two Philosophical Families of ISA

Microcontrollers and processors broadly fall into two opposing ISA design philosophies, both of which are tested heavily in KTU exams:

| ISA Family | Full Form | Philosophy | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CISC** | Complex Instruction Set Computer | Many instructions, variable length, many addressing modes, microcoded | Intel 8051, x86 |
| **RISC** | Reduced Instruction Set Computer | Few, simple, fixed-length instructions, load/store architecture, hardwired | ARM Cortex-M, AVR, MIPS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** The **8051 is technically a CISC** processor, but with a strong RISC flavor in its instruction timing. Many textbooks classify it as a *CISC* with a *RISC-like* timing model. The **ARM Cortex-M** used in modern embedded systems is a pure **RISC** with **Thumb-2** (mixed 16/32-bit) encoding.

## 1.4 Why ISA Matters in Embedded Systems

In embedded systems, the ISA is the **first design decision** a system architect makes. The choice cascades into:

- **Code density** (how much Flash memory is consumed)
- **Performance** (cycles per instruction, CPI)
- **Power consumption** (critical for IoT/portable devices)
- **Compiler complexity** (RISC makes compilers easier; CISC optimizes hand-written assembly)
- **Determinism** (predictable execution time for real-time systems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Bold:**
> - **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** = average clock cycles to execute one instruction
> - **MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second)** = (Clock Frequency in Hz) / (CPI × 10⁶)
> - **Code Density** = bytes of program memory required to express an algorithm
> - **Addressing Modes** = the number of distinct ways operands can be specified (typically 5–12)

## 1.5 The Layered View of a Computer System

The ISA sits in the **sweet spot** between software and hardware, making it the most crucial abstraction in computer engineering:

```
        [ Application Software ]
        [   System Software     ]   ← Compilers, Assemblers, OS
        [    Instruction Set    ]   ← ISA (this topic)
        [    Microarchitecture  ]   ← Pipeline, cache, ALU design
        [   Hardware / Logic    ]   ← Transistors, gates, VLSI
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** *"ISA = Interface between Software and Architecture."* Everything above the ISA is **software-visible**; everything below is **implementation-defined** and can change without breaking compatibility.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of an Instruction Set Architecture

A complete ISA specification contains **seven mandatory components**. Every KTU question on this topic ultimately tests one or more of these.

### 🔹 2.1.1 Instruction Set (Opcode Repertoire)

This is the **complete catalog** of machine-level operations the processor can perform. Each operation is encoded as a unique **binary opcode** (typically 8 bits in 8051, 16 bits in ARM Thumb, 32 bits in ARM).

For the **8051**, the instruction set has **111 distinct instructions** divided into functional groups:

1. **Data Transfer Instructions** — `MOV`, `MOVX`, `MOVC`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`
2. **Arithmetic Instructions** — `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `DA A`
3. **Logical Instructions** — `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CLR`, `CPL`, `RL`, `RR`, `RLC`, `RRC`, `SWAP`
4. **Boolean (Bit) Instructions** — `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C`, `ORL C`, `MOV C`, bit-level JUMP
5. **Program Branching** — `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `AJMP`, `LJMP`, `SJMP`, `JMP @A+DPTR`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`
6. **Special Instructions** — `NOP`, `RET`, `RETI`

### 🔹 2.1.2 Instruction Format (Encoding)

The **instruction format** is the binary structure of a machine instruction. It tells the CPU **which fields mean what**.

A generic instruction has:

$$\text{Instruction Word} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{\text{Operation}} \;\;+\;\; \underbrace{\text{Operand(s)}}_{\text{Data or Address}}$$

For the 8051, instructions are **1, 2, or 3 bytes** long, with **opcode + addressing-mode bits** cleverly packed. Three main formats exist:

| Format | Length | Structure | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type 1** | 1 byte | `opcode` only | `NOP`, `RET`, `RL A` |
| **Type 2** | 2 bytes | `opcode` + `operand` | `MOV A, #data`, `ADD A, Rn` |
| **Type 3** | 3 bytes | `opcode` + `data16` (or addr16) | `MOV DPTR,#data16`, `LJMP addr16` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The 8051 has **49 single-byte instructions**, **45 two-byte instructions**, and **17 three-byte instructions**. Single-byte instructions use **implied addressing** (no operand field needed — register is implied by the opcode itself, e.g., `INC A`).

### 🔹 2.1.3 Data Types

The ISA defines what kinds of data the hardware can manipulate directly:

- **Bit** — 1 bit (8051 has a dedicated Boolean processor)
- **Nibble** — 4 bits (used in `SWAP A` and BCD operations)
- **Byte** — 8 bits (the 8051's primary data size)
- **Word** — 16 bits (DPTR is a 16-bit register)
- **Signed/Unsigned** — Most 8051 arithmetic is **unsigned**; signed math must be emulated

### 🔹 2.1.4 Registers (Programmer-Visible Register Set)

The **register set** is the fastest storage in the processor. The 8051 has four register banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7), plus special function registers (SFRs):

| Register | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `A` (Accumulator) | 8-bit | Primary operand for arithmetic/logic |
| `B` | 8-bit | Multiplication/Division helper |
| `R0–R7` | 8-bit × 8 (4 banks) | General-purpose scratch registers |
| `SP` (Stack Pointer) | 8-bit (8051) | Points to top of stack in internal RAM |
| `DPTR` (Data Pointer) | 16-bit | Used for MOVX and code lookup |
| `PC` (Program Counter) | 16-bit | Address of next instruction |
| `PSW` (Program Status Word) | 8-bit | Flags: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, –, P |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the 8051 registers:** *"A B R0-7, SP DPTR PC, PSW flags for free!"*

### 🔹 2.1.5 Addressing Modes (DEEP DIVE)

The **addressing mode** is the method by which an instruction locates its operand(s). The 8051 supports **five primary** and **several derived** addressing modes. This is the most heavily tested sub-topic in KTU.

| # | Mode | Syntax | Effective Address | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | `#data` | The literal value itself | `MOV A, #25H` |
| 2 | **Register** | `Rn` | R0–R7 of selected bank | `MOV A, R3` |
| 3 | **Direct** | `direct` | Internal RAM or SFR (00H–7FH / 80H–FFH) | `MOV A, 30H` |
| 4 | **Register Indirect** | `@Rn` or `@DPTR` | Content of Rn/DPTR is the address | `MOV A, @R0` |
| 5 | **Indexed (Base+Index)** | `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` | Sum of A and DPTR/PC | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** The 8051 does **NOT** support a true **"Indirect with 16-bit address"** natively; instead, it uses `@DPTR` for 16-bit data pointer indirection. The `@Rn` is restricted to **8-bit** addresses (internal RAM only, 00H–FFH).

### 🔹 2.1.6 Memory Architecture (Harvard Model in 8051)

The 8051 uses a **Harvard architecture** — separate address and data buses for program memory (ROM/Flash) and data memory (RAM). This allows **simultaneous** code fetch + data access.

| Memory Type | Size (8051 base) | Address Range | Bus Width |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Program Memory (Code ROM)** | 64 KB (max) | `0000H – FFFFH` | 16-bit PC |
| **Internal Data RAM (IRAM)** | 128 B (256 in 8052) | `00H – 7FH` | 8-bit |
| **SFR Space** | 128 B | `80H – FFH` | 8-bit |
| **External Data RAM (XRAM)** | 64 KB (max) | `0000H – FFFFH` | 16-bit via MOVX |
| **Bit-Addressable RAM** | 16 B (128 bits) | `20H – 2FH` | Bit-wise |
| **External Code Memory** | 64 KB | `0000H – FFFFH` | via PSEN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **bit-addressable** region (20H–2FH) is unique to the 8051 ISA. It allows **single-bit set/clear/test** operations on 128 individual bits using instructions like `SETB 20H.0` and `JB 20H.0, LABEL`.

### 🔹 2.1.7 Interrupt & I/O Model

The 8051 ISA provides:
- **5 interrupt sources** (8051 base) — INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial Port (TI/RI)
- **Two priority levels**
- **Vectored interrupts** at fixed code addresses (e.g., `0003H` for INT0)
- **I/O via SFRs** — `P0`, `P1`, `P2`, `P3` are memory-mapped I/O ports

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula and metric** you must know for KTU exam problems on this topic.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | Seconds |
| **MIPS Rating** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions/s |
| **Clock Period** | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Seconds |
| **Program Length** | $N = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i$ | Total instructions executed |
| **Average CPI** | $\text{CPI}_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (IC_i \times CPI_i) \;/\; N$ | Weighted average |
| **Code Density** | $\text{Density} = \dfrac{\text{Program Bytes}}{IC_{exec}}$ | Bytes/instruction |
| **Memory Addressing Capacity** | $C = 2^n$ bytes | where $n$ = address bus width |
| **Instruction Cycle Count (8051)** | **1-byte, 1-cycle** (12 oscillator cycles), varies for others | 8051 uses 12-clock machine cycle |
| **Machine Cycle Time** | $T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{clk}$ | 8051 specific |
| **Effective Address (Direct)** | $EA = \text{address field itself}$ | 8051 internal RAM/SFR |
| **Effective Address (Register Indirect)** | $EA = (Rn)$ or $(DPTR)$ | Parenthesis = "content of" |
| **Effective Address (Indexed)** | $EA = (A) + (DPTR)$ or $(A) + (PC)$ | Base+Offset |
| **Stack Growth (8051)** | Push: $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$, Pop: $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ | **Grows UP** (unusual!) |
| **PC Increment** | $PC \leftarrow PC + (\text{instruction length in bytes})$ | Always auto-increments |
| **Power Dissipation (Dynamic)** | $P = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | RISC generally lower $\alpha$ |
| **Interrupt Vector Addr (8051)** | `0003H` INT0, `000BH` T0, `0013H` INT1, `001BH` T1, `0023H` Serial | Fixed |
| **Branch Penalty** | $N_{flush} = \text{pipeline depth}$ | For pipelined RISC |

> [!TIP]
> **For any CPI problem:** If the 8051 uses a **12-oscillator-cycle machine cycle** and a particular instruction takes 1 machine cycle, then $CPI_{clk} = 12$ (in terms of oscillator clocks). This is the most common trap in KTU numericals.

---

## 2.3 RISC vs CISC — The Engineering Trade-Off

This is a **permanent favorite** in KTU Part B questions. You must be able to **compare and justify** in writing.

| Parameter | CISC (8051) | RISC (ARM Cortex-M) |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction count** | Large (111 in 8051) | Small (≈ 50–100 in Thumb-2) |
| **Instruction length** | Variable (1–3 bytes in 8051) | Fixed (16/32 bits) |
| **Addressing modes** | Many (5+ in 8051) | Few (3–4) |
| **Memory access** | Register + Memory in any instruction | **Load/Store only** |
| **Clock cycles/instruction** | 1–4 machine cycles | **Always 1 cycle (in pipeline)** |
| **Pipelining** | Difficult | Trivial & effective |
| **Code density** | High (compact) | Lower (Thumb-2 improves it) |
| **Compiler friendliness** | Harder | Easier |
| **Hardware complexity** | High (microcode) | Low (hardwired) |
| **Power efficiency** | Lower | Higher |
| **Example use cases** | Legacy 8-bit MCUs, control | Modern IoT, mobile, automotive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** ARM's **Thumb-2 ISA** (used in Cortex-M0/M3/M4) is the **dominant embedded ISA** in 2024 — over **250 billion** ARM-based chips shipped. The 8051, despite being a 1980s design, is still **mass-produced** for cost-sensitive applications (toys, appliance controllers, toys, smart cards).

---

## 2.4 Architectural Styles: Von Neumann vs Harvard

| Feature | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| Memory for code & data | **Single shared bus** | **Separate buses** |
| Concurrent code+data fetch | ❌ No (bottleneck) | ✅ Yes |
| Hardware cost | Lower | Higher |
| Speed | Slower (Von Neumann bottleneck) | Faster |
| Used in | General-purpose CPUs | Microcontrollers (8051, AVR, PIC, ARM) |
| 8051 classification | — | **Modified Harvard** (code can be read from data space via MOVC) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical: CPU Execution Time & MIPS Calculation

This is a **standard KTU 2-mark sub-question**. Let us derive the result step by step.

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A microcontroller runs at $f_{clk} = 24\,\text{MHz}$. A program contains 1000 instructions, with the instruction mix: 40% need 1 machine cycle, 30% need 2 machine cycles, 30% need 3 machine cycles. The 8051 uses **12 oscillator cycles per machine cycle**. Find (a) the average CPI, (b) the total execution time, and (c) the MIPS rating.

### 🔸 Part (a): Average CPI Derivation

The **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** for the 8051 is expressed in terms of **machine cycles**. We first compute the average machine cycles per instruction, then convert to oscillator clocks.

$$\text{Average MCPI} = \sum_{i} (IC_i \times MC_i) \;/\; N$$

Substituting the given fractions:

$$\text{Average MCPI} = \frac{(0.40 \times 1) + (0.30 \times 2) + (0.30 \times 3)}{1}$$

$$\text{Average MCPI} = \frac{0.40 + 0.60 + 0.90}{1} = 1.90 \text{ machine cycles/instr}$$

Now convert to **oscillator clock cycles** using the 8051's 12-clock machine cycle:

$$\text{Average CPI}_{clk} = \text{MCPI} \times 12$$

$$\text{Average CPI}_{clk} = 1.90 \times 12 = 22.8 \text{ oscillator clocks/instr}$$

### 🔸 Part (b): Total Execution Time

The execution time formula is:

$$T_{CPU} = N \times \text{CPI}_{avg} \times T_{clk}$$

Compute the clock period:

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{24 \times 10^{6}} = 41.667 \text{ ns}$$

Substitute into the execution-time formula:

$$T_{CPU} = 1000 \times 22.8 \times 41.667 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 22{,}800 \times 41.667 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 949{,}999.6 \times 10^{-9} \text{ s} \approx 950 \;\mu\text{s}$$

### 🔸 Part (c): MIPS Rating

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{\text{CPI}_{avg} \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{24 \times 10^{6}}{22.8 \times 10^{6}} = 1.053 \text{ MIPS}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing **machine cycles** with **oscillator cycles**. The 8051 is unique in having the 12:1 divider. ARM Cortex-M uses **1:1** (1 oscillator clock = 1 machine cycle). Always **state your assumption** explicitly in KTU answers.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Instruction Length & Memory Occupancy

> **Problem:** A control program for the 8051 contains: 80 single-byte, 120 two-byte, and 40 three-byte instructions. (a) What is the total program memory consumed? (b) What is the average instruction length in bytes? (c) Compare code density with a pure 32-bit RISC ISA where every instruction is 4 bytes.

### 🔸 Part (a): Total Program Memory

$$M = (80 \times 1) + (120 \times 2) + (40 \times 3)$$

$$M = 80 + 240 + 120 = 440 \text{ bytes}$$

### 🔸 Part (b): Average Instruction Length

First, total instruction count:

$$N = 80 + 120 + 40 = 240 \text{ instructions}$$

Then average length:

$$L_{avg} = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{440}{240} = 1.833 \text{ bytes/instruction}$$

### 🔸 Part (c): Code Density Comparison

For the 32-bit RISC ISA:

$$M_{RISC} = 240 \times 4 = 960 \text{ bytes}$$

The 8051 saves:

$$\Delta M = 960 - 440 = 520 \text{ bytes}$$

Percentage savings:

$$\text{Saving} = \frac{520}{960} \times 100\% \approx 54.2\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculation is **why the 8051 remains popular** in cost-sensitive 8-bit applications — **its variable-length encoding** yields ~54% code density advantage over fixed 32-bit RISC. This is one of the **central KTU exam justifications** for why CISC architectures can outperform RISC in memory-constrained embedded systems, despite having higher CPI.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical: Addressing Mode Analysis

> **Problem (KTU-style 7-mark Part A):** Identify the addressing mode of each instruction below, compute the effective address (EA) where applicable, and state the operation performed. Assume R0 = 30H, R1 = 40H, A = 55H, DPTR = 2000H, and internal RAM address 30H = 0AAH.
> 1. `MOV A, #25H`
> 2. `MOV A, R0`
> 3. `MOV A, 30H`
> 4. `MOV A, @R0`
> 5. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

### 🔸 Solution Table

| # | Instruction | Addressing Mode | Effective Address | Operation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `MOV A, #25H` | **Immediate** | Operand is the literal `25H` | `A ← 25H` |
| 2 | `MOV A, R0` | **Register** | R0 itself | `A ← (R0) = 30H` |
| 3 | `MOV A, 30H` | **Direct** | Address `30H` itself | `A ← (30H) = 0AAH` |
| 4 | `MOV A, @R0` | **Register Indirect** | `EA = (R0) = 30H` | `A ← ((R0)) = (30H) = 0AAH` |
| 5 | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | **Indexed (Base+Index)** | `EA = (A) + (DPTR) = 55H + 2000H = 2055H` | `A ← Code[2055H]` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-level indirection trap:** For `MOV A, @R0`, the operation is `A ← (M[(R0)])`. The **@** means "the content of the address given by R0", not the address itself. Many KTU students lose marks by stopping at the first level.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: 8051 Instruction Classification by Size & Timing

| Instruction Class | Examples | Bytes | Machine Cycles | Total Oscillator Clocks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1-byte, 1-cycle** | `NOP`, `INC A`, `RR A`, `CLR A`, `CPL A` | 1 | 1 | 12 |
| **1-byte, 2-cycle** | `RET`, `RETI` | 1 | 2 | 24 |
| **2-byte, 1-cycle** | `MOV A, #data`, `ADD A, Rn` | 2 | 1 | 12 |
| **2-byte, 2-cycle** | `AJMP addr11`, `ACALL addr11` | 2 | 2 | 24 |
| **3-byte, 1-cycle** | `MOV direct, #data`, `CJNE A, direct, rel` | 3 | 1–2 | 12–24 |
| **3-byte, 2-cycle** | `LJMP addr16`, `LCALL addr16` | 3 | 2 | 24 |
| **3-byte, 4-cycle** | `MUL AB`, `DIV AB` | 1 | 4 | 48 |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check formula:** Total execution time = $N_{cycles} \times 12 \times T_{clk}$. For a `MUL AB` at 24 MHz: $T = 4 \times 12 \times 41.67\text{ns} = 2 \,\mu\text{s}$.

---

## 3.5 RISC vs CISC Quantitative Example

> **Problem (KTU Part B style):** A benchmark program on a CISC processor takes 800,000 instructions with avg CPI = 3, clock = 100 MHz. A RISC processor executes the same program in 1,200,000 instructions with avg CPI = 1, clock = 100 MHz. Compare the two.

**CISC execution time:**

$$T_{CISC} = 800{,}000 \times 3 \times \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 0.024 \text{ s} = 24 \text{ ms}$$

**RISC execution time:**

$$T_{RISC} = 1{,}200{,}000 \times 1 \times \frac{1}{100 \times 10^{6}} = 0.012 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms}$$

**Conclusion:** RISC is **2× faster** here, despite executing **50% more instructions**, because its CPI is 3× lower. The RISC MIPS rating:

$$\text{MIPS}_{RISC} = \frac{100 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{6}} = 100 \text{ MIPS}$$

$$\text{MIPS}_{CISC} = \frac{100 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{6}} \approx 33.33 \text{ MIPS}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall in KTU valuation:** MIPS is **not a fair performance metric** between ISAs because instruction *semantics* differ. A single CISC instruction may do the work of multiple RISC instructions. Always use **execution time** as the ultimate metric, not MIPS.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The ISA Abstraction Layer — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER\nC Programs / RTOS / Firmware"]
    sysLayer["SYSTEM SOFTWARE LAYER\nCompiler / Assembler / Linker"]
    isaLayer["INSTRUCTION SET ARCHITECTURE\nOpcodes, Registers, Addressing Modes,\nData Types, Memory Model, Interrupts"]
    microLayer["MICROARCHITECTURE LAYER\nPipeline, ALU, Cache, Buses,\nControl Unit, Datapath"]
    hwLayer["PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER\nTransistors, VLSI, Clocks, I/O Pads"]

    appLayer -->|compiles to| sysLayer
    sysLayer -->|generates| isaLayer
    isaLayer -->|implemented by| microLayer
    microLayer -->|realized in| hwLayer

    appLayer -.uses.-> isaLayer
    microLayer -.hides details from.-> isaLayer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The ISA is the **only layer** that the programmer directly interacts with. Above it is software, below it is hardware implementation. The same ISA can have **many microarchitectural implementations** (e.g., ARMv7-M runs on Cortex-M0, M3, M4 — same ISA, different hardware).

---

## 4.2 8051 Programmer's Model — Register Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SFR["SPECIAL FUNCTION REGISTERS (SFRs) - 80H to FFH"]
        ACC["ACC (E0H)\n8-bit Accumulator"]
        BREG["B (F0H)\nMultiplication Helper"]
        PSW["PSW (D0H)\nFlags: CY AC F0 RS1 RS0 OV - P"]
        SPREG["SP (81H)\n8-bit Stack Pointer"]
        DPTRLO["DPTR-L (82H)\nData Pointer Low"]
        DPTRHI["DPTR-H (83H)\nData Pointer High"]
        P0["P0 (80H)\nPort 0"]
        P1["P1 (90H)\nPort 1"]
        P2["P2 (A0H)\nPort 2"]
        P3["P3 (B0H)\nPort 3 + Alt Fn"]
    end

    subgraph BANKS["REGISTER BANKS - 00H to 1FH"]
        B0["Bank 0: R0-R7\n(00-07)"]
        B1["Bank 1: R8-R15\n(08-0F)"]
        B2["Bank 2: R16-R23\n(10-17)"]
        B3["Bank 3: R24-R31\n(18-1F)"]
    end

    PC["PC (16-bit)\nProgram Counter\n0000H - FFFFH"]

    ACC <--> BREG
    ACC <--> PSW
    B0 -.selected by PSW.-> B1
    B1 -.selected by PSW.-> B2
    B2 -.selected by PSW.-> B3
    DPTRLO <--> DPTRHI
```

---

## 4.3 8051 Instruction Encoding — Format Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F1["1-BYTE INSTRUCTION (49 opcodes)"]
        F1A["[7:0] OPCODE\nExamples: NOP, INC A, RET"]
    end

    subgraph F2["2-BYTE INSTRUCTION (45 opcodes)"]
        F2A["Byte 1: [7:0] OPCODE"]
        F2B["Byte 2: [7:0] DATA / ADDR"]
    end

    subgraph F3["3-BYTE INSTRUCTION (17 opcodes)"]
        F3A["Byte 1: [7:0] OPCODE"]
        F3B["Byte 2: [7:0] ADDR-HI / DATA-HI"]
        F3C["Byte 3: [7:0] ADDR-LO / DATA-LO"]
    end

    F1 --> F2 --> F3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Encoding tip for KTU:** A 2-byte instruction `MOV A, #25H` is encoded as `74H 25H` in machine code. The `74H` is the opcode for "Move immediate to A", and `25H` is the literal data byte. When writing assembly, the assembler does this conversion automatically.

---

## 4.4 8051 Memory Architecture — Harvard Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRG["PROGRAM MEMORY (64 KB Max) - 0000H to FFFFH"]
        PRG0["0000H: Reset Vector\nLJMP to main"]
        PRG1["0003H: INT0 ISR"]
        PRG2["000BH: Timer 0 ISR"]
        PRG3["0013H: INT1 ISR"]
        PRG4["001BH: Timer 1 ISR"]
        PRG5["0023H: Serial ISR"]
        PRG6["0030H-FFFFH: User Code"]
    end

    subgraph DATA["INTERNAL DATA MEMORY (IRAM 128B + SFR 128B)"]
        subgraph LOWER["Lower 128: 00H-7FH"]
            L0["00-1FH: Register Banks 0-3 (32B)"]
            L1["20-2FH: Bit-Addressable (16B = 128 bits)"]
            L2["30-7FH: General-Purpose RAM (80B)"]
        end
        subgraph UPPER["Upper 128: 80H-FFH (SFRs)"]
            U0["80H, 90H, A0H, B0H: P0-P3"]
            U1["D0H: PSW | E0H: ACC | F0H: B"]
            U2["81H: SP | 82-83H: DPTR"]
            U3["88H: TCON | 98H: SCON | A8H: IE | B8H: IP"]
        end
    end

    subgraph XDATA["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY (64 KB) - Accessed via MOVX"]
        X0["0000H-FFFFH\nExternal RAM / I/O devices"]
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Addressing Mode — Operand Location Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INST["CPU fetches instruction\nopcode + operand field(s)"]
    INST --> DECODE{"Decode opcode:\nWhat addressing mode?"}

    DECODE -->|Immediate| IMM["Operand IS the data\nNo memory access\nExample: MOV A,#25H"]
    DECODE -->|Register| REG["Operand in Rn\nNo memory access\nExample: MOV A,R3"]
    DECODE -->|Direct| DIR["Operand is 8-bit address\nRead from internal RAM/SFR\nExample: MOV A,30H"]
    DECODE -->|Register Indirect| RI["Operand is @Rn or @DPTR\nEA = (Rn) or (DPTR)\nExample: MOV A,@R0"]
    DECODE -->|Indexed| IDX["EA = (A) + (DPTR) or (A) + (PC)\nRead from CODE memory via MOVC\nExample: MOVC A,@A+DPTR"]

    IMM --> EXEC["Execute ALU operation"]
    REG --> EXEC
    DIR --> EXEC
    RI --> EXEC
    IDX --> EXEC
    EXEC --> RESULT["Write result to destination"]
```

---

## 4.6 Instruction Execution Pipeline (Conceptual for RISC)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F["FETCH\nRead instr\nfrom memory"] --> D["DECODE\nIdentify opcode\nand operands"]
    D --> EX["EXECUTE\nALU operation\nor memory access"]
    EX --> WB["WRITEBACK\nStore result\nto register/memory"]
    F -.next instr.-> D
    D -.next instr.-> EX
    EX -.next instr.-> WB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 (CISC) is **NOT pipelined** in the classical RISC sense — its instructions are completed before the next begins. ARM Cortex-M (RISC) **is pipelined** with 2 or 3 stages, achieving throughput of 1 instruction per cycle once the pipeline is full.

---

## 4.7 Stack Operation in 8051 (Unusual Upward Growth)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STACK["Internal RAM Stack Region (default: 07H upward)"]
        S0["Address: 07H (initial SP after reset)"]
        S1["Address: 08H"]
        S2["Address: 09H"]
        S3["Address: 0AH"]
        S4["Address: 0BH"]
    end

    PUSH["PUSH instruction:\nSP = SP + 1 (increment FIRST)\nM[SP] = data"]
    POP["POP instruction:\ndata = M[SP]\nSP = SP - 1 (decrement AFTER)"]

    S0 --> PUSH
    PUSH --> S1
    S1 --> PUSH
    PUSH --> S2

    S4 --> POP
    POP --> S3
    S3 --> POP
    POP --> S2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
