> **Critical KTU fact:** Most processors (including ARM, x86) have a **descending stack** (grows downward). The 8051's **ascending stack** (grows upward) is a frequent source of bugs for students porting code between architectures.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (with Model Answers)

### ❓ Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — Define Instruction Set Architecture (ISA). List its main components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** is the abstract model and programmer-visible interface of a computer system that defines the complete set of machine instructions, registers, data types, addressing modes, memory model, and I/O mechanisms that the hardware can directly execute. It acts as the **boundary between software and hardware**, allowing programs to run on any implementation of the same ISA.

**Main components of an ISA:**
1. **Instruction set / opcodes** — complete list of operations (e.g., `ADD`, `MOV`, `JMP`)
2. **Instruction format / encoding** — binary layout of opcode + operands
3. **Data types** — bit, nibble, byte, word, signed/unsigned
4. **Registers** — programmer-visible storage (ACC, B, R0–R7, PC, SP, DPTR, PSW)
5. **Addressing modes** — Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed
6. **Memory architecture** — Harvard vs Von Neumann, address space layout
7. **Interrupt and I/O model** — exception handling and device interface

---

### ❓ Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Compare RISC and CISC architectures. Give one example processor for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | RISC | CISC |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Reduced Instruction Set Computer | Complex Instruction Set Computer |
| Instruction count | Small, fixed | Large, variable |
| Instruction length | Fixed (16/32 bits) | Variable (1–3 bytes in 8051) |
| Memory access | Load/Store only | Register + Memory |
| CPI | 1 (pipelined) | 1–4+ |
| Pipelining | Easy, efficient | Difficult |
| Code density | Lower | Higher |
| Example | **ARM Cortex-M** (Thumb-2) | **Intel 8051** |

RISC emphasizes simple, fast instructions optimized for pipelining and compiler efficiency, while CISC emphasizes compact code and powerful addressing. Modern embedded systems often use RISC (ARM), but CISC (8051) still dominates in cost-sensitive 8-bit applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *In ESE Part B, students must answer one full question (with sub-parts a and b) from a choice of two for that module. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — ISA Fundamentals + Numerical

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Explain the different **addressing modes of the 8051 microcontroller** with one example instruction for each. Show the calculation of the effective address where applicable. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A program on the 8051 runs at $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$. It contains 500 single-byte 1-cycle instructions, 300 two-byte 1-cycle instructions, and 200 three-byte 2-cycle instructions. Calculate:
>   (i) Average CPI in terms of oscillator clocks
>   (ii) Total program memory consumed
>   (iii) Total execution time **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The 8051 supports **five primary addressing modes** that determine how the operand is located:

1. **Immediate Addressing (1 mark)** — The operand is a literal constant embedded in the instruction.
   - Example: `MOV A, #25H` → loads `A` with `25H`. No memory access needed.
   
2. **Register Addressing (1 mark)** — The operand is one of the eight registers R0–R7 of the currently selected bank.
   - Example: `MOV A, R3` → copies the content of R3 into A. The bank is selected by PSW bits RS1:RS0.

3. **Direct Addressing (1 mark)** — The 8-bit address field directly specifies a location in internal RAM (00H–7FH) or SFR (80H–FFH).
   - Example: `MOV A, 30H` → loads `A` from internal RAM address `30H`. The effective address is the value in the instruction itself: `EA = 30H`.

4. **Register Indirect Addressing (1.5 marks)** — The address is the *content* of a register. `Rn` gives 8-bit indirection (R0, R1 only); `@DPTR` gives 16-bit indirection.
   - Example: `MOV A, @R0` → effective address `EA = (R0)`. If R0 = 40H, then `A ← M[40H]`.

5. **Indexed Addressing (1.5 marks)** — Effective address = sum of accumulator and a 16-bit base register (DPTR or PC). Used only with `MOVC` for code lookup tables.
   - Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → `EA = (A) + (DPTR)`. If A = 05H and DPTR = 2000H, then `EA = 2005H`; `A ← Code[2005H]`.

> **Note:** The 8051 also supports **implied addressing** (1-byte instructions like `INC A`, `NOP`) where the operand is implicit in the opcode.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given data:**
- $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$
- Instruction set A: 500 × 1-byte × 1 machine cycle
- Instruction set B: 300 × 2-byte × 1 machine cycle
- Instruction set C: 200 × 3-byte × 2 machine cycles

**Step 1: Total program memory (2 marks)**

$$M = (500 \times 1) + (300 \times 2) + (200 \times 3)$$
$$M = 500 + 600 + 600 = 1700 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2: Average CPI in oscillator clocks (3 marks)**

First compute average machine cycles per instruction (MCPI):

$$N_{total} = 500 + 300 + 200 = 1000 \text{ instructions}$$

$$\text{MCPI} = \frac{(500 \times 1) + (300 \times 1) + (200 \times 2)}{1000}$$

$$\text{MCPI} = \frac{500 + 300 + 400}{1000} = \frac{1200}{1000} = 1.2 \text{ MC/instr}$$

The 8051 uses **12 oscillator clocks per machine cycle**, so:

$$\text{CPI}_{clk} = 1.2 \times 12 = 14.4 \text{ clocks/instr}$$

**Step 3: Total execution time (2 marks)**

$$T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 83.33 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{CPU} = N \times \text{CPI}_{clk} \times T_{clk}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 1000 \times 14.4 \times 83.33 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$T_{CPU} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.2 \text{ ms}$$

**Final Results:**
- Program memory = **1700 bytes**
- Average CPI = **14.4 oscillator clocks/instruction**
- Execution time = **1.2 ms**

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Memory Architecture + ISA Design)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Pattern, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Compare **Harvard and Von Neumann memory architectures** with neat diagrams. Explain why the 8051 is classified as a **Modified Harvard** architecture. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **programmer's model of the 8051**. List the SFRs and the four register banks. Describe the role of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register in detail, including the significance of each flag bit. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Von Neumann Architecture (2 marks):**
A single shared memory holds both program code and data, accessed via a **single bus**. The CPU must fetch instruction, then fetch data, sequentially. This creates the famous **"Von Neumann bottleneck"** — only one memory access at a time.

**Harvard Architecture (2 marks):**
Uses **physically separate** program memory (ROM/Flash) and data memory (RAM), each with its own bus. The CPU can **simultaneously** fetch the next instruction (from code memory) and read/write a data operand (from data memory) — doubling throughput for the same clock rate.

**Why 8051 is "Modified Harvard" (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pure Harvard | 8051 (Modified Harvard) |
|---|---|---|
| Code & Data memory | Strictly separate | Separate **but** with limited overlap |
| Code can be read as data? | No | **Yes** — via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` and `@A+PC` |
| Access code from data space? | No | **Yes** — code memory is mapped into the same 64 KB address space |
| Different bus widths? | Yes | **Same 16-bit address bus** for both code and external data, but separate control signals (`PSEN` for code, `RD/WR` for data) |

The "modification" allows the 8051 to perform **constant table lookups** (`MOVC` instructions) — reading program memory as if it were data — a feature impossible in a pure Harvard. This makes the 8051 an excellent choice for **lookup-table-driven control algorithms** (e.g., sinusoidal PWM, sensor calibration curves).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Programmer's Model (2 marks):** The 8051 programmer's model consists of:
- **Accumulator (A)** — primary operand for arithmetic/logic
- **B register** — used by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`
- **R0–R7** — 8 general-purpose registers in 4 banks
- **SP (Stack Pointer, 8-bit)** — points to top of internal RAM stack
- **DPTR (16-bit)** — data pointer for MOVX and MOVC
- **PC (Program Counter, 16-bit)** — always points to the next instruction
- **PSW** — program status word with flags

**SFRs (1.5 marks):** Port registers (P0–P3 at 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H), TCON, TMOD, TL0/TH0, TL1/TH1, SCON, SBUF, IE, IP, PCON, ACC, B, SP, DPTR, PSW.

**Register Banks (1 mark):** Banks 0–3 occupy internal RAM 00H–1FH (32 bytes total, 8 bytes per bank). Selected by PSW[RS1:RS0]:
- Bank 0 (RS=00) → 00H–07H
- Bank 1 (RS=01) → 08H–0FH
- Bank 2 (RS=10) → 10H–17H
- Bank 3 (RS=11) → 18H–1FH

**PSW Layout (2.5 marks):** Bit-addressable at D0H.

| Bit | Name | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PSW.7** | **CY** (Carry) | D7H | Set on carry out of bit 7 in ADD/SUB; used by `JC`/`JNC` |
| **PSW.6** | **AC** (Aux Carry) | D6H | Carry between bits 3 and 4; used for BCD correction (`DA A`) |
| **PSW.5** | **F0** (Flag 0) | D5H | User-defined general-purpose flag |
| **PSW.4** | **RS1** | D4H | Register bank select high |
| **PSW.3** | **RS0** | D3H | Register bank select low |
| **PSW.2** | **OV** (Overflow) | D2H | Set on signed arithmetic overflow; used by `JB OV` |
| **PSW.1** | **–** (Reserved) | D1H | Reserved for future use |
| **PSW.0** | **P** (Parity) | D0H | Hardware sets if A has an odd number of 1s (even parity) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget that **PSW is bit-addressable** and that the **P flag is set automatically by hardware** (not writable directly). Also, RS1:RS0 are **active at all times** — accidentally writing to them can silently switch register banks and cause bizarre "variable changed itself" bugs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 Most Common Mark-Deduction Reasons in Module 4 (ISA):**
>
> 1. **Confusing machine cycles with oscillator cycles** — The 8051's 12-clock machine cycle is unique. ARM uses 1:1. If the question says $f_{osc} = 12\,\text{MHz}$ and you forget the 12× divider, your execution-time answer will be off by **12×**. (**Up to −2 marks**)
> 2. **Misidentifying addressing modes** — `MOV A, 30H` is **Direct**, not "memory addressing"; `MOV A, @R0` is **Register Indirect**, not "pointer addressing." Use the **exact textbook terminology**. (**Up to −1 mark per part**)
> 3. **Confusing the stack direction** — 8051 stack grows **upward** (SP increments on PUSH). If you draw a descending stack (like ARM/x86), you lose marks. (**Up to −1 mark**)
> 4. **Forgetting the 8051 has FOUR register banks** — Many students write "R0–R7 are 8 registers" but miss that there are 4 selectable banks. (**Up to −1 mark**)
> 5. **Treating the 8051 as pure CISC** — The 8051 has a **CISC opcode repertoire** but **RISC-like 1-cycle-per-instruction timing**. Mentioning both gives full credit. (**Up to −1 mark**)
> 6. **Wrong PSW bit order** — Some textbooks list PSW bits MSB→LSB, others LSB→MSB. KTU expects **CY (PSW.7) as the MSB**. (**Up to −1 mark**)
> 7. **Not stating units in numericals** — A CPI of "1.2" is meaningless without saying "machine cycles per instruction" or "oscillator clocks per instruction." (**Up to −1 mark**)
> 8. **Forgetting the 8051 code/data memory overlap via MOVC** — Critical for the "Modified Harvard" classification. (**Up to −2 marks**)

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Instruction Set Architecture**

### 🟢 Core Definitions
- [x] **ISA** = contract between hardware and low-level software (firmware/compiler output)
- [x] **CISC** = Complex Instruction Set Computer (e.g., 8051)
- [x] **RISC** = Reduced Instruction Set Computer (e.g., ARM Cortex-M)
- [x] **CPI** = Clock cycles per instruction (oscillator clocks for 8051)
- [x] **MIPS** = Million Instructions per Second = $f_{clk} / (CPI \times 10^6)$
- [x] **Harvard** = separate code/data memory & buses
- [x] **Von Neumann** = shared memory & single bus
- [x] **Modified Harvard (8051)** = separate physically, but MOVC allows code-as-data access

### 🟢 8051 ISA Specifics
- [x] **111 instructions**, in **49 / 45 / 17** = 1/2/3-byte categories
- [x] **5 primary addressing modes**: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed
- [x] **Registers**: A, B, R0–R7 (4 banks), SP (8-bit), DPTR (16-bit), PC (16-bit), PSW
- [x] **Bit-addressable region**: 20H–2FH = 128 individually addressable bits
- [x] **SFR space**: 80H–FFH
- [x] **Stack grows UPWARD** (SP increments on PUSH)
- [x] **12 oscillator clocks = 1 machine cycle** (unique to 8051)
- [x] **5 interrupts** at vector addresses `0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H`
- [x] **PSW flags**: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, –, P
- [x] **Program memory**: 64 KB max (0000H–FFFFH), 16-bit PC
- [x] **External data memory**: 64 KB max via MOVX
- [x] **4 register banks** selected by PSW[RS1:RS0]

### 🟢 Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [x] $T_{CPU} = N \times \text{CPI} \times T_{clk}$
- [x] $\text{MIPS} = f_{clk} / (\text{CPI} \times 10^6)$
- [x] $T_{clk} = 1/f_{clk}$
- [x] $T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{clk}$ (8051 specific)
- [x] $C_{mem} = 2^n$ bytes, where $n$ = address bus width
- [x] Average CPI = $\sum (IC_i \times CPI_i) / N_{total}$

### 🟢 RISC vs CISC Comparison Verbs
- [x] RISC: **Fixed** length, **few** addressing modes, **load/store**, **1 CPI** (pipelined), **compiler-friendly**, **low power**
- [x] CISC: **Variable** length, **many** addressing modes, **register+memory**, **>1 CPI**, **hand-assembler friendly**, **high code density**

### 🟢 Common KTU Question Patterns
- [x] "List the **addressing modes** with examples" → always 5 modes
- [x] "Calculate **execution time** for instruction mix" → use $T_{CPU}$ formula
- [x] "Compare **RISC and CISC**" → always end with "modern trend favors RISC, but CISC still used in 8-bit"
- [x] "Explain **Modified Harvard**" → mention MOVC explicitly
- [x] "What is the role of **DPTR**?" → 16-bit address register for MOVX and MOVC
- [x] "How is the **register bank switched**?" → via PSW[RS1:RS0]

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - *"**5 Modes** in 8051: **I** **R** **D** **I** **I**"* → Immediate, Register, Direct, Indirect, Indexed
> - *"**Harvard = H**igh **A**ccess **R**ate (s**V**e, **A**RD **R**eally **D**ifferent)"* — code and data in separate places
> - *"**PSW** = **P**rogrammer's **S**napshot of **W**ork"* — tells you what just happened in the ALU
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of "Operating Modes"

In the context of the **MCS-51 (8051) family of microcontrollers**, the term **"Operating Modes"** refers to the four distinct, programmable functional configurations in which the **on-chip Timer/Counter peripheral** can operate, along with the four operating configurations of the **on-chip Serial Port (UART)**. These modes are selected by writing specific bit patterns into the **Timer/Counter Mode Control Register (TMOD)** and the **Serial Port Control Register (SCON)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT501 – Module 4):**
> "Operating Modes of 8051 — Timer/Counter Modes (Mode 0, Mode 1, Mode 2, Mode 3) and Serial Port Modes (Mode 0, Mode 1, Mode 2, Mode 3)."

The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counters (T0 and T1)** and **one full-duplex UART**. The way they count, roll over, generate flags, and interact with external pins changes dramatically depending on which mode is selected — making "Operating Modes" a high-weight, frequently-tested topic in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Timer vs. Stopwatch" Analogy

Imagine you are cooking in a kitchen with **two timing devices**:
- A **kitchen timer** that you set to a fixed duration (e.g., 5 minutes) and which automatically resets to 5 minutes the moment it beeps.
- A **stopwatch** that you must manually reset every time it reaches 99 minutes 60 seconds.

The 8051's operating modes are exactly this:
- **Mode 0 and Mode 1** behave like the **stopwatch** — they count up to a maximum and overflow, but you (the firmware) must manually reload the starting value every time.
- **Mode 2** behaves like the **kitchen timer** — it automatically reloads the starting value, freeing the CPU.
- **Mode 3** is a special "split-twin" mode where one timer becomes two independent 8-bit counters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **TMOD** – Timer Mode register (non-bit-addressable). Selects mode and operation (Timer vs. Counter).
> - **TCON** – Timer Control register (bit-addressable). Contains the `TR0`, `TR1` run-control bits and overflow flags `TF0`, `TF1`.
> - **SCON** – Serial Port Control register (bit-addressable). Selects serial mode and contains RI/TI flags.
> - **SMOD** – Serial baud-rate doubler bit in the **PCON** power-control register.
> - **f_osc** – The crystal oscillator frequency (typically **11.0592 MHz** in KTU lab boards, not 12 MHz — this is critical for baud-rate accuracy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Timer/Counter Operating Modes — Detailed Logic Steps

The operating mode of a timer is selected by the two **M1 and M0** bits in the `TMOD` register corresponding to that timer.

### 2.1.1 Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (Compatible with MCS-48)

**How it Works (Step-by-Step):**
1. The 16-bit register is split: **8 bits go to the high byte (THx)** and only the **lower 5 bits of TLx** are used (the upper 3 bits of TLx are ignored).
2. The total count width is therefore **13 bits**, which counts from **0000H (0)** to **1FFFH (8191)**.
3. When the count rolls over from `1FFFH` to `0000H`, the **Timer Overflow Flag (TFx)** is set.
4. CPU must **manually reload** THx and TLx if a continuous, non-zero initial count is required.

**Why does this mode exist?**
It is a legacy compatibility mode retained so that 8051 code can be ported from the older **Intel 8048 microcontroller** family. It is **rarely used in modern designs** but **frequently asked in KTU theory papers**.

### 2.1.2 Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (Most Commonly Used)

1. The full **16-bit register (THx:TLx)** is used.
2. Count range: **0000H (0) to FFFFH (65535)**.
3. On overflow from FFFFH → 0000H, the **TFx flag is set**.
4. CPU must manually reload the initial value.

**Formula for Time Delay Generation:**
$$T_{delay} = (2^{16} - \text{Initial Value}) \times T_{machine}$$
$$T_{delay} = (65536 - N) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

where `N` is the 16-bit value loaded into THx:TLx.

### 2.1.3 Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Timer

1. Only **TLx acts as the actual 8-bit counter** (range 00H to FFH).
2. **THx holds the reload value**, which never changes during counting.
3. When TLx overflows from **FFH → 00H**:
   - The **TFx flag is set**.
   - **TLx is automatically reloaded from THx** (the "auto-reload" magic).
4. This produces a **highly precise, repetitive timing waveform** without CPU intervention.

**Reload Formula:**
$$T_{delay} = (2^{8} - \text{THx value}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why Mode 2 is preferred in real engineering:** Generating a baud rate for serial communication almost always uses **Timer 1 in Mode 2** because the auto-reload mechanism guarantees glitch-free, jitter-free timing — essential for UART communication.

### 2.1.4 Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode (Timer 0 ONLY)

1. When Timer 0 is placed in Mode 3, it is **split into two independent 8-bit timers**: **TL0** and **TH0**.
2. **TL0** uses Timer 0's control bits (`TR0`, `TF0`) and can act as a Timer or Counter.
3. **TH0** is **locked into Timer function** only, and uses Timer 1's control bits (`TR1`, `TF1`) — but **Timer 1 is stopped** (TR1 = 0 has no effect; the UART baud-rate generator is disabled).
4. Timer 1 can still be used in Modes 0, 1, or 2, but its overflow flag will not set TF1, and it cannot generate interrupts. It is essentially used as a baud-rate generator only.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "Mode 3 works for both T0 and T1". This is **WRONG**. Mode 3 is available **ONLY for Timer 0**. Timer 1 in Mode 3 simply stops counting.

## 2.2 Serial Port Operating Modes — Detailed Logic

The serial port mode is selected by the **SM0 and SM1** bits of the **SCON** register.

| SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed: f_osc / 12 |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 8-bit UART (Standard) | Variable (Timer 1 overflow) |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 9-bit UART | Fixed: f_osc / 32 or f_osc / 64 |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | 9-bit UART | Variable (Timer 1 overflow) |

### 2.2.1 Mode 0 — 8-Bit Shift Register Mode
- Data is transmitted/received **LSB first** through **RxD pin**.
- **TxD pin acts as the shift clock output** (drives an external 74LS164 / 74LS165 shift register).
- Used to expand I/O ports cheaply in 8051-based industrial systems.

### 2.2.2 Mode 1 — Standard 8-Bit UART
- 10 bits are transmitted per frame: **1 start bit (0), 8 data bits (LSB first), 1 stop bit (1)**.
- Baud rate is **variable, controlled by Timer 1 overflow** (almost always Timer 1 in Mode 2).

**Baud Rate Formula (Mode 1, with SMOD = 0):**
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

For SMOD = 0:
$$\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times 32 \times \text{Baud Rate}} = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz crystal?** This specific frequency is chosen because it is **exactly divisible by the standard baud rates** (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200), producing **0% error**. With 12 MHz, the calculated TH1 is a non-integer, producing a small but cumulative error.

### 2.2.3 Mode 2 — 9-Bit UART, Fixed Baud Rate
- 11 bits per frame: 1 start, 9 data, 1 stop.
- The 9th data bit can be used for **multiprocessor communication** (TB8/RB8 in SCON).
- Baud rate is **fixed**:
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{64} \times f_{osc}$$
For SMOD = 0 → Baud = f_osc / 64. For SMOD = 1 → Baud = f_osc / 32.

### 2.2.4 Mode 3 — 9-Bit UART, Variable Baud Rate
- Identical frame format to Mode 2, but the baud rate is **variable, like Mode 1**, controlled by Timer 1.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Concept | Formula / Configuration | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mc} = 12 / f_{osc}$ | Crystal frequency |
| 2 | Mode 0 count width | **13 bits** (5 in TLx, 8 in THx) | Max count = 8192 |
| 3 | Mode 1 count width | **16 bits** | Max count = 65536 |
| 4 | Mode 2 count width | **8 bits** + auto-reload | Max count = 256 |
| 5 | Mode 1 Time Delay | $T = (65536 - N) \times 12 / f_{osc}$ | N = 16-bit initial value |
| 6 | Mode 2 Time Delay | $T = (256 - THx) \times 12 / f_{osc}$ | THx = reload value |
| 7 | Serial Mode 0 Baud | $f_{osc} / 12$ | Fixed |
| 8 | Serial Mode 1, 3 Baud | $2^{SMOD} \cdot f_{osc} / (32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1))$ | Timer 1 in Mode 2 |
| 9 | Serial Mode 2 Baud | $2^{SMOD} \cdot f_{osc} / 64$ | Doubled if SMOD=1 |
| 10 | TH1 (Mode 1, 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz) | $TH1 = 256 - (11059200 / (384 \times 9600)) = 253 = 0xFD$ | Common exam value |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mode 2 (auto-reload)** is the workhorse for **baud-rate generation** in 8051 UART, IoT sensor nodes, and industrial Modbus links.
- **Mode 3 (split timer)** is used in **multi-tasking embedded systems** where a single 8051 must service multiple periodic events (e.g., a motor controller reading a sensor every 1 ms and updating a display every 100 ms).
- **Serial Mode 0** is used in **low-cost port expansion** with shift registers, common in 7-segment display drivers and keypad scanning ICs.
- **Serial Modes 2 and 3 (9-bit)** enable **multiprocessor 8051 networks** (one master, many slaves), used in legacy automotive ECUs and SCADA systems.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Mode 1 Time Delay Calculation

> **Worked Problem (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> *The 8051 uses a 12 MHz crystal. Write a program to generate a 50 ms delay using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Show all calculations.*

### Step 1: Calculate the Machine Cycle Period

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to produce the machine cycle.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \, \mu s$$

So every count of the timer corresponds to exactly **1 microsecond**.

### Step 2: Determine the Total Number of Timer Counts Required

We need a total delay of 50 ms. Since each count takes 1 µs, the total number of counts needed is:

$$N_{counts} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 3: Compute the 16-Bit Initial Value

The 16-bit timer counts from the initial value up to 65536 (i.e., 2^16 = 65536) before overflowing. Therefore, the initial value N is:

$$N = 65536 - N_{counts} = 65536 - 50000 = 15536$$

### Step 4: Convert N to Hexadecimal and Split into TH0 and TL0

$$15536_{10} = 3CB0H$$

Therefore:

$$TH0 = 3CH \quad \text{and} \quad TL0 = B0H$$

### Step 5: Verify the Calculation

The number of timer increments is:

$$65536 - 15536 = 50000 \text{ counts}$$

The time delay is:

$$T_{delay} = 50000 \times 1 \, \mu s = 50{,}000 \, \mu s = 50 \, ms \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 6: Write the 8051 Assembly Code (Model Solution)

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Timer mode
        MOV TH0, #3CH       ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #B0H       ; Load low byte
        SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE       ; Wait for overflow flag
        CLR TR0             ; Stop timer
        CLR TF0             ; Clear flag
        SJMP $              ; Halt
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — TH1 Calculation for 9600 Baud (Mode 1 Serial)

> **Worked Problem:**
> *Find the value to be loaded into TH1 for serial communication at 9600 baud, Mode 1, with f_osc = 11.0592 MHz and SMOD = 0.*

### Step 1: Recall the Baud Rate Formula for Mode 1

The 8051 Mode 1 baud rate when Timer 1 is in auto-reload Mode 2 is:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values

- Baud Rate = 9600
- f_osc = 11.0592 × 10⁶ Hz
- SMOD = 0 → 2^SMOD = 1

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

### Step 3: Rearrange to Solve for TH1

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times 32 \times 9600}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Denominator

$$12 \times 32 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400$$

### Step 5: Compute (256 − TH1)

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3$$

### Step 6: Solve for TH1

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$$

### Step 7: Verify the Baud Rate

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 36} = 9600 \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Timer Modes

| Feature | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Counter Size | 13-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit | 8-bit × 2 |
| Register Used | THx + TLx(5) | THx + TLx | TLx (counts), THx (reload) | TL0 + TH0 |
| Auto-Reload? | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Max Count Value | 8192 | 65536 | 256 | 256 |
| Used for Baud Rate? | No | No | **Yes (Timer 1)** | No |
| Available For | T0, T1 | T0, T1 | T0, T1 | **T0 only** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timer Mode Selection Logic (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Power On / Reset] --> initA[TMOD register loaded by user]
    initA --> checkA{M1 bit in TMOD}
    checkA -->|0| checkB{M0 bit in TMOD}
    checkA -->|1| checkC{M0 bit in TMOD}
    checkB -->|0| mode0[Mode 0: 13-bit Timer Mode]
    checkB -->|1| mode1[Mode 1: 16-bit Timer Mode]
    checkC -->|0| mode2[Mode 2: 8-bit Auto-Reload]
    checkC -->|1| mode3a{Is this Timer 0?}
    mode3a -->|Yes| mode3b[Mode 3: Split Timer - Two 8-bit counters]
    mode3a -->|No| mode3c[Timer 1 stops counting in Mode 3]
    mode0 --> runA[TRx set to 1 by TCON]
    mode1 --> runA
    mode2 --> runA
    mode3b --> runA
```

## 4.2 Timer Mode 2 Auto-Reload Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_Bus[8051 CPU Bus]
        BUS((Internal Data Bus))
    end
    subgraph TIMER_UNIT[Timer/Counter Hardware Unit]
        TLx[TLx Register - 8-bit Up Counter]
        THx[THx Register - 8-bit Reload Latch]
        MUX[Reload Multiplexer]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[Control Logic]
        OVF[Overflow Detector]
        FLAG[TFx Flag in TCON]
        RELOAD[Reload Pulse Generator]
    end
    BUS -- initial value --> THx
    BUS -- initial value --> TLx
    TLx -- count pulse --> OVF
    OVF -- overflow detected --> FLAG
    FLAG -- sets TFx --> BUS
    OVF -- overflow detected --> RELOAD
    RELOAD -- triggers --> MUX
    THx -- holds reload value --> MUX
    MUX -- automatic transfer on overflow --> TLx
    TLx -- continues counting --> OVF
```

## 4.3 Serial Port Mode Selection Logic (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startS[8051 Reset] --> initS[SCON register loaded by user program]
    initS --> sconCheck{SM0 bit in SCON}
    sconCheck -->|0| sconCheckA{SM1 bit in SCON}
    sconCheck -->|1| sconCheckB{SM1 bit in SCON}
    sconCheckA -->|0| smode0[Serial Mode 0: 8-bit Shift Register - Baud = f_osc/12]
    sconCheckA -->|1| smode1[Serial Mode 1: 8-bit UART - Variable Baud from Timer 1]
    sconCheckB -->|0| smode2[Serial Mode 2: 9-bit UART - Baud = f_osc/32 or /64]
    sconCheckB -->|1| smode3[Serial Mode 3: 9-bit UART - Variable Baud from Timer 1]
    smode1 --> t1cfg[Configure Timer 1 in Mode 2 Auto-Reload for baud generation]
    smode2 --> smodbit[SMOD bit in PCON selects /32 or /64]
    smode3 --> t1cfg
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — 8051 Operating Modes Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PERIPHERALS[8051 On-Chip Peripherals]
        T0[Timer/Counter 0]
        T1[Timer/Counter 1]
        UART[Serial Port UART]
    end
    subgraph REGS[Control Registers]
        TMOD_REG[TMOD - Mode Selection for Timers]
        SCON_REG[SCON - Mode Selection for Serial Port]
        TCON_REG[TCON - Run Control and Flags]
        PCON_REG[PCON - SMOD Baud Doubler]
    end
    subgraph INPUTS[Configuration Inputs from Programmer]
        MODE_BITS[Mode Bits M1 M0]
        SM_BITS[Serial Mode Bits SM0 SM1]
        RUN_BITS[Run Bits TR0 TR1]
    end
    MODE_BITS --> TMOD_REG
    TMOD_REG --> T0
    TMOD_REG --> T1
    SM_BITS --> SCON_REG
    SCON_REG --> UART
    PCON_REG --> UART
    RUN_BITS --> TCON_REG
    TCON_REG --> T0
    TCON_REG --> T1
    T0 --> TF0_FLAG[Sets TF0 on overflow]
    T1 --> TF1_FLAG[Sets TF1 on overflow]
    TF0_FLAG --> TCON_REG
    TF1_FLAG --> TCON_REG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Mermaid schematics depict the *control flow* of mode selection. The 8051 does not physically rewire itself — the same hardware timer block is reconfigured digitally based on the bits loaded into TMOD and SCON, which is the essence of how "Operating Modes" actually work in silicon.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
**Explain the different operating modes of Timer/Counter in 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 Timer/Counter can operate in four modes, selected by the M1 and M0 bits in the **TMOD** register.

- **Mode 0 — 13-bit Timer:** A legacy mode compatible with the Intel 8048. Uses 5 bits of TLx and all 8 bits of THx. Max count = 8192. CPU must reload manually.
- **Mode 1 — 16-bit Timer:** The most commonly used mode. Full 16-bit counter (THx:TLx). Max count = 65536. Manual reload required.
- **Mode 2 — 8-bit Auto-Reload:** TLx is the 8-bit counter, THx holds the reload value. On overflow, TLx is automatically reloaded from THx. Used for baud-rate generation.
- **Mode 3 — Split Timer (Timer 0 only):** Splits Timer 0 into two independent 8-bit timers (TL0 and TH0). Timer 1 stops functioning in this mode.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**List the four serial port operating modes of 8051. Mention the baud rate for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 serial port has four operating modes, controlled by **SM0** and **SM1** bits of the **SCON** register:

| Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Baud Rate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mode 0 | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed: **f_osc / 12** |
| Mode 1 | 0 | 1 | 8-bit UART | Variable: Timer 1 overflow |
| Mode 2 | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART | Fixed: **f_osc / 32** or **f_osc / 64** (SMOD bit) |
| Mode 3 | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART | Variable: Timer 1 overflow |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Timer Modes Focus**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the four operating modes of the 8051 Timer/Counter. Compare the count ranges, reload mechanism, and the availability for Timer 0 and Timer 1. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Generate a delay of **exactly 1 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 2 (auto-reload)**. Compute the value to be loaded into TH0 and write the complete 8051 assembly program. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks:**

- **Mode 0 (13-bit):** Explanation, register usage THx+TLx(5 bits), max count 8192, manual reload, legacy mode — **1.5 marks**
- **Mode 1 (16-bit):** Full THx:TLx, max count 65536, manual reload, most commonly used — **1.5 marks**
- **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload):** TLx counts, THx holds reload, automatic reload on overflow, used for baud-rate generation — **2 marks**
- **Mode 3 (Split Timer):** Available only for Timer 0, splits into TL0 (Timer/Counter) and TH0 (Timer only), Timer 1 stops — **2 marks**

Neat comparison table: **included above in Section 3.3** to be drawn on the answer script.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Machine cycle period**
$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2: Number of counts required**
$$N_{counts} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 921.6 \approx 922 \text{ counts}$$

(For exact delay we use 922 — accepting a tiny error; for 1 ms exactly we would need an 11.0592 MHz × 12 / 1 ms = 921.6, hence the standard approach is to choose a count of 922 or use 1.085 µs as the time base.)

**Step 3: TH0 value (8-bit auto-reload)**
$$TH0 = 256 - 922$$
$$TH0 = -666 \quad \text{(INVALID — 8-bit unsigned cannot be negative)}$$

**Resolution:** Since one 8-bit timer cannot generate 1 ms directly, we use a **loop-based approach**: generate a 100 µs delay and loop it 10 times. For 100 µs delay:
$$N_{counts} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 92 \text{ counts}$$
$$TH0 = 256 - 92 = 164 = 0xA4$$

**Step 4: Complete Assembly Program (2 marks)**
```assembly
        MOV TMOD, #02H      ; Timer 0, Mode 2, Timer mode
        MOV TH0, #0A4H      ; Reload value for 100 µs
        MOV R0, #0AH        ; Loop counter = 10
LOOP:   SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0
BACK:   JNB TF0, BACK       ; Wait for overflow
        CLR TF0             ; Clear flag
        CLR TR0             ; Stop timer
        DJNZ R0, LOOP       ; Repeat 10 times = 1 ms total
```

**Final answer: TH0 = 0A4H**, total delay = 100 µs × 10 = **1 ms** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Serial Port Modes Focus**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a table, explain the four operating modes of the 8051 serial port. Explain the role of the **SMOD bit** in the PCON register. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The 8051 serial port operates in **Mode 1** at a baud rate of **4800** with **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**. Find the value to be loaded into **TH1**, assuming **SMOD = 0** and Timer 1 is in auto-reload Mode 2. Show all calculations and verify the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks:**

- Description of **Mode 0** (8-bit shift register, baud = f_osc/12, LSB first, TxD as clock) — **1.5 marks**
- Description of **Mode 1** (8-bit UART, 10-bit frame: 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop, variable baud) — **1.5 marks**
- Description of **Mode 2** (9-bit UART, 11-bit frame, fixed baud = f_osc/32 or /64) — **1.5 marks**
- Description of **Mode 3** (9-bit UART, variable baud, multiprocessor communication) — **1 mark**
- **Role of SMOD** in PCON: when SMOD = 1, the baud rate is **doubled** (applies to Modes 1, 2, 3). Software-only control, used to fine-tune baud rate — **1.5 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Baud rate formula (Mode 1, SMOD = 0, Timer 1 Mode 2)**
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute known values**
$$4800 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3: Simplify**
$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 4800}$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator**
$$32 \times 12 \times 4800 = 1{,}843{,}200$$

**Step 5: Compute (256 − TH1)**
$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{1{,}843{,}200} = 6$$

**Step 6: Solve for TH1**
$$TH1 = 256 - 6 = 250 = 0xFA$$

**Step 7: Verification (2 marks)**
$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - 250)} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 72} = 4800 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 8: Program fragment**
```assembly
        MOV TMOD, #20H      ; Timer 1, Mode 2
        MOV TH1, #0FAH      ; 4800 baud reload value
        MOV SCON, #50H      ; Mode 1, REN enabled
        SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Mode 3 availability:** Writing "Mode 3 is available for both Timer 0 and Timer 1" — **deduct 1 mark** outright. Mode 3 is **Timer 0 ONLY**.
> 2. **Confusing SMOD location:** SMOD is in the **PCON** register, not in SCON. Many students write "SMOD bit of SCON" — **deduct 0.5 to 1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the 2^SMOD factor in baud rate formula:** Always include the 2^SMOD / 32 term. Writing Baud = f_osc / (32 × 12 × (256 − TH1)) without the SMOD factor loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Using 12 MHz in serial calculations:** KTU expects the use of **11.0592 MHz crystal** for UART problems. If you use 12 MHz, the TH1 will be a non-integer and you must state that the system uses 11.0592 MHz for accurate baud rate.
> 5. **Not clearing TF0/TF1 in polling method:** Showing a code that does not have `CLR TF0` after the timer overflows — **deduct 0.5 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing 13-bit Mode 0 count:** Students sometimes say "Mode 0 is 16-bit". The correct answer is **13-bit** (5 bits in TLx, 8 bits in THx). **Deduct 0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- ☐ **TMOD** is **not bit-addressable**; it must be loaded as a whole byte (e.g., `MOV TMOD, #01H`).
- ☐ **TCON** is **bit-addressable**; `TR0`, `TR1` are run-control bits; `TF0`, `TF1` are overflow flags.
- ☐ **Mode 0** = 13-bit (5+8), legacy 8048 compatibility. **Mode 1** = 16-bit. **Mode 2** = 8-bit auto-reload. **Mode 3** = split timer (T0 only).
- ☐ **Auto-reload** feature exists **only in Mode 2** — THx value is auto-copied to TLx on overflow.
- ☐ **Machine cycle** = **12 / f_osc**. With 11.0592 MHz, T_mc = 1.085 µs.
- ☐ **Mode 1 delay formula:** $T = (65536 - N) \times 12 / f_{osc}$
- ☐ **Mode 2 delay formula:** $T = (256 - THx) \times 12 / f_{osc}$
- ☐ **Serial Mode 0 baud = f_osc / 12**, fixed.
- ☐ **Serial Mode 2 baud = f_osc / 32** (SMOD=1) or **f_osc / 64** (SMOD=0).
- ☐ **Serial Modes 1 & 3** use **Timer 1 in Mode 2** for baud rate generation.
- ☐ **Baud rate formula:** $BR = (2^{SMOD}/32) \times f_{osc} / (12 \times (256 - TH1))$
- ☐ **Common TH1 values (SMOD=0, 11.0592 MHz):** 9600 baud → 0xFD, 4800 baud → 0xFA, 2400 baud → 0xF4, 1200 baud → 0xE8.
- ☐ **SMOD bit** is in **PCON** register; setting it **doubles** the baud rate for Modes 1, 2, 3.
- ☐ **Mode 3 split timer:** TL0 uses T0 control bits; TH0 uses T1 control bits; Timer 1 cannot be used for anything else.
- ☐ **Serial Mode 2 vs Mode 3:** Same frame format (9-bit + start + stop), only baud rate generation differs (fixed vs variable).
- ☐ **Multiprocessor communication** uses **TB8/RB8** (the 9th bit) in SCON, enabled by Modes 2 and 3.
- ☐ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred for serial communication because it is exactly divisible by standard baud rates — **0% error** vs error with 12 MHz.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Register Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Register Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8051 microcontroller family**, the **Register Set** (also termed the *Programmer's Model* or *Programming Model*) is defined as the complete collection of on-chip CPU registers and Special Function Registers (SFRs) that are directly accessible to the programmer through instruction execution. The 8051 register set is broadly classified into two principal categories:

1. **General Purpose Registers (GPRs):** A set of **32 byte-addressable 8-bit registers** organized as **four register banks** (Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, Bank 3), each containing eight registers named **R0 through R7**.
2. **Special Function Registers (SFRs):** A mapped region of **128 bytes of internal RAM** occupying the address range **0x80 to 0xFF**, which houses the control, status, accumulator, B-register, I/O port latches, stack pointer, data pointer, and peripheral control registers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PCEOT501 Module 4**
> The Register Set forms the foundation for understanding instruction execution, addressing modes, memory banking, stack operations, and interrupt handling. Every KTU Module 4 sub-topic (I/O Ports, Timers, Serial Port, Interrupts) is intrinsically dependent on a thorough understanding of the SFR layout.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Worker's Desk" Intuition

Imagine a highly skilled office worker (the **ALU**) sitting at a desk. To perform any task, the worker needs:

- **A small notepad** for scratch calculations → the **Accumulator (A)**
- **A spare notepad** for helper calculations like multiplication/division → the **B Register**
- **A bookmark** that tracks the current line in a long instruction manual → the **Program Counter (PC)**
- **A finger pointing to the top of a stack of pending files** → the **Stack Pointer (SP)**
- **A folder index** pointing to data stored in a large external filing cabinet → the **Data Pointer (DPTR)**
- **A status board** displaying flags (carry, overflow, parity) → the **Program Status Word (PSW)**
- **Four drawers** (Banks 0–3), each containing **eight labeled slots (R0–R7)**, where only one drawer is open at a time → the **General Purpose Register Banks**

Just as the office worker switches between drawers using the status board, the 8051 switches between register banks using the **RS0 and RS1 bits of the PSW**. Everything the CPU operates upon must first be brought into these registers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Constants (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - Internal RAM size: **128 bytes** (00H to 7FH)
> - SFR address space: **128 bytes** (80H to FFH)
> - Total register banks: **4** (occupying addresses 00H–1FH)
> - Bit-addressable region: **16 bytes** (20H–2FH), providing **128 individually addressable bits**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Categorical Breakdown of the 8051 Register Set

The 8051 programmer's model consists of the following distinct register groups:

#### A. Core CPU Registers
- **Accumulator (ACC, address E0H):** The primary 8-bit register used in almost all arithmetic (ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV), logical (ANL, ORL, XRL), and data transfer operations. The ACC is also **bit-addressable** (bits E0H–E7H).

- **B Register (address F0H):** An 8-bit register used in conjunction with the Accumulator for the **MUL AB** and **DIV AB** instructions. For MUL AB, the 16-bit result is stored with the high byte in B and the low byte in A. For DIV AB, the quotient goes to A and the remainder to B. In all other operations, B can be used as a general-purpose scratch register.

- **Program Counter (PC):** A **16-bit register** (range 0000H to FFFFH) that always points to the memory address of the **next instruction to be fetched**. After every instruction fetch, the PC is auto-incremented by the length of the executed instruction. The PC is **not** mapped into the SFR space; it is a dedicated hardware register.

- **Stack Pointer (SP, address 81H):** An 8-bit register that holds the address of the **top of the stack** in internal RAM. The SP is initialized to **07H** upon reset, meaning the stack begins at address 08H (overlapping Bank 1). The stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses) in the 8051, which is the opposite of most processors.

- **Data Pointer (DPTR, address 82H–83H):** A **16-bit register** formed by combining two 8-bit SFRs: **DPL (82H, low byte)** and **DPH (83H, high byte)**. The DPTR is used to access external data memory and program memory tables using instructions like **MOVX A,@DPTR** and **MOVC A,@A+DPTR**.

#### B. Program Status Word (PSW, address D0H)

The PSW is the most critical status register. It is **bit-addressable** (D0H–D7H) and contains the condition flags and register bank select bits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PSW Bit Map — Critical for KTU Problems:**
>
> | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
> |-----|--------|---------|----------|
> | PSW.7 | CY | D7H | **Carry Flag** — Set on carry out of bit 7 in arithmetic; borrow in subtraction |
> | PSW.6 | AC | D6H | **Auxiliary Carry** — Set on carry between bits 3 and 4 (BCD correction) |
> | PSW.5 | F0 | D5H | **User-Defined Flag** — General purpose for software |
> | PSW.4 | RS1 | D4H | **Register Bank Select 1** |
> | PSW.3 | RS0 | D3H | **Register Bank Select 0** |
> | PSW.2 | OV | D2H | **Overflow Flag** — Set on signed arithmetic overflow |
> | PSW.1 | – | D1H | **Reserved** (undefined) |
> | PSW.0 | P | D0H | **Parity Flag** — Set if A has odd number of 1s (even parity) |

#### C. Register Bank Selection Logic

The RS1:RS0 bits function as a **2-to-4 decoder** selecting one of four register banks. The physical RAM addresses occupied are:

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | RAM Address Range | Reset Default |
|------|------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 00H – 07H | **Yes (active after reset)** |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 08H – 0FH | No |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 10H – 17H | No |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 18H – 1FH | No |

#### D. Special Function Registers (SFRs) — Complete Map

| SFR Name | Address | Bit-Addressable | Primary Function |
|----------|---------|-----------------|------------------|
| P0 | 80H | Yes | Port 0 latch / Address low byte (external memory) |
| SP | 81H | No | Stack Pointer |
| DPL | 82H | No | Data Pointer Low byte |
| DPH | 83H | No | Data Pointer High byte |
| PCON | 87H | No | Power Control Register |
| TCON | 88H | Yes | Timer/Counter Control |
| TMOD | 89H | No | Timer/Counter Mode |
| TL0 | 8AH | No | Timer 0 Low byte |
| TL1 | 8BH | No | Timer 1 Low byte |
| TH0 | 8CH | No | Timer 0 High byte |
| TH1 | 8DH | No | Timer 1 High byte |
| P1 | 90H | Yes | Port 1 latch |
| SCON | 98H | Yes | Serial Port Control |
| SBUF | 99H | No | Serial Data Buffer |
| P2 | A0H | Yes | Port 2 latch / Address high byte |
| IE | A8H | Yes | Interrupt Enable |
| P3 | B0H | Yes | Port 3 latch / Alternate functions |
| IP | B8H | Yes | Interrupt Priority |
| PSW | D0H | Yes | Program Status Word |
| ACC | E0H | Yes | Accumulator |
| B | F0H | Yes | B Register |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Warning:** Only SFR addresses ending in **0H or 8H** are bit-addressable. Writing to an SFR address that is not implemented is allowed but has no effect. Reading from an unimplemented SFR returns an undefined value (typically 0xFF in simulation).

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051 register set design is foundational in embedded systems for several reasons:
- **Register banking** enables ultra-fast context switching in real-time operating systems (RTOS) by simply changing RS1:RS0 instead of pushing 16–32 bytes onto the stack.
- **Bit-addressability** of 16 SFRs (totaling **128 bits**) and the 16 bytes of internal RAM (20H–2FH) provides **256 individually addressable bits**, enabling single-instruction control of pins, flags, and Boolean variables — the origin of the **MCS-51 "Boolean processor."**
- **Dual role of ports** (P0/P2 serve as multiplexed address/data bus during external memory access) demonstrates a deliberate architectural trade-off that students must understand for system design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Base Address of a Register Bank

The 8051 maps the four register banks contiguously in the lower 32 bytes of internal RAM. The base address of any register bank can be derived using the bit-weighted contribution of the RS bits.

**Logic:** With RS1 and RS0, the bank index is the 2-bit binary number $b = RS1 \cdot 2^1 + RS0 \cdot 2^0$. Since each bank occupies 8 bytes, the base address is:

$$
\text{BaseAddress} = b \times 8
$$

**Step-by-step evaluation for each bank:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bank 0:} \quad & b = (0)(2) + (0)(1) = 0 \\
& \text{BaseAddress} = 0 \times 8 = 0\text{x}00 \\
& \text{Range} = 0\text{x}00 - 0\text{x}07 \\[6pt]
\text{Bank 1:} \quad & b = (0)(2) + (1)(1) = 1 \\
& \text{BaseAddress} = 1 \times 8 = 0\text{x}08 \\
& \text{Range} = 0\text{x}08 - 0\text{x}0\text{F} \\[6pt]
\text{Bank 2:} \quad & b = (1)(2) + (0)(1) = 2 \\
& \text{BaseAddress} = 2 \times 8 = 0\text{x}10 \\
& \text{Range} = 0\text{x}10 - 0\text{x}17 \\[6pt]
\text{Bank 3:} \quad & b = (1)(2) + (1)(1) = 3 \\
& \text{BaseAddress} = 3 \times 8 = 0\text{x}18 \\
& \text{Range} = 0\text{x}18 - 0\text{x}1\text{F}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Number of Bit-Addressable Bits

The 8051 has two distinct bit-addressable regions. The total number of addressable bits is the sum of the bits in the 16 internal RAM bytes (20H–2FH) and the 16 bit-addressable SFRs.

**Step 1 — Bits in internal RAM:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RAM bytes} &= 0\text{x}2\text{F} - 0\text{x}20 + 1 = 0\text{x}10 = 16 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Bits} &= 16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 128 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bits in bit-addressable SFRs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit-addressable SFRs} &= 16 \text{ registers (P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, PSW, ACC, B, plus 6 reserved)} \\
\text{Bits} &= 16 \text{ registers} \times 8 \text{ bits/register} = 128 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total addressable bits:**

$$
\text{TotalBits} = 128 + 128 = 256 \text{ bits}
$$

The bit addresses for internal RAM 20H–2FH range from **0x00 to 0x7F**, and for SFRs they range from **0x80 to 0xFF**, forming a contiguous 256-bit address space.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stack Pointer Behavior After Reset

Upon system reset, the SP is loaded with **0x07**. Therefore, the very first PUSH instruction will write to address **0x08**, which is R0 of Bank 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reset value of SP} &= 0\text{x}07 \\
\text{First PUSH writes to} &= \text{SP} + 1 = 0\text{x}07 + 1 = 0\text{x}08
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Best Practice:** Embedded C programmers using Keil µVision typically begin the program with `SP = 0x60;` to relocate the stack to the upper internal RAM (30H–7FH), avoiding collision with register banks used by interrupt service routines.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Parity Flag Calculation

Suppose the Accumulator holds the value **0x5A** = `0101 1010` in binary.

**Step 1 — Count the number of 1s:**

$$
\text{Number of 1s} = 0+1+0+1+1+0+1+0 = 4
$$

**Step 2 — Determine parity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 \text{ is even} \Rightarrow \text{Parity Flag } P &= 1 \text{ (8051 uses even parity convention)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — PSW content after instruction (assuming previous PSW = 0x00):**

$$
\text{PSW} = 0\text{x}01 \quad (\text{only } P \text{ bit set})
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: SFR Decoding Logic

To determine whether a direct address `addr` in the range 0x80–0xFF is bit-addressable, apply the bit test:

$$
\text{IsBitAddressable} = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } (\text{addr} \bmod 8) = 0 \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Worked example for address 0xA8H (IE register):**

$$
0\text{xA}8 \bmod 8 = 168 \bmod 8 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Bit-addressable (confirmed)}
$$

**Worked example for address 0x99H (SBUF):**

$$
0\text{x}99 \bmod 8 = 153 \bmod 8 = 1 \Rightarrow \text{Not bit-addressable (confirmed)}
$$

This modular arithmetic rule is a high-yield KTU short-answer technique.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 8051 Programmer's Model

The following Mermaid diagram maps the structural relationship between the CPU core registers, register banks, and the SFR memory map. It is designed for direct rendering in Mermaid v10+.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU_CORE["CPU CORE REGISTERS - NOT SFR MAPPED"]
        PC["PC - 16 bit Program Counter"]
        ALU["ALU - 8 bit Arithmetic Logic Unit"]
    end

    subgraph SFR_MAP["SFR MEMORY MAP - 80H to FFH - 128 bytes"]
        ACC["ACC - Accumulator - E0H - bit addressable"]
        B_REG["B Register - F0H - bit addressable"]
        SP_REG["SP - Stack Pointer - 81H"]
        DPTR["DPTR - DPL 82H and DPH 83H - 16 bit"]
        PSW_REG["PSW - Program Status Word - D0H - bit addressable"]
        P0_PORT["P0 - Port 0 - 80H - bit addr"]
        P1_PORT["P1 - Port 1 - 90H - bit addr"]
        P2_PORT["P2 - Port 2 - A0H - bit addr"]
        P3_PORT["P3 - Port 3 - B0H - bit addr alt fns"]
        TCTRL["TCON 88H - TMOD 89H - TH TL 8AH to 8DH"]
        SCTRL["SCON 98H - SBUF 99H - Serial"]
        IECTRL["IE A8H - IP B8H - Interrupt"]
        PCON_REG["PCON 87H - Power Control"]
    end

    subgraph GPR_BANKS["GENERAL PURPOSE REGISTERS - 00H to 1FH - 32 bytes"]
        BANK0["BANK 0 - 00H to 07H - Default after reset"]
        BANK1["BANK 1 - 08H to 0FH"]
        BANK2["BANK 2 - 10H to 17H"]
        BANK3["BANK 3 - 18H to 1FH"]
    end

    subgraph BIT_AREA["BIT ADDRESSABLE AREA - 128 bits"]
        BITRAM["Internal RAM 20H to 2FH - 128 bits - 00 to 7FH"]
        BITSFR["16 bit addressable SFRs - 128 bits - 80 to FFH"]
    end

    ALU --> ACC
    ALU --> B_REG
    PC -. fetches .-> ALU
    PSW_REG -. RS1 RS0 controls .-> BANK0
    PSW_REG -. RS1 RS0 controls .-> BANK1
    PSW_REG -. RS1 RS0 controls .-> BANK2
    PSW_REG -. RS1 RS0 controls .-> BANK3
    ALU -. sets flags .-> PSW_REG
    BITRAM --- BIT_AREA
    BITSFR --- BIT_AREA
    DPTR -. addresses .-> P0_PORT
    DPTR -. addresses .-> P2_PORT
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: PSW Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CY["PSW 7 - CY - Carry"]
    AC["PSW 6 - AC - Aux Carry"]
    F0["PSW 5 - F0 - User Flag"]
    RS1["PSW 4 - RS1 - Bank Select 1"]
    RS0["PSW 3 - RS0 - Bank Select 0"]
    OV["PSW 2 - OV - Overflow"]
    RSVD["PSW 1 - Reserved"]
    P["PSW 0 - P - Parity"]

    CY --- AC
    AC --- F0
    F0 --- RS1
    RS1 --- RS0
    RS0 --- OV
    OV --- RSVD
    RSVD --- P

    RS1 --> DECODER["2 to 4 Decoder"]
    RS0 --> DECODER
    DECODER --> B0["Bank 0 - 00H"]
    DECODER --> B1["Bank 1 - 08H"]
    DECODER --> B2["Bank 2 - 10H"]
    DECODER --> B3["Bank 3 - 18H"]
```

### 4.3 Memory Map Schematic: Internal RAM and SFR Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A00["00H to 07H - Bank 0 - R0 to R7"]
    A08["08H to 0FH - Bank 1 - R0 to R7"]
    A10["10H to 17H - Bank 2 - R0 to R7"]
    A18["18H to 1FH - Bank 3 - R0 to R7"]
    A20["20H to 2FH - Bit addressable 16 bytes"]
    A30["30H to 7FH - General purpose scratch RAM"]
    A80["80H to FFH - SFR space - 128 bytes"]
    FFFF["FFFFH - Maximum PC reach - 64 KB code memory"]

    A00 --> A08 --> A10 --> A18 --> A20 --> A30
    A30 -. gap from 80H to 7FH is unused .- A80
    A80 --> FFFF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*List the four general purpose register banks of 8051 with their RAM address ranges. How are they selected?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The 8051 has **4 register banks** of 8 bytes each:
- **Bank 0:** 00H – 07H
- **Bank 1:** 08H – 0FH
- **Bank 2:** 10H – 17H
- **Bank 3:** 18H – 1FH

They are selected using the **RS1 and RS0 bits of the PSW register** (PSW.4 and PSW.3). After reset, RS1:RS0 = 00 selects Bank 0. (3 marks: 1 for listing, 1 for addresses, 1 for selection mechanism)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*What is the significance of the Parity (P) flag in the PSW of 8051? When is it set/reset?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Parity flag (PSW.0) is an **even-parity indicator** for the Accumulator. It is **set (P=1) if the Accumulator contains an even number of 1-bits** and reset (P=0) if the number of 1-bits is odd. It is automatically updated after every instruction that modifies the Accumulator and is used for serial communication error checking. (3 marks: 1 for meaning, 1 for condition, 1 for application)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions for Module 4 follow the **internal choice pattern**: the student attempts **one** of two alternative questions. Both questions are designed to be of equal cognitive demand and assess CO4 (Apply register concepts in assembly programming and system design).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — PSW Deep Dive**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the bit-format of the Program Status Word (PSW) of 8051 and explain the function of **each bit**. How is the register bank selection performed using PSW?

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the following with respect to 8051:
- (i) The role of the Carry flag in arithmetic and rotation instructions.
- (ii) How the Auxiliary Carry flag is used in BCD arithmetic.
- (iii) The Overflow flag detection logic for signed addition.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | – | P |
| Address | D7H | D6H | D5H | D4H | D3H | D2H | D1H | D0H |

**Bit functions (1 mark each, 5 marks):**
- **CY (PSW.7):** Carry flag from ALU; set on unsigned overflow.
- **AC (PSW.6):** Auxiliary carry from bit 3 to bit 4; used by `DA A` instruction for BCD.
- **F0 (PSW.5):** User-defined general purpose flag.
- **RS1, RS0 (PSW.4, PSW.3):** Select one of 4 register banks as per Section 2.1 table.
- **OV (PSW.2):** Overflow flag for signed arithmetic.
- **PSW.1:** Reserved.
- **P (PSW.0):** Even parity of Accumulator.

**Register bank selection (2 marks):** As shown in the Section 2.1 table; base address = (RS1·2 + RS0·1) × 8.

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**(i) Carry flag in arithmetic and rotation (2 marks):**
The CY flag holds the carry out of bit 7 after ADD, ADDC, SUBB. It is also the **single-bit rotated** operand in `RLC A` and `RRC A`. In subtraction, CY indicates borrow (set if A < operand).

**(ii) AC in BCD arithmetic (2 marks):**
After adding two BCD digits, the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust) instruction checks AC. **If AC=1 OR lower nibble > 9, add 06H to A.** Similarly, **if CY=1 OR upper nibble > 9, add 60H to A.** This converts binary addition to valid BCD.

**(iii) OV detection logic (3 marks):**
For signed addition using two's complement, overflow occurs when the carry into bit 7 (C6) and the carry out of bit 7 (C7) are different:

$$
\text{OV} = C_6 \oplus C_7
$$

Equivalently, for `ADD A, R0`:
- **OV=1** if both operands are positive (bit 7 = 0) and result sign bit = 1, OR both are negative (bit 7 = 1) and result sign bit = 0.
- **OV=0** otherwise. The `JB OV` and `JNB OV` branch instructions test this flag.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Special Function Registers**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is an SFR in 8051? List the **addresses and functions of any ten** Special Function Registers. What is the rule for identifying bit-addressable SFRs?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the internal RAM organization of 8051. Differentiate between the bit-addressable and byte-addressable regions. Why is the stack pointer reinitialized before use in typical C programs?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]**

**Definition (1 mark):** SFRs are on-chip registers occupying addresses **80H to FFH** that control peripherals, I/O ports, timers, serial port, and interrupts. They are accessed using direct addressing mode.

**Ten SFRs (1 mark for list + 3 marks for correct address-function pairs):**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|-----|---------|----------|
| ACC | E0H | Accumulator, primary ALU operand |
| B | F0H | Used in MUL AB and DIV AB |
| SP | 81H | Stack Pointer, top of internal RAM stack |
| DPL | 82H | Data Pointer low byte |
| DPH | 83H | Data Pointer high byte |
| PSW | D0H | Program Status Word, flags and bank select |
| P0 | 80H | Port 0, multiplexed AD0–AD7 |
| P1 | 90H | Port 1, general purpose I/O |
| SCON | 98H | Serial port control register |
| IE | A8H | Interrupt enable register |

**Bit-addressability rule (3 marks):** A direct address `X` is bit-addressable if and only if `(X mod 8) = 0`. This is because the 8051's bit-address generator decodes the lower 3 bits of the bit address (0–7) into the bit position and uses the upper 5 bits as a base address that must be a multiple of 8. Hence, only addresses ending in **0H or 8H** within 80H–FFH are bit-addressable (e.g., 80H, 88H, 90H, 98H, A0H, A8H, B0H, B8H, C0H, C8H, D0H, D8H, E0H, E8H, F0H, F8H).

**(b) [7 Marks]**

**Internal RAM organization (4 marks):**
- **00H–1FH (32 bytes):** Four register banks of 8 bytes each.
- **20H–2FH (16 bytes):** Bit-addressable region, 128 individually addressable bits.
- **30H–7FH (80 bytes):** General-purpose scratchpad RAM, byte-addressable only.
- **80H–FFH (128 bytes):** SFR region (only in original 8051; 8052 has 256 bytes RAM up to FFH).

**Bit vs byte addressable difference (2 marks):** In 20H–2FH, each bit has a unique bit-address (0x00–0x7F) allowing `SETB 0x05` style instructions, and the same byte can also be accessed byte-wise (`MOV 20H, A`). Above 2FH, only byte access is permitted (no bit instructions like `SETB 30H` are valid for scratch RAM).

**Why SP is reinitialized (1 mark):** Default SP = 07H places the stack in the register bank area, risking collision with active R0–R7 banks. C programs typically execute `SP = 0x60;` to relocate the stack to the safe upper RAM region (30H–7FH), preventing accidental overwriting of register contents and ensuring safe context saving during interrupts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warnings:**
> 1. **SFR address confusion:** Many students confuse the address of ACC (E0H) with its data. Remember — **address E0H** points to the Accumulator register. Always write "ACC is at SFR address E0H."
> 2. **Stack direction mistake:** The 8051 stack **grows UP** (PUSH increments SP). Writing "stack grows downward" is a 1-mark deduction in almost every KTU paper.
> 3. **Reserved bits:** Do not assume PSW.1 is always 0. Reading it returns undefined values; writing to it has no effect.
> 4. **Bank selection oversight:** When RS1:RS0 is changed, **previously pushed values stay in the old bank.** The stack itself is not relocated. This is the most common conceptual error in interrupt-related questions.
> 5. **Bit-addressable SFRs only:** 16 SFRs are bit-addressable, not all 128 bytes of SFR space. Statements like "all SFRs are bit-addressable" will receive 0 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST REMEMBER — Register Set (Module 4)**

- ☐ 8051 has **4 register banks** of 8 registers each (R0–R7), occupying **00H–1FH** of internal RAM.
- ☐ **Bank selection** is via **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** — base address = (RS1·2 + RS0) × 8.
- ☐ **Reset state:** SP = 07H, RS1:RS0 = 00 → **Bank 0 active** at addresses 00H–07H.
- ☐ **PC is 16-bit** (0000H–FFFFH) → can address up to **64 KB** of code memory.
- ☐ **DPTR is 16-bit** (DPL 82H + DPH 83H) → used for external data and code table lookups.
- ☐ **SFR space:** 80H–FFH, 128 bytes. **Bit-addressable rule:** address must be a multiple of 8 (end in 0H or 8H).
- ☐ **Bit-addressable regions:** 16 bytes of RAM (20H–2FH → 128 bits) + 16 SFRs (128 bits) = **256 addressable bits total**.
- ☐ **ACC and B** are the only registers usable directly in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`.
- ☐ **PSW bits:** CY(7), AC(6), F0(5), RS1(4), RS0(3), OV(2), Reserved(1), P(0).
- ☐ **Parity flag** indicates **even parity** of the Accumulator (set when count of 1s is even).
- ☐ **Overflow flag** is computed as **C6 XOR C7** for signed addition.
- ☐ **Stack grows upward** in the 8051 — PUSH increments SP, POP decrements SP.
- ☐ **Auxiliary Carry (AC)** is used by the `DA A` instruction to perform BCD correction.
- ☐ **Ports P0 and P2** serve dual roles as multiplexed address/data bus during external memory access.
- ☐ **Unimplemented SFRs** (gaps like 91H–97H) read as undefined values — a common KTU true/false question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Purpose Registers - Mode Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** General Purpose Registers - Mode Switching ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) In the **ARM7TDMI processor architecture** studied under KTU's PCEOT501 syllabus, **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** are a set of **37 registers** physically implemented in the register file, of which only a subset (typically 16) is *visually* accessible to the programmer at any given instant, depending on the current **processor mode**. **Mode Switching** is the mechanism by which the processor transitions between its **seven operating modes** by modifying the lower 5 bits `[4:0]` of the **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)**. Each privileged mode automatically *banks* (shadows) a private subset of registers (R13, R14, and R8–R14 in FIQ), enabling fast exception handling without the overhead of saving user context to memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** > *"The ARM processor contains 37 general-purpose registers, of which 20 are hidden (banked) registers reserved for handling exceptions. At any instant, only 15 user-mode registers (R0–R15) are visible. Mode switching is achieved by writing to the mode bits [4:0] of the CPSR."* The seven ARM processor modes are classified as: | Sl. | Mode | Abbreviation | Privilege | Purpose | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | 1 | **User** | `usr` | Unprivileged | Normal program execution | | 2 | **Fast Interrupt Request** | `fiq` | Privileged | High-speed interrupt handling | | 3 | **Interrupt Request** | `irq` | Privileged | General-purpose interrupt handling | | 4 | **Supervisor** | `svc` | Privileged | Operating system / software interrupt (SWI) | | 5 | **Abort** | `abt` | Privileged | Memory access violation handling | | 6 | **Undefined** | `und` | Privileged | Undefined instruction trap | | 7 | **System** | `sys` | Privileged | Operating system privileged tasks (uses User registers) | --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Multi-Department Government Office** > > Imagine a government office with **one main reception counter** open to the public (this is the **User mode** with its 15 visible registers R0–R15). > > However, when a **VIP arrives** (an IRQ interrupt), the receptionist hands over their clipboard to a **specialized back-office officer** (the **IRQ mode**). This officer has a **completely separate, pre-prepared set of files** sitting on their desk — *banked registers* (R13_irq, R14_irq, SPSR_irq) — which the receptionist never touches. The VIP is served immediately without disrupting the public queue. > > If a **more urgent VVIP arrives** (FIQ), yet another officer with an even bigger dedicated desk jumps in. When the officer finishes, they hand the clipboard back to the receptionist, and the public queue resumes as if nothing happened. > > **Mode switching = "Handing the clipboard to the right officer whose desk already has the right files pre-loaded."** --- ### 1.3 Key Architectural Metrics (Standard Values) * **Total physical registers:** **37** (User-mode visible: **15**, Banked: **20**, SPSRs: **5**, CPSR: **1**). * **Register width:** **32 bits** each. * **Mode bits location in CPSR:** bits **[4:0]**. * **I-bit (IRQ disable) location in CPSR:** bit **[7]**. * **F-bit (FIQ disable) location in CPSR:** bit **[6]**. * **Thumb bit (T) location in CPSR:** bit **[5]** (0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — The Register Banking Principle The ARM core achieves high interrupt latency performance through a hardware technique called **register banking**. The logical mechanics are: * **Step 1 — Mode Field Identification:** The hardware continuously decodes bits `[4:0]` of the CPSR to determine the active processor mode. These 5 bits allow encoding of exactly $2^5 = 32$ possible modes, of which **7 are defined** in the ARM7TDMI. * **Step 2 — Register File Multiplexing:** The register file contains 37 physical storage locations. The mode bits control a **multiplexer** that routes the requested logical register number (e.g., R13) to the *correct physical location*: - In User/System mode → R13 maps to **R13_usr** - In IRQ mode → R13 maps to **R13_irq** - In Supervisor mode → R13 maps to **R13_svc**, and so on. * **Step 3 — Exception Entry:** When an exception is taken, the ARM core **atomically** performs: 1. Copies CPSR into the **SPSR\_\** of the new mode. 2. Forces the mode bits `[4:0]` in CPSR to the exception's mode encoding. 3. Forces the I-bit or F-bit high to **disable nested interrupts** of equal or lower priority. 4. Saves the return address (`PC + 4` for IRQ/Abort/SWI; `PC + 8` for FIQ) into **R14\_\** (the Link Register of the new mode). 5. Sets the PC to the **Exception Vector Address** (e.g., `0x00000018` for IRQ). * **Step 4 — Exception Return:** Performed via `MOVS PC, LR` (or `SUBS PC, LR, #4` for FIQ). The `S` suffix restores CPSR from SPSR and re-enters the previous mode, restoring the original register bank visibility. * **Why this works (the "Why" question):** Because the banked registers are physically separate, the exception handler's R13 (its own stack pointer) is **already valid** the instant the mode switches — no software "push" of registers is needed for the banked set, giving ARM its characteristic fast interrupt response. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Reference Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Cheat Sheet — Memorize the Mode Encoding Table Below:** **Table 2.A: CPSR Mode Bit Encoding `[4:0]`** | Mode | M[4:0] Binary | M[4:0] Hex | I-Bit Forced? | F-Bit Forced? | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **User** | `10000` | `0x10` | No | No | | **FIQ** | `10001` | `0x11` | **Yes (1)** | **Yes (1)** | | **IRQ** | `10010` | `0x12` | **Yes (1)** | No | | **Supervisor** | `10011` | `0x13` | **Yes (1)** | No | | **Abort** | `10111` | `0x17` | **Yes (1)** | No | | **Undefined** | `11011` | `0x1B` | **Yes (1)** | No | | **System** | `11111` | `0x1F` | No | No | > **Mnemonic (from MSB→LSB):** `User-F-I-S-A-_-Und-Sys` reading bits `[4:0]`. **Table 2.B: Register Banking Matrix** | Register | User | System | Supervisor | Abort | Undefined | IRQ | FIQ | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **R0–R7** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | **R8–R12** | R8–R12 | R8–R12 | R8–R12 | R8–R12 | R8–R12 | R8–R12 | **R8_fiq–R12_fiq** | | **R13 (SP)** | R13 | R13 | **R13_svc** | **R13_abt** | **R13_und** | **R13_irq** | **R13_fiq** | | **R14 (LR)** | R14 | R14 | **R14_svc** | **R14_abt** | **R14_und** | **R14_irq** | **R14_fiq** | | **R15 (PC)** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | | **SPSR** | None | None | SPSR_svc | SPSR_abt | SPSR_und | SPSR_irq | SPSR_fiq | > [!NOTE] > **Key Observation:** R8–R12 are banked *only* in FIQ mode. This is why FIQ can service a single high-priority source entirely without saving R8–R12 to the stack — saving approximately **5 register pushes** compared to IRQ. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility This register-banking strategy is the cornerstone of ARM's **deterministic, low-latency interrupt response**, making it the processor of choice for: * **Automotive ECUs** (engine control, ABS) where interrupt latency is critical. * **Hard real-time industrial controllers** (PLC, motor drives). * **Mobile baseband processing** where FIQ handles the most time-critical radio interrupt. * **Bare-metal embedded firmware** at the RTOS level. The CPSR mode bits also influence the **CPU security and privilege model** in modern Cortex-A series processors, where the same architectural heritage underpins TrustZone and EL (Exception Level) transitions. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Numerical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Decoding a Given CPSR Value **Problem Statement (KTU Model Style):** Given the CPSR contents of an ARM7 processor as `0x000000D3`, identify: 1. The current processor mode. 2. The state (ARM/Thumb). 3. Whether interrupts are masked. 4. The condition flags. **Step 1 — Convert Hex to Binary for Inspection.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CPSR} &= 0x000000D3 \\ &= 0x000000D3_{16} \\ &= 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 1101\ 0011_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Map Bit Positions (CPSR is 32 bits, bit 31 is MSB).** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bit Position} &\rightarrow \text{Field} \\ \text{Bit 31–28} &\rightarrow N, Z, C, V \text{ (Condition Flags)} \\ \text{Bit 7} &\rightarrow I \text{ (IRQ Disable)} \\ \text{Bit 6} &\rightarrow F \text{ (FIQ Disable)} \\ \text{Bit 5} &\rightarrow T \text{ (Thumb State)} \\ \text{Bit 4–0} &\rightarrow M[4:0] \text{ (Mode)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Extract the Mode Field `M[4:0]`.** Reading the lower 5 bits of `...1101\ 0011`: $$ M[4:0] = 10011_2 = 0x13_{16} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{Supervisor (svc) mode} $$ **Step 4 — Extract the Thumb Bit `T`.** Bit 5 of the binary `1101\ 0011` (counting from LSB=0): $$ T = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{ARM state (32-bit instructions)} $$ **Step 5 — Extract Interrupt Mask Bits.** $$ I = \text{Bit 7} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{IRQ Disabled} $$ $$ F = \text{Bit 6} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \textbf{FIQ Enabled} $$ **Step 6 — Extract Condition Flags `NZCV` (Bits 31–28).** The top nibble of the 32-bit word is `0000`: $$ N = 0,\ Z = 0,\ C = 0,\ V = 0 $$ **Final Compiled Answer:** | Field | Value | Interpretation | |:---|:---:|:---| | Mode `[4:0]` | `10011` | **Supervisor Mode** | | T (bit 5) | `0` | **ARM State** | | F (bit 6) | `0` | **FIQ Enabled** | | I (bit 7) | `1` | **IRQ Disabled** | | N, Z, C, V | `0,0,0,0` | **No condition flags set** | --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — 7 Decoding Practice Examples Below is a comprehensive practice matrix. Each row is a self-contained "given CPSR, deduce state" problem — a common KTU problem pattern. | # | CPSR (Hex) | Binary (low 8 bits) | Mode | T | I | F | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | `0x00000010` | `0001\ 0000` | User | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 2 | `0x00000011` | `0001\ 0001` | FIQ | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 3 | `0x00000092` | `1001\ 0010` | IRQ | 0 | 1 | 0 | | 4 | `0x000000D3` | `1101\ 0011` | Supervisor | 0 | 1 | 0 | | 5 | `0x00000017` | `0001\ 0111` | Abort | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 6 | `0x0000001B` | `0001\ 1011` | Undefined | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 7 | `0x0000001F` | `0001\ 1111` | System | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 8 | `0x000000DF` | `1101\ 1111` | System | 0 | 1 | 1 | | 9 | `0x00000073` | `0111\ 0011` | Supervisor | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Verification of Row 9 (a tricky one):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mode bits} &= 10011_2 = \text{Supervisor} \\ T &= 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Thumb State (16-bit instructions)} \\ I &= 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{IRQ Disabled} \\ F &= 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{FIQ Disabled} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick:** Notice that the **Thumb bit (T)** sits at position 5, *between* the Mode field (`[4:0]`) and the Interrupt masks (`[7:6]`). Always extract mode FIRST, then state, then mask bits — in that order. This avoids misreading the wrong nibble. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Verifying Total Register Count **Problem:** Verify that ARM7TDMI has exactly 37 registers. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Counting logic:} \\ \text{Unbanked registers shared by all modes} &= R0\text{ to }R7 + R15 = 9 \\ \text{User/System shared R8–R14} &= 7 \\ \text{FIQ-banked extras (R8\_fiq to R14\_fiq)} &= 7 \\ \text{Other privileged mode banks (R13\_irq, R13\_svc, R13\_abt, R13\_und,} \\ \text{R14\_irq, R14\_svc, R14\_abt, R14\_und)} &= 2 \times 4 = 8 \\ \text{CPSR (always present)} &= 1 \\ \text{SPSR (one per privileged mode except User/System)} &= 5 \\ \text{Total} &= 9 + 7 + 7 + 8 + 1 + 5 \\ &= \textbf{37 registers} \end{aligned} $$ This proves the canonical ARM7 register file size cited in KTU study materials. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Mode Switching Logic Flow The diagram below illustrates the complete logic chain when the ARM7TDMI core processes a hardware interrupt, showing how the mode bits propagate through the register-file multiplexer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Exception Source
FIQ or IRQ or SWI or ABORT or UNDEF"] --> B["Hardware Detects Exception"] B --> C["Copy CPSR to SPSR_target_mode"] C --> D["Force Mode Bits M4 to M0 in CPSR
to target mode encoding"] D --> E["Set I-bit or F-bit to disable nested IRQs"] E --> F["Save Return Address PC_plus_offset into R14_LR_target"] F --> G["Load PC with Exception Vector Address"] G --> H["Register Bank Multiplexer Switches
