R13 and R14 and R8 to R14 to banked copies"] H --> I["Exception Handler Executes in Privileged Mode"] I --> J["Handler Executes MOVS PC LR or SUBS PC LR 4"] J --> K["Restore CPSR from SPSR of old mode"] K --> L["Restore Previous Mode Bits in CPSR"] L --> M["Register Bank Multiplexer Restores User Registers"] M --> N["Original Program Resumes Execution"] ``` --- ### 4.2 Register Banking Visual Map The diagram below shows the visible register window from each mode's perspective, highlighting which registers are banked (shadowed) and which are shared. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USR_SYS["USER and SYSTEM MODE - 16 Registers Visible"] uR0["R0"] --- uR1["R1"] --- uR2["R2"] --- uR3["R3"] uR4["R4"] --- uR5["R5"] --- uR6["R6"] --- uR7["R7"] uR8["R8"] --- uR9["R9"] --- uR10["R10"] uR11["R11"] --- uR12["R12"] --- uR13["R13 SP"] uR14["R14 LR"] --- uR15["R15 PC"] end subgraph FIQ["FIQ MODE - 7 Banked Extras"] fR8["R8 fiq"] --- fR9["R9 fiq"] --- fR10["R10 fiq"] fR11["R11 fiq"] --- fR12["R12 fiq"] fR13["R13 fiq SP"] --- fR14["R14 fiq LR"] end subgraph IRQ_MODE["IRQ MODE - 2 Banked"] iR13["R13 irq SP"] --- iR14["R14 irq LR"] end subgraph SVC_MODE["SVC MODE - 2 Banked"] sR13["R13 svc SP"] --- sR14["R14 svc LR"] end subgraph ABT_MODE["ABT MODE - 2 Banked"] aR13["R13 abt SP"] --- aR14["R14 abt LR"] end subgraph UND_MODE["UND MODE - 2 Banked"] unR13["R13 und SP"] --- unR14["R14 und LR"] end ``` --- ### 4.3 CPSR Bit Layout Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR N["Bit 31
N flag"] --> Z["Bit 30
Z flag"] Z --> C["Bit 29
C flag"] C --> V["Bit 28
V flag"] V --> RSV1["Bits 27 to 8
Reserved"] RSV1 --> IF["Bit 7
I - IRQ disable"] IF --> FF["Bit 6
F - FIQ disable"] FF --> TT["Bit 5
T - Thumb state"] TT --> MODE["Bits 4 to 0
M4 M3 M2 M1 M0
Processor Mode"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** **Explain the role of CPSR and SPSR registers in ARM processor mode switching. List any three processor modes supported by ARM7.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** * **CPSR (Current Program Status Register):** A 32-bit register that holds the current processor mode, state (ARM/Thumb), interrupt mask bits (I and F), and condition flags (N, Z, C, V). It is always accessible in privileged modes. * **SPSR (Saved Program Status Register):** A 32-bit banked register, one per privileged mode (except User/System). When an exception occurs, the CPSR is copied into the SPSR of the target mode so that the original CPSR can be restored upon exception return. * **Three processor modes:** 1. **User mode (usr)** — unprivileged normal execution. 2. **Supervisor mode (svc)** — entered on reset or SWI instruction. 3. **IRQ mode** — entered when the processor accepts an IRQ interrupt. **Distribution:** Definition of CPSR (1 m) + Definition of SPSR (1 m) + Three modes (1 m). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **What is register banking in ARM7? Why are R8–R12 banked only in FIQ mode?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** * **Register Banking** is the hardware technique of providing separate physical copies of certain registers for each privileged processor mode. When the mode changes, the same logical register number (e.g., R13) maps to a different physical storage cell. * **Why R8–R12 are banked only in FIQ:** 1. FIQ is designed to service the **single highest-priority interrupt source** that must complete without saving registers to memory. 2. By banking R8–R12, the FIQ handler can use these registers freely **without stacking them**, saving 5 memory-write cycles on entry and 5 on exit. 3. IRQ, by contrast, is expected to handle **multiple sources** and must save context via stack, so banking R8–R12 in IRQ would be wasteful silicon. **Distribution:** Banking definition (1.5 m) + FIQ rationale (1.5 m). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- **Q3.A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, ESE Module Question Pattern]** **With a neat diagram, explain the General Purpose Register organization of ARM7 processor. Discuss in detail how mode switching is achieved using the CPSR register, including the mode encoding for all seven modes.** **OR** **Q3.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module Question Pattern]** **Explain the seven operating modes of the ARM7 processor. With suitable diagrams, describe the register banking mechanism and explain why FIQ mode has additional banked registers (R8_fiq–R14_fiq) compared to other privileged modes.** --- ### Model Solution for Q3.A (14 marks) **Part (a) — General Purpose Register Organization (7 marks)** The ARM7 processor has **37 physical registers** organized as follows: * **R0 – R7:** Unbanked registers, shared by **all** modes. * **R8 – R12:** Shared by all modes except FIQ. In FIQ, they are replaced by banked copies `R8_fiq – R12_fiq`. * **R13 (Stack Pointer) and R14 (Link Register):** Banked in every privileged mode, giving a separate copy per mode. * **R15 (Program Counter):** Unbanked, shared by all modes. * **CPSR:** A single unbanked register holding current status. * **SPSR:** Banked — one per privileged mode except User and System. A diagrammatic representation: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SHARED["Shared R0 to R7 and R15 - All Modes"] S1["R0"] --- S2["R1"] --- S3["R2"] --- S4["R3"] S5["R4"] --- S6["R5"] --- S7["R6"] --- S8["R7"] S9["R15 PC"] end subgraph USER_BANK["User and System Modes"] UB1["R8"] --- UB2["R9"] --- UB3["R10"] --- UB4["R11"] --- UB5["R12"] UB6["R13 SP"] --- UB7["R14 LR"] end subgraph FIQ_BANK["FIQ Mode Extras"] FB1["R8 fiq"] --- FB2["R9 fiq"] --- FB3["R10 fiq"] FB4["R11 fiq"] --- FB5["R12 fiq"] FB6["R13 fiq SP"] --- FB7["R14 fiq LR"] --- FB8["SPSR fiq"] end subgraph PRIV_BANK["IRQ SVC ABT UND - Banked Pairs"] PB1["R13 SP and R14 LR per mode"] --- PB2["SPSR per mode"] end ``` **Part (b) — Mode Switching via CPSR (7 marks)** The CPSR's lower 5 bits `[4:0]` encode the current processor mode: | Mode | Encoding M[4:0] | |:---|:---:| | User | `10000` (0x10) | | FIQ | `10001` (0x11) | | IRQ | `10010` (0x12) | | Supervisor | `10011` (0x13) | | Abort | `10111` (0x17) | | Undefined | `11011` (0x1B) | | System | `11111` (0x1F) | **Mode Switching Sequence on Exception:** 1. **CPSR → SPSR\_\** (atomic copy). 2. **Mode bits updated** to target mode encoding. 3. **I-bit or F-bit set** to mask interrupts. 4. **Return address stored** in R14\_\ (LR). 5. **PC loaded** with exception vector address. **Mode Switching on Exception Return:** $$ \text{MOVS } PC, LR \quad \text{(atomic CPSR restore from SPSR)} $$ **Example — Supervisor mode entry CPSR = `0x000000D3`:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Binary} &= 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 1101\ 0011 \\ M[4:0] &= 10011_2 = 0x13 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Supervisor} \\ T &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{ARM State} \\ I &= 1 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{IRQ Disabled} \\ F &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{FIQ Enabled} \end{aligned} $$ **Mark Allocation (a):** Diagram (3 m) + Description of 37-register organization (4 m) = 7 m. **Mark Allocation (b):** Mode encoding table (3 m) + Switching sequence (4 m) = 7 m. --- ### Model Solution for Q3.B (14 marks) **Part (a) — Seven Operating Modes of ARM7 (7 marks)** ARM7 supports seven operating modes classified as: **(i) User Mode (usr) — Mode bits `10000`:** The normal unprivileged mode under which most application tasks execute. Cannot access privileged registers or change CPSR control fields directly. **(ii) FIQ Mode (fiq) — Mode bits `10001`:** Entered when a high-priority fast interrupt is raised. The F-bit is automatically set to disable further FIQs, and the I-bit is set to disable IRQs. **(iii) IRQ Mode (irq) — Mode bits `10010`:** Entered when a normal interrupt request is raised. The I-bit is set to prevent nested IRQs but FIQs remain enabled for higher-priority service. **(iv) Supervisor Mode (svc) — Mode bits `10011`:** Entered on **reset** and on execution of the **SWI (Software Interrupt)** instruction. The OS kernel uses this mode for system calls. **(v) Abort Mode (abt) — Mode bits `10111`:** Entered when the memory system signals a failed instruction fetch (prefetch abort) or data access (data abort). **(vi) Undefined Mode (und) — Mode bits `11011`:** Entered when the processor decodes an instruction it does not implement; used for software emulation of coprocessors. **(vii) System Mode (sys) — Mode bits `11111`:** A privileged mode that uses the User mode register set, allowing OS tasks to access privileged CPSR fields without banking overhead. **Part (b) — Register Banking & FIQ Extras (7 marks)** Register banking provides each privileged mode with **its own physical copies of R13 (SP), R14 (LR), and SPSR**, so context switching requires no register save/restore for these registers. **FIQ has *additional* banked registers R8_fiq through R12_fiq because:** 1. **Single-source optimisation:** FIQ is intended to service one specific high-priority interrupt source (e.g., a DMA controller or radio baseband). 2. **Zero-stack entry overhead:** The FIQ handler can freely use R8–R12 without first pushing them onto the stack, saving **5 STR + 5 LDR = 10 memory cycles** on entry/exit. 3. **Latency guarantee:** With all working registers pre-loaded, the FIQ routine begins useful computation in **1 clock cycle** of actual work, critical for hard real-time deadlines. 4. **IRQ comparison:** IRQ typically multiplexes many sources, so handlers must save R0–R12 and the return context anyway. Banking R8–R12 in IRQ would waste silicon area with no latency benefit. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["FIQ Entry - 0 Stack Push Needed for R8 to R14"] --> B["Use R8 fiq to R14 fiq directly"] B --> C["Execute FIQ Handler in Single Burst"] C --> D["FIQ Exit - 0 Stack Pop Needed"] D --> E["Return to Interrupted Code via SUBS PC LR 8"] ``` **Mark Allocation (a):** Listing 7 modes with bit patterns and purposes (7 m). **Mark Allocation (b):** Banking definition (2 m) + Four reasons for FIQ extras (5 m) = 7 m. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention SPSR** in the register list — students often cite only 36 registers instead of 37. > 2. **Writing R13_fiq for both R13 and R14** in FIQ — they are *separate* registers. R13 is SP, R14 is LR. Do not confuse. > 3. **Swapping I-bit and F-bit positions** — F-bit is at position 6, I-bit is at position 7. Mnemonic: **F-I → 6-7** (alphabetical order). > 4. **Forgetting that System mode has NO SPSR** — only 5 SPSRs exist (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND). User and System modes share the unbanked register set but neither has an SPSR. > 5. **Confusing LR offset** — For IRQ/Abort/SWI, the saved PC is `PC + 4`; for FIQ it is `PC + 8` (because FIQ pipelines one extra fetch stage). > 6. **Misnaming the modes** — The official ARM mnemonic for Supervisor is `svc`, NOT `sup`. Always use `svc`. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Pin This to Your Memory:** - **Total physical registers in ARM7:** **37** (Unbanked shared: 9, User/System R8–R14: 7, FIQ extras: 7, Other mode banks: 8, CPSR: 1, SPSRs: 5). - **Total visible registers in any mode:** **16** (R0–R15 in User/System; R0–R7 + R8_fiq–R14_fiq + R15 = 16 in FIQ). - **CPSR mode bits location:** `[4:0]` of CPSR. - **Mode encodings to memorize:** - `10000` = User, `10001` = FIQ, `10010` = IRQ, `10011` = SVC - `10111` = ABT, `11011` = UND, `11111` = SYS - **Thumb bit (T):** bit `[5]` of CPSR; **0 = ARM, 1 = Thumb**. - **I-bit:** bit `[7]`; F-bit: bit `[6]`. Both high = masked. - **Banked pairs:** R13/R14 exist in 6 physical copies (User + 5 privileged). - **FIQ-exclusive banks:** R8_fiq–R12_fiq (5 extra registers). - **SPSR count:** **5** (one per privileged mode EXCEPT User and System). - **Mode-switch trigger (hardware):** Exception entry → atomic (CPSR→SPSR, mode bits set, I/F set, LR set, PC = vector). - **Mode-switch trigger (software):** `MSR CPSR_c, #mode_encoding` (in privileged mode only). - **Exception return instruction:** `MOVS PC, LR` (general) or `SUBS PC, LR, #4` (FIQ-specific to compensate for `+8` offset). - **Vector addresses (ARM7):** Reset = `0x00000000`, UND = `0x00000004`, SVC = `0x00000008`, ABT = `0x0000000C`, (Reserved `0x10`), IRQ = `0x00000018`, FIQ = `0x0000001C`. - **Why FIQ is faster:** More banked registers + larger vector offset block + higher priority = faster, non-overlapping handling of the most critical interrupt. - **Most-tested fact in KTU papers:** "Total registers in ARM7 = 37" + "Mode bits are bits [4:0] of CPSR".

topics.title: Conditional Flags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conditional Flags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, **Conditional Flags** are single-bit status indicators housed within the **Program Status Word (PSW) register** — a special-function register (SFR) located at internal RAM address **`D0H`**. These flags are *implicitly* updated (set to logic `1` or reset to logic `0`) by the ALU following the execution of most arithmetic, logical, and data-transfer instructions. They serve as the hardware-level "memory" of the CPU, recording the outcome of the most recent operation so that the program can make decisions (conditional jumps, branches, loops) without re-computing the result.

The 8051 PSW is an **8-bit register** containing **6 conditional flags + 2 register-bank select bits**:

```
PSW [D0H] : CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | -- | P
              7    6   5    4    3    2   1  0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition to Memorize:**  
> "Conditional flags are 1-bit status registers in the PSW that reflect the result of the last ALU operation and are used by conditional instructions like `JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JB`, `JNB`, and `DJNZ` to alter program flow."

### 1.2 The 8051 PSW Register Bit Map (Byte Address `D0H`)

| Bit | Symbol | Name | Type | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **PSW.7** | **CY** | **Carry Flag** | Conditional | Set when an arithmetic operation produces a carry-out from bit 7 (addition) or a borrow into bit 7 (subtraction). |
| **PSW.6** | **AC** | **Auxiliary Carry Flag** | Conditional | Set when there is a carry-out from bit 3 to bit 4 (i.e., from the lower nibble to the upper nibble). Used implicitly for **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) correction** via the `DA A` instruction. |
| **PSW.5** | **F0** | **Flag Zero / User Flag** | User-defined | Available to the programmer for any custom purpose (general-purpose status bit). |
| **PSW.4** | **RS1** | **Register Bank Select 1** | Bank Select | Upper bit of the 2-bit code that selects one of the four register banks (RB0–RB3). |
| **PSW.3** | **RS0** | **Register Bank Select 0** | Bank Select | Lower bit of the 2-bit code. |
| **PSW.2** | **OV** | **Overflow Flag** | Conditional | Set when a **signed arithmetic** operation produces a result that overflows the 8-bit signed range `[-128, +127]`. |
| **PSW.1** | **—** | **Reserved** | Unused | Reserved for future use; user software **must write `0`** to this bit. |
| **PSW.0** | **P** | **Parity Flag** | Conditional | Automatically set/reset to make the accumulator (`A`) contain an **even number of 1-bits** (even parity). |

> [!NOTE]
> The **P (Parity) flag** is unique — it is set/reset *with every instruction that writes to the Accumulator* and reflects the parity of the new value of `A`, **not** the operation's result in a generic sense.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> **Analogy — The "Scoreboard" of a Calculator** 🎯  
> Imagine you are using a hand calculator. After pressing `999 + 1 =`, the display shows `000` and a small red "CARRY" light turns on. That little light is exactly what a *conditional flag* is in a microcontroller.  
> - The **calculator's processor** is the **ALU**.  
> - The **red "CARRY" light** is the **CY flag**.  
> - The **"AUX" light** that blinks when the lower digits roll over is the **AC flag**.  
> - The **"OVF" light** for very large additions is the **OV flag**.  
> - The **"PARITY" light** is the **P flag** (it lights up if an even number of segments glow on the 7-segment display).  
> - The **"BANK" selector switch** (RB0–RB3) is the **RS1:RS0 bits**.  
> - The **"USER" programmable LED** is the **F0 flag** (you decide what it means).  

The CPU "looks at" these flags using conditional jump instructions — like a referee looking at the scoreboard to decide whether to blow the whistle (jump) or let play continue (fall through).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **PSW SFR Address:** `D0H` (bit-addressable — each of the 8 bits can be individually accessed with instructions like `SETB CY` or `CLR P`).
- **Reset Value of PSW:** `00H` (i.e., CY=0, AC=0, OV=0, P=0, RS1=0, RS0=0 → defaults to **Register Bank 0**).
- **Default Register Bank after RESET:** **Bank 0** (internal RAM addresses `00H–07H`).
- **Overflow Range for Signed 8-bit numbers:** `-128 to +127` (i.e., `-2⁷` to `+2⁷-1`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Functionality of Each Conditional Flag

#### 2.1.1 The Carry Flag (CY) — PSW.7

The **CY flag** is set (`CY = 1`) whenever an arithmetic operation produces a **carry-out of the Most Significant Bit (MSB, bit 7)**.

- **For `ADD` / `ADDC`:** `CY = 1` if the unsigned sum exceeds `255` (i.e., `FFH`).
- **For `SUBB`:** `CY = 1` if a borrow was required (i.e., minuend < subtrahend in unsigned subtraction).
- **For `MUL AB`:** `CY = 0` always (overflow is stored in `OV`); however, if the product is greater than `255`, the high byte goes to `B` and `OV` is set.
- **For `DIV AB`:** `CY = 0` always; `OV` is set if the divisor (`B`) was `0`.
- **For `RLC A` / `RRC A`:** CY receives the bit rotated out of the accumulator.
- **For Boolean bit operations (`ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`, `MOV C,bit`):** CY is the *destination* (and/or source) operand.
- **It is the only flag that can be directly complemented** with the `CPL C` instruction.

#### 2.1.2 The Auxiliary Carry Flag (AC) — PSW.6

The **AC flag** indicates a **carry (or borrow) from bit 3 to bit 4** of the result. It is the lower-nibble equivalent of CY.

- **Critical Use Case:** It is required by the **`DA A` (Decimal-Adjust Accumulator)** instruction to perform valid **BCD arithmetic**.
- `DA A` adds `00H`, `06H`, `60H`, or `66H` to `A` depending on the state of CY and AC, so the result is a valid BCD number.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** If asked *"Why is AC flag important?"* the model answer is: *"AC flag is essential for BCD arithmetic using the `DA A` instruction because it detects the lower-nibble-to-upper-nibble carry that occurs when a BCD digit exceeds 9."*

#### 2.1.3 The Overflow Flag (OV) — PSW.2

The **OV flag** is set when a **signed** arithmetic operation produces a result that **cannot be represented in 8-bit two's complement form** (i.e., it falls outside the range `[-128, +127]`).

- For **unsigned** arithmetic, ignore OV and use CY.
- For **signed** arithmetic, ignore CY and use OV.
- **Formula:**  
  $$\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6$$  
  where $C_7$ = carry into bit 7 and $C_6$ = carry into bit 6 (from the lower 6 bits).

- For `ADD`/`ADDC`: `OV = 1` when **both operands have the same sign** but the result has the **opposite sign**.
- For `SUBB`: `OV = 1` when the minuend and subtrahend have **different signs** and the result has the **same sign as the subtrahend**.

#### 2.1.4 The Parity Flag (P) — PSW.0

- After any instruction that writes to the Accumulator (`A`), the P flag is **automatically set** to make the total number of `1`-bits in `A` an **even number**.
- Example: If `A = 1001 0101` (four `1`-bits, already even), then `P = 0`? — **No!** P is set so that `A + P` (treating P as bit 0 of A) has an even count.
- Standard rule: P = 1 if the number of `1`s in `A` is **odd**; P = 0 if the number of `1`s in `A` is **even**.
- This is useful for **serial communication parity checking** protocols.

#### 2.1.5 User Flag (F0) — PSW.5

- A general-purpose flag completely under software control.
- Use it as a custom boolean, semaphore, or mode indicator.
- Instructions: `SETB F0`, `CLR F0`, `MOV C, F0`, `MOV F0, C`, `ANL F0, /bit` (only with bit), etc.

#### 2.1.6 Register Bank Select Bits (RS1, RS0) — PSW.4, PSW.3

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | RAM Address Range (R0–R7) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | `00H` – `07H` |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | `08H` – `0FH` |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | `10H` – `17H` |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | `18H` – `1FH` |

- Use `SETB RS1` / `CLR RS1` etc., or the entire PSW byte.
- Critical for **interrupt handling**: a different bank can be selected in the ISR to avoid pushing R0–R7 onto the stack.

### 2.2 Instructions That DO NOT Affect Flags

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following instructions **do not alter** any of the conditional flags:
> - `INC` (does **not** affect CY; it does affect AC in some references — verify per KTU module)
> - Data-transfer instructions: `MOV`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH`, `XCHD`
> - `DA A` (uses CY/AC, but does **not** alter them)
> - `JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, `JNZ` (control transfer — do not alter flags)
> - `NOP`

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Rule** | **Expression** | **Use Case** |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Unsigned overflow detection | $\text{CY} = 1 \iff \text{sum} > \text{FF}_{\text{H}}$ | Multi-byte unsigned addition |
| Signed overflow detection | $\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6$ | Signed 8-bit arithmetic |
| Parity flag rule | $\text{P} = \text{parity}(A)$ (P=1 for odd number of 1s) | Serial comms error checking |
| Register Bank select | $\text{Bank} = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}$ | Multitasking / ISR context |
| AC (auxiliary carry) | $\text{AC} = 1$ if carry from bit 3 to bit 4 | BCD via `DA A` |
| BCD correction | $A \leftarrow A + 00\text{H} \mid 06\text{H} \mid 60\text{H} \mid 66\text{H}$ | `DA A` instruction |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Multi-byte Arithmetic:** Carry flag chains `ADD` and `ADDC` to add 16-bit, 24-bit, or N-byte numbers.
2. **BCD Arithmetic for Real-Time Clocks (RTCs):** Time is kept in BCD; AC + `DA A` keeps arithmetic valid.
3. **Serial Communication (UART):** P flag is used in software-implemented parity check (`MOV C, P`).
4. **Decision-making in Control Loops:** Conditional jumps (`JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `DJNZ`) read these flags.
5. **Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs):** Switching RS1:RS0 to Bank 1/2/3 lets the ISR use a private set of R0–R7 — saves stack push/pop cycles.
6. **Lookup Table Indexing (with `JC`/`JNC`):** Carry flag is used as a 1-bit branch selector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Carry Flag Behavior in `ADD A, R0`

> **Problem:** `A = FFH`, `R0 = 02H`. Show the binary addition, identify CY, AC, and P.

**Step 1 — Convert operands to binary:**

$$
A = \text{FFH} = 1111\,1111_2
$$

$$
R0 = 02H = 0000\,0010_2
$$

**Step 2 — Perform binary addition bit-by-bit (LSB → MSB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 1 + 0 = 1,\ \text{no carry} \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 1 + 1 = 0,\ \text{carry } 1 \to C_1 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1 + 0 + C_1 = 0,\ \text{carry } 1 \to C_2 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 1 + 0 + C_2 = 0,\ \text{carry } 1 \to C_3 \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 1 + 0 + C_3 = 0,\ \text{carry } 1 \to C_4 \quad \text{← AC SET HERE (carry from bit 3 to 4)} \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1 + 0 + C_4 = 0,\ \text{carry } 1 \to C_5 \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 1 + 0 + C_5 = 0,\ \text{carry } 1 \to C_6 \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 1 + 0 + C_6 = 0,\ \text{carry } 1 \to C_7 = \text{CY} \quad \text{← CY SET HERE}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final accumulator value and flags:**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 0000\,0001_2 = 01\text{H}
$$

| Flag | Value | Reason |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **CY** | **1** | Carry-out from bit 7 (unsigned sum 257 → fits only "1" + remainder 01H) |
| **AC** | **1** | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 |
| **OV** | **0** | $C_7 = 1$ and $C_6 = 1$, so $C_7 \oplus C_6 = 0$ |
| **P** | **1** | `A = 0000 0001` has only one `1` (odd), so P = 1 to force even parity of A + P |

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Overflow Flag ($OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$) — Why XOR?

**Setup:** Consider signed addition: `A = +7EH (+126)`, `R0 = +02H (+2)`. Expected result = `+128`, but this **exceeds** the 8-bit signed range, so OV must be `1`.

**Step 1 — Binary representation:**

$$
+126 = 0111\,1110_2, \quad +2 = 0000\,0010_2
$$

**Step 2 — Add bit-by-bit and track carries into bit 7 and into bit 6:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 0+0=0,\ C_1=0 \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 1+1=0,\ C_2=1 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1+0+1=0,\ C_3=1 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 1+0+1=0,\ C_4=1 \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 1+0+1=0,\ C_5=1 \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1+0+1=0,\ C_6=1 \quad \text{(this is the carry INTO bit 6)} \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 1+0+1=0,\ C_7=1 \quad \text{(this is the carry INTO bit 7)} \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 0+0+1=1,\ \text{no further carry}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

Wait — this gives `OV = 0`, but the signed result `1000 0000` represents `−128`, which *is* a valid signed 8-bit value! The two positive operands did *not* produce an overflow — they produced the most-negative number. So the formula is **correct** for this case. ✅

**Now consider a true signed overflow:** `A = +7FH (+127)`, `R0 = +01H (+1)`.

$$
+127 = 0111\,1111_2, \quad +1 = 0000\,0001_2
$$

Sum (ignoring final carry-out) = `1000 0000` = `−128` in two's complement. This **is** a signed overflow.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Carry into bit 6} = C_6 & = 1 \\
\text{Carry into bit 7} = C_7 & = 1 \\
\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Hmm — still 0? Let's recheck bit-by-bit carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 1+1 = 10 \Rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ C_1=1 \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 1+0+1=10 \Rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ C_2=1 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1+0+1=10 \Rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ C_3=1 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 1+0+1=10 \Rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ C_4=1 \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 1+0+1=10 \Rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ C_5=1 \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1+0+1=10 \Rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ C_6=1 \quad (\text{carry INTO bit 6})\\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 1+0+1=10 \Rightarrow \text{sum}=0,\ C_7=1 \quad (\text{carry INTO bit 7})\\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 0+0+1=1,\ \text{no carry-out} \quad \Rightarrow CY=0
\end{aligned}
$$

The sum is `1000 0000` = `−128` in two's complement. Sign bit flipped from 0 to 1 ⇒ **signed overflow**. But by the formula above, OV = 0. 

**Resolution:** The textbook formula for OV in **8051 ADD** is:
$$\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6$$
where $C_7$ is the **carry OUT OF bit 7** (which equals CY) and $C_6$ is the **carry INTO bit 6**. Let me re-verify with the actual convention used in Mazidi/McKinlay:

For this example, $C_7$ (carry out of bit 7) = 0 (since bit 7 sum was 0+0+1=1, no carry), and $C_6$ (carry into bit 6) = 1. So:

$$
\text{OV} = 0 \oplus 1 = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

So **OV = 1** is correctly obtained. The formula is:
$$\boxed{\text{OV} = C_7^{\text{out}} \oplus C_6^{\text{in}}}$$
where $C_7^{\text{out}}$ = carry-out of MSB (= CY for ADD) and $C_6^{\text{in}}$ = carry into bit 6.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Parity Flag Computation

**Given:** `A = 1011 0101B` after an `ADD` instruction.

**Step 1 — Count the number of `1` bits in A:**

$$
\text{Bit positions with 1: } \{7, 5, 4, 2, 0\} \Rightarrow \text{count} = 5
$$

**Step 2 — Apply parity rule:** P is set so that the number of 1s in the combination `(A[7:1], P)` is **even**.

- Current 1-count in `A[7:0]` = 5 (odd).
- To make it even, we need P = 1 (so the total becomes 5 + 1 = 6, even).
- Therefore: **P = 1**.

**Step 3 — General rule:**

$$
P = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if number of 1s in } A \text{ is odd} \\ 0 & \text{if number of 1s in } A \text{ is even} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Flag Behavior Across Key Instructions

| Instruction | CY | AC | OV | P | Notes |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `ADD A, src` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | All 4 flags affected |
| `ADDC A, src` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Includes prior CY |
| `SUBB A, src` | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | CY = borrow |
| `MUL AB` | 0 | — | ✓ | — | OV=1 if product > 255 |
| `DIV AB` | 0 | — | ✓ | — | OV=1 if divisor (B)=0 |
| `INC A` | — | — | — | ✓ | CY not affected |
| `DEC A` | — | — | — | ✓ | CY not affected |
| `DA A` | — | — | — | ✓ | Uses but does not change CY/AC |
| `ANL/ORL/XRL A, src` | — | — | — | ✓ | Logical ops don't touch CY/AC/OV |
| `RLC A` | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | CY receives rotated bit |
| `RRC A` | ✓ | — | — | ✓ | CY receives rotated bit |
| `CLR C` | 0 | — | — | — | Direct CY clear |
| `SETB C` | 1 | — | — | — | Direct CY set |
| `CPL C` | ¬CY | — | — | — | Complement CY |
| `MOV C, bit` | ✓ | — | — | — | Copy a bit to CY |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legend:** ✓ = flag updated, 0 = always cleared, — = not affected, ¬ = complemented.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PSW Register Bit-Level Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ALU["ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit"]
    OP8["8-bit Operand Bus"]

    subgraph SFR["SFR Internal RAM D0H - Program Status Word"]
        direction TB
        B7["PSW.7 CY - Carry Flag"]
        B6["PSW.6 AC - Auxiliary Carry"]
        B5["PSW.5 F0 - User Flag"]
        B4["PSW.4 RS1 - Bank Select 1"]
        B3["PSW.3 RS0 - Bank Select 0"]
        B2["PSW.2 OV - Overflow Flag"]
        B1["PSW.1 Reserved - Read 0"]
        B0["PSW.0 P - Parity Flag"]
    end

    subgraph BANKS["Register Banks in IRAM"]
        RB0["Bank 0 - 00H to 07H"]
        RB1["Bank 1 - 08H to 0FH"]
        RB2["Bank 2 - 10H to 17H"]
        RB3["Bank 3 - 18H to 1FH"]
    end

    subgraph JUMP["Conditional Jump Unit"]
        JC["JC rel - Jump if CY=1"]
        JNC["JNC rel - Jump if CY=0"]
        JZ["JZ rel - Jump if A=0"]
        JNZ["JNZ rel - Jump if A!=0"]
        JB["JB bit rel - Jump if bit=1"]
        JNB["JNB bit rel - Jump if bit=0"]
        DJNZ["DJNZ Rn rel - Decrement and Jump if not zero"]
    end

    ALU -- "result bits" --> OP8
    OP8 -- "bit 7 carry" --> B7
    OP8 -- "bit 3 to 4 carry" --> B6
    OP8 -- "bit 7 XOR bit 6 carry" --> B2
    OP8 -- "parity of result" --> B0

    B4 -- "RS1" --> BANKS
    B3 -- "RS0" --> BANKS
    RB0 ~~~ RB1 ~~~ RB2 ~~~ RB3

    B7 --> JC
    B7 --> JNC
    B5 --> JB
    B5 --> JNB
    B0 --> JB
    B0 --> JNB
```

### 4.2 Flag-Decision Flow for Arithmetic Operation (Cascading Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Opcode Fetch ADD/SUBB/MUL"] --> B["ALU Executes Operation"]
    B --> C{"Check Unsigned Overflow"}
    C -- "Carry out of bit 7?" --> C1["Set CY = 1"] & C0["CY = 0"]
    B --> D{"Check BCD Carry"}
    D -- "Carry from bit 3 to 4?" --> D1["Set AC = 1"] & D0["AC = 0"]
    B --> E{"Check Signed Overflow"}
    E -- "C7 XOR C6?" --> E1["Set OV = 1"] & E0["OV = 0"]
    B --> F["Write result to Accumulator A"]
    F --> G["Compute parity of A"]
    G --> H1["P = 1 - odd 1s"] & H0["P = 0 - even 1s"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C1 fill:#ffcccc
    style D1 fill:#ffcccc
    style E1 fill:#ffcccc
    style H1 fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.3 Register Bank Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RS1["PSW.4 - RS1"] --> MUX
    RS0["PSW.3 - RS0"]
    MUX{"2-to-4 Decoder"}
    RS0 --> MUX
    MUX --> B0["Select Bank 0 - 00H"]
    MUX --> B1["Select Bank 1 - 08H"]
    MUX --> B2["Select Bank 2 - 10H"]
    MUX --> B3["Select Bank 3 - 18H"]
    B0 --> R["R0 to R7 Active for current context"]
    B1 --> R
    B2 --> R
    B3 --> R
```

### 4.4 Conditional Flag Interaction with the Instruction Decoder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IR["Instruction Register - Opcode Byte"]
    ID["Instruction Decoder - Hardwired PLA"]
    subgraph FLAGS["Program Status Word D0H"]
        CY["CY"]
        AC["AC"]
        OV["OV"]
        P["P"]
        F0["F0"]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL["Program Control Unit"]
        PC["Program Counter"]
    end
    subgraph JUMPS["Conditional Branch Set"]
        JC["JC"]
        JNC["JNC"]
        JB["JB"]
        JNB["JNB"]
        JBC["JBC"]
        CJNE["CJNE"]
    end

    IR --> ID
    ID -- "test CY" --> JC
    ID -- "test !CY" --> JNC
    ID -- "test bit" --> JB
    ID -- "test !bit" --> JNB
    ID -- "test+clear bit" --> JBC
    ID -- "compare" --> CJNE
    JC --> CY
    JNC --> CY
    JB --> F0
    JNB --> F0
    JBC --> F0
    CY --> PC
    AC --> PC
    OV --> PC
    P --> PC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]
Explain the function of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register of the 8051 microcontroller. List all its flags with their bit positions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an **8-bit Special Function Register (SFR)** at internal RAM address **`D0H`** that contains status flags and register-bank select bits. It is *bit-addressable*, so individual bits can be set/cleared.

| Bit | Flag | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| PSW.7 | **CY (Carry)** | Set on carry-out from bit 7 in arithmetic ops |
| PSW.6 | **AC (Auxiliary Carry)** | Set on carry from bit 3 to bit 4; used by `DA A` for BCD |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | User-definable general-purpose flag |
| PSW.4 | **RS1** | Register Bank Select bit 1 |
| PSW.3 | **RS0** | Register Bank Select bit 0 |
| PSW.2 | **OV (Overflow)** | Set on signed arithmetic overflow |
| PSW.1 | **—** | Reserved (user must write 0) |
| PSW.0 | **P (Parity)** | Set to make even parity of Accumulator `A` |

> [!NOTE]
> *Awarding scheme:* 1 mark for PSW address + SFR identification, 1 mark for function, 1 mark for the bit table.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]
What is the difference between the **Carry Flag (CY)** and the **Overflow Flag (OV)** in 8051? When is each set?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Carry Flag (CY) | Overflow Flag (OV) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Type of arithmetic** | Unsigned | Signed (two's complement) |
| **Set condition** | Carry-out from bit 7 of the result | Carry into bit 7 XOR Carry out of bit 7 (i.e., $C_7 \oplus C_6$ for ADD) |
| **When to use** | When operands are treated as unsigned 8-bit (`0` to `255`) | When operands are treated as signed 8-bit (`-128` to `+127`) |
| **Example** | `A = FFH + 02H → CY = 1` (sum = 257, exceeds 255) | `A = 7FH + 01H → OV = 1` (sum = 128, exceeds +127 in signed range) |

> [!NOTE]
> A single operation can set **CY but not OV**, **OV but not CY**, **both**, or **neither** — they are independent.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **PSW register** of the 8051 microcontroller. Describe the role of **CY, AC, OV, and P flags** in detail.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Accumulator `A` contains `A5H` and register `R3` contains `7CH`. After executing the instruction `ADD A, R3`, determine the values of `A`, **CY, AC, OV, and P**. Show all intermediate steps.  **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Refer to Section 1.2 and Section 2.1 of these notes. The PSW is at `D0H`, bit-addressable, 8 bits. The full bit-map table earns 3 marks. Then describe each of the 4 flags (CY, AC, OV, P) in 1 mark each (use Section 2.1.1, 2.1.2, 2.1.3, 2.1.4).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Hex to binary conversion:**

$$
A5H = 1010\,0101_2, \qquad 7CH = 0111\,1100_2
$$

**Step 2 — Bit-by-bit binary addition with carry propagation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 1+0=1,\ C_1=0 \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 0+0+0=0,\ C_2=0 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1+1+0=0,\ C_3=1 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 0+1+1=0,\ C_4=1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{AC SET} \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 0+1+1=0,\ C_5=1 \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1+1+1=1,\ C_6=1 \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 0+1+1=0,\ C_7=1 \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 1+0+1=0,\ C_8=1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{CY SET}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Result and flag derivation:**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 0001\,0001_2 = 11H
$$

| Flag | Value | Derivation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **A** | `11H` | From sum above |
| **CY** | `1` | Carry-out of bit 7 (C₈ = 1) |
| **AC** | `1` | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (C₄ = 1) |
| **OV** | `0` | $C_7^{\text{out}} \oplus C_6^{\text{in}} = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$ |
| **P** | `1` | `A = 0001 0001` has two `1`s (even), so P = 0 to keep even parity... wait — `A = 0001 0001` has **two** `1`s. Count = 2 (even). So **P = 0**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students wrongly compute P. Remember: P=1 when count of 1s in A is **ODD**, P=0 when count is **EVEN**. With A = `0001 0001`, the count of 1s is **2 (even)**, so P = 0. Do not be misled by an initial "looks odd" impression — count carefully. **Common loss: 1 mark.**

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]

**(a)** Explain the role of the **Register Bank Select bits (RS1, RS0)** in the PSW. Show how to switch between register banks using an assembly language example. Discuss its use in **interrupt service routines (ISRs)**.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the following pairs of 8051 conditional instructions, with one example of each:
  (i) `JC` vs `JNC`
  (ii) `JB` vs `JNB`
  (iii) `JBC` vs `JB`
  (iv) `CJNE` vs `DJNZ`
  **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

RS1 (PSW.4) and RS0 (PSW.3) form a 2-bit code that selects one of **four register banks** in the lower 32 bytes of internal RAM:

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address | Default After RESET? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | `00H`–`07H` | ✓ Yes |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | `08H`–`0FH` | — |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | `10H`–`17H` | — |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | `18H`–`1FH` | — |

**Assembly example to switch to Bank 2:**

```assembly
        SETB    RS1     ;RS1 = 1
        CLR     RS0     ;RS0 = 0  → Bank 2 active
        MOV     R0, #55H  ;R0 now refers to RAM 10H, not 00H
```

**Use in ISRs:** When an interrupt fires, the 8051 automatically selects **Bank 1 (or any bank preconfigured via the ISR's prologue)** by setting RS1:RS0. This means the ISR can use R0–R7 in the chosen bank **without pushing them onto the stack**, saving cycles. The main program keeps using Bank 0; on `RETI`, the bank-switch is reversed.

> *Mark split:* Bit function (1), Bank table (2), Code example (2), ISR use (2).

**Part (b) — 7 marks (1.75 marks per pair)**

**(i) `JC` vs `JNC`:**
- `JC rel` — **Jump if CY = 1**. Used right after `ADD`/`SUBB` to test unsigned overflow.
- `JNC rel` — **Jump if CY = 0**. Used to continue when no overflow/borrow.
- Example: `ADD A, R0` followed by `JC OVERFLOW` → jumps if unsigned sum > 255.

**(ii) `JB` vs `JNB`:**
- `JB bit, rel` — Jump if the addressed **bit = 1**.
- `JNB bit, rel` — Jump if the addressed **bit = 0**.
- Example: `JB P, PARITY_ERROR` — branches if parity bit indicates odd count of 1s.

**(iii) `JBC` vs `JB`:**
- `JBC bit, rel` — Jump if bit = 1 **AND automatically clear the bit** to 0.
- `JB bit, rel` — Jump if bit = 1 **without clearing it**.
- Example: `JBC F0, FLAG_HANDLER` — acknowledges a user-flag event by clearing F0.

**(iv) `CJNE` vs `DJNZ`:**
- `CJNE A, #data, rel` — **Compare and Jump if Not Equal**; also sets CY if A < data.
- `DJNZ Rn, rel` — **Decrement Rn and Jump if Not Zero**; classic loop counter.
- Example: `CJNE A, #0FFH, LOOP` vs `DJNZ R2, REPEAT`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Conditional Flags (8051 PSW)**

- ✅ **PSW is at address `D0H`** and is **bit-addressable** — all 8 bits can be set/cleared individually.
- ✅ **CY** (PSW.7) = unsigned carry/borrow — chain `ADDC` for multi-byte addition.
- ✅ **AC** (PSW.6) = lower-nibble carry — used **only by `DA A`** for BCD arithmetic.
- ✅ **F0** (PSW.5) = user-defined; use it as a custom semaphore.
- ✅ **RS1, RS0** (PSW.4, PSW.3) = four register banks at `00H`, `08H`, `10H`, `18H`. Default = Bank 0.
- ✅ **OV** (PSW.2) = signed overflow. Formula: $\text{OV} = C_7^{\text{out}} \oplus C_6^{\text{in}}$.
- ✅ **P** (PSW.0) = even-parity of `A`. P = 1 when A has an **odd** number of 1s.
- ✅ **Logical instructions** (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`) **do not affect CY, AC, OV** — only P.
- ✅ **`INC` does not affect CY** (a common interview/KTU trick question).
- ✅ **MUL/DIV** clear CY but set OV if the result/divisor is invalid.
- ✅ **`JC`/`JNC`** test CY; **`JB`/`JNB`/`JBC`** test any bit-addressable location; **`CJNE`** compares two values; **`DJNZ`** decrements a register and loops.
- ✅ The reserved bit (PSW.1) must always be **written as 0** by user software.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple ALP Programs on Arithmetic & Logical Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simple ALP Programs on Arithmetic & Logical Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧩 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Assembly Language Program (ALP)** is a low-level, mnemonic-based symbolic representation of machine instructions that the **8051 microcontroller** can execute directly after passing through an assembler. In the context of **Arithmetic and Logical Operations**, an ALP is a sequence of mnemonics drawn from the 8051 instruction set that performs mathematical computations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, decimal adjustment) and bitwise logical operations (AND, OR, XOR, complement, rotate) on data held in the **Accumulator (A)**, **Register B**, internal **RAM**, or supplied as **immediate constants**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 4, students must be capable of writing, tracing, and debugging simple ALPs that demonstrate mastery of the **Arithmetic Instruction Set** and the **Logical Instruction Set** of the 8051 family, along with an understanding of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** flags affected by each operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 is an **8-bit** CISC microcontroller. All arithmetic operations are fundamentally performed by its **8-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, with the **Accumulator (A)** acting as one of the implicit operands and the destination for most results. Operations on **16-bit data** must be decomposed into a *low-byte operation* followed by a *high-byte operation with carry/borrow*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chef's Recipe"

Imagine a tiny chef (the 8051) standing in a kitchen (the CPU) with only **two hands** (Registers A and B), a **pantry** (Internal RAM 00H–7FH), and a **scrap notebook** (PSW flags) where he scribbles notes like *"did the pot overflow?"* or *"was the dish too sour?"* after each cooking step.

- The **Accumulator (A)** is the chef's *left hand* — it always holds one ingredient and is where the final mixed dish lands.
- **Register B** is the *right hand* — used only for multiplication, division, and as a temporary storage buffer.
- **PSW Flags** (CY, AC, OV, P) are the *scribbled notes* the chef keeps beside the stove to remember what just happened.
- An **ALP** is the printed recipe (sequence of mnemonics) the chef follows line-by-line. Each line (e.g., `ADD A, R3`) is one chop, one stir, or one pour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why learn this in Module 4?** Because every real embedded system — from a washing machine timer to an anti-lock braking system — must perform arithmetic on sensor data and apply bitwise logic for control. ALP is the closest a programmer can get to the *bare metal* of the silicon.

## 1.3 Classification of Operations Covered

| Class | Operations | Mnemonic Family |
|:------|:-----------|:----------------|
| **Arithmetic** | Add, Add with Carry, Subtract with Borrow, Increment, Decrement | `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC` |
| **Multiply / Divide** | 8×8 Multiplication, 8÷8 Division | `MUL AB`, `DIV AB` |
| **Decimal Adjust** | BCD Correction after ADD | `DA A` |
| **Logical (Bitwise)** | AND, OR, XOR, NOT (1's complement), Clear | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `CLR` |
| **Shift / Rotate** | Rotate left/right through carry, nibble swap | `RL`, `RLC`, `RR`, `RRC`, `SWAP` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the 8051 ALU

The 8051's ALU is an **8-bit binary adder/subtractor** built around a parallel array of **full adders**. It supports the following internal data pathways:

1. **Accumulator ← Accumulator ⊕ Operand** (where ⊕ is ADD, ADDC, SUBB, ANL, ORL, or XRL)
2. **Accumulator ← A × B** (MUL AB) — produces a 16-bit result split as `[B:A]`
3. **Accumulator ← A ÷ B** (DIV AB) — quotient in A, remainder in B

Every arithmetic instruction updates the **PSW (Program Status Word, address D0H)** selectively:

$$
\text{PSW} = \underbrace{CY}_{D7}\;\underbrace{AC}_{D6}\;\underbrace{F0}_{D5}\;\underbrace{RS1}_{D4}\;\underbrace{RS0}_{D3}\;\underbrace{OV}_{D2}\;\underbrace{-}_{D1}\;\underbrace{P}_{D0}
$$

## 2.2 Detailed Instruction Behaviour

### 2.2.1 Addition Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Flags Affected | Allowed Operands |
|:---------|:----------|:---------------|:-----------------|
| `ADD A, src` | A ← A + src | **CY, AC, OV, P** | Rn, direct, @Ri, #data |
| `ADDC A, src` | A ← A + src + CY | **CY, AC, OV, P** | Rn, direct, @Ri, #data |
| `INC byte` | byte ← byte + 1 | **None** | A, Rn, direct, @Ri |
| `INC DPTR` | DPTR ← DPTR + 1 | **None** | (16-bit) |
| `DA A` | BCD adjust | **CY** (and AC implicitly) | A only |

> [!NOTE]
> **The CY flag in ADDC is the *carry-out* of the previous operation**, allowing multi-precision arithmetic chains. It is *not* automatically cleared before the next ADDC.

### 2.2.2 Subtraction Instruction

$$
A \leftarrow A - \text{src} - \overline{CY}
$$

The 8051 has **no plain SUB** — all subtraction uses `SUBB` (Subtract with Borrow). To perform `A = A - src` cleanly, the programmer **must clear CY** (`CLR C`) before the instruction.

| Mnemonic | Operation | Flags Affected |
|:---------|:----------|:----------------|
| `SUBB A, src` | A ← A − src − CY | **CY (borrow), AC, OV, P** |

- **CY = 1** means a borrow was required (i.e., result went negative).
- **OV = 1** means a signed overflow occurred (operands of same sign produced a result of opposite sign).

### 2.2.3 Multiplication & Division

**Multiplication (`MUL AB`):**

$$
[A:B] \leftarrow A \times B
$$

- Operates on **unsigned** 8-bit operands only.
- A holds the **low byte** of the 16-bit product, B holds the **high byte**.
- **OV = 1** if product > 255 (i.e., B ≠ 0).
- CY is **always cleared**.

**Division (`DIV AB`):**

$$
A \leftarrow A \div B, \quad B \leftarrow A \bmod B
$$

- A = **quotient**, B = **remainder**.
- **OV = 1** if B = 0 (division by zero trap) — this is the only way the program knows division failed.
- CY is **always cleared**.

### 2.2.4 Decimal Adjust — `DA A`

The `DA A` instruction is the **BCD-correction** step. It inspects the lower and upper nibbles of A and the AC/CY flags, then conditionally adds **06H**, **60H**, **66H**, or **0H** to produce a valid BCD result. This is *only* valid **immediately after** an `ADD` or `ADDC` instruction.

**Rule table:**

$$
\text{DA A adjustment logic} = 
\begin{cases}
A \leftarrow A + 06H & \text{if (lower nibble of A)} > 9 \;\text{or}\; AC = 1 \\
A \leftarrow A + 60H & \text{if (upper nibble of A)} > 9 \;\text{or}\; CY = 1 \\
A \leftarrow A + 66H & \text{if both conditions true} \\
A \leftarrow A & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.5 Logical (Bitwise) Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | Flags Affected |
|:---------|:----------|:----------------|
| `ANL A, src` / `ANL direct, A` / `ANL direct, #data` | Bitwise AND | **P** only |
| `ORL A, src` / `ORL direct, A` / `ORL direct, #data` | Bitwise OR | **P** only |
| `XRL A, src` / `XRL direct, A` / `XRL direct, #data` | Bitwise XOR | **P** only |
| `CPL A` | A ← $\overline{A}$ (1's complement) | **None** |
| `CLR A` | A ← 00H | **None** |
| `RL A` | Rotate left within A (8-bit circular) | **P** only |
| `RLC A` | Rotate left through CY (9-bit) | **CY, P** |
| `RR A` | Rotate right within A | **P** only |
| `RRC A` | Rotate right through CY | **CY, P** |
| `SWAP A` | Exchange upper & lower nibbles | **None** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bitwise operations are performed on entire bytes**, not on individual bits — for individual bit manipulation, the 8051 provides a separate set of bit instructions (`SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C`, etc.) which are covered separately.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Range | Use Case |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------|:-------------|:---------|
| 1 | 8-bit ADD | `A = A + src` | 00H–FFH (0–255) | Basic addition |
| 2 | ADD with carry (16-bit) | Low: `A = A + src`; High: `A = A + src + CY` | 0000H–FFFFH | Multi-precision |
| 3 | SUBB | `A = A − src − CY`; clear CY first | 00H–FFH | 8-bit subtraction |
| 4 | MUL | `[B:A] = A × B`; OV=1 if > FFH | 0000H–FFFFH | 8×8 multiplication |
| 5 | DIV | `A = A÷B`, `B = A mod B`; OV=1 if B=0 | Q,R ∈ 00H–FFH | 8÷8 division |
| 6 | DA A | Add 06H, 60H, or 66H conditionally | BCD digit 0–9 | Packed BCD addition |
| 7 | AND for masking | `ANL A, #0FH` extracts lower nibble | 8-bit | Bit-masking |
| 8 | OR for setting | `ORL A, #80H` sets MSB | 8-bit | Bit-setting |
| 9 | XOR for toggle | `XRL A, #FFH` is 1's complement | 8-bit | Selective inversion |
| 10 | SWAP for nibble exchange | `SWAP A` ↔ `(A << 4) >> 4` | 8-bit | Packed BCD packing |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sensor scaling:** `MUL AB` followed by `DIV AB` is used to convert raw ADC values to engineering units (e.g., temperature, pressure).
- **BCD arithmetic in clocks/calculators:** The `DA A` instruction is the heart of every 7-segment display-based digital clock — binary addition alone produces garbage on a decimal display.
- **Bit-masking for GPIO control:** `ANL P1, #11110111B` clears a specific pin without disturbing others — used heavily in embedded firmware.
- **Parity checking in serial communication:** The hardware **P flag** in PSW is automatically set/cleared by every instruction, giving free even-parity generation for UART.
- **Checksum computation in network protocols:** XOR-folding of bytes (`XRL`) is the standard way to compute IP header checksums.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every program below is given with **line-by-line execution trace**. The trace is exhaustive: the contents of A, B, the PSW flags, and the relevant RAM cell are shown *after* each instruction executes. This is the exact format KTU examiners expect in the "Trace" sub-question of ALP questions.

---

## 3.1 Program 1 — 8-Bit Addition of Two Numbers

**Problem Statement:** Add the contents of internal RAM locations **30H** and **31H**. Store the sum in **32H** and the carry (if any) in **33H**.

### 3.1.1 Algorithm Derivation

We need to handle a *potential* carry out of bit-7. The 8051 ADD instruction does **not** automatically save CY to a RAM location — we must explicitly do so.

$$
\text{Result} = [30H] + [31H] \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = [30H], \quad A = A + [31H], \quad \text{then copy CY to RAM}
$$

### 3.1.2 Assembly Code

```asm
        ORG  0000H
        MOV   A, 30H       ; A ← [30H]
        ADD   A, 31H       ; A ← A + [31H], updates CY
        MOV   32H, A       ; [32H] ← A (low byte of sum)
        MOV   A, #00H      ; A ← 0 (clear for carry capture)
        ADDC  A, #00H      ; A ← 0 + 0 + CY = CY
        MOV   33H, A       ; [33H] ← CY
        END
```

### 3.1.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Trace

Assume `[30H] = A5H` (165) and `[31H] = 5BH` (91). Expected sum = `A5H + 5BH = 100H` → low byte = 00H, carry = 1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{MOV A, 30H} \\
              & A \leftarrow A5H \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\;\; \text{[32H,33H unchanged]} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{ADD A, 31H} \\
              & A \leftarrow A5H + 5BH = 100H \\
              & \text{8-bit truncation: } A = 00H,\; CY = 1,\; AC = 1,\; P = 0 \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{MOV 32H, A} \\
              & [32H] \leftarrow 00H \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{MOV A, \#00H} \\
              & A \leftarrow 00H,\; \text{no flags affected} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{ADDC A, \#00H} \\
              & A \leftarrow 00H + 00H + 1 = 01H,\; CY = 0 \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{MOV 33H, A} \\
              & [33H] \leftarrow 01H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final State:** `[32H] = 00H`, `[33H] = 01H` → 0100H = 256 ✓

---

## 3.2 Program 2 — 16-Bit Addition (Multi-Precision)

**Problem Statement:** Add two 16-bit unsigned numbers. The first number is stored in **30H (low)** and **31H (high)**. The second is in **32H (low)** and **33H (high)**. Store the result in **34H (low)** and **35H (high)**.

### 3.2.1 Algorithm Derivation

The 16-bit addition is split into two byte-wise operations chained through the CY flag:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low byte: } & A \leftarrow [30H] + [32H] \\
\text{High byte: } & A \leftarrow [31H] + [33H] + CY_{\text{low}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Assembly Code

```asm
        MOV   A, 30H       ; A ← low byte of first operand
        ADD   A, 32H       ; A ← A + low byte of second operand
        MOV   34H, A       ; store sum-low
        MOV   A, 31H       ; A ← high byte of first operand
        ADDC  A, 33H       ; A ← A + high byte of second + CY
        MOV   35H, A       ; store sum-high
```

### 3.2.3 Exhaustive Trace

Let `[30H]=34H, [31H]=12H` (number = 1234H) and `[32H]=78H, [33H]=56H` (number = 5678H). Expected sum = 68ACH.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{MOV A, 30H} \rightarrow A = 34H \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{ADD A, 32H} \rightarrow A = 34H + 78H = ACH,\; CY = 0 \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{MOV 34H, A} \rightarrow [34H] = ACH \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{MOV A, 31H} \rightarrow A = 12H \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{ADDC A, 33H} \rightarrow A = 12H + 56H + 0 = 68H,\; CY = 0 \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{MOV 35H, A} \rightarrow [35H] = 68H
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final State:** `[34H] = ACH`, `[35H] = 68H` → 68ACH = 26796 ✓

---

## 3.3 Program 3 — BCD Addition with `DA A`

**Problem Statement:** Add two packed BCD numbers **47** and **25** (stored as 47H and 25H in A and B). Store the BCD-correct sum in A.

> [!NOTE]
> Packed BCD stores two decimal digits per byte: 47H = decimal 47, not 0x47 = decimal 71.

### 3.3.1 Algorithm Derivation

For 47 + 25, normal binary addition gives:

$$
47H + 25H = 6CH
$$

But 6CH is *not* valid BCD (the upper nibble C = 12). The `DA A` instruction fixes this by adding 06H because the lower nibble of (47+25) = 0xC > 9.

$$
6CH + 06H = 72H \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{Valid BCD} = 72
$$

### 3.3.2 Assembly Code

```asm
        MOV   A, #47H      ; A ← 47H (packed BCD 47)
        ADD   A, #25H      ; A ← 47H + 25H = 6CH, CY=0, AC=0
        DA    A            ; BCD adjust → A = 72H, CY=0
```

### 3.3.3 Exhaustive Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{MOV A, \#47H} \rightarrow A = 47H \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{ADD A, \#25H} \rightarrow A = 47H + 25H = 6CH \\
              & \text{Binary: } 0110\,1100 \\
              & \text{Flags: } CY=0,\; AC=0,\; P=0 \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{DA A evaluation:} \\
              & \text{Lower nibble C > 9? Yes} \Rightarrow \text{add 06H} \\
              & \text{Upper nibble 6 > 9? No} \Rightarrow \text{no add 60H} \\
              & A = 6CH + 06H = 72H \\
              & \text{Flags: } CY=0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final State:** `A = 72H` → Decimal 72 ✓

**Counter-example — a case triggering 60H add:**
A = 99H, add 01H → 9AH. Lower nibble A > 9 → +06H = A0H. Upper nibble A > 9 → +60H = 00H with CY = 1.
Result: `A = 00H, CY = 1` → BCD 100 (carry 1, value 00) ✓

---

## 3.4 Program 4 — 8-Bit Multiplication & Division

### 3.4.1 Multiplication

**Problem:** Multiply **12H** (18) and **05H** (5). Result = 90 (5AH). Expected: low byte A = 5AH, high byte B = 00H.

```asm
        MOV   A, #12H      ; A = 12H
        MOV   B, #05H      ; B = 05H
        MUL   AB           ; [B:A] = 12H × 05H = 005AH
                           ; A = 5AH, B = 00H, OV = 0
```

### 3.4.2 Division (with divide-by-zero check)

**Problem:** Divide **C8H** (200) by **0AH** (10). Expected: quotient = 14H (20), remainder = 00H.

```asm
        MOV   A, #0C8H     ; A = 200
        MOV   B, #0AH      ; B = 10
        DIV   AB           ; A = 14H (quotient), B = 00H (remainder), OV = 0
```

**With overflow check:**

```asm
        MOV   A, #0C8H
        MOV   B, #0AH
        DIV   AB
        JNB   OV, OK       ; if OV=0, division succeeded
        ; ... handle divide-by-zero error here
OK:     ; quotient is in A
```

---

## 3.5 Program 5 — Logical Operations & Bit Masking

### 3.5.1 Extract Lower Nibble

**Problem:** Extract the lower nibble of the byte at internal RAM location 40H. Store at 41H.

$$
\text{Lower nibble} = X \;\text{AND}\; 0FH
$$

```asm
        MOV   A, 40H       ; A = XX
        ANL   A, #0FH      ; A = 0XH (lower 4 bits preserved, upper cleared)
        MOV   41H, A
```

**Trace:** Let `[40H] = 5AH`. `5AH AND 0FH = 0AH`. `[41H] = 0AH` ✓

### 3.5.2 Set the MSB (Bit 7)

```asm
        MOV   A, 40H       ; A = 5AH
        ORL   A, #80H      ; Set bit 7 → A = DAH
```

**Trace:** `5AH = 0101\,1010`, OR with `80H = 1000\,0000` → `1101\,1010 = DAH` ✓

### 3.5.3 Toggle All Bits (1's Complement)

```asm
        MOV   A, 40H       ; A = 5AH
        XRL   A, #0FFH     ; Toggle every bit → A = A5H
```

**Trace:** `5AH = 0101\,1010`, XOR with `FFH = 1111\,1111` → `1010\,0101 = A5H` ✓ (Note: this is identical to `CPL A`.)

### 3.5.4 Pack Two ASCII Digits into One BCD Byte

**Problem:** Convert two ASCII digits stored at `40H` and `41H` into a single packed BCD byte at `42H`.

**Example:** `'7' (37H)` and `'9' (39H)` → packed BCD `79H`.

**Derivation:** ASCII `'0'–'9'` = `30H–39H`. Stripping the upper nibble gives the decimal digit. To pack: swap upper digit left, then OR with lower digit.

```asm
        MOV   A, 40H       ; A = 37H (ASCII '7')
        ANL   A, #0FH      ; A = 07H  (decimal 7 in lower nibble)
        SWAP  A            ; A = 70H  (moved to upper nibble)
        MOV   R0, A        ; R0 = 70H (temporary save)

        MOV   A, 41H       ; A = 39H (ASCII '9')
        ANL   A, #0FH      ; A = 09H
        ORL   A, R0        ; A = 70H OR 09H = 79H
        MOV   42H, A       ; [42H] = 79H (packed BCD for 79)
```

---

## 3.6 Program 6 — Subtraction with Borrow

**Problem:** Subtract `[30H] − [31H]`. Store difference in `32H` and a "borrow occurred" flag in `33H` (00H if no borrow, FFH if borrow).

### 3.6.1 Algorithm Derivation

`SUBB` subtracts *both* the operand and CY. So we must **explicitly clear CY** first.

$$
A \leftarrow [30H] - [31H] - 0
$$

### 3.6.2 Assembly Code

```asm
        CLR   C            ; CY ← 0  (MANDATORY before first SUBB)
        MOV   A, 30H
        SUBB  A, 31H       ; A = [30H] - [31H] - 0
        MOV   32H, A
        MOV   A, #00H
        ADDC  A, #00H      ; Same trick as ADD — capture CY
        MOV   33H, A       ; [33H] = 00H or FFH
```

### 3.6.3 Exhaustive Trace

Let `[30H] = 30H (48)`, `[31H] = 50H (80)`. Expected: 48 − 80 = negative, so CY = 1, A = E0H (= -32 in 2's complement).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{CLR C} \rightarrow CY = 0 \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{MOV A, 30H} \rightarrow A = 30H \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{SUBB A, 31H} \rightarrow A = 30H - 50H - 0 \\
              & = -20H = E0H \text{ (2's complement)}, CY = 1 \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{MOV 32H, A} \rightarrow [32H] = E0H \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{MOV A, \#00H} \rightarrow A = 00H \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{ADDC A, \#00H} \rightarrow A = 00H + 00H + 1 = 01H \\
\text{Step 7: } & \text{MOV 33H, A} \rightarrow [33H] = 01H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: a non-zero value of [33H] indicates a borrow — the magnitude can be recovered by 1's-complementing or negating [32H].

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Common Programs at a Glance

| # | Program | Key Instruction(s) | Flags Tracked | Common Pitfall |
|:-:|:--------|:-------------------|:--------------|:---------------|
| 1 | 8-bit ADD | ADD, ADDC | CY, AC, OV, P | Forgetting to save CY |
| 2 | 16-bit ADD | ADD + ADDC | CY, AC, OV, P | Byte order assumption (LSB-first vs MSB-first) |
| 3 | BCD ADD | ADD, DA A | CY | Using DA A after anything other than ADD/ADDC |
| 4 | 8×8 MUL | MUL AB | OV | Ignoring that result spans B:A |
| 5 | 8÷8 DIV | DIV AB | OV | Not checking OV after DIV for B=0 |
| 6 | SUB | SUBB | CY, AC, OV | Forgetting CLR C before the first SUBB |
| 7 | Bit-mask | ANL, ORL, XRL | P | Confusing "AND to clear" with "OR to set" |
| 8 | 1's complement | CPL A, XRL #FFH | None | Mixing up CPL (no operand) with XRL |
| 9 | Nibble swap | SWAP A | None | Forgetting that result is in A, not in the operand |
| 10 | ASCII→BCD pack | ANL + SWAP + ORL | P | Doing ORL before SWAP — destroys upper digit |

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Internal Architecture & Data Flow (Functional Block Diagram)

The diagram below maps the **internal data paths** relevant to arithmetic and logical operations. It shows how the Accumulator, B register, PSW, and internal RAM interact with the ALU.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ACC([Accumulator A<br>8-bit register])
    BREG([B Register<br>8-bit])
    PSW([PSW Flags<br>CY AC OV P])
    ALU{{8-bit ALU<br>Arithmetic Logic Unit}}
    RAM[(Internal RAM<br>00H to 7FH<br>128 bytes)]
    SFR[(SFR Space<br>80H to FFH)]
    TMP([Temporary Registers<br>TMP1 TMP2])

    ACC -->|operand 1| ALU
    BREG -->|operand 2 for MUL DIV| ALU
    TMP -->|operand from RAM or SFR| ALU
    RAM --> TMP
    SFR --> TMP
    IMM([Immediate Constant<br>in instruction]) --> TMP

    ALU -->|result| ACC
    ALU -->|status| PSW
    ALU -->|high byte for MUL| BREG

    PSW -.->|CY feeds back| ALU

    classDef regStyle fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aluStyle fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef memStyle fill:#7FDBFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class ACC,BREG,TMP,IMM regStyle
    class ALU aluStyle
    class RAM,SFR,PSW memStyle
```

**Key reading of the diagram:**
- The **ALU** is the single computational heart. It has **two operand inputs** — one always from the Accumulator, the other from a *temporary register* loaded just before the operation.
- The **CY flag** is special: it loops back as an *operand* to `ADDC` and `SUBB`, making it both an output (status) and an input (operand).
- After `MUL AB`, the **high byte of the product is written to B**, not to the RAM or to a generic register.

---

## 4.2 Flowchart — Generic 16-Bit Addition Program

The flowchart below shows the **control flow** of Program 2 (16-bit ADD). It is what an examiner draws when asking "draw the flowchart for this ALP".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START])
    A1[Initialize program<br>no-op for this code]
    A2[A ← RAM 30H<br>load low byte of operand1]
    A3[A ← A + RAM 32H<br>ADD low bytes]
    A4[RAM 34H ← A<br>store result low byte]
    A5[A ← RAM 31H<br>load high byte of operand1]
    A6[A ← A + RAM 33H + CY<br>ADDC high bytes]
    A7[RAM 35H ← A<br>store result high byte]
    A8([END / HALT])

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8
```

**Reading the flowchart:**
- The **single CY flag** acts as the "thread of carry" that propagates the low-byte overflow into the high-byte addition.
- There is **no conditional branch** here — the program is linear because ADD and ADDC always proceed; the CY simply records what happened.

---

## 4.3 State Diagram — The DA A Decimal-Adjust Decision Tree

`DA A` is the most "branchy" single-byte instruction in the 8051 set. Its internal logic is best understood as a four-way decision based on the nibbles of A and the AC/CY flags.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0([After ADD or ADDC])
    D1{Lower nibble of A<br>greater than 9<br>OR AC = 1?}
    D2{Upper nibble of A<br>greater than 9<br>OR CY = 1?}
    D3[A ← A + 00H<br>no change]
    D4[A ← A + 06H<br>fix lower digit]
    D5[A ← A + 60H<br>fix upper digit]
    D6[A ← A + 66H<br>fix both digits<br>CY ← 1]
    D7([Exit DA A])

    D0 --> D1
    D1 -->|No| D3
    D1 -->|Yes| D2
    D2 -->|No| D4
    D2 -->|Yes| D6
    D3 --> D7
    D4 --> D7
    D5 --> D7
    D6 --> D7
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The "if both" path (D6) automatically sets CY = 1 because adding 66H always generates a carry out.
- The "upper-only" path (D5) can also generate a carry out if the upper nibble was 9 and adding 60H causes bit-7 to overflow — in that case CY = 1.
- `DA A` is *the* source of the "BDA bug" students hit in KTU exams when they try to use it after `INC A` (which doesn't update AC) — the result is silently wrong.

---

## 4.4 Register & Memory Map for the 16-bit ADD Example

| Address | Contents (after execution) | Role |
|:-------:|:--------------------------:|:-----|
| 30H | 34H | Low byte of operand 1 (1234H) |
| 31H | 12H | High byte of operand 1 |
| 32H | 78H | Low byte of operand 2 (5678H) |
| 33H | 56H | High byte of operand 2 |
| 34H | ACH | **Low byte of result (68ACH)** |
| 35H | 68H | **High byte of result** |

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. Explain the function of the `DA A` instruction in the 8051. Is it valid after every arithmetic instruction?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction corrects the result of a **packed BCD addition** so that the accumulator holds a valid BCD value. It works by inspecting the lower and upper nibbles of A along with the AC and CY flags, then conditionally adding 06H, 60H, or 66H.

- If the **lower nibble of A** is greater than 9 **or AC = 1**, it adds 06H.
- If the **upper nibble of A** is greater than 9 **or CY = 1**, it adds 60H.
- If both conditions are true, it adds 66H and sets CY = 1.

**Validity constraint (1 mark):** `DA A` is **valid only when used immediately after `ADD A, src` or `ADDC A, src`**. Using it after any other instruction (e.g., `INC A`, `SUBB`, `MUL`) gives **undefined / incorrect results** because the AC and CY flags are not in the expected state.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Differentiate between `SUBB A, R1` and the operation "A = A − R1" in terms of what the 8051 actually does.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 has **no plain SUB instruction** — only `SUBB` (Subtract with Borrow). The instruction

```asm
SUBB A, R1
```

performs the operation

$$
A \leftarrow A - R_1 - CY
$$

where **CY is the carry flag from the previous operation**, *not* a fresh borrow for this subtraction. Therefore, to compute the clean "A = A − R1", the programmer **must explicitly clear CY** before the SUBB:

```asm
CLR C           ; mandatory
SUBB A, R1
```

**Common pitfall (1 mark):** Forgetting `CLR C` is the #1 reason student code returns wrong results in multi-step subtraction chains, because a *stale* CY from an earlier ADD or rotate silently skews the answer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 marks, with internal choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Write an 8051 ALP to add two **16-bit unsigned numbers** stored in internal RAM locations 30H–31H (operand-1, LSB first) and 32H–33H (operand-2, LSB first). Store the 16-bit result in 34H–35H. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with an example, how the **OV flag** behaves in the `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 16-bit Addition ALP

**Algorithm:**
1. Load low byte of operand-1 into A.
2. Add low byte of operand-2 (ADD — generates carry if any).
3. Store the low-byte result.
4. Load high byte of operand-1.
5. Add high byte of operand-2 *with carry* (ADDC).
6. Store the high-byte result.

**Assembly Code:**

```asm
        ORG   0000H
        MOV   A, 30H       ; A = low byte of operand 1
        ADD   A, 32H       ; A = A + low byte of operand 2
        MOV   34H, A       ; store result-low
        MOV   A, 31H       ; A = high byte of operand 1
        ADDC  A, 33H       ; A = A + high byte of operand 2 + carry
        MOV   35H, A       ; store result-high
        END
```

**Trace example:** Operand-1 = 1234H, Operand-2 = 5678H → Result = 68ACH.

| Step | Instruction | A | [34H] | [35H] | CY | AC |
|:----:|:------------|:-:|:-----:|:-----:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | MOV A, 30H | 34H | – | – | – | – |
| 2 | ADD A, 32H | ACH | – | – | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | MOV 34H, A | ACH | ACH | – | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | MOV A, 31H | 12H | ACH | – | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | ADDC A, 33H | 68H | ACH | – | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | MOV 35H, A | 68H | ACH | 68H | 0 | 0 |

**Mark-split (7 marks):** Algorithm steps (2), Code (2), Trace table (2), Correctness comment (1).

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — OV Flag in MUL & DIV

**`MUL AB` (1 mark for setup, 2 marks for explanation):**

The `MUL AB` instruction multiplies the unsigned 8-bit values in A and B, placing the 16-bit result in `[B:A]` (B = high byte, A = low byte). Because the result occupies 16 bits but is stored in two 8-bit registers, the 8051 uses the **OV flag** to indicate when the high byte is non-zero — i.e., when the product exceeded 8 bits.

- `OV = 0` → Product fits in 8 bits (B = 00H).
- `OV = 1` → Product > 255 (B ≠ 00H).

**Example:** A = 64H (100), B = 05H (5). `A × B = 320 = 0140H`. After MUL: A = 40H, B = 01H, OV = 1.

**`DIV AB` (1 mark for setup, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for trap):**

The `DIV AB` instruction divides A by B (unsigned). The quotient is placed in A, and the remainder in B. The CY flag is always cleared. The **OV flag is set if B = 0** (division by zero trap) — this is the **only** way the 8051 signals that division failed, because the CPU cannot generate an exception.

**Example:** A = C8H (200), B = 0AH (10). After DIV: A = 14H (20, quotient), B = 00H (remainder), OV = 0. ✓
**Trap case:** A = FFH, B = 00H. After DIV: A = FFH, B = FFH, OV = 1. Programmer must follow DIV with `JNB OV, ok` to safely skip the error.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The OV flag is the 8051's only overflow indicator for MUL/DIV, and acts as a *trapped-error sentinel* for the divide-by-zero condition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Write an 8051 ALP to perform **packed BCD addition** of two two-digit BCD numbers stored in internal RAM locations 40H and 41H, and store the BCD result in 42H (with the carry, if any, in 43H). Show the trace for the input values **40H = 47H** and **41H = 25H**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to convert two ASCII decimal digits stored in internal RAM locations 44H (`'7' = 37H`) and 45H (`'9' = 39H`) into a **packed BCD byte** and store the result in 46H. Show the trace. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Packed BCD Addition ALP

**Assembly Code:**

```asm
        MOV   A, 40H       ; A = 47H
        ADD   A, 41H       ; A = 47H + 25H = 6CH
        DA    A            ; A = 72H (BCD correct)
        MOV   42H, A       ; [42H] = 72H
        MOV   A, #00H
        ADDC  A, #00H      ; capture carry
        MOV   43H, A       ; [43H] = 00H (no carry for this input)
        END
```

**Trace (4 marks):**

| Step | Instruction | A | [42H] | [43H] | CY | AC |
|:----:|:------------|:-:|:-----:|:-----:|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | MOV A, 40H | 47H | – | – | – | – |
| 2 | ADD A, 41H | 6CH | – | – | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | DA A | 72H | – | – | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | MOV 42H, A | 72H | 72H | – | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | MOV A, #00H | 00H | 72H | – | 0 | 0 |
| 6 | ADDC A, #00H | 00H | 72H | – | 0 | 0 |
| 7 | MOV 43H, A | 00H | 72H | 00H | 0 | 0 |

**DA A reasoning (2 marks):** 47H + 25H = 6CH. Lower nibble C = 12 > 9 → add 06H → 72H. Upper nibble 6 ≤ 9 → no add 60H. Final: 72H. Valid BCD for 72. Carry = 0.

**Alternative trace with carry:** If `[40H] = 99H` and `[41H] = 01H`: 99H + 01H = 9AH. DA A logic: lower A > 9 → +06H = A0H. Upper A > 9 → +60H = 00H with CY = 1. Result: A = 00H, CY = 1, AC = 1 → BCD value 100 (carry 1, digit 00).

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — ASCII to Packed BCD ALP

**Algorithm:** Mask both ASCII digits with 0FH, then shift one into the upper nibble and OR them together.

**Assembly Code:**

```asm
        MOV   A, 44H       ; A = 37H (ASCII '7')
        ANL   A, #0FH      ; A = 07H  (digit 7 in lower nibble)
        SWAP  A            ; A = 70H  (moved to upper nibble)
        MOV   R0, A        ; save 70H

        MOV   A, 45H       ; A = 39H (ASCII '9')
        ANL   A, #0FH      ; A = 09H
        ORL   A, R0        ; A = 79H  (packed BCD)
        MOV   46H, A       ; [46H] = 79H
        END
```

**Trace (3 marks):**

| Step | Instruction | A | R0 | [46H] |
|:----:|:------------|:-:|:--:|:-----:|
| 1 | MOV A, 44H | 37H | – | – |
| 2 | ANL A, #0FH | 07H | – | – |
| 3 | SWAP A | 70H | – | – |
| 4 | MOV R0, A | 70H | 70H | – |
| 5 | MOV A, 45H | 39H | 70H | – |
| 6 | ANL A, #0FH | 09H | 70H | – |
| 7 | ORL A, R0 | 79H | 70H | – |
| 8 | MOV 46H, A | 79H | 70H | 79H |

**Correctness (1 mark):** `37H AND 0FH = 07H` → swap → `70H`. `39H AND 0FH = 09H`. `70H OR 09H = 79H`. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deducting Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Missing `CLR C` before `SUBB`** → Marks deducted in *any* subtraction question. Examiners actively look for this line.
> 2. **Using `DA A` after `INC` or `MUL`** → Question explicitly says "BCD addition" — examiners will *intentionally* give inputs where the wrong usage produces a wrong answer, and deduct 2 marks if `DA A` is misplaced.
> 3. **Forgetting the 16-bit result split in `MUL AB`** → Many students write only `A = result`. You must mention that the **high byte goes to B** and that **OV indicates overflow**.
> 4. **Not handling `B = 0` in `DIV AB`** → Even if the question doesn't ask for it, a single line `JNB OV, OK` shows depth and earns a bonus impression mark.
> 5. **Confusing `ORL direct, A` with `ORL A, direct`** → The destination is the *first* operand. `ORL 30H, A` modifies RAM, not A.
> 6. **Wrong byte order in 16-bit addition** → Always state "LSB first" convention clearly. If the question says "MSB first", adapt the trace.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as a last-minute revision sheet before your KTU ESE.**

- ✅ The 8051 ALU is **8-bit**; all operations are byte-wise unless explicitly using `MUL` (16-bit result) or `INC DPTR`.
- ✅ The **Accumulator (A)** is *always* one operand and the destination for `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `RL`, `RR`, `CPL`, `CLR`, `SWAP`.
- ✅ `SUBB` **always subtracts CY** — clear CY before the first subtraction in a chain.
- ✅ `ADDC` **adds CY** to the result — do *not* clear CY between bytes of a multi-precision addition.
- ✅ `MUL AB` → result in **`[B:A]`** (B = high, A = low); `OV = 1` if B ≠ 0.
- ✅ `DIV AB` → `A = quotient`, `B = remainder`; **`OV = 1` means B was 0** (divide-by-zero).
- ✅ `DA A` is valid **only after `ADD`/`ADDC`**; adds 06H, 60H, or 66H based on nibbles + AC/CY.
- ✅ Logical instructions (`ANL`/`ORL`/`XRL`) affect **only the P flag** — they never touch CY, AC, or OV.
- ✅ `ANL` is for **clearing** bits (mask with 0), `ORL` is for **setting** bits (mask with 1), `XRL` is for **toggling** bits.
- ✅ `CPL A` (no operand) is identical to `XRL A, #0FFH` but cheaper in code size.
- ✅ `SWAP A` exchanges upper and lower nibbles — its main use is in **packed BCD packing**.
- ✅ **Rotate through carry** (`RLC`/`RRC`) treats A and CY as a single 9-bit register; rotate within A (`RL`/`RR`) does not touch CY.
- ✅ The **PSW parity flag (P)** is set if A has an odd number of 1-bits — useful for serial-parity generation.
- ✅ For **16-bit arithmetic**, the rule is *low-byte first* with `ADD`, then *high-byte* with `ADDC` (or `SUBB` with prior `CLR C`).
- ✅ Always **state the addressing mode** in your answer — examiners award partial credit for clarity.
- ✅ When tracing, show **every flag** (CY, AC, OV, P) that the instruction could possibly modify, even if unchanged — this signals mastery.
- ✅ **Don't use `DA A` after `SUBB`** — subtraction is binary; use 10's complement if you need BCD subtraction.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT501 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Addition # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition — Addition in 8051 Microcontroller** > **Addition** is an *Arithmetic Logical Unit (ALU)* operation performed by the microcontroller to compute the binary sum of two 8-bit operands and store the result in the destination register (typically the **Accumulator, A**). The 8051 instruction set provides several flavors of addition: direct `ADD`, add-with-carry `ADDC`, and the dedicated **Decimal Adjust Accumulator (`DA A`)** instruction for **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD)** arithmetic. In the 8051 architecture, addition is an **8-bit binary operation** executed by the hardware ALU. Although the operands are 8 bits, the 8051 internally extends the result to 9 bits using the **Carry Flag (CY)** to capture the overflow bit, and detects **signed overflow** using the **Overflow Flag (OV)**. The four addition-related instructions in 8051 are: | Mnemonic | Operation | Addressing Modes Allowed | |----------|-----------|--------------------------| | `ADD A, ` | A = A + src | Register, Direct, Register-Indirect, Immediate | | `ADDC A, ` | A = A + src + CY | Register, Direct, Register-Indirect, Immediate | | `INC ` | byte = byte + 1 | Register, Direct, Register-Indirect | | `DA A` | BCD correction of A | Accumulator only | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Cashier Adding Your Bill"** > Imagine a cashier adding up items on a bill. Each item has a **price** (up to 255 rupees — the 8-bit limit). The cashier writes the running total on a notepad. If the total exceeds 255, the cashier doesn't have a big enough column, so they make a small **carry mark** on the side and start fresh — that small mark is the **Carry Flag (CY)**. If the cashier is adding *signed* numbers (e.g., debits and credits) and the answer doesn't fit in the signed range, they put up a red flag — that's the **Overflow Flag (OV)**. The cashier's notepad is the **Accumulator (A)**, and the items being added are the **source operands**.
